This schematic traces the **transformation chain** from applied AC source to delivered load voltage, highlighting each intermediate stage of energy conversion.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Stage 1: Sinusoidal V1 applied to primary] --> S2[Stage 2: AC magnetizing current flows]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3: Alternating flux phi produced in core]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4: Flux cuts primary turns - self induced EMF e1]
    S3 --> S5[Stage 5: Flux cuts secondary turns - mutually induced EMF e2]
    S4 --> S6[Stage 6: e1 opposes V1 by Lenz law]
    S6 --> S7[Stage 7: Net primary current equals magnetizing plus load referred current]
    S5 --> S8[Stage 8: e2 drives current I2 through load]
    S8 --> S9[Stage 9: Load receives voltage V2 equal to K times V1]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram — Step-Up vs Step-Down

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SU[Step Up Transformer K greater than 1]
      SU1[Primary N1 fewer turns] --> SU2[Secondary N2 more turns]
      SU2 --> SU3[V2 greater than V1]
      SU3 --> SU4[I2 less than I1]
    end

    subgraph SD[Step Down Transformer K less than 1]
      SD1[Primary N1 more turns] --> SD2[Secondary N2 fewer turns]
      SD2 --> SD3[V2 less than V1]
      SD3 --> SD4[I2 greater than I1]
    end

    BIOMED[Biomedical Use Cases] --> SU
    BIOMED --> SD
    SU --> AP1[Defibrillator HV capacitor charger]
    SU --> AP2[X ray tube high voltage supply]
    SD --> AP3[Patient isolation 1 is to 1 or low voltage device supply]
    SD --> AP4[MRI gradient amplifier low voltage high current]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to "explain the working of a transformer with a diagram" in 14-mark questions, examiners expect **at minimum** the primary–core–secondary coupling with flux linkage arrows, labelling of $V_1$, $V_2$, $N_1$, $N_2$, $\phi$, $i_1$, $i_2$. The block diagrams above can be redrawn by hand in simplified form during the exam.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pattern Reminder:** Part A questions in KTU 2024 scheme ESE are **3-mark direct-answer questions**. They require concise, definition-style or short-derivation answers in **2 to 3 sentences** plus one supporting equation or diagram. Excessive writing wastes time.

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**  
*State and explain Faraday's Laws of Electromagnetic Induction as applied to the working of a transformer.* **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Faraday's First Law states that whenever the magnetic flux linking with a coil changes, an electromotive force (EMF) is induced in the coil. Faraday's Second Law states that the magnitude of the induced EMF is directly proportional to the rate of change of flux linkage.

Mathematically:
$$e = -N \frac{d\phi}{dt}$$

In a transformer, alternating current in the primary winding produces a time-varying flux in the core. This flux links the secondary winding, inducing an EMF that drives current through the connected load — this is the fundamental principle of operation. **(3 Marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]**  
*Define an ideal transformer. List any two assumptions made in its elementary theory.* **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

An **ideal transformer** is a hypothetical, lossless static device that transfers electrical energy from one circuit to another through mutual electromagnetic induction, with 100 % efficiency and no losses of any kind.

**Assumptions:**
1. The magnetic core has **infinite permeability**, so the magnetizing current required is zero and the core reluctance is zero.
2. The primary and secondary windings have **zero resistance**, so there are no copper (I²R) losses.

*(Any two valid assumptions from: no leakage flux, no hysteresis loss, no eddy current loss, infinite permeability, zero winding resistance — full credit.)* **(3 Marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and typically offers an *internal choice*. The two sub-parts (a) and (b) carry **7 marks each**. Below, two alternate questions are provided; in the actual exam paper, only ONE will appear, and you must answer the chosen one entirely.

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Derive the EMF equation of a single-phase transformer and state the meaning of each term. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The flux in the core is sinusoidal:
$$\phi(t) = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t)$$

By Faraday's Law, the EMF induced in a coil of $N$ turns is:
$$e = N \frac{d\phi}{dt}$$

Differentiating the flux expression:
$$\frac{d\phi}{dt} = \omega \Phi_m \cos(\omega t)$$

Hence the instantaneous EMF in the primary winding ($N_1$ turns) is:
$$e_1 = N_1 \omega \Phi_m \cos(\omega t)$$

The peak value occurs when $\cos(\omega t) = 1$:
$$E_{m1} = 2\pi f N_1 \Phi_m$$

Converting to RMS by dividing by $\sqrt{2}$:
$$E_1 = \frac{2\pi}{\sqrt{2}} \, f \, N_1 \, \Phi_m = 4.44 \, f \, N_1 \, \Phi_m$$

Similarly, for the secondary:
$$E_2 = 4.44 \, f \, N_2 \, \Phi_m$$

**Meaning of Terms:**
- $E_1, E_2$ — RMS induced EMFs (V) in primary and secondary.
- $f$ — supply frequency (Hz).
- $N_1, N_2$ — number of turns in primary and secondary.
- $\Phi_m$ — maximum (peak) flux in the core (Wb).
- $4.44$ — form factor constant ($4 \cdot K_f = 4 \cdot 1.11$). **(7 Marks)**

---

**(b)** A single-phase transformer has 500 turns on the primary and 1000 turns on the secondary. If the primary is connected to a 230 V, 50 Hz AC supply, calculate:  
(i) the maximum flux in the core,  
(ii) the secondary EMF on no-load,  
(iii) the transformation ratio. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $N_1 = 500$, $N_2 = 1000$, $V_1 = 230\ \text{V}$, $f = 50\ \text{Hz}$.

For an ideal transformer, $E_1 \approx V_1 = 230\ \text{V}$.

**(i) Maximum flux $\Phi_m$:**  
Rearranging the EMF equation:
$$\Phi_m = \frac{E_1}{4.44 \, f \, N_1} = \frac{230}{4.44 \times 50 \times 500}$$

$$\Phi_m = \frac{230}{111{,}000} = 2.072 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{Wb} = 2.072\ \text{mWb}$$

**(ii) Secondary EMF on no-load:**  
$$\frac{E_2}{E_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} \Rightarrow E_2 = E_1 \cdot \frac{N_2}{N_1} = 230 \cdot \frac{1000}{500} = 460\ \text{V}$$

**(iii) Transformation ratio:**  
$$K = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{1000}{500} = 2$$

Since $K > 1$, this is a **step-up transformer**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of an ideal transformer with a neat sketch. Derive the relation between primary and secondary voltages in terms of the turns ratio. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:**
When a sinusoidal AC voltage $V_1$ is applied to the primary winding of $N_1$ turns, an alternating current $i_1$ flows. This current produces an alternating MMF ($N_1 i_1$) which, due to the closed highly-permeable iron core, generates an alternating flux $\phi$ confined to the core. This flux also links the secondary winding of $N_2$ turns.

By Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction, an EMF $e_2$ is induced in the secondary:
$$e_2 = N_2 \frac{d\phi}{dt}$$

Simultaneously, an EMF $e_1$ is induced in the primary, which (in the ideal case) exactly opposes $V_1$. The induced EMF in the secondary drives current $i_2$ through the connected load.

**Voltage Relation Derivation:**

Both windings link the same flux $\phi$, so:
$$e_1 = N_1 \frac{d\phi}{dt}, \quad e_2 = N_2 \frac{d\phi}{dt}$$

Taking the ratio:
$$\frac{e_1}{e_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2}$$

For an ideal transformer, $V_1 = e_1$ and $V_2 = e_2$ (no internal voltage drops):
$$\boxed{\frac{V_1}{V_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2}}$$

This shows that voltages are directly proportional to the turns ratio. **(7 Marks)**

---

**(b)** A 5 kVA, 1100/220 V, 50 Hz single-phase ideal transformer is supplying a load of 4 kVA at 0.8 power factor lagging. Calculate:  
(i) the primary and secondary full-load currents,  
(ii) the magnetizing current (assume zero),  
(iii) the primary current actually drawn from the source. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** Rating = 5 kVA, $V_1 = 1100\ \text{V}$, $V_2 = 220\ \text{V}$, $f = 50\ \text{Hz}$, Load = 4 kVA at 0.8 pf lag.

**(i) Full-load currents:**
$$I_{1,\text{FL}} = \frac{S}{V_1} = \frac{5000}{1100} = 4.545\ \text{A}$$

$$I_{2,\text{FL}} = \frac{S}{V_2} = \frac{5000}{220} = 22.727\ \text{A}$$

**(ii) Magnetizing current (ideal case):**
For an ideal transformer, the magnetizing current is **zero** ($I_m = 0$) because the core has infinite permeability and zero reluctance.

**(iii) Primary current drawn from source:**

In an ideal transformer, the primary current equals the secondary current reflected to the primary side. The reflected secondary current is:
$$I_2' = \frac{I_2 \cdot V_2}{V_1} = \frac{22.727 \times 220}{1100} = 4.545\ \text{A}$$

Since the load is 4 kVA instead of full 5 kVA, the actual secondary current is:
$$I_{2,\text{actual}} = \frac{4000}{220} = 18.182\ \text{A}$$

Reflected to the primary:
$$I_{1,\text{actual}} = \frac{18.182 \times 220}{1100} = 3.636\ \text{A}$$

*(Note: The power factor 0.8 only affects the phase angle, not the magnitude — for an ideal transformer, $\vert I_1 \vert V_1 = \vert I_2 \vert V_2$.)* **(7 Marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the constant 4.44** — Students often write $E = 2\pi f N \Phi$ (peak value) instead of $E = 4.44 f N \Phi_m$ (RMS value). When the question specifies "voltage," examiners expect **RMS**. Using 2π yields **partial credit loss** (typically 1–2 marks out of 7).
> 
> 2. **Mixing up $N_1$ and $N_2$ in the transformation ratio** — Always define $K = N_2 / N_1$. Some students write $K = N_1 / N_2$ and then conclude the wrong direction of step-up/step-down. **Examiner's tip:** Define $K$ explicitly before using it.
> 
> 3. **Assuming $E_1 = V_1$ in non-ideal problems** — This is valid ONLY for ideal transformers. In real transformer problems (no-load phasor diagrams), $V_1 \neq E_1$ due to winding resistance and leakage reactance. KTU examiners often embed this trap in 14-mark problems.
> 
> 4. **Ignoring units** — Writing $\Phi_m = 2.072 \times 10^{-3}$ without stating "Wb" loses **0.5 marks**. Always include SI units.
> 
> 5. **No diagram in working-principle questions** — A 14-mark "explain working principle" question without a labelled diagram typically loses **2 marks**. Always include a clear sketch with $\phi$, $N_1$, $N_2$, $V_1$, $V_2$, and arrows for induced EMFs.
> 
> 6. **Confusing instantaneous, peak, and RMS values** — Use lowercase $e$, uppercase $E_m$, and uppercase $E$ (or $V$) respectively. Mixing these notations confuses examiners.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2, Ideal Transformer:**

- A **transformer** is a static AC device operating on the principle of **mutual electromagnetic induction**.
- **Ideal transformer assumptions:** infinite permeability core, zero winding resistance, no leakage flux, no core losses.
- **Working principle in 5 keywords:** AC supply → alternating flux in core → mutual induction → induced EMFs in both windings → power transfer to load.
- **EMF equation (RMS):** $E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_m$ — **memorize this equation verbatim** along with each term's meaning.
- **Transformation ratio:** $K = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{E_2}{E_1} = \dfrac{I_1}{I_2}$.
- **Golden rule of ideal transformer:** $\dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{I_2}{I_1}$.
- **Power balance (ideal):** $V_1 I_1 = V_2 I_2 \Rightarrow$ 100 % efficiency.
- **Step-up** ($K > 1$) and **step-down** ($K < 1$) — for biomedical isolation, $K = 1$.
- **Form factor constant 4.44** originates from $4 \times 1.11 = 4 \times \dfrac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}}$.
- **Biomedical relevance:** isolation transformers in ECG/EEG, step-up in defibrillators/X-ray, step-down in MRI gradient amplifiers.
- **Examiner traps to avoid:** wrong form-factor (2π vs 4.44), swapped $N_1$/$N_2$ in $K$, assuming $E_1 = V_1$ in real transformers, missing units, no diagram.
- **Key relation for problems:** $\Phi_m = \dfrac{V_1}{4.44 \, f \, N_1}$ — use this whenever voltage, turns, and flux must be linked.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constructional Features, EMF Equation, and Turns Ratio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT411 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY FOR BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Constructional Features, EMF Equation, and Turns Ratio ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Transformer (Static Electromagnetic Device):** > A transformer is a **static electrical machine** that transfers electrical energy from one alternating-current (AC) circuit to another through **electromagnetic induction**, without any change in frequency. It consists of two or more magnetically coupled windings (primary and secondary) linked by a common **magnetic core** made of laminated silicon steel. For a single-phase, two-winding transformer, the fundamental relationship is governed by Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction, where the magnitude of induced EMF in a winding is **proportional** to the number of turns, the frequency of operation, and the maximum flux in the core. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Voltage Gear-Box" Think of a transformer as a **voltage gear-box** for AC electricity, much like the gearbox in a car. Just as a gearbox changes the speed/torque ratio of a rotating shaft without touching the engine: - The **engine** is the AC source (mains). - The **gear ratio** is the **Turns Ratio (N₁/N₂)**. - The **output shaft speed** corresponds to the secondary voltage. - The **torque** corresponds to the current capability. If the secondary has *more turns* (overdrive gear), the voltage steps **up**. If the secondary has *fewer turns* (reduction gear), the voltage steps **down**. > [!NOTE] > **Why This Matters in Biomedical Engineering:** > Isolation transformers in **ICU/Operation Theatres**, **defibrillators**, **MRI gradient amplifiers**, and **patient monitoring systems** rely entirely on this voltage conversion principle. A typical **1:1 isolation transformer** decouples the patient from direct mains connection, preventing microshock hazards (currents as low as **10 μA** through the heart can be lethal). ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value | Significance | |---|---|---| | **Operating Frequency (f)** | **50 Hz** (India) | Standard mains frequency | | **Lamination Thickness** | **0.35 mm** | Reduces eddy current losses | | **Lamination Material** | **CRGO Silicon Steel** | High permeability, low hysteresis | | **Stacking Factor** | **~0.95** | Ratio of steel to total core volume | | **Standard Sine Form Factor** | **1.11** | Kf = RMS / Average of half sine wave | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Constructional Features of a Single-Phase Transformer A transformer is built from **two major sub-systems**: the **magnetic circuit** (core) and the **electric circuit** (windings), supported by auxiliary structures for insulation, cooling, and protection. #### 2.1.1 The Magnetic Core - **Material:** Cold Rolled Grain Oriented (CRGO) silicon steel with **3–4% silicon content** to increase electrical resistivity (reduces eddy current loss). - **Lamination:** Thin sheets of **0.35 mm** thickness, insulated from one another by a thin coat of **varnish or oxide layer**. This breaks the eddy current paths. - **Joint:** Alternating layers are staggered to avoid an "air gap" at corners (butt-lap joints). - **Two Core Topologies:** - **Core Type:** Windings surround a part of the core (good for **HV** applications). - **Shell Type:** Core surrounds the windings (good for **LV** applications, better mechanical protection). #### 2.1.2 The Windings - **Primary Winding:** The winding that receives electrical input from the AC source. - **Secondary Winding:** The winding from which electrical output is drawn. - **Conductor Material:** **Copper** (preferred for high efficiency) or **Aluminium** (cheaper, lighter). - **Winding Placement Rule:** The **LV winding is placed next to the core** (closer to the limb), and the **HV winding is placed on top of it** with additional insulation in between. - **Winding Types:** - **Cylindrical (Layer) winding:** Used for small transformers. - **Cross-over winding:** Used for small distribution transformers. - **Disc winding:** Used for HV transformers. - **Helical winding:** Used for LV, high-current transformers. #### 2.1.3 Insulating Materials - **Between core and windings:** Pressboard, varnished paper. - **Between turns:** Enamel coating on the conductor. - **Between windings:** Pressboard cylinders, epoxy resin. - **Class A insulation** (105°C) typically used; Class B (130°C) for special biomedical applications. #### 2.1.4 Transformer Tank and Cooling - **Tank:** Houses the core-coil assembly, filled with **transformer oil** (mineral oil) for insulation and cooling. - **Cooling Methods (as per IS 2026):** - **ONAN** (Oil Natural, Air Natural) — small transformers. - **ONAF** (Oil Natural, Air Forced) — medium transformers. - **OFAF** (Oil Forced, Air Forced) — large power transformers. - **Conservator:** An auxiliary oil reservoir mounted above the tank to allow for oil expansion. - **Breather:** Contains **silica gel** to absorb moisture from air entering the conservator. - **Bushings:** Insulated terminals (porcelain/epoxy) to bring out connections safely. ### 2.2 The EMF Equation — Logical Building Blocks Let us break down *why* the EMF equation looks the way it does: - **Step 1 — Flux is sinusoidal:** When an AC voltage is applied to the primary, the magnetising current produces a flux that is **sinusoidal** in nature: Φ(t) = Φ_m sin(ωt). - **Step 2 — Faraday's Law in action:** The flux change induces an EMF in the coil given by **e = -N × dΦ/dt**. - **Step 3 — Differentiate the flux:** dΦ/dt = ωΦ_m cos(ωt), giving the EMF a cosine waveform (90° leading over flux). - **Step 4 — Find peak EMF:** E_max = N × ω × Φ_m = 2πfNΦ_m. - **Step 5 — Convert peak to RMS:** Since RMS = Peak/√2, multiply by **1/√2**, giving E_rms = 2πfNΦ_m / √2. - **Step 6 — Simplify the constant:** 2π/√2 = 2π × 0.7071 = **4.44**, yielding the famous result. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_m$ | **EMF Equation** (RMS induced EMF) | Volts (V) | | 2 | $\dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{E_1}{E_2}$ | Turns Ratio = Voltage Ratio (ideal) | Dimensionless | | 3 | $K = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$ | Transformation Ratio (secondary ÷ primary) | Dimensionless | | 4 | $\dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{I_2}{I_1}$ | Inverse relation between V and I (ideal) | Dimensionless | | 5 | $\dfrac{E}{N} = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m$ | **Volts per turn** (V/T) — design constant | V/turn | | 6 | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | Angular frequency | rad/s | | 7 | $E_{max} = 2\pi f N \Phi_m$ | Peak (maximum) induced EMF | V | | 8 | $K_f = 1.11$ | Form factor of a sine wave (RMS/Avg) | Dimensionless | | 9 | $\Phi_m = \dfrac{E}{4.44 f N}$ | Maximum core flux (rearranged) | Webers (Wb) | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** > The **volts-per-turn** ($E/N$) is the single most important design parameter. Once a designer fixes the **core cross-section** and **operating frequency**, the V/T value is locked. The number of turns on **any** winding is then simply $N = V / (E/N)$. This is the principle behind every power adapter, mobile charger, and medical isolation transformer. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the EMF Equation of a Transformer **Given:** A single-phase transformer winding has $N$ turns, operating at supply frequency $f$ Hz, and the alternating flux in the core varies sinusoidally between $+\Phi_m$ and $-\Phi_m$. **To Prove:** $E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_m$ (RMS value of induced EMF). **Step 1 — Assume a sinusoidal flux waveform** The magnetic flux $\Phi$ varies sinusoidally with respect to time $t$: $$ \Phi(t) = \Phi_m \, \sin(\omega t) $$ where $\Phi_m$ is the **maximum flux** in Webers and $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the angular frequency in rad/s. **Step 2 — Apply Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** The instantaneous EMF induced in a coil of $N$ turns is: $$ e = -N \, \frac{d\Phi}{dt} $$ **Step 3 — Differentiate the flux with respect to time** $$ \frac{d\Phi}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\left[\Phi_m \sin(\omega t)\right] = \omega \, \Phi_m \, \cos(\omega t) $$ **Step 4 — Substitute back into Faraday's Law** $$ e = -N \, \omega \, \Phi_m \, \cos(\omega t) $$ **Step 5 — Identify the maximum (peak) value of the EMF** The cosine function has a maximum value of $1$, so the peak EMF is: $$ E_{max} = N \, \omega \, \Phi_m $$ **Step 6 — Substitute $\omega = 2\pi f$** $$ E_{max} = N \times 2\pi f \times \Phi_m = 2\pi f N \Phi_m $$ **Step 7 — Convert peak value to RMS (Root Mean Square) value** For a sinusoidal waveform, the RMS value is related to the peak value by: $$ E_{rms} = \frac{E_{max}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{2\pi f N \Phi_m}{\sqrt{2}} $$ **Step 8 — Simplify the constant term** $$ \frac{2\pi}{\sqrt{2}} = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 0.7071 \approx 4.44 $$ **Step 9 — Write the final EMF Equation** $$ \boxed{E_{rms} = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_m} $$ This equation applies to **both** the primary and the secondary winding of a transformer. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of the Turns Ratio Relationship **Given:** An ideal transformer with $N_1$ primary turns, $N_2$ secondary turns, primary voltage $V_1$, secondary voltage $V_2$, primary current $I_1$, and secondary current $I_2$. The core is lossless and the flux is fully confined to the magnetic circuit. **Step 1 — Write the EMF equation for each winding** $$ E_1 = 4.44 \, f \, N_1 \, \Phi_m $$ $$ E_2 = 4.44 \, f \, N_2 \, \Phi_m $$ **Step 2 — Take the ratio of the two EMFs** $$ \frac{E_1}{E_2} = \frac{4.44 \, f \, N_1 \, \Phi_m}{4.44 \, f \, N_2 \, \Phi_m} $$ **Step 3 — Cancel common terms ($4.44$, $f$, $\Phi_m$)** $$ \frac{E_1}{E_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2} $$ **Step 4 — Apply the ideal-transformer convention** In an ideal transformer, the induced EMF equals the terminal voltage (no internal drop): $$ \frac{V_1}{V_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2} $$ **Step 5 — Derive the current relationship using power balance** For an ideal (lossless) transformer, input power = output power: $$ V_1 \, I_1 = V_2 \, I_2 $$ Rearranging: $$ \frac{I_2}{I_1} = \frac{V_1}{V_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2} $$ **Step 6 — State the complete turns-ratio identity** $$ \boxed{\frac{V_1}{V_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{I_2}{I_1}} $$ The transformation ratio $K$ is conventionally defined as: $$ K = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{V_2}{V_1} = \frac{I_1}{I_2} $$ --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Demonstration **Problem:** A single-phase transformer has **500 turns** on the primary and **2500 turns** on the secondary. The primary is connected to a **230 V, 50 Hz** supply. Find: (i) Turns ratio, (ii) Secondary voltage, (iii) Maximum core flux, (iv) Volts per turn. **Solution:** **(i) Turns Ratio** $$ \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{500}{2500} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2 $$ Hence $N_1 : N_2 = 1 : 5$. This is a **step-up** transformer. **(ii) Secondary Voltage** $$ V_2 = V_1 \times \frac{N_2}{N_1} = 230 \times 5 = 1150 \text{ V} $$ **(iii) Maximum Core Flux** Using the EMF equation for the primary: $$ V_1 = 4.44 \, f \, N_1 \, \Phi_m $$ $$ 230 = 4.44 \times 50 \times 500 \times \Phi_m $$ $$ 230 = 111{,}000 \times \Phi_m $$ $$ \Phi_m = \frac{230}{111{,}000} = 2.072 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Wb} \approx 2.07 \text{ mWb} $$ **(iv) Volts per Turn** $$ \frac{E}{N} = \frac{230}{500} = 0.46 \text{ V/turn} $$ **Verification (using secondary side):** $$ \frac{E_2}{N_2} = \frac{1150}{2500} = 0.46 \text{ V/turn} \quad \checkmark $$ The volts-per-turn is identical on both sides, confirming **consistency of design**. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Constructional Layout of a Core-Type Single-Phase Transformer The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **block-level functional architecture** of a single-phase transformer, mapping the physical constructional elements and the flow of magnetic flux between the primary and secondary windings via the laminated core. ```mermaid graph TD A1["AC MAINS SUPPLY
230 V 50 Hz"] --> B1["PRIMARY WINDING
N1 TURNS COPPER
LV SIDE"] B1 --> C1["LAMINATED CORE
CRGO SILICON STEEL
0.35 MM THICK"] C1 --> D1["MAGNETIC FLUX PATH
PHI MAX WEBERS
SINUSOIDAL"] D1 --> C1 C1 --> B2["SECONDARY WINDING
N2 TURNS COPPER
HV SIDE"] B2 --> A2["LOAD TERMINALS
V2 VOLTS"] A2 --> B2 E1["INSULATION LAYER
PRESSBOARD PAPER
CLASS A 105 C"] -.-> B1 E2["INTER WINDING
INSULATION"] -.-> B2 E3["TANK WITH OIL
MINERAL OIL
COOLING MEDIUM"] -.-> C1 E4["BUSHINGS PORCELAIN
HV TERMINALS"] -.-> B2 E5["CONSERVATOR AND
SILICA GEL BREATHER"] -.-> E3 style A1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C1 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style A2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style E1 fill:#F0F0F0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,stroke-dasharray:5 style E2 fill:#F0F0F0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,stroke-dasharray:5 style E3 fill:#F0F0F0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,stroke-dasharray:5 style E4 fill:#F0F0F0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,stroke-dasharray:5 style E5 fill:#F0F0F0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,stroke-dasharray:5 ``` ### 4.2 Core-Type vs Shell-Type Topology — Functional Comparison ```mermaid graph LR CT["CORE TYPE TRANSFORMER
WINDINGS SURROUND
THE CORE LIMB"] --> CT1["APPLICATION HV
DISTRIBUTION"] CT --> CT2["EASY COOLING OF
WINDINGS"] CT --> CT3["LARGE FLUX PATH
TWO LIMBS"] ST["SHELL TYPE TRANSFORMER
CORE SURROUNDS
THE WINDINGS"] --> ST1["APPLICATION LV
INSTRUMENTATION"] ST --> ST2["BETTER MECHANICAL
PROTECTION"] ST --> ST3["SHORT FLUX PATH
THREE LIMBS"] style CT fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style ST fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style CT1 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style CT2 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style CT3 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style ST1 fill:#F0FFF0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style ST2 fill:#F0FFF0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style ST3 fill:#F0FFF0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** The arrows in **solid lines** (primary $\rightarrow$ core $\rightarrow$ secondary $\rightarrow$ load) represent the **path of energy transfer** via magnetic flux. The arrows in **dashed lines** represent **supporting subsystems** (insulation, tank, bushings, breather) that do not carry active flux but are essential for safe operation. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Define the transformation ratio of a transformer and state its relationship with the primary and secondary currents.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The transformation ratio $K$ of a transformer is defined as the ratio of the number of turns on the secondary winding to the number of turns on the primary winding: $$ K = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{V_2}{V_1} $$ The relationship with currents is **inverse** to the turns ratio (for an ideal transformer): $$ K = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{I_1}{I_2} $$ If $K > 1$, the transformer is a **step-up** transformer; if $K < 1$, it is a **step-down** transformer. **(3 marks)** --- **Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`** **Why are transformer cores laminated? What is the typical lamination thickness used?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Transformer cores are laminated to **minimise eddy current losses**. When the core carries alternating flux, induced circulating currents (eddy currents) flow in the core material, dissipating energy as heat. By using **thin laminations (typically 0.35 mm)** insulated from one another by varnish, the area available for eddy current circulation is reduced, which in turn reduces the $I^2R$ loss. The laminations are made of **CRGO silicon steel** (with 3–4% silicon) to further increase electrical resistivity and reduce both **eddy current** and **hysteresis** losses. **(3 marks)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Rule (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Internal choice is mandatory between the two question sets provided below. --- ### **Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`** **(a) Derive the EMF equation of a single-phase transformer, clearly stating the assumptions made. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Assumptions:** 1. The flux in the core varies **sinusoidally** with time: $\Phi = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t)$. 2. The windings are **uniformly and tightly coupled** (no leakage flux). 3. The core has **infinite permeability** and zero losses (ideal magnetic circuit). 4. The primary and secondary resistances are negligible. **Derivation:** The instantaneous flux is: $$ \Phi(t) = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t) $$ By Faraday's Law, the instantaneous EMF induced in a winding of $N$ turns is: $$ e = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt} = -N \, \omega \, \Phi_m \cos(\omega t) $$ The **maximum (peak) value** of EMF occurs when $\cos(\omega t) = 1$: $$ E_{max} = 2\pi f N \Phi_m $$ The **RMS value** of a sinusoidal waveform is $E_{max}/\sqrt{2}$: $$ E = \frac{2\pi f N \Phi_m}{\sqrt{2}} = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_m \quad \text{volts} $$ This is the required **EMF equation**, valid for both primary and secondary windings. **(7 marks)** --- **(b) A 100 kVA, 1100/220 V, 50 Hz single-phase transformer has 80 turns on the secondary winding. Calculate: (i) the number of primary turns, (ii) full-load primary and secondary currents, and (iii) the maximum flux in the core. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given Data:** - Rating: $S = 100 \text{ kVA}$ - $V_1 = 1100 \text{ V}$, $V_2 = 220 \text{ V}$ - $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$ - $N_2 = 80$ turns **(i) Number of Primary Turns** $$ \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{V_1}{V_2} = \frac{1100}{220} = 5 $$ $$ N_1 = 5 \times 80 = \boxed{400 \text{ turns}} $$ **(ii) Full-Load Currents** Secondary current: $$ I_2 = \frac{S}{V_2} = \frac{100{,}000}{220} = \boxed{454.55 \text{ A}} $$ Primary current: $$ I_1 = \frac{S}{V_1} = \frac{100{,}000}{1100} = \boxed{90.91 \text{ A}} $$ **Verification:** $I_1/I_2 = 90.91/454.55 = 1/5 = N_2/N_1$ ✓ **(iii) Maximum Core Flux** Using the primary EMF equation: $$ V_1 = 4.44 \, f \, N_1 \, \Phi_m $$ $$ 1100 = 4.44 \times 50 \times 400 \times \Phi_m $$ $$ 1100 = 88{,}800 \times \Phi_m $$ $$ \Phi_m = \frac{1100}{88{,}800} = 0.01239 \text{ Wb} = \boxed{12.39 \text{ mWb}} $$ **(7 marks)** --- ### **Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2022]`** **(a) With the help of a neat diagram, explain the constructional features of a single-phase core-type transformer. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** A single-phase core-type transformer consists of the following constructional parts: **1. Core (Magnetic Circuit):** The core is built up of **thin laminations (0.35 mm)** of **CRGO silicon steel** stacked together. The laminations are insulated from each other by **varnish or paper** to reduce eddy current loss. The core has **two vertical limbs** connected by a horizontal **yoke**. **2. Windings (Electric Circuit):** The transformer has **two windings** — the **primary** (input side) and the **secondary** (output side), wound on the two limbs. Conductors used are **copper (or aluminium)**, insulated with enamel. The **LV winding is placed closer to the core** and the **HV winding is placed above it** with extra insulation in between. **3. Insulation:** Insulation is provided (a) between core and windings, (b) between turns, and (c) between primary and secondary windings. Common materials are **pressboard, paper, varnish, and mica**. **4. Transformer Tank:** The core-coil assembly is placed inside a **steel tank** filled with **transformer oil**, which serves both as insulation and as a cooling medium. **5. Cooling Accessories:** - **Conservator:** An overhead oil drum to allow thermal expansion of oil. - **Radiators/Fins:** External surfaces for heat dissipation. - **Breather:** A container with **silica gel** that dries the air entering the conservator. **6. Bushings:** **Porcelain bushings** are used to safely bring the HV and LV terminals out of the tank. **7. Tap Changer:** An off-load or on-load tap changer is provided on the HV side to vary the turns ratio and thereby regulate the secondary voltage. A neat labelled diagram should accompany this answer (refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1). **(7 marks)** --- **(b) A 5 kVA, 200/400 V, 50 Hz single-phase transformer has 100 turns on the primary. Determine: (i) the transformation ratio, (ii) the secondary turns, (iii) the maximum flux in the core, and (iv) the volts per turn. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $S = 5 \text{ kVA}$ - $V_1 = 200 \text{ V}$, $V_2 = 400 \text{ V}$ - $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$ - $N_1 = 100$ turns **(i) Transformation Ratio** $$ K = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{V_2}{V_1} = \frac{400}{200} = \boxed{2} $$ Since $K > 1$, this is a **step-up transformer**. **(ii) Number of Secondary Turns** $$ N_2 = K \times N_1 = 2 \times 100 = \boxed{200 \text{ turns}} $$ **(iii) Maximum Core Flux** Using the primary EMF equation: $$ V_1 = 4.44 \, f \, N_1 \, \Phi_m $$ $$ 200 = 4.44 \times 50 \times 100 \times \Phi_m $$ $$ 200 = 22{,}200 \times \Phi_m $$ $$ \Phi_m = \frac{200}{22{,}200} = 9.009 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Wb} \approx \boxed{9.01 \text{ mWb}} $$ **(iv) Volts per Turn** $$ \frac{E}{N} = \frac{V_1}{N_1} = \frac{200}{100} = \boxed{2 \text{ V/turn}} $$ **Verification:** $V_2/N_2 = 400/200 = 2$ V/turn ✓ **(7 marks)** --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Easy Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting the constant 4.44:** Many students write $E = fN\Phi_m$ or $E = 2\pi f N \Phi_m$ without the final RMS conversion. The form factor 1.11 is **mandatory**. You lose **1–2 marks** for this omission. > > 2. **Confusing $E$ with $V$:** In an ideal transformer, $E_1 = V_1$, but in a real transformer, the no-load primary current causes a small voltage drop. For Module 2 problems (ideal transformer), this is acceptable, but write a clear note: "Assuming an ideal transformer, $V_1 = E_1$." > > 3. **Units Slip-up:** Frequency must be in **Hz**, flux in **Wb**, and turns in pure numbers. Mixing up mWb with Wb is the **#1 numerical-error** in KTU papers. > > 4. **Turns Ratio Direction:** KTU examiners check whether you defined $K$ as $N_2/N_1$ or $N_1/N_2$. Always **state the convention** explicitly in your first line. > > 5. **No Diagram, No Marks:** In 7-mark constructional questions, a **labelled diagram is compulsory**. Even a hand-drawn block diagram carries 2–3 marks. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Constructional Features, EMF Equation & Turns Ratio** - [x] **Transformer** is a **static** AC-AC energy transfer device operating on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**. - [x] **Core material:** CRGO silicon steel, **0.35 mm laminations**, varnish-insulated. - [x] **Core Type** = windings surround the core; **Shell Type** = core surrounds the windings. - [x] **LV winding is placed nearest to the core**; HV winding is placed on top with extra insulation. - [x] **Transformer oil** provides both **insulation** and **cooling**. - [x] **EMF Equation (universal):** $E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_m$ — valid for both primary and secondary. - [x] The constant **4.44 = $2\pi/\sqrt{2} = 4 \times 1.11$** is the product of half-cycle averaging (factor 4) and the form factor (1.11). - [x] **Turns Ratio Identity:** $V_1/V_2 = N_1/N_2 = I_2/I_1$ (ideal transformer). - [x] **Transformation ratio** $K = N_2/N_1$; $K > 1$ = step-up, $K < 1$ = step-down. - [x] **Volts per turn** ($E/N = 4.44 f \Phi_m$) is a **constant for a given design** and is the cornerstone of transformer design. - [x] **Biomedical relevance:** Isolation transformers, MRI gradient power supplies, defibrillator charging circuits, and patient monitoring isolation barriers all rely on these principles. - [x] **Remember:** $1$ Tesla $= 1 \text{ Wb/m}^2$; typical transformer flux density is **1.1–1.4 T**.

topics.title: Efficiency, Regulation, and Losses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT411 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY FOR BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Efficiency, Regulation, and Losses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Efficiency (η)

**Formal Definition:**
The **efficiency** of an electrical machine (DC generator or DC motor) is defined as the ratio of useful output power to the input power supplied. It is a dimensionless quantity, always expressed as a percentage in engineering practice.

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Output Power}}{\text{Input Power}} \times 100\%$$

Equivalently, it can be expressed in terms of losses:

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Output} + \text{Losses}} \times 100\% = 1 - \frac{\text{Losses}}{\text{Input}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Efficiency is a **performance parameter** that determines the economic operation of any DC machine. A perfectly ideal machine would have η = 100%, but practical machines always have losses.

### 1.2 Regulation

**Formal Definition:**
In a **DC Generator**, voltage regulation is defined as the change in terminal voltage from no-load to full-load condition, expressed as a percentage of full-load voltage, while the load is removed at constant speed and constant field excitation.

$$\text{Voltage Regulation (\% VR)} = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\%$$

Where:
- $V_{NL}$ = No-load terminal voltage
- $V_{FL}$ = Full-load terminal voltage

> [!NOTE]
> For a **DC Motor**, the analogous concept is **Speed Regulation**:
> $$\text{Speed Regulation} = \frac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100\%$$
> where $N_{NL}$ and $N_{FL}$ are no-load and full-load speeds.

### 1.3 Losses

**Formal Definition:**
**Losses** in a DC machine refer to the input electrical (or mechanical) power that is converted into unwanted forms of energy, primarily heat, sound, and vibration, without contributing to the useful output.

### 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 1 — Efficiency as a Fuel Filter:** Imagine a fuel filter in a car. You pour in 100 ml of fuel, but only 85 ml actually reaches the engine. The efficiency is 85%. Similarly, in a DC machine, you supply 1000 W of electrical input, but only 850 W becomes useful mechanical (or electrical) output — the rest is "wasted" as heat and noise.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 2 — Regulation as a Water Tap Pressure:** Open a tap with no bucket below (no-load): pressure is high. Now place a heavy bucket below it (full-load): the pressure drops because the pipe has resistance. The percentage drop in pressure is analogous to regulation. A "good" generator has very low regulation (pressure barely changes).

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 3 — Losses as a Leaky Bucket:** A bucket has a small hole. Water you pour in (input) is partially lost (losses), and what remains at the bottom (output) is what you actually need. The losses are like energy leaking out as heat through the bucket's walls.

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
- **Mechanical Equivalent of Heat:** **1 HP = 746 W**
- **Efficiency:** Always between **0% and 100%** (0 ≤ η ≤ 1)
- **Standard Reference Temperature for Copper Resistance:** **75 °C** (as per IEEE standards)
- **Specific Resistance of Copper at 75 °C:** **0.0213 Ω·m/m²**

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Losses in a DC Machine

Losses in any DC machine are broadly classified into **three categories**:

#### A. Copper Losses (Variable Losses / I²R Losses)
- **Armature Copper Loss:** $I_a^2 R_a$
- **Shunt Field Copper Loss:** $I_{sh}^2 R_{sh}$
- **Series Field Copper Loss:** $I_{se}^2 R_{se}$ (only in compound/series machines)
- **Brush Contact Loss:** $V_b \times I_a$ (often lumped with armature copper loss)
- **Nature:** Directly proportional to the square of load current → **Variable Losses**

#### B. Iron Losses (Core Losses / Constant Losses)
- **Hysteresis Loss:** $P_h = \eta \cdot B_{max}^{1.6} \cdot f \cdot V$ (Steinmetz equation)
- **Eddy Current Loss:** $P_e = K_e \cdot B_{max}^2 \cdot f^2 \cdot t^2$
- **Nature:** Practically independent of load → **Constant Losses**

#### C. Mechanical Losses (Constant Losses)
- **Friction Loss** (brush friction, bearing friction)
- **Windage Loss** (air resistance on rotating parts)
- **Nature:** Independent of load (at constant speed) → **Constant Losses**

#### D. Stray Load Losses
- Additional small losses due to non-uniform flux distribution under load.
- Approximately **1% of output** (as per empirical approximation).

### 2.2 Constant Losses vs Variable Losses

| Property | Constant Losses ($P_c$) | Variable Losses ($P_v$) |
|---|---|---|
| Composition | Iron + Mechanical | Copper (I²R) |
| Load dependence | Independent | ∝ (Load current)² |
| At no-load | Present | Zero |
| At full-load | Same as no-load | Maximum |

### 2.3 Power Flow in a DC Machine

**For a DC Shunt Motor:**
$$\text{Electrical Input} = V I_L = V(I_a + I_{sh})$$

$$\text{Back EMF Power Developed} = E_b \cdot I_a = \text{Electrical Input} - \text{Armature Cu loss} - \text{Brush loss}$$

$$\text{Mechanical Power Developed} = E_b I_a = T_g \cdot \omega$$

$$\text{Output Power (Shaft)} = \text{Mechanical Developed} - \text{Friction} - \text{Windage}$$

### 2.4 Condition for Maximum Efficiency

The total losses are: $P_L = P_c + P_v = P_c + I_a^2 R_a$

Efficiency is maximum when the rate of change of efficiency with respect to load is zero. Mathematically:

$$\frac{d\eta}{dI_a} = 0 \implies P_c = P_v$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Result:** A DC machine operates at **maximum efficiency when variable losses equal constant losses**.

**Load current at maximum efficiency:**

$$I_a = \sqrt{\frac{P_c}{R_a}}$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | General efficiency | % |
| 2 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{losses}} \times 100$ | Efficiency in terms of losses | % |
| 3 | $P_c = P_h + P_e + P_{mech}$ | Total constant loss | W |
| 4 | $P_v = I_a^2 R_a$ | Armature variable loss | W |
| 5 | $\text{VR} = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100$ | Generator voltage regulation | % |
| 6 | $\text{SR} = \dfrac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100$ | Motor speed regulation | % |
| 7 | $\eta_{max}$ when $P_c = I_a^2 R_a$ | Max efficiency condition | — |
| 8 | $I_a(\eta_{max}) = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_c}{R_a}}$ | Load current at $\eta_{max}$ | A |
| 9 | $P_h = \eta_h B_{max}^{1.6} f V$ | Hysteresis loss (Steinmetz) | W |
| 10 | $P_e = K_e B_{max}^2 f^2 t^2$ | Eddy current loss | W |
| 11 | $P_{stray} \approx 0.01 \times P_{out}$ | Stray load loss (approx.) | W |
| 12 | $\eta_{gen} = \dfrac{VI_L}{V I_L + P_c + I_a^2 R_a}$ | Generator efficiency | — |
| 13 | $\eta_{mot} = \dfrac{V I_L - P_c - I_a^2 R_a}{V I_L}$ | Motor efficiency | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In biomedical equipment like **infusion pumps, ventilators, MRI machines, and surgical robots**, highly efficient DC motors are used because battery life, heat dissipation, and precision directly impact patient safety. Even a 5% drop in efficiency can mean shorter battery backup and dangerous overheating in compact medical devices.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Condition for Maximum Efficiency

**Starting Point — Power Balance for a DC Shunt Generator:**

The total electrical input to the armature is $V I_a + I_a^2 R_a$ where the EMF induced is $E_b$.

Let:
- $P_{out} = V I_L$ (output delivered to external load)
- $P_c = $ Total constant losses (iron + mechanical)
- $P_v = I_a^2 R_a$ = Armature variable copper loss

Therefore:

$$P_{in} = P_{out} + P_c + P_v$$

So efficiency becomes:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_c + P_v}$$

**Step 1:** Consider that for a DC shunt machine at constant excitation, $E_b$ is nearly constant (≈ $V + I_a R_a$). The output power $P_{out}$ is proportional to load current $I_L \approx I_a$.

So we can rewrite $P_{out} = V I_a$ (ignoring small field current difference):

$$\eta = \frac{V I_a}{V I_a + P_c + I_a^2 R_a}$$

**Step 2:** Divide numerator and denominator by $I_a$:

$$\eta = \frac{V}{V + \dfrac{P_c}{I_a} + I_a R_a}$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate $\eta$ with respect to $I_a$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{d\eta}{dI_a} = \frac{-V \left(-\dfrac{P_c}{I_a^2} + R_a\right)}{\left(V + \dfrac{P_c}{I_a} + I_a R_a\right)^2} = 0$$

**Step 4:** For the derivative to be zero, the numerator must vanish:

$$-\frac{P_c}{I_a^2} + R_a = 0$$

**Step 5:** Solving:

$$R_a = \frac{P_c}{I_a^2} \implies P_c = I_a^2 R_a$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Maximum efficiency occurs when **Constant Losses = Variable Losses**, i.e., when $P_c = P_v$.

**Step 6:** The corresponding load current:

$$I_a = \sqrt{\frac{P_c}{R_a}}$$

This is the load current at which maximum efficiency is obtained. The corresponding load is called the **"most efficient load"**.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** A 10 kW, 250 V DC shunt generator has an armature resistance of 0.1 Ω and a shunt field resistance of 125 Ω. The iron and friction losses together amount to 500 W. Find (a) Full-load efficiency, (b) Load current for maximum efficiency, (c) Maximum efficiency.

**Given Data:**
- Rated Power: $P_{rated} = 10 \text{ kW} = 10{,}000$ W
- Rated Voltage: $V = 250$ V
- Armature Resistance: $R_a = 0.1\ \Omega$
- Shunt Field Resistance: $R_{sh} = 125\ \Omega$
- Constant Losses: $P_c = 500$ W

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Full-load current:**
$$I_L = \frac{P_{out}}{V} = \frac{10{,}000}{250} = 40\ \text{A}$$

**Step 2 — Shunt field current:**
$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{125} = 2\ \text{A}$$

**Step 3 — Armature current (full-load):**
$$I_a = I_L + I_{sh} = 40 + 2 = 42\ \text{A}$$

**Step 4 — Armature copper loss (full-load):**
$$P_v = I_a^2 R_a = (42)^2 \times 0.1 = 1764 \times 0.1 = 176.4\ \text{W}$$

**Step 5 — Full-load efficiency:**
$$\eta_{FL} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_c + P_v} \times 100$$

$$\eta_{FL} = \frac{10{,}000}{10{,}000 + 500 + 176.4} \times 100$$

$$\eta_{FL} = \frac{10{,}000}{10{,}676.4} \times 100 = 93.66\%$$

**Step 6 — Load current at maximum efficiency:**
$$I_a(\eta_{max}) = \sqrt{\frac{P_c}{R_a}} = \sqrt{\frac{500}{0.1}} = \sqrt{5000} = 70.71\ \text{A}$$

**Step 7 — Variable loss at max efficiency (should equal $P_c$):**
$$P_v(\eta_{max}) = I_a^2 R_a = (70.71)^2 \times 0.1 = 5000 \times 0.1 = 500\ \text{W}\ \checkmark$$

**Step 8 — Output at max efficiency:**
$$P_{out}(\eta_{max}) = V \times I_L = V \times (I_a - I_{sh}) = 250 \times (70.71 - 2) = 250 \times 68.71 = 17{,}177.5\ \text{W}$$

**Step 9 — Maximum efficiency:**
$$\eta_{max} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_c + P_v} \times 100$$

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{17{,}177.5}{17{,}177.5 + 500 + 500} \times 100$$

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{17{,}177.5}{18{,}177.5} \times 100 = 94.49\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** The maximum efficiency (94.49%) occurs at a load **greater** than full-load rating (40 A). This is a typical design choice — manufacturers rate machines so that maximum efficiency occurs near full-load operation.

### 3.3 Regulation Derivation

For a **separately excited / shunt generator** at constant speed and constant field current:

$$\text{No-load: } V_{NL} = E_b$$

$$\text{Full-load: } V_{FL} = E_b - I_a R_a - V_{brush}$$

$$\therefore V_{NL} - V_{FL} = I_a R_a + V_{brush}$$

$$\%VR = \frac{I_a R_a + V_{brush}}{V_{FL}} \times 100$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a **DC Motor**, the regulation typically refers to **speed regulation**, derived from the EMF equation $E_b = V - I_a R_a = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$, leading to:
> $$\% \text{Speed Regulation} = \frac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100$$
> where $N_{NL} \approx \dfrac{V}{\Phi K}$ and $N_{FL} \approx \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{\Phi K}$.

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Power Flow Diagram of a DC Machine

The following **Mermaid block** maps the power flow from input to output, identifying all losses along the path.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["ELECTRICAL INPUT (P_in = V * I_L)"]:::inputNode
    B["ARMATURE COPPER LOSS (I_a^2 * R_a)"]:::lossNode
    C["BRUSH CONTACT LOSS (V_b * I_a)"]:::lossNode
    D["MECHANICAL POWER DEVELOPED (E_b * I_a)"]:::powerNode
    E["IRON LOSS (Hysteresis + Eddy Current)"]:::lossNode
    F["FRICTION AND WINDAGE LOSS"]:::lossNode
    G["SHAFT OUTPUT POWER (P_out = T_sh * omega)"]:::outputNode
    H["STRAY LOAD LOSS (1% of Output)"]:::lossNode

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> H
    H --> G

    classDef inputNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef lossNode fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef powerNode fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#006666,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outputNode fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#006666,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Yellow box = Input energy
- Red boxes = Losses (each subtracting from available power)
- Teal boxes = Useful intermediate / final power
- Green box = Final shaft output
