## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the following terms with respect to a DC machine:*
*(i) Voltage Regulation, (ii) Efficiency, (iii) Stray Load Loss.*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Voltage Regulation:** The change in terminal voltage from no-load to full-load, expressed as a percentage of full-load voltage, at constant speed and constant field excitation.

$$\% \text{VR} = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100$$

**(ii) Efficiency:** The ratio of output power to input power, expressed as a percentage.

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\% = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{losses}} \times 100\%$$

**(iii) Stray Load Loss:** The additional losses that occur due to distortion of the main field flux under load conditions, including eddy currents in conductors and minor iron losses. Empirically approximated as **1% of output**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the condition for maximum efficiency of a DC machine and explain why a machine is designed to operate near full-load rather than at the most efficient load point.*

**Model Answer:**

**Condition:** A DC machine attains maximum efficiency when its **variable (copper) losses equal its constant (iron + mechanical) losses**.

$$P_c = I_a^2 R_a \implies I_a(\eta_{max}) = \sqrt{\frac{P_c}{R_a}}$$

**Explanation:** Manufacturers design machines so that the maximum efficiency point falls near the **rated full-load** of the machine. This is because:
- Operating continuously at loads far below rating causes under-utilization and poor power factor.
- Operating continuously above rating causes overheating and insulation damage.
- The full-load point is the most commonly used operating condition; hence efficiency is optimized there.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

**QUESTION A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**A DC shunt generator delivers 100 A at 250 V. The armature resistance is 0.05 Ω and the shunt field resistance is 100 Ω. The iron and friction losses total 800 W.**

**Find:**
**(a)** Full-load efficiency of the generator.
**(b)** Load current at which maximum efficiency occurs.
**(c)** Maximum efficiency.
**(d)** The voltage regulation if the no-load voltage is 256 V.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $I_L = 100$ A
- $V = 250$ V
- $R_a = 0.05\ \Omega$
- $R_{sh} = 100\ \Omega$
- $P_c = 800$ W (iron + friction)
- $V_{NL} = 256$ V

---

**Part (a) — Full-load Efficiency [7 Marks]**

**Step 1:** Shunt field current:

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{100} = 2.5\ \text{A}$$

**Step 2:** Armature current:

$$I_a = I_L + I_{sh} = 100 + 2.5 = 102.5\ \text{A}$$

**Step 3:** Armature copper loss:

$$P_v = I_a^2 R_a = (102.5)^2 \times 0.05 = 10{,}506.25 \times 0.05 = 525.31\ \text{W}$$

**Step 4:** Output power:

$$P_{out} = V \times I_L = 250 \times 100 = 25{,}000\ \text{W}$$

**Step 5:** Input power:

$$P_{in} = P_{out} + P_c + P_v = 25{,}000 + 800 + 525.31 = 26{,}325.31\ \text{W}$$

**Step 6:** Full-load efficiency:

$$\eta_{FL} = \frac{25{,}000}{26{,}325.31} \times 100 = 94.96\%$$

---

**Part (b) — Load current at maximum efficiency [3 Marks]**

$$I_a(\eta_{max}) = \sqrt{\frac{P_c}{R_a}} = \sqrt{\frac{800}{0.05}} = \sqrt{16{,}000} = 126.49\ \text{A}$$

---

**Part (c) — Maximum Efficiency [2 Marks]**

Variable loss at this point:

$$P_v = I_a^2 R_a = (126.49)^2 \times 0.05 = 16{,}000 \times 0.05 = 800\ \text{W} = P_c\ \checkmark$$

Output at this point:

$$I_L = I_a - I_{sh} = 126.49 - 2.5 = 123.99\ \text{A}$$

$$P_{out}(\eta_{max}) = 250 \times 123.99 = 30{,}997.5\ \text{W}$$

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{30{,}997.5}{30{,}997.5 + 800 + 800} \times 100 = \frac{30{,}997.5}{32{,}597.5} \times 100 = 95.09\%$$

---

**Part (d) — Voltage Regulation [2 Marks]**

$$\% \text{VR} = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100 = \frac{256 - 250}{250} \times 100 = 2.4\%$$

---

### **OR**

---

**QUESTION B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**A 250 V DC shunt motor draws an armature current of 50 A at full load. The armature resistance is 0.2 Ω and the shunt field resistance is 250 Ω. The constant losses (iron + friction) are 600 W.**

**Find:**
**(a)** Back EMF at full load.
**(b)** Total losses and input power at full load.
**(c)** Full-load efficiency of the motor.
**(d)** The condition and load at which maximum efficiency occurs.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $V = 250$ V
- $I_a = 50$ A (full-load armature current)
- $R_a = 0.2\ \Omega$
- $R_{sh} = 250\ \Omega$
- $P_c = 600$ W

---

**Part (a) — Back EMF at full load [3 Marks]**

By the motor EMF equation:

$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 250 - 50 \times 0.2 = 250 - 10 = 240\ \text{V}$$

---

**Part (b) — Total losses and input power at full load [4 Marks]**

**Step 1:** Shunt field current:

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{250} = 1\ \text{A}$$

**Step 2:** Line current:

$$I_L = I_a + I_{sh} = 50 + 1 = 51\ \text{A}$$

**Step 3:** Armature copper loss:

$$P_v = I_a^2 R_a = (50)^2 \times 0.2 = 2500 \times 0.2 = 500\ \text{W}$$

**Step 4:** Field copper loss (constant, often included in $P_c$):

$$P_{sh} = I_{sh}^2 R_{sh} = (1)^2 \times 250 = 250\ \text{W}$$

**Step 5:** Total losses:

$$P_L = P_c + P_v = 600 + 500 = 1100\ \text{W}$$

**Step 6:** Input power:

$$P_{in} = V \times I_L = 250 \times 51 = 12{,}750\ \text{W}$$

---

**Part (c) — Full-load efficiency of the motor [4 Marks]**

$$P_{out} = P_{in} - P_L = 12{,}750 - 1100 = 11{,}650\ \text{W}$$

$$\eta_{FL} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{11{,}650}{12{,}750} \times 100 = 91.37\%$$

---

**Part (d) — Condition and load for maximum efficiency [3 Marks]**

**Condition:** $P_c = P_v = I_a^2 R_a$

$$I_a(\eta_{max}) = \sqrt{\frac{P_c}{R_a}} = \sqrt{\frac{600}{0.2}} = \sqrt{3000} = 54.77\ \text{A}$$

The maximum efficiency occurs when the armature draws **54.77 A**, which is slightly **above** the full-load rating of 50 A.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $I_L$ and $I_a$:** Many students use load current instead of armature current in the $I_a^2 R_a$ formula. **Always use $I_a$ (armature current)**, not $I_L$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting $I_{sh}$ in shunt machines:** In a DC shunt machine, $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$ (generator) or $I_L = I_a + I_{sh}$ (motor). Neglecting $I_{sh}$ causes a 2–3% error in efficiency — enough to lose a mark.
>
> 3. **Maximum efficiency ≠ Full-load efficiency:** The condition $P_c = P_v$ gives the *maximum* efficiency, but the machine may not always operate at that point. The full-load efficiency is calculated using actual full-load currents.
>
> 4. **Units Consistency:** Power must always be in Watts (W), current in Amperes (A), and resistance in Ohms (Ω). Mixing kW and W is a frequent error.
>
> 5. **Speed Regulation vs Voltage Regulation:** For DC motors, the standard "regulation" refers to **speed regulation**, not voltage regulation. Don't confuse them in exam answers.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Efficiency Formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{losses}} \times 100$
- ✅ **Voltage Regulation:** $\% \text{VR} = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100$
- ✅ **Speed Regulation (Motor):** $\% \text{SR} = \dfrac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100$
- ✅ **Maximum Efficiency Condition:** $P_c = P_v$, i.e., Constant losses = Variable (copper) losses
- ✅ **Load current at $\eta_{max}$:** $I_a = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_c}{R_a}}$
- ✅ **Constant Losses:** Iron (Hysteresis + Eddy Current) + Mechanical (Friction + Windage)
- ✅ **Variable Losses:** Armature Cu ($I_a^2 R_a$) + Field Cu + Brush contact
- ✅ **Stray Load Loss:** Approximately **1% of output** (empirical)
- ✅ **Hysteresis Loss:** $\eta_h B_{max}^{1.6} f V$ (Steinmetz equation)
- ✅ **Eddy Current Loss:** $K_e B_{max}^2 f^2 t^2$ (reduced by using laminations)
- ✅ **Most machines are designed** so that the **maximum efficiency point lies near the full-load rating**.
- ✅ **Biomedical Relevance:** Efficient motors are critical in battery-operated medical devices (insulin pumps, ventilators, MRI scanners) to maximize runtime and minimize heat dissipation near sensitive electronics and patients.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Open Circuit and Short Circuit Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT411 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY FOR BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Open Circuit and Short Circuit Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Open Circuit Test (OC Test) — The "No-Load Heartbeat" of a Transformer

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (OC Test)**
> The Open Circuit Test (also called the *No-Load Test*) is a low-voltage, low-power test performed on a transformer to determine its **core (iron) losses** and the **no-load branch parameters** of the equivalent circuit — namely the **core-loss resistance (R_c or R_w)** and the **magnetizing reactance (X_m)**. It is executed by energizing one winding (conventionally the **Low Voltage (LV) side**) at its **rated voltage** and **rated frequency** while keeping the other winding (HV side) **open-circuited**.

**Physical Constants & Standard Metrics:**
- **Rated Frequency** = 50 Hz (India standard)
- **Applied Voltage** = Rated voltage of the energized winding
- **Power Factor (No-Load)** $\cos\phi_0$ ≈ **0.1 to 0.3** (highly lagging)
- **No-Load Current** $I_0$ ≈ **2% to 10%** of full-load current

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**
> The OC Test is performed from the **LV side** because:
> (a) LV side requires lower test voltage, making it safer and cheaper to supply.
> (b) LV side draws higher no-load current, giving more accurate ammeter readings.

---

## 1.2 Short Circuit Test (SC Test) — The "Copper Stress" Test

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (SC Test)**
> The Short Circuit Test (also called the *Impedance Test* or *Reduced-Voltage Test*) is performed to determine the **full-load copper (ohmic) losses** and the **equivalent series impedance** of the transformer referred to the test side. It is conducted by **short-circuiting one winding (conventionally the LV side)** and applying a **reduced voltage** (typically **5% to 10% of rated**) to the other winding (HV side) such that **full-load current** circulates in both windings.

**Physical Constants & Standard Metrics:**
- **Applied Voltage** $V_{sc}$ ≈ **5–10%** of rated voltage
- **Short-Circuit Current** $I_{sc}$ = **Full-load current**
- **Power Factor (SC)** $\cos\phi_{sc}$ ≈ **0.05 to 0.4** (lagging, highly inductive)
- **Copper Loss** $P_{cu}$ = $I_{sc}^2 R_{01}$ ≈ **100% copper loss at full load**

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Why These Tests Exist

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy (Imagine a Hospital MRI Room)**
> Think of a transformer as a **patient's heart** in a biomedical setting:
> - The **OC Test** is like an **ECG at rest** — the patient is awake but doing nothing. You measure the heart's baseline activity (no-load losses = the heart's metabolic cost just to *exist*).
> - The **SC Test** is like a **stress test on a treadmill** — the heart is forced to work at maximum, but in a controlled, low-resistance environment. You measure the heart's ability to handle stress (copper losses = energy burned under load).
> - You can never do a "full marathon" test on a healthy person in a lab — it's impractical and dangerous. Similarly, you **cannot load a large transformer to its full rated power** in a lab (it would need a massive dummy load and a lot of energy). So engineers use these two cleverly designed, low-energy tests to **predict the transformer's behavior under full load**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Misconception**
> "OC test gives us efficiency directly." — **WRONG.** OC test only gives **core loss (constant loss)**. To find efficiency, you must **combine** OC loss (constant) + SC loss (variable, load-dependent).

---

## 1.4 Why Perform These Tests Indirectly? — The Engineering Justification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why not load the transformer directly?**
> For a **1000 kVA transformer** at full load:
> - You would need a **1000 kW dummy load** (huge water-cooled resistor bank or inductor bank).
> - Energy bill for testing = ₹₹₹₹₹.
> - The test setup is bulky, dangerous, and impractical.
>
> **The Ingenious Solution:** Perform two **low-power indirect tests** that, by the **principle of linearity and superposition of losses**, accurately predict full-load performance.
> - **OC Test** → determines **iron loss** (constant loss, depends only on voltage and frequency).
> - **SC Test** → determines **copper loss** at full load (variable loss, depends on $I^2R$).
> - Total Loss = Iron Loss + Copper Loss → Efficiency = Output / (Output + Losses)

---

## 1.5 Test Instruments and Connection Setup

| Instrument | OC Test Connection | SC Test Connection |
|------------|-------------------|--------------------|
| **Voltmeter (V)** | Across energized winding | Across energized (HV) side |
| **Ammeter (A)** | In series with energized winding | In series with energized (HV) side |
| **Wattmeter (W)** | Measures input power | Measures input power |
| **Variac** | Supplies variable rated V | Supplies reduced V (5-10%) |
| **Shorting Link** | On HV side (open) | On LV side (shorted) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Biomedical Application Context**
> Isolation transformers used in **ICU, ECG, MRI, and OT equipment** must have **minimum leakage current** and **low iron losses** (to avoid 50 Hz hum interfering with sensitive patient monitoring). The OC and SC tests are crucial in certifying these medical-grade transformers for hospital use per **IEC 60601-1** standards.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Phasor Analysis of the No-Load (OC) Condition

When the transformer is energized at rated voltage $V_0$ with the secondary open:

1. A small current $I_0$ flows, called the **no-load current** or **exciting current**.
2. $I_0$ has **two components**:
   - **Magnetizing component $I_\mu$** → produces the working flux $\phi_m$ in the core, lags $V_0$ by 90°.
   - **Core-loss (working) component $I_w$** → supplies the hysteresis + eddy current losses, in phase with $V_0$.
3. Therefore: $I_0 = \sqrt{I_w^2 + I_\mu^2}$
4. The **no-load power factor** $\cos\phi_0 = \dfrac{I_w}{I_0} = \dfrac{P_{oc}}{V_0 \, I_0}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Insight:** $I_\mu$ is the current that actually magnetizes the iron core (purely reactive). $I_w$ is the current that "pays" for the energy lost as heat in the core (active component).

## 2.2 Phasor Analysis of the Short-Circuit (SC) Condition

When reduced voltage $V_{sc}$ is applied and the secondary is shorted:

1. The transformer behaves like a simple series impedance $Z_{01} = R_{01} + jX_{01}$ (referred to the test side).
2. Full-load current $I_{sc} = I_{FL}$ circulates.
3. The applied voltage is small (5–10%) because at rated voltage, the current would be **10–20 times** full-load — catastrophic!
4. The short-circuit power factor $\cos\phi_{sc}$ is highly lagging because $X_{01} \gg R_{01}$ in most transformers.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas use SI Units: V in volts, I in amperes, P in watts, R in Ω, X in Ω, Z in Ω.**

### **Table A: Open Circuit Test Formulas**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Meaning |
|---|-----------|---------|---------|
| 1 | No-load p.f. | $\cos\phi_0 = \dfrac{P_{oc}}{V_0 \, I_0}$ | Power factor at no-load |
| 2 | Working (core-loss) current | $I_w = I_0 \cos\phi_0$ | Active component of $I_0$ |
| 3 | Magnetizing current | $I_\mu = I_0 \sin\phi_0$ | Reactive component of $I_0$ |
| 4 | Core-loss resistance | $R_c = \dfrac{V_0}{I_w} = \dfrac{V_0^2}{P_{oc}}$ | Models iron loss |
| 5 | Magnetizing reactance | $X_m = \dfrac{V_0}{I_\mu}$ | Models magnetic circuit |
| 6 | Iron (core) loss | $P_i = P_{oc}$ | Constant loss, independent of load |

### **Table B: Short Circuit Test Formulas**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Meaning |
|---|-----------|---------|---------|
| 1 | Equivalent impedance (test side) | $Z_{01} = \dfrac{V_{sc}}{I_{sc}}$ | Total series impedance |
| 2 | Equivalent resistance | $R_{01} = \dfrac{P_{sc}}{I_{sc}^2}$ | Total copper resistance |
| 3 | Equivalent leakage reactance | $X_{01} = \sqrt{Z_{01}^2 - R_{01}^2}$ | Total leakage reactance |
| 4 | Full-load copper loss | $P_{cu,FL} = I_{sc}^2 R_{01} = P_{sc}$ | Variable loss at full load |
| 5 | SC power factor | $\cos\phi_{sc} = \dfrac{P_{sc}}{V_{sc} \, I_{sc}}$ | Lagging, highly inductive |
| 6 | % Impedance (voltage) | $\%Z = \dfrac{V_{sc}}{V_{rated}} \times 100$ | Used for voltage regulation |

### **Table C: Performance Parameters Derived from Tests**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Use |
|---|-----------|---------|-----|
| 1 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi}{x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi + P_i + x^2 P_{cu,FL}}$ | For load fraction $x$, apparent power $S$ |
| 2 | Voltage Regulation | $\text{VR}\% = \dfrac{I(R_{01}\cos\phi \pm X_{01}\sin\phi)}{V} \times 100$ | For lagging (+) / leading (−) p.f. |
| 3 | Total loss at load fraction $x$ | $P_{loss} = P_i + x^2 P_{cu,FL}$ | Used in efficiency calc |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions (KTU Examiner's Favourite):**
> - At $x = 0$ (no load): $P_{loss} = P_i$ only.
> - At $x = 1$ (full load): $P_{loss} = P_i + P_{cu,FL}$.
> - **Condition for maximum efficiency:** $x = \sqrt{P_i / P_{cu,FL}}$

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where these tests are used in industry:**
> 1. **Transformer Manufacturing** — every transformer leaves the factory with a "Test Certificate" showing OC and SC test results.
> 2. **Medical Equipment Certification** — IEC 60601-1 mandates isolation transformer testing for patient safety.
> 3. **Power Grid Commissioning** — Before energizing a new grid transformer, utilities run OC and SC tests for acceptance.
> 4. **Condition Monitoring** — Periodic OC tests can detect winding short-circuits (decreased magnetizing reactance) or core degradation.
> 5. **Biomedical Imaging** — MRI gradient coils use transformers; their SC test determines fault current capacity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: No-Load Branch Parameters from OC Test

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Point:** We measure $V_0$, $I_0$, and $P_{oc}$ from the wattmeter in the OC test.

### **Step 1: Determine the No-Load Power Factor**

The wattmeter reads the **real input power** at no-load, which equals:
$$P_{oc} = V_0 \cdot I_0 \cdot \cos\phi_0$$

Solving for the power factor:
$$\cos\phi_0 = \frac{P_{oc}}{V_0 \cdot I_0}$$

This is the **first quantity** to compute.

### **Step 2: Resolve $I_0$ into Perpendicular Components**

The phasor diagram of the no-load condition shows $I_0$ leading/lagging $V_0$ by angle $\phi_0$. The active (in-phase) and reactive (quadrature) components are:

$$I_w = I_0 \cdot \cos\phi_0 \quad \text{(in phase with } V_0\text{)}$$

$$I_\mu = I_0 \cdot \sin\phi_0 \quad \text{(lagging } V_0 \text{ by 90°)}$$

By Pythagoras:
$$I_0 = \sqrt{I_w^2 + I_\mu^2}$$

### **Step 3: Compute Core-Loss Resistance $R_c$**

$R_c$ is the resistance that, when connected across $V_0$, would dissipate the same real power $P_{oc}$:
$$P_{oc} = \frac{V_0^2}{R_c} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_c = \frac{V_0^2}{P_{oc}}$$

Alternative form using $I_w$:
$$R_c = \frac{V_0}{I_w} \quad \text{(since } I_w = V_0/R_c\text{)}$$

### **Step 4: Compute Magnetizing Reactance $X_m$**

$X_m$ is the reactance that draws the same reactive power (magnetizing VARs) as the core:
$$Q_{oc} = V_0 \cdot I_0 \cdot \sin\phi_0 = \frac{V_0^2}{X_m}$$

Therefore:
$$X_m = \frac{V_0^2}{Q_{oc}} = \frac{V_0^2}{V_0 \cdot I_0 \cdot \sin\phi_0} = \frac{V_0}{I_\mu}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final OC Test Result:** The shunt branch of the equivalent circuit (referred to LV side) is:
> $$\boxed{R_c = \frac{V_0^2}{P_{oc}}, \qquad X_m = \frac{V_0}{I_\mu}, \qquad P_i = P_{oc}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Series Impedance from SC Test

### **Step 1: Apply Reduced Voltage, Measure Quantities**

We measure $V_{sc}$ (low), $I_{sc} = I_{FL}$, and $P_{sc}$ (full-load copper loss).

### **Step 2: Compute Equivalent Impedance**

By Ohm's law on the equivalent series circuit:
$$Z_{01} = \frac{V_{sc}}{I_{sc}}$$

### **Step 3: Compute Equivalent Resistance**

The wattmeter reading equals $I_{sc}^2 R_{01}$ (only copper loss exists; iron loss is negligible due to small applied voltage):
$$P_{sc} = I_{sc}^2 \cdot R_{01} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_{01} = \frac{P_{sc}}{I_{sc}^2}$$

### **Step 4: Compute Equivalent Leakage Reactance**

From the impedance triangle:
$$Z_{01}^2 = R_{01}^2 + X_{01}^2$$

Solving for $X_{01}$:
$$X_{01} = \sqrt{Z_{01}^2 - R_{01}^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final SC Test Result:** The series branch (referred to HV side, in this case) is:
> $$\boxed{Z_{01} = \frac{V_{sc}}{I_{sc}}, \quad R_{01} = \frac{P_{sc}}{I_{sc}^2}, \quad X_{01} = \sqrt{Z_{01}^2 - R_{01}^2}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Efficiency from OC and SC Test Data

**Given:** Rating $S$ (VA), load fraction $x$, load power factor $\cos\phi$, OC loss $P_i$, SC full-load copper loss $P_{cu,FL}$.

### **Step 1: Output Power**
$$P_{out} = x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi$$

### **Step 2: Total Losses**
Iron loss is **constant** (depends on $V$ only, which is constant at rated voltage). Copper loss varies as $I^2 \propto x^2$:
$$P_{loss} = P_i + x^2 \cdot P_{cu,FL}$$

### **Step 3: Efficiency Formula**
$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{loss}} = \frac{x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi}{x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi + P_i + x^2 \cdot P_{cu,FL}}$$

### **Step 4: Maximum Efficiency Condition**
Differentiating numerator and denominator logic — efficiency is maximum when **variable loss = constant loss**:
$$x^2 \cdot P_{cu,FL} = P_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{x = \sqrt{\frac{P_i}{P_{cu,FL}}}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A transformer is most efficient at that specific load fraction. Designers often size transformers so that typical operating load ≈ this fraction.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A 5 kVA, 200 V / 400 V, 50 Hz single-phase transformer gave the following test results:
> - **OC Test** (LV side open): $V_0 = 200$ V, $I_0 = 0.5$ A, $P_{oc} = 60$ W
> - **SC Test** (LV side shorted, HV energized): $V_{sc} = 20$ V, $I_{sc} = 12.5$ A, $P_{sc} = 100$ W
>
> **Find:** (a) Core loss resistance and magnetizing reactance referred to LV side.
> (b) Equivalent impedance, resistance, and reactance referred to HV side.
> (c) Efficiency at full load, 0.8 p.f. lagging.

### **Solution:**

**Part (a) — OC Test (Referred to LV Side):**

Full-load current on LV side: $I_{FL,LV} = 5000/200 = 25$ A. So $I_0/I_{FL} = 2\%$ ✓

Step 1: No-load power factor:
$$\cos\phi_0 = \frac{P_{oc}}{V_0 \cdot I_0} = \frac{60}{200 \times 0.5} = \frac{60}{100} = 0.6$$

Step 2: Working current and magnetizing current:
$$I_w = I_0 \cos\phi_0 = 0.5 \times 0.6 = 0.3 \text{ A}$$
$$I_\mu = I_0 \sin\phi_0 = 0.5 \times 0.8 = 0.4 \text{ A}$$

Step 3: Core-loss resistance:
$$R_c = \frac{V_0^2}{P_{oc}} = \frac{200^2}{60} = \frac{40000}{60} = 666.67 \text{ Ω}$$

Step 4: Magnetizing reactance:
$$X_m = \frac{V_0}{I_\mu} = \frac{200}{0.4} = 500 \text{ Ω}$$

**Part (b) — SC Test (Referred to HV Side):**

Step 1: Full-load current on HV side: $I_{FL,HV} = 5000/400 = 12.5$ A ✓

Step 2: Equivalent impedance:
$$Z_{01,HV} = \frac{V_{sc}}{I_{sc}} = \frac{20}{12.5} = 1.6 \text{ Ω}$$

Step 3: Equivalent resistance:
$$R_{01,HV} = \frac{P_{sc}}{I_{sc}^2} = \frac{100}{12.5^2} = \frac{100}{156.25} = 0.64 \text{ Ω}$$

Step 4: Equivalent reactance:
$$X_{01,HV} = \sqrt{Z_{01}^2 - R_{01}^2} = \sqrt{1.6^2 - 0.64^2} = \sqrt{2.56 - 0.4096} = \sqrt{2.1504} = 1.466 \text{ Ω}$$

**Part (c) — Efficiency at Full Load, 0.8 p.f. Lagging:**

Output power: $P_{out} = S \cos\phi = 5000 \times 0.8 = 4000$ W

Total loss = $P_i + P_{cu,FL} = 60 + 100 = 160$ W

$$\eta = \frac{4000}{4000 + 160} = \frac{4000}{4160} = 0.9615 = 96.15\%$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Open Circuit Test — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Supply["AC Supply (Variac at Rated V, 50 Hz)"]
        src1[(LV Terminals)]
    end

    subgraph OCMetering["Metering Panel"]
        volt1[V: Voltmeter]
        amp1[A: Ammeter]
        watt1[W: Wattmeter]
    end

    subgraph XfmrLV["Transformer LV Winding"]
        pri[Primary Coil]
    end

    subgraph XfmrHV["Transformer HV Winding"]
        sec[Secondary Coil - OPEN]
    end

    src1 --> volt1
    volt1 --> amp1
    amp1 --> watt1
    watt1 --> pri
    pri -.flux coupling.-> sec

    note1["Readings: V0, I0, Poc"]
    note1@{ shape: doc}
    note2["Iron Loss Pi = Poc"]
    note2@{ shape: doc}
    note3["Compute Rc and Xm"]
    note3@{ shape: doc}

    watt1 --> note1
    note1 --> note2
    note2 --> note3
```

## 4.2 Short Circuit Test — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Supply2["AC Supply (Variac at 5-10% Rated V)"]
        src2[(HV Terminals)]
    end

    subgraph SCMetering["Metering Panel"]
        volt2[V: Voltmeter]
        amp2[A: Ammeter]
        watt2[W: Wattmeter]
    end

    subgraph XfmrHV2["Transformer HV Winding"]
        pri2[Primary Coil]
    end

    subgraph XfmrLV2["Transformer LV Winding"]
        sec2[Secondary Coil - SHORTED]
    end

    src2 --> volt2
    volt2 --> amp2
    amp2 --> watt2
    watt2 --> pri2
    pri2 -.flux coupling.-> sec2

    short[Short Circuit Link]
    sec2 --- short

    note4["Readings: Vsc, Isc, Psc"]
    note4@{ shape: doc}
    note5["Copper Loss Pcu = Psc at IFL"]
    note5@{ shape: doc}
    note6["Compute R01, X01, Z01"]
    note6@{ shape: doc}

    watt2 --> note4
    note4 --> note5
    note5 --> note6
```

## 4.3 Equivalent Circuit with OC and SC Test Results Mapped

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PrimarySide["Primary (LV) Side - OC Test Gives Shunt Branch"]
        V1[V1 - Applied]
        R01p["R01 - from SC Test"]
        X01p["X01 - from SC Test"]
        Rc["Rc - from OC Test"]
        Xm["Xm - from OC Test"]
    end

    V1 --> R01p
    R01p --> X01p
    X01p --> nodeA((A))
    nodeA --- Rc
    nodeA --- Xm
    nodeA --> SecSide[Secondary Side]

    noteOc["OC Test: V0 across shunt branch only (R01, X01 negligible at no-load)"]
    noteOc@{ shape: note}
    noteSc["SC Test: Vsc across series branch only (Rc, Xm negligible at low V)"]
    noteSc@{ shape: note}

    Rc -.- noteOc
    R01p -.- noteSc
```

## 4.4 Calculation Pipeline Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: Transformer Data + Test Readings])
    Oc["OC: V0, I0, Poc"]
    Sc["SC: Vsc, Isc, Psc"]
    Pi["Pi = Poc (Iron Loss)"]
    Pcu["Pcu = Psc (Copper Loss)"]
    Rc["Rc = V0² / Poc"]
    Xm["Xm = V0 / Imu"]
    Z01["Z01 = Vsc / Isc"]
    R01["R01 = Psc / Isc²"]
    X01["X01 = sqrt Z01² - R01²"]
    Eta["Eta = xS cos phi / xS cos phi + Pi + x² Pcu"]
    VR["VR percent = I R01 cos phi + X01 sin phi / V times 100"]
    Done([Efficiency and Regulation Output])

    Start --> Oc
    Start --> Sc
    Oc --> Pi
    Oc --> Rc
    Oc --> Xm
    Sc --> Pcu
    Sc --> Z01
    Sc --> R01
    Sc --> X01
    Pi --> Eta
    Pcu --> Eta
    R01 --> VR
    X01 --> VR
    Eta --> Done
    VR --> Done
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Why is the Open Circuit Test performed on the Low Voltage (LV) side of a transformer?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The OC test is performed on the LV side for the following reasons:
1. **Safety** — LV side requires a lower test voltage (e.g., 200 V instead of 11 kV), making it safer for personnel. **(1 mark)**
2. **Economy** — Standard laboratory variacs and meters are designed for LV ratings. **(1 mark)**
3. **Higher no-load current on LV side** — Since $I_0 = V_0 / Z_m$ and the magnetizing impedance referred to LV is lower, $I_0$ is larger, giving more accurate ammeter and wattmeter readings. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**List the instruments used in the Short Circuit Test and state what each instrument reads.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Voltmeter** — connected across the energized (HV) winding; reads the **reduced applied voltage $V_{sc}$** (5–10% of rated). **(1 mark)**
2. **Ammeter** — connected in series with the energized winding; reads the **short-circuit current $I_{sc}$** = full-load current. **(1 mark)**
3. **Wattmeter** — connected to measure input power; reads the **full-load copper loss $P_{cu} = P_{sc}$**. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students forget to convert the no-load current to the correct side when computing $R_c$ and $X_m$. **Always use the test-side voltage and current.**
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Students confuse the **OC test power factor** with the **load power factor**. The OC p.f. is computed from $P_{oc}, V_0, I_0$ — NOT given in the problem.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** In efficiency calculations, students forget that **iron loss is constant** but **copper loss varies as $x^2$**.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Voltage regulation sign: **lagging p.f. gives + (drop)**, **leading p.f. gives − (rise)**.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Unit mismatch — $P_{cu}$ in kW vs $P_i$ in W. Convert before adding.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**A 10 kVA, 500 V / 250 V, 50 Hz single-phase transformer gave the following test results:**
- **OC Test (LV side energized, HV open):** $V_0 = 250$ V, $I_0 = 1.2$ A, $P_{oc} = 80$ W
- **SC Test (HV side energized, LV shorted):** $V_{sc} = 25$ V, $I_{sc} = 20$ A, $P_{sc} = 120$ W

**Find:**
**(a)** (7 marks) The parameters of the equivalent circuit referred to the HV side:
- Core loss resistance $R_c$
- Magnetizing reactance $X_m$
- Equivalent resistance $R_{01}$
- Equivalent reactance $X_{01}$

**(b)** (7 marks) 
- (i) The efficiency at **half full-load** at 0.85 p.f. lagging. (3 marks)
- (ii) The **voltage regulation** at full load, 0.8 p.f. lagging. (4 marks)

---

### **Complete Model Solution for Question A**

#### **Solution to Part (a):**

**Step 1: Identify full-load currents.**
- LV side: $I_{FL,LV} = 10000/250 = 40$ A
- HV side: $I_{FL,HV} = 10000/500 = 20$ A ✓ (matches SC test current)

**Step 2: OC Test — Compute $\cos\phi_0$ and branch currents.**

$$\cos\phi_0 = \frac{P_{oc}}{V_0 \cdot I_0} = \frac{80}{250 \times 1.2} = \frac{80}{300} = 0.2667$$

$$\sin\phi_0 = \sqrt{1 - 0.2667^2} = \sqrt{1 - 0.0711} = \sqrt{0.9289} = 0.9638$$

$$I_w = I_0 \cos\phi_0 = 1.2 \times 0.2667 = 0.32 \text{ A}$$
$$I_\mu = I_0 \sin\phi_0 = 1.2 \times 0.9638 = 1.157 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Compute $R_c$ and $X_m$ referred to LV side.**

$$R_c = \frac{V_0}{I_w} = \frac{250}{0.32} = 781.25 \text{ Ω}$$
$$X_m = \frac{V_0}{I_\mu} = \frac{250}{1.157} = 216.16 \text{ Ω}$$

**Step 4: Refer $R_c$ and $X_m$ to HV side using $K = V_{HV}/V_{LV} = 500/250 = 2$.**

The transformation ratio is $K = N_1/N_2 = 2$ (HV/LV). Impedances transform as $K^2$:

$$R_{c,HV} = K^2 \cdot R_c = 4 \times 781.25 = 3125 \text{ Ω}$$
$$X_{m,HV} = K^2 \cdot X_m = 4 \times 216.16 = 864.64 \text{ Ω}$$

**Step 5: SC Test — Compute series parameters (already on HV side).**

$$Z_{01} = \frac{V_{sc}}{I_{sc}} = \frac{25}{20} = 1.25 \text{ Ω}$$
$$R_{01} = \frac{P_{sc}}{I_{sc}^2} = \frac{120}{20^2} = \frac{120}{400} = 0.30 \text{ Ω}$$
$$X_{01} = \sqrt{Z_{01}^2 - R_{01}^2} = \sqrt{1.25^2 - 0.30^2} = \sqrt{1.5625 - 0.09} = \sqrt{1.4725} = 1.213 \text{ Ω}$$

> **[7 marks] All equivalent circuit parameters obtained.** ✅

---

#### **Solution to Part (b):**

**(b)(i) — Efficiency at half full-load, 0.85 p.f. lagging:**

Output power at $x = 0.5$:
$$P_{out} = x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi = 0.5 \times 10000 \times 0.85 = 4250 \text{ W}$$

Total loss = Iron loss + Copper loss at $x = 0.5$:
- Iron loss: $P_i = P_{oc} = 80$ W (constant)
- Copper loss at half load: $P_{cu} = x^2 \cdot P_{cu,FL} = (0.5)^2 \times 120 = 0.25 \times 120 = 30$ W

$$P_{loss} = 80 + 30 = 110 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{loss}} = \frac{4250}{4250 + 110} = \frac{4250}{4360} = 0.9748 = \mathbf{97.48\%}$$

> **[3 marks]** ✅

---

**(b)(ii) — Voltage Regulation at full load, 0.8 p.f. lagging:**

Given: $I = I_{FL,HV} = 20$ A, $\cos\phi = 0.8$ (lagging), $\sin\phi = 0.6$, $R_{01} = 0.30$ Ω, $X_{01} = 1.213$ Ω, $V_1 = V_{rated,HV} = 500$ V.

Using the approximate voltage regulation formula (KTU standard):
$$\text{VR} = \frac{I(R_{01}\cos\phi + X_{01}\sin\phi)}{V_1} \times 100$$

Numerator:
$$I(R_{01}\cos\phi + X_{01}\sin\phi) = 20 \times (0.30 \times 0.8 + 1.213 \times 0.6)$$
$$= 20 \times (0.24 + 0.7278) = 20 \times 0.9678 = 19.356 \text{ V}$$

$$\text{VR}\% = \frac{19.356}{500} \times 100 = \mathbf{3.87\%}$$

> **[4 marks]** ✅

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** (7 marks) Explain with neat connection diagrams:
(i) The Open Circuit Test on a single-phase transformer. State which winding is energized and why. What are the quantities measured?
(ii) The Short Circuit Test on a single-phase transformer. State which side is shorted and why. What are the quantities measured?

**(b)** (7 marks) A 2 kVA, 200 V / 100 V, 50 Hz transformer has the following test data:
- OC test (LV open): $V_0 = 100$ V, $I_0 = 0.4$ A, $P_{oc} = 20$ W
- SC test (LV shorted): $V_{sc} = 10$ V, $I_{sc} = 10$ A, $P_{sc} = 40$ W

Find: (i) Equivalent circuit parameters referred to HV side. (4 marks) (ii) Efficiency at 75% of full load, 0.9 p.f. leading. (3 marks)

---

### **Model Solution Outline for Question B (Key Steps)**

**(a) Explanation:**

**OC Test (i):**
- The **LV winding is energized** at its rated voltage (here 100 V) using a variac. The **HV winding is kept open**.
- Reason: LV side is safe, requires standard meters, and draws higher $I_0$ for better accuracy.
- Meters: Voltmeter across LV, Ammeter in series with LV, Wattmeter measuring input.
- Gives: $V_0$, $I_0$, $P_{oc}$.
- Yields: Iron loss $P_i$, $R_c$, $X_m$. **(3.5 marks)**

**SC Test (ii):**
- The **LV winding is short-circuited** through an ammeter (or a link). The **HV winding is energized** at a **reduced voltage** (5–10% of rated).
- Reason: At full rated voltage, current would be 10–20× full-load — destructive. Reduced V is enough to circulate full-load current.
- Meters: Voltmeter across HV, Ammeter in series with HV, Wattmeter measuring input.
- Gives: $V_{sc}$, $I_{sc}$, $P_{sc}$.
- Yields: $R_{01}$, $X_{01}$, $Z_{01}$, full-load copper loss. **(3.5 marks)**

**(b) Calculations:**

Transformation ratio $K = 200/100 = 2$.

OC test on LV side: $\cos\phi_0 = 20/(100 \times 0.4) = 0.5$, so $I_w = 0.2$ A, $I_\mu = 0.346$ A.
- $R_c = 100/0.2 = 500$ Ω; $X_m = 100/0.346 = 289$ Ω (on LV side)
- Referred to HV: $R_{c,HV} = 4 \times 500 = 2000$ Ω; $X_{m,HV} = 4 \times 289 = 1156$ Ω

SC test on HV side: $Z_{01} = 10/10 = 1$ Ω; $R_{01} = 40/100 = 0.4$ Ω; $X_{01} = \sqrt{1 - 0.16} = 0.917$ Ω **(4 marks)**

Efficiency at $x = 0.75$, p.f. = 0.9 leading:
- $P_{out} = 0.75 \times 2000 \times 0.9 = 1350$ W
- $P_{cu} = (0.75)^2 \times 40 = 22.5$ W; $P_i = 20$ W
- $P_{loss} = 42.5$ W
- $\eta = 1350/(1350 + 42.5) = 96.95\%$ **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **🚀 KTU Rapid Revision Checklist — Open Circuit & Short Circuit Test**

- ✅ **OC Test** = LV energized, HV open, **rated voltage** applied → gives **iron loss** and **shunt branch** ($R_c$, $X_m$).
- ✅ **SC Test** = LV shorted, HV energized, **reduced voltage (5–10%)** applied → gives **full-load copper loss** and **series branch** ($R_{01}$, $X_{01}$, $Z_{01}$).
- ✅ **No-load current** is small (2–10% of $I_{FL}$), at **low p.f. (0.1–0.3 lagging)**.
- ✅ **SC current = full-load current**, SC voltage is small, SC p.f. is **highly inductive**.
- ✅ **Iron loss is constant** (depends on $V, f$ only); **Copper loss varies as $x^2$** with load fraction $x$.
- ✅ **Maximum efficiency** occurs when $x = \sqrt{P_i / P_{cu,FL}}$.
- ✅ **Approximate voltage regulation** for lagging p.f.: $\text{VR} = I(R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)/V$ — note the **plus sign** (both drops add).
- ✅ **For leading p.f.**, regulation can be negative: $(R\cos\phi - X\sin\phi)$.
- ✅ **Equivalent circuit parameters** are referred to the side where the test was performed.
- ✅ **Impedances transfer** across sides as $K^2$, where $K = V_{test\,side}/V_{other\,side}$.
- ✅ **Biomedical relevance:** These tests certify **medical isolation transformers** (IEC 60601-1) used in ICU, MRI, OT, and ECG machines.
- ✅ **Why not load directly?** A 1000 kVA load test would need a 1000 kW dummy resistor — impractical, expensive, and dangerous. OC + SC tests are the elegant indirect method.
- ✅ **KTU Pitfall:** Never confuse the **transformer ratio K = V₁/V₂ = N₁/N₂** with the **current ratio 1/K = I₂/I₁**. Impedances always scale as $K^2$.
- ✅ **Examiner's Tip:** Always state the **direction of impedance transformation** explicitly in your answer to score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Auto Transformer and Isolation Transformer: Working Principle and Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT411 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY FOR BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Auto Transformer and Isolation Transformer: Working Principle and Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Auto Transformer — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Auto Transformer (Definition per KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> An **auto-transformer** is a type of electrical transformer in which a **single continuous winding** serves as both the primary and the secondary winding. The two windings are not electrically isolated from each other; instead, they share a common portion of the same coil. The voltage transformation is achieved by **tapping the winding at appropriate points**, rather than by employing two separate magnetically-coupled coils.

In an auto-transformer, the primary and secondary windings are physically and electrically connected (they form **one continuous winding**), as opposed to the conventional two-winding transformer where primary and secondary are isolated.

**Mathematical Statement of Transformation Ratio:**

$$K = \frac{V_2}{V_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{I_1}{I_2}$$

Where:
- $V_1$ = Primary Voltage, $V_2$ = Secondary Voltage
- $N_1$ = Total number of primary turns, $N_2$ = Number of secondary (tapped) turns
- $I_1$ = Primary current, $I_2$ = Secondary current
- $K$ = Transformation ratio

### 1.2 Isolation Transformer — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Isolation Transformer (Definition per KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> An **isolation transformer** is a transformer with **separately wound primary and secondary windings** that are magnetically coupled but **electrically isolated** from each other. The primary and secondary windings have a turns ratio of typically **1:1** (though it can vary), and its primary function is to provide **galvanic isolation** between the source and the load, eliminating direct electrical connection.

**Key Symbol:** The turns ratio is denoted as $a = N_1/N_2$.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

**Analogy 1 — Auto Transformer as a "Variable Water Tap":**
Imagine a water pipe (single winding) that has multiple outlet taps along its length. You can draw water from any tap, and the pressure at each tap is different depending on its distance from the source. The auto-transformer works exactly like this — there is **one coil** with multiple connection points (taps), and the output voltage is "tapped" from an appropriate point. The portion of the winding common to both input and output is like the "shared" segment of the pipe.

**Analogy 2 — Isolation Transformer as a "Safety Buffer Pool":**
Imagine a deep swimming pool (the magnetic core) separating two river banks (primary and secondary). Water from the first bank flows into the pool, and water is pumped out to the other bank. However, the water on one side can **never** physically touch the water on the other side — the pool acts as a buffer. This is exactly what an isolation transformer does — the magnetic field transfers energy, but the primary and secondary circuits are **electrically isolated**, making it ideal for biomedical applications where patient safety is paramount.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Design Parameters:**
> - **Standard Frequencies:** **50 Hz (India/Europe)**, 60 Hz (USA)
> - **Core Material (biomedical isolation):** Silicon steel with high permeability (μ > 5000) for 50 Hz operation
> - **Typical Isolation Voltage Rating:** **1.5 kV to 5 kV** for medical-grade isolation transformers
> - **Leakage Current Standard for Medical Equipment:** < **100 μA** (per IEC 60601-1) for patient-connected equipment
> - **K (Transformation Ratio) Auto-Transformer:** Step-down K > 1, Step-up K < 1
> - **Biomedical Application Standard:** Patient protection requires isolation up to **4000 V AC** for 1 minute (per IEC 60601)

---

### 1.5 Real-World Examples (Biomedical Context)

| Application | Transformer Type | Why Used |
|-------------|------------------|----------|
| ECG/EEG machines | Isolation Transformer | Patient safety from leakage currents |
| X-ray machines | Auto-Transformer | Smooth voltage control for kV selection |
| Autoclave heater control | Auto-Transformer | Variable AC voltage for temperature control |
| Defibrillator power supply | Isolation Transformer | Isolation between mains and patient circuit |
| Surgical diathermy | Auto-Transformer + Isolation | Voltage regulation and patient isolation |
| MRI room power | Isolation Transformer | Ground fault isolation |

---

### 1.6 Block Diagram (Conceptual)

```
AUTOCONFIG:
   ┌─────────────── Single Winding ───────────────┐
   │  ●─────●─────●─────●─────●─────●─────●       │
   │  A     B     C     D     E     F     G       │
   │  (Common Port)    (Tapped Output)            │
   └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
   
ISOLATION CONFIG:
   ┌────────┐    Magnetic Core    ┌────────┐
   │Primary │  ←── Magnetic ──→   │Secondary│
   │  Wdg   │      Coupling        │   Wdg   │
   └────┬───┘                       └────┬───┘
        │  Electrically ISOLATED           │
   Source Side                          Load Side
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Auto-Transformer — Operational Theory

#### 2.1.1 Construction
An auto-transformer has a **single winding** wound on a laminated silicon steel core. The entire winding is divided into two parts:
- **Common winding (N_c)**: The part shared between primary and secondary
- **Series winding (N_se)**: The part exclusive to the primary or secondary

#### 2.1.2 Step-Down Auto-Transformer Working Principle
> [!NOTE]
> **Step-Down Operation:** When used as a step-down transformer, the primary is connected across the **entire winding**, and the secondary is connected across a **portion** of the winding (tapped point).

**Step-by-Step Logic:**
1. Primary voltage $V_1$ is applied across the **full winding** (N₁ turns).
2. By electromagnetic induction, an EMF is induced per turn: $E_t = V_1 / N_1$.
3. The secondary output is taken across $N_2$ turns, where $N_2 < N_1$ (for step-down).
4. Secondary voltage: $V_2 = E_t \times N_2 = V_1 \times (N_2/N_1) = V_1 \times K$.
5. For step-down: $K < 1$, so $V_2 < V_1$.

#### 2.1.3 Step-Up Auto-Transformer Working Principle
1. Primary is connected across a **portion** of the winding.
2. Secondary is taken across the **full winding** (N₂ > N₁).
3. For step-up: $K > 1$, so $V_2 > V_1$.

#### 2.1.4 Current Relationship & Energy Transfer
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Auto-Transformers are more efficient than two-winding transformers:**
> In a two-winding transformer, energy is transferred entirely **magnetically** through the core. In an auto-transformer, part of the energy is transferred **conductively** through the common winding, and the rest is transferred magnetically. This results in **lower losses**, **smaller size**, and **higher efficiency** (typically **98–99%**).

The current in the common portion of the winding is the **difference** between primary and secondary currents (not the sum, as in a two-winding transformer).

For a step-down auto-transformer:

$$I_{common} = I_2 - I_1 = I_1(K - 1)$$

Where $I_2$ is the load current (secondary) and $I_1$ is the input current (primary).

#### 2.1.5 Power Conducted vs Power Transformed
The total apparent power transferred has two components:
- **Conducted Power (P_cond):** Transferred directly through the common winding
- **Transformed Power (P_trans):** Transferred magnetically through the core

$$S_{total} = S_{conducted} + S_{transformed}$$

The transformed (magnetic) power is the **rated power** of the auto-transformer. The saving in conductor material and cost comes from the conducted portion.

### 2.2 Auto-Transformer Advantages & Disadvantages

| **Advantages** | **Disadvantages** |
|----------------|-------------------|
| Higher efficiency (98–99%) | No electrical isolation between primary & secondary |
| Smaller size and weight | Short-circuit current is very high (no isolation barrier) |
| Lower cost (less copper required) | Not suitable where safety isolation is required |
| Better voltage regulation | Dangerous if used in biomedical patient-connected circuits without additional protection |
| Continuous variable output (if tap is variable) | |

### 2.3 Isolation Transformer — Operational Theory

#### 2.3.1 Construction
An isolation transformer has **two physically and electrically separate windings**:
- **Primary Winding (N₁)** — connected to the source
- **Secondary Winding (N₂)** — connected to the load
- Both windings are wound on a **common magnetic core** (typically shell-type or core-type construction)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Design Feature:** The insulation between the primary and secondary windings is **reinforced** (double or triple insulation) to withstand high voltages — this is critical for medical applications.

#### 2.3.2 Working Principle
1. AC voltage $V_1$ is applied to the primary winding.
2. An alternating magnetic flux is set up in the core: $\phi = \phi_m \sin(\omega t)$.
3. This flux links the secondary winding through the core.
4. By **Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction**, an EMF is induced in the secondary.
5. Since there is **no electrical connection** between primary and secondary, the load is **galvanically isolated** from the source.

#### 2.3.3 Mathematical Relationships

**Voltage Relation:**
$$\frac{V_1}{V_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = a$$

For a 1:1 isolation transformer: $N_1 = N_2$, so $V_1 = V_2$.

**Current Relation:**
$$\frac{I_1}{I_2} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{1}{a}$$

**Power Relation (ideal):**
$$S_1 = S_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_1 I_1 = V_2 I_2$$

#### 2.3.4 Why Isolation Transformers are Essential in Biomedical Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Biomedical Significance:**
> - **Patient Safety:** A fault current from the live wire to the patient can be **fatal**, especially during procedures like ECG, EEG, surgery, or dialysis. Isolation transformers prevent any direct conductive path between the mains supply and the patient.
> - **Leakage Current Suppression:** Reduces leakage current to less than **100 μA** (IEC 60601-1 standard).
> - **Ground Loop Elimination:** Prevents ground loops that introduce noise in sensitive biomedical signal measurements.
> - **Common-Mode Noise Rejection:** Since the primary and secondary share **only magnetic coupling**, common-mode noise (interference that appears on both lines) is rejected.

#### 2.3.5 Isolated Power Systems in Hospitals
Modern hospitals use an **Isolated Power System (IPS)** with isolation transformers feeding areas like:
- Operating rooms
- ICUs
- Cardiac catheterization labs
- Dialysis units

The **Line Isolation Monitor (LIM)** continuously checks the impedance of the isolation system and alerts if leakage current exceeds safe limits.

### 2.4 Comparison: Auto-Transformer vs Isolation Transformer

| **Parameter** | **Auto-Transformer** | **Isolation Transformer** |
|---------------|----------------------|---------------------------|
| Number of windings | One continuous winding | Two separate windings |
| Electrical isolation | No (common connection) | Yes (full galvanic isolation) |
| Efficiency | Very high (98–99%) | Lower (95–97%) |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Size & Weight | Smaller | Larger |
| Safety | Not safe for medical patient use | Extremely safe for medical use |
| Voltage regulation | Better | Moderate |
| Short-circuit current | Very high (dangerous) | Limited |
| Biomedical use | Only for equipment (not patient) | For patient-connected devices |
| Conductor material saving | Significant (1 – 1/K) | None (full material) |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — AUTO-TRANSFORMER & ISOLATION TRANSFORMER**

| **#** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|-------|-------------|-----------------|-----------|
| 1 | $K = \dfrac{V_2}{V_1} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{I_1}{I_2}$ | Transformation ratio (Auto-transformer) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $S_{rated} = V_2 I_2 = V_1 I_1$ | Apparent power rating (ideal) | VA |
| 3 | $I_{common} = I_2 - I_1 = I_1(K - 1)$ | Current in common winding (step-down) | A |
| 4 | $S_{transformed} = (1 - 1/K) \times S_{total}$ | Power transferred magnetically | VA |
| 5 | $S_{conducted} = (1/K) \times S_{total}$ | Power transferred conductively | VA |
| 6 | $\text{Saving in material} = (1 - 1/K) \times 100\%$ | Material saved vs two-winding | % |
| 7 | $Z_{p}^{auto} = (1 - 1/K)^2 \times Z_{p}^{two-wdg}$ | Equivalent impedance referred to primary | Ω |
| 8 | $\dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = a$ | Voltage ratio (Isolation transformer) | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $\dfrac{I_1}{I_2} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{1}{a}$ | Current ratio (Isolation transformer) | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $V_1 = 4.44 f N_1 \phi_m$ | EMF equation per primary | V |
| 11 | $V_2 = 4.44 f N_2 \phi_m$ | EMF equation per secondary | V |
| 12 | $I_{leakage, max} < 100 \, \mu A$ | Medical safety limit (IEC 60601-1) | μA |
| 13 | $V_{isolation} \geq 4000 \, V_{AC}$ | Withstand voltage for medical isolation | V |

> **Note:** Use $\vert x \vert$ instead of $|x|$ in case of absolute value to avoid markdown table syntax conflicts.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

**Auto-Transformer Applications:**
- **AC voltage regulators** in laboratories
- **Variable voltage supplies** for motor starting (reduced voltage starting)
- **Audio systems** (impedance matching)
- **Power transmission** (as booster/regulator transformers)
- **Cubic voltage control** (1/1, 2/1, 3/1 connections)

**Isolation Transformer Applications in Biomedical Engineering:**
- Patient monitoring systems (ECG, EEG, EMG)
- Defibrillators (isolation between mains and patient electrodes)
- Surgical equipment (electrosurgical units)
- Incubators (neonatal care)
- Dialysis machines
- Imaging equipment (CT, MRI power supplies)
- Hospital isolated power systems (IPS)
- Patient warming/cooling devices

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: EMF Equation of a Transformer (Foundation for both types)

Starting from Faraday's law, the EMF induced in a coil with N turns when flux $\phi$ varies:

$$e = -N \dfrac{d\phi}{dt}$$

Assume sinusoidal flux: $\phi = \phi_m \sin(\omega t)$.

$$e = -N \dfrac{d}{dt}\big[\phi_m \sin(\omega t)\big] = -N \phi_m \omega \cos(\omega t)$$

Maximum value: $E_m = N \phi_m \omega = 2\pi f N \phi_m$.

RMS value: $E = \dfrac{E_m}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{2\pi f N \phi_m}{\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2} \pi f N \phi_m$.

Computing: $\sqrt{2}\pi = 4.44288 \approx 4.44$.

$$\boxed{E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \phi_m}$$

Applying this to primary and secondary of an isolation transformer:
- $V_1 \approx E_1 = 4.44 f N_1 \phi_m$
- $V_2 \approx E_2 = 4.44 f N_2 \phi_m$

Taking the ratio:
$$\dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = a$$

This confirms the **turns ratio = voltage ratio** for an ideal transformer (and isolation transformer).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Power Transformed vs Power Conducted in an Auto-Transformer

**Given:**
- Total apparent power rating: $S = V_2 I_2 = V_1 I_1$
- Transformation ratio: $K = N_2/N_1$

**Step 1:** Write the current in the common winding.

For a step-down auto-transformer, applying KCL at the tapping point:

$$I_{common} = I_2 - I_1$$

**Step 2:** Express the secondary current in terms of primary current.

From $K = I_1/I_2$:

$$I_2 = \dfrac{I_1}{K}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute in the common winding current equation:

$$I_{common} = \dfrac{I_1}{K} - I_1 = I_1 \left( \dfrac{1 - K}{K} \right)$$

**Step 4:** Compute the apparent power transferred **magnetically** (transformed power).

The transformed power is the power handled by the **series winding** (the part not shared):

$$S_{transformed} = V_1 (I_2 - I_1) = V_1 \times I_1 \left( \dfrac{1 - K}{K} \right)$$

Since $V_1 I_1 = S$:

$$S_{transformed} = S \times \dfrac{(1 - K)}{K} = S \left( 1 - \dfrac{1}{K} \right)$$

**Step 5:** Compute the apparent power transferred **conductively**.

The conducted power passes through the common winding without any magnetic transformation:

$$S_{conducted} = V_1 I_1 - S_{transformed} = S - S \left( 1 - \dfrac{1}{K} \right)$$

$$\boxed{S_{conducted} = \dfrac{S}{K}}$$

**Step 6:** Verify total power:

$$S_{transformed} + S_{conducted} = S \left( 1 - \dfrac{1}{K} \right) + \dfrac{S}{K} = S - \dfrac{S}{K} + \dfrac{S}{K} = S \checkmark$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Equivalent Impedance of an Auto-Transformer Referred to Primary Side

**Given:** A two-winding transformer has primary impedance $Z_p^{two-wdg}$ referred to the primary side. We want to find the equivalent primary impedance when the same transformer is **reconnected as an auto-transformer**.

**Setup:** When connected as a two-winding transformer, rated primary voltage = $V_1$ and rated primary current = $I_1$, with transformation ratio $K = N_2/N_1$.

**Step 1:** The voltage across the series winding = $V_1 - V_2 = V_1 - K V_1 = V_1 (1 - K)$.

Wait — for step-down ($K < 1$): $V_2 < V_1$, so the series winding sees $V_1 - V_2 = V_1(1 - K)$.

**Step 2:** When reconnected as an auto-transformer, the primary is across the full $V_1$ (total winding), and the secondary is at $V_2 = K V_1$.

**Step 3:** The equivalent primary impedance of the auto-transformer:

$$Z_p^{auto} = \dfrac{V_1}{I_1^{auto}}$$

**Step 4:** Compare with two-winding case: in the two-winding version, primary current is $I_1^{two-wdg}$ at the same voltage. The auto-transformer version can supply a higher current (since less current passes through magnetic transformation).

**Step 5:** For the auto-transformer, the current in the series winding is the same as in the two-winding primary, but the **total input current** is **higher** by factor $1/(1 - 1/K)$.

Therefore:

$$Z_p^{auto} = Z_p^{two-wdg} \left( 1 - \dfrac{1}{K} \right)^2$$

$$\boxed{Z_p^{auto} = \left( 1 - \dfrac{1}{K} \right)^2 Z_p^{two-wdg}}$$

This shows that an auto-transformer presents a **lower impedance** to the source compared to a two-winding transformer of the same physical size — explaining its better voltage regulation.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Problem 1 (KTU-style)

> **Problem:** A 10 kVA, 2000/200 V single-phase transformer is reconnected as a step-down auto-transformer with the 2000 V winding as the primary. Calculate:
> (a) The transformation ratio K
> (b) The currents in the primary, secondary, and common winding
> (c) The power transferred magnetically (transformed) and conductively
> (d) The percentage saving in conductor material

**Given Data:**
- $S = 10 \, \text{kVA} = 10{,}000 \, \text{VA}$
- $V_1 = 2000 \, \text{V}$ (HV side / primary)
- $V_2 = 200 \, \text{V}$ (LV side / secondary)

**Part (a): Transformation Ratio**

$$K = \dfrac{V_2}{V_1} = \dfrac{200}{2000} = 0.1$$

Since $K < 1$, this is a **step-down** auto-transformer. ✓

**Part (b): Currents**

Primary current (input):

$$I_1 = \dfrac{S}{V_1} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{2000} = 5 \, \text{A}$$

Secondary current (load):

$$I_2 = \dfrac{S}{V_2} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{200} = 50 \, \text{A}$$

Common winding current:

$$I_{common} = I_2 - I_1 = 50 - 5 = 45 \, \text{A}$$

**Part (c): Power Components**

Transformed (magnetic) power:

$$S_{transformed} = S \left( 1 - \dfrac{1}{K} \right) = 10{,}000 \left( 1 - \dfrac{1}{0.1} \right)$$

$$S_{transformed} = 10{,}000 \times (1 - 10) = 10{,}000 \times (-9)$$

The negative sign indicates this analysis applies directly to step-up. For step-down, we use the correct formula:

For a step-down auto-transformer, transformed power is:

$$S_{transformed} = S (1 - K) = 10{,}000 \times (1 - 0.1) = 10{,}000 \times 0.9 = 9{,}000 \, \text{VA} = 9 \, \text{kVA}$$

Conducted power:

$$S_{conducted} = S \times K = 10{,}000 \times 0.1 = 1{,}000 \, \text{VA} = 1 \, \text{kVA}$$

Verification: $9 \, \text{kVA} + 1 \, \text{kVA} = 10 \, \text{kVA} = S$ ✓

**Part (d): Saving in Conductor Material**

$$\text{Saving} = (1 - K) \times 100\% = (1 - 0.1) \times 100\% = 90\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Only **9 kVA** out of 10 kVA is actually transformed magnetically. The remaining 1 kVA flows conductively. The auto-transformer needs to be **sized** (rated magnetically) for only 9 kVA — this is the **9/10** of the two-winding design. Hence, 90% saving in core material and conductor.

---

### 3.5 Numerical Problem 2 (KTU-style)

> **Problem:** A 5 kVA, 200/400 V two-winding transformer is to be used as a step-up auto-transformer from a 200 V source to deliver 400 V to a load. Calculate:
> (a) Transformation ratio
> (b) Primary and secondary currents of the auto-transformer
> (c) The rating of the auto-transformer
> (d) The power transferred magnetically

**Given Data:**
- $S_{two-wdg} = 5 \, \text{kVA} = 5{,}000 \, \text{VA}$
- Low voltage (LV) winding: 200 V
- High voltage (HV) winding: 400 V
- Used as step-up auto-transformer from 200 V to 400 V

**Part (a): Transformation Ratio**

For step-up: Primary = 200 V (LV), Secondary = 400 V (HV).

$$K = \dfrac{V_2}{V_1} = \dfrac{400}{200} = 2.0$$

Since $K > 1$, this is a **step-up** auto-transformer. ✓

**Part (b): Auto-Transformer Currents**

The two-winding transformer ratings:
- LV winding current: $I_{LV} = 5000/200 = 25 \, \text{A}$
- HV winding current: $I_{HV} = 5000/400 = 12.5 \, \text{A}$

When reconnected as auto-transformer:
- Primary (input) current $I_1$: Limited by LV winding = **25 A** (since the primary is connected across the LV winding)

Actually, let me reconsider. In an auto-transformer:

Primary current = 25 A (at 200 V input)

The load (secondary) current at 400 V output is determined by the rating:

$$I_2 = \dfrac{S_{auto}}{V_2}$$

**Part (c): Auto-Transformer Rating**

Power handling of auto-transformer = Power that can be transformed magnetically (through the two-winding transformer core) PLUS the conducted power.

The auto-transformer rating = (rated current of LV) × (HV voltage) = 25 A × 400 V = 10,000 VA = **10 kVA**.

OR equivalently: $S_{auto} = S_{two-wdg} \times K = 5 \times 2 = 10 \, \text{kVA}$ ✓

**Part (c) verification using formula:**

$$S_{auto} = \dfrac{S_{two-wdg}}{(1 - 1/K)} = \dfrac{5}{(1 - 1/2)} = \dfrac{5}{0.5} = 10 \, \text{kVA} \checkmark$$

**Part (d): Power Transformed Magnetically**

The transformed (magnetic) power equals the two-winding rating:

$$S_{transformed} = 5 \, \text{kVA}$$

**Conducted Power:**

$$S_{conducted} = S_{auto} - S_{transformed} = 10 - 5 = 5 \, \text{kVA}$$

**Verification via formula:**

$$S_{conducted} = S \times (1/K) = 10 \times (1/2) = 5 \, \text{kVA} \checkmark$$

**Primary and Secondary Currents of the Auto-Transformer:**

$$I_1 = \dfrac{S_{auto}}{V_1} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{200} = 50 \, \text{A}$$

$$I_2 = \dfrac{S_{auto}}{V_2} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{400} = 25 \, \text{A}$$

---

### 3.6 Numerical Problem 3 (Isolation Transformer — KTU-style)

> **Problem:** A 1:1 isolation transformer is used to supply a 230 V, 50 Hz, 500 W biomedical instrument (ECG machine). The transformer has a core cross-section of 20 cm² and a maximum flux density of 1.2 T. Calculate:
> (a) The number of turns in the primary and secondary windings
> (b) The full-load primary and secondary currents
> (c) The maximum flux in the core

**Given Data:**
- $V_1 = V_2 = 230 \, \text{V}$ (1:1 ratio)
- $f = 50 \, \text{Hz}$
- $P = 500 \, \text{W}$
- $A = 20 \, \text{cm}^2 = 20 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^2 = 0.002 \, \text{m}^2$
- $B_m = 1.2 \, \text{T}$

**Part (a): Number of Turns**

Maximum flux:

$$\phi_m = B_m \times A = 1.2 \times 0.002 = 0.0024 \, \text{Wb} = 2.4 \, \text{mWb}$$

Using EMF equation: $V_1 = 4.44 f N_1 \phi_m$

$$N_1 = \dfrac{V_1}{4.44 \, f \, \phi_m} = \dfrac{230}{4.44 \times 50 \times 0.0024}$$

Computing denominator: $4.44 \times 50 = 222$; $222 \times 0.0024 = 0.5328$.

$$N_1 = \dfrac{230}{0.5328} = 431.68 \approx 432 \, \text{turns}$$

For 1:1 ratio: $N_2 = N_1 = 432 \, \text{turns}$.

**Part (b): Full-Load Currents**

Assuming power factor $\cos\phi = 1$ (resistive load, e.g., heater):

$$I_1 = I_2 = \dfrac{P}{V} = \dfrac{500}{230} = 2.174 \, \text{A}$$

**Part (c): Maximum Flux**

Already computed: $\phi_m = 0.0024 \, \text{Wb} = 2.4 \, \text{mWb}$.

---

### 3.7 Numerical Problem 4 (KTU-style — Biomedical Application)

> **Problem:** A medical isolation transformer is rated at 5 kVA, 230 V/230 V, 50 Hz. It supplies an ECG machine that draws 2 A at 230 V at unity power factor. The transformer is connected in an isolated power system with a Line Isolation Monitor (LIM). Calculate:
> (a) The apparent power and active power consumed
> (b) The power transferred magnetically from primary to secondary
> (c) The maximum permissible leakage current per IEC 60601-1, and explain why the isolation transformer is critical here

**Given Data:**
- $V_1 = V_2 = 230 \, \text{V}$
- $I_2 = 2 \, \text{A}$
- $\cos\phi = 1$
- $S_{rated} = 5 \, \text{kVA}$

**Part (a):**

Apparent power consumed: $S = V_2 \times I_2 = 230 \times 2 = 460 \, \text{VA} = 0.46 \, \text{kVA}$.

Active power: $P = S \times \cos\phi = 460 \times 1 = 460 \, \text{W}$.

**Part (b):** All 460 W is transferred **magnetically** through the core (no conductive path in isolation transformer). Therefore, $P_{magnetic} = 460 \, \text{W}$.

**Part (c):**

Per IEC 60601-1: Maximum leakage current for patient-connected equipment:
- Normal condition: $I_{leak,max} = 100 \, \mu A = 0.1 \, \text{mA}$
- Single-fault condition: $I_{leak,max} = 500 \, \mu A = 0.5 \, \text{mA}$

The isolation transformer is critical because:
1. It breaks the direct galvanic path between the mains and patient.
2. It limits leakage current to safe values.
3. It prevents microshock hazards (currents as low as 10 μA through the heart can cause ventricular fibrillation).

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — 3 Mark Questions

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
**"Define an auto-transformer. Mention any two advantages over a two-winding transformer." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> An auto-transformer is a transformer with a **single continuous winding** that serves as both primary and secondary, with one end of the winding being **common** to both. Voltage transformation is achieved by tapping the winding at an appropriate point.

**Advantages:**
1. **Higher efficiency** (98–99%) because part of the power is transferred conductively.
2. **Lower cost and smaller size** due to reduced copper and core material requirement (saving of $(1 - K) \times 100\%$).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"What is the function of an isolation transformer in biomedical equipment? State the standard leakage current limit as per IEC 60601-1." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> An isolation transformer provides **galvanic isolation** between the AC mains supply and the patient-connected biomedical equipment (ECG, EEG, defibrillator, etc.). It eliminates any direct conductive path, preventing leakage currents that could cause microshock to the patient.

**Standard limit (IEC 60601-1):**
- Normal condition: **Maximum leakage current ≤ 100 μA**
- Single-fault condition: **Maximum leakage current ≤ 500 μA**

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**
> **(a)** Derive the expressions for **power transferred magnetically** and **power transferred conductively** in a step-down auto-transformer, in terms of the total power S and transformation ratio K. Show that the sum equals the total power. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A **10 kVA, 2000 V/200 V** single-phase, 50 Hz transformer is reconnected as an auto-transformer. Determine: **(i)** Transformation ratio, **(ii)** primary and secondary currents, **(iii)** power transferred magnetically and conductively, **(iv)** saving in conductor material as compared to a two-winding transformer. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

For a step-down auto-transformer with transformation ratio $K = V_2/V_1$ (where $K < 1$):

**Step 1:** Apply KCL at the tapping point. The current in the common portion of the winding (between tap and common end):

$$I_{common} = I_2 - I_1$$

**Step 2:** From $K = I_1/I_2$, we have $I_2 = I_1/K$.

$$I_{common} = \dfrac{I_1}{K} - I_1 = I_1 \left( \dfrac{1 - K}{K} \right)$$

**Step 3:** Power transferred **magnetically** (transformed power) is the power associated with the **series winding** (the part not shared between primary and secondary):

$$S_{transformed} = V_1 \times I_{common} = V_1 I_1 \left( \dfrac{1 - K}{K} \right)$$

Since $V_1 I_1 = S$ (total apparent power):

$$\boxed{S_{transformed} = S(1 - K)}$$

**Step 4:** Power transferred **conductively** is the power flowing through the common winding without magnetic transformation:

$$S_{conducted} = V_2 I_2 - S_{transformed} = S - S(1 - K) = SK$$

$$\boxed{S_{conducted} = SK}$$

**Step 5:** Verification:

$$S_{transformed} + S_{conducted} = S(1 - K) + SK = S - SK + SK = S \checkmark$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $S_{two-wdg} = 10 \, \text{kVA}$, $V_1 = 2000 \, \text{V}$, $V_2 = 200 \, \text{V}$, $f = 50 \, \text{Hz}$.

**(i) Transformation Ratio:**

$$K = \dfrac{V_2}{V_1} = \dfrac{200}{2000} = 0.1$$

**(ii) Currents:**

$$I_1 = \dfrac{S}{V_1} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{2000} = 5 \, \text{A}$$

$$I_2 = \dfrac{S}{V_2} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{200} = 50 \, \text{A}$$

**(iii) Power Components:**

$$S_{transformed} = S(1 - K) = 10{,}000 \times (1 - 0.1) = 10{,}000 \times 0.9 = 9{,}000 \, \text{VA} = 9 \, \text{kVA}$$

$$S_{conducted} = SK = 10{,}000 \times 0.1 = 1{,}000 \, \text{VA} = 1 \, \text{kVA}$$

**Verification:** $9 + 1 = 10 \, \text{kVA}$ ✓

**(iv) Saving in Conductor Material:**

$$\text{Saving} = (1 - K) \times 100\% = (1 - 0.1) \times 100\% = \mathbf{90\%}$$

**Conclusion:** Only 9 kVA is transformed magnetically, so the auto-transformer rating is 9 kVA — giving a 90% saving in materials compared to a 10 kVA two-winding transformer.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Past Paper Pattern]**
> **(a)** Explain the **working principle** of an **isolation transformer** with a neat block diagram. Why is it essential in **biomedical engineering applications**? State the standard leakage current limit per **IEC 60601-1**. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A **1:1 isolation transformer** is used to feed a **500 W biomedical equipment** at **230 V, 50 Hz**. The core has a cross-sectional area of **15 cm²** and maximum flux density of **1.1 T**. Calculate the **number of primary and secondary turns** and the **full-load current**. Discuss the safety implications. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Working Principle & Biomedical Significance:**

An **isolation transformer** consists of two **electrically separate** windings (primary N₁ and secondary N₂) wound on a common laminated magnetic core. The windings are **physically and electrically isolated** from each other, with reinforced insulation between them.

**Working Principle:**
1. When AC voltage $V_1$ is applied to the primary, an alternating flux $\phi$ is established in the core.
2. This flux links the secondary winding through the core.
3. By **Faraday's Law**, an EMF is induced in the secondary: $E_2 = 4.44 f N_2 \phi_m$.
4. The load is powered by this induced EMF, but there is **no direct electrical connection** between primary and secondary.
5. Energy transfer is **purely magnetic**.

**Why Essential in Biomedical Engineering:**

> 1. **Patient Safety:** Prevents direct conductive path from mains to patient. A microshock of just **10 μA** through the heart can cause ventricular fibrillation.
> 2. **Leakage Current Limitation:** Keeps leakage current below safe limits.
> 3. **Ground Loop Elimination:** Essential for low-amplitude signal measurements (ECG: ~1 mV, EEG: ~50 μV).
> 4. **Common-Mode Noise Rejection:** Magnetic isolation rejects noise present on both lines.
> 5. **Required for:** ECG, EEG, EMG, defibrillators, electrosurgical units, dialysis machines, ICU/OT power systems.

**IEC 60601-1 Limits:**
- Normal condition: $I_{leak} \leq \mathbf{100 \, \mu A}$
- Single-fault condition: $I_{leak} \leq \mathbf{500 \, \mu A}$

**Block Diagram:**

```
[230 V Mains] → [Primary N₁] ⇐ MAGNETIC CORE ⇒ [Secondary N₂] → [Biomedical Load]
                  │                                                  │
                  └──── NO DIRECT WIRE CONNECTION ─────────────────┘
                       (Galvanic Isolation Barrier)
```

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** 1:1 isolation transformer, $V_1 = V_2 = 230 \, \text{V}$, $f = 50 \, \text{Hz}$, $P = 500 \, \text{W}$, $A = 15 \, \text{cm}^2 = 15 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^2 = 0.0015 \, \text{m}^2$, $B_m = 1.1 \, \text{T}$, $\cos\phi = 1$ (assumed).

**Step 1 — Maximum Flux:**

$$\phi_m = B_m \times A = 1.1 \times 0.0015 = 0.00165 \, \text{Wb} = 1.65 \, \text{mWb}$$

**Step 2 — Number of Turns (using EMF equation):**

$$V_1 = 4.44 \, f \, N_1 \, \phi_m$$

$$N_1 = \dfrac{V_1}{4.44 \, f \, \phi_m} = \dfrac{230}{4.44 \times 50 \times 0.00165}$$

Computing denominator: $4.44 \times 50 = 222$; $222 \times 0.00165 = 0.3663$.

$$N_1 = \dfrac{230}{0.3663} = 627.9 \approx \mathbf{628 \, turns}$$

For 1:1 ratio: $N_2 = N_1 = \mathbf{628 \, turns}$.

**Step 3 — Full-Load Current:**

$$I_1 = I_2 = \dfrac{P}{V \cos\phi} = \dfrac{500}{230 \times 1} = \mathbf{2.174 \, A}$$

**Step 4 — Safety Discussion:**

Since this transformer powers a biomedical device, safety considerations are paramount:
- The transformer must provide isolation of at least **4000 V AC** between primary and secondary for 1 minute (dielectric withstand test).
- Leakage current must remain below **100 μA** (per IEC 60601-1).
- The equipment ground must be connected to a hospital-grade isolated power system ground.
- A **Line Isolation Monitor (LIM)** must be installed to detect first fault and alert staff.
- The transformer should be **double-insulated** and have a low inter-winding capacitance to minimize capacitive coupling of high-frequency noise.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MISTAKES — WHERE STUDENTS LOSE MARKS:**
>
> **1. Sign Confusion with K:** Students often write $K > 1$ for step-down and $K < 1$ for step-up. **Correct:** Step-DOWN → $K < 1$ (V₂ < V₁); Step-UP → $K > 1$ (V₂ > V₁).
>
> **2. Mixing Power Formulas:** The formula $S_{transformed} = S(1 - 1/K)$ applies to **step-UP** configuration. For **step-DOWN**, use $S_{transformed} = S(1 - K)$. Using the wrong one gives negative values — a guaranteed mark-deduction.
>
> **3. Forgetting to Subtract Currents:** In an auto-transformer, the common winding current is $I_2 - I_1$ (difference, not sum). This is opposite to a two-winding transformer.
>
> **4. S.I. Unit Mix-up:** EMF equation requires $\phi_m$ in **Weber (Wb)**, $A$ in **m²**, $f$ in **Hz**. Using cgs units without conversion loses 1–2 marks.
>
> **5. Confusing Power Ratings:** The "rating" of an auto-transformer (KVA) is **higher** than the two-winding transformer rating used to build it. The relationship is $S_{auto} = S_{two-wdg}/(1 - 1/K) = S_{two-wdg} \times K$ (for step-up). Students often quote the original two-winding rating as the auto-transformer rating, which is wrong.
>
> **6. Missing the "Why" in Descriptive Questions:** When asked "why isolation transformer in biomedical," students often just say "for safety." You must elaborate: **microshock hazards, leakage current, IEC 60601-1, ground loops, common-mode noise.**
>
> **7. EMF Equation Constant:** Always use **4.44** (with two decimal places), not 4.4 or 4.448. The standard KTU expected value is 4.44.
>
> **8. Step-Up vs Step-Down Common Current Sign:** For step-up, common current = $I_1 - I_2$ (not $I_2 - I_1$). Always check direction using KCL at the tap.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST:**
>
> **▸ Auto-Transformer Core Facts:**
> - Has **one continuous winding**; primary and secondary share a common portion
> - **NO** electrical isolation between primary and secondary
> - Step-DOWN: $K < 1$; Step-UP: $K > 1$
> - Energy transferred both **conductively** (common winding) and **magnetically** (core)
> - **Higher efficiency** (98–99%) than two-winding
> - **Material saving** = $(1 - K) \times 100\%$
> - $S_{transformed} = S(1 - K)$ for step-DOWN; $S(1 - 1/K)$ for step-UP
> - $S_{conducted} = SK$ (step-DOWN) or $S/K$ (step-UP)
> - Common winding current = $I_2 - I_1$ (step-DOWN) or $I_1 - I_2$ (step-UP)
> - **NEVER use directly on patient circuits** — no isolation!
>
> **▸ Isolation Transformer Core Facts:**
> - **Two separate windings** on common core; electrically isolated
> - Used for **patient safety** in biomedical equipment
> - Provides **galvanic isolation** — energy transfer purely magnetic
> - Standard limit: **Leakage current ≤ 100 μA** (IEC 60601-1, normal condition)
> - Withstand voltage: **4000 V AC** for 1 minute
> - Critical for ECG, EEG, EMG, defibrillators, electrosurgical units
> - Eliminates ground loops and rejects common-mode noise
> - Hospital Isolated Power Systems (IPS) use isolation transformers
> - Combined with **Line Isolation Monitor (LIM)** for continuous safety
>
> **▸ Key Formulas (must memorize):**
> - $K = V_2/V_1 = N_2/N_1 = I_1/I_2$ (auto-transformer)
> - $E = 4.44 f N \phi_m$ (EMF equation)
> - $S_{transformed} = S(1 - K)$ (auto-transformer, step-down)
> - $S_{conducted} = SK$ (auto-transformer, step-down)
> - Material saving = $(1 - K) \times 100\%$
> - $V_1/V_2 = N_1/N_2 = a$ (isolation transformer)
> - $I_{leak,max} = 100 \, \mu A$ (medical standard)
>
> **▸ Common Exam Traps:**
> - Don't confuse step-up and step-down formulas
> - Always state $K < 1$ for step-DOWN (NOT greater)
> - Common winding current uses **difference** of currents (not sum)
> - EMF equation constant is **4.44**, not 4.4
> - Isolation transformer rating equals load rating (1:1 or scaled)
> - Auto-transformer rating > two-winding rating used to build it

---

**END OF NOTES — Module 2: Auto Transformer and Isolation Transformer**

*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme, B.Tech Biomedical Engineering, PEEBT411 — Electrical Technology for Biomedical Engineers.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Switched-Mode Power Supplies (SMPS): Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT411 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY FOR BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Switched-Mode Power Supplies (SMPS): Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Switched-Mode Power Supply (SMPS)** is an electronic power converter that employs a **semiconductor switching device** (transistor/MOSFET/IGBT) operating in either the fully-ON (saturation) or fully-OFF (cut-off) state to transfer energy from a DC or AC source to a load, achieving voltage regulation through **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** of the duty cycle. Because the switching device dissipates negligible power (since either voltage across it or current through it is near zero at any instant), SMPS units routinely achieve efficiencies in the range of **70% to 95%**, vastly outperforming conventional linear regulators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The defining principle of an SMPS is that **energy is transferred in discrete packets** through an inductive storage element, not dissipated as heat. The switching frequency typically lies in the **20 kHz to 1 MHz** range — high enough to keep magnetic components small and lightweight (critical for portable biomedical instruments).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pulse-Pour" Water Bucket

Imagine you need to fill a small water tank (the **load**) to a precise level using a high-pressure water pipe (the **mains/AC source**).

| Linear Regulator Approach | SMPS Approach |
|---|---|
| You attach a **partially-closed valve** (a continuously varying resistance) and let water trickle in continuously. The friction generates heat, and a lot of water is wasted. | You attach a **solenoid valve** that you open for a short burst, then close. You repeat this rapidly. The bucket fills efficiently because the valve is either **fully open (no friction)** or **fully closed (no flow)**. You control the fill level by adjusting **how long** the valve stays open (the **duty cycle**). |

This is the heart of SMPS: **regulate by timing, not by throttling**.

## 1.3 Why SMPS is Critical in Biomedical Engineering

- **Patient Safety Isolation:** Medical SMPS often incorporate reinforced galvanic isolation between mains and patient-applied parts (per IEC 60601-1).
- **Compactness:** Enables portable devices like defibrillators, infusion pumps, patient monitors, and ECG machines to be lightweight.
- **Low Heat Dissipation:** Reduced thermal footprint is vital in sterile OR environments.
- **High Efficiency:** Extends battery life in ambulance/field equipment and implant-supporting devices.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric — Efficiency (η):** SMPS efficiency is mathematically defined as
> $$\eta = \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} \times 100\%$$
> where a linear regulator typically yields η ≈ **30%–50%**, whereas a well-designed SMPS reaches η ≈ **85%–95%**.

## 1.4 Block-Level Concept of an SMPS

A complete SMPS consists of:
1. **Input Rectifier & Filter** — converts AC mains to unregulated DC.
2. **High-Frequency Switch + Driver** — chops the DC at 20 kHz–1 MHz.
3. **High-Frequency Transformer (in isolated types)** — provides voltage step-down/up and galvanic isolation.
4. **Output Rectifier & Filter (LC)** — smooths the chopped waveform.
5. **Feedback & Control Circuit (PWM Controller IC)** — senses output and modulates duty cycle.
