The switching device is **never** operated in the linear (active) region — this is the fundamental distinction that makes SMPS highly efficient.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — The Switching Action

The semiconductor switch is driven by a **PWM waveform** of period $T_s$ and frequency $f_s = 1/T_s$. The **duty cycle** is defined as:

$$D = \frac{t_{\text{on}}}{T_s}$$

The average value of the chopped DC voltage at the switch output is:

$$V_{\text{avg}} = D \cdot V_{\text{in}}$$

By varying $D$ (typically between 0.1 and 0.9), the controller regulates the output. The error amplifier compares the sensed output voltage with an internal reference and adjusts $D$ accordingly — this is **closed-loop voltage-mode control**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Operating Regions of the Inductor Current:**
> - **CCM (Continuous Conduction Mode):** Inductor current never falls to zero. Smooth operation, easier to analyze.
> - **DCM (Discontinuous Conduction Mode):** Inductor current falls to zero for a portion of the cycle. Occurs at light loads.

## 2.2 Basic Non-Isolated SMPS Topologies

### (A) Buck Converter (Step-Down)
The switch is in series with the source; the diode provides a freewheeling path. The inductor and capacitor form an LC low-pass filter. Output is **always less than** input.

### (B) Boost Converter (Step-Up)
The switch is connected in parallel with the load (across the inductor). When the switch is ON, energy is stored in the inductor. When the switch turns OFF, the inductor releases energy *in series* with the input source, **adding** to it. Output is **always greater than** input.

### (C) Buck-Boost Converter
Combines both actions. Output can be either higher or lower than input, and is **inverted (negative)** with respect to the input.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Critical for Exams)

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize the three core conversion ratios below. They appear in nearly every KTU ESE question on SMPS.**

| Topology | Voltage Conversion Ratio $M(D) = V_o/V_{in}$ | Inductor Design Equation | Output Voltage Ripple (peak-to-peak) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Buck** | $M = D$ | $L_{\min} = \dfrac{(1-D) R}{2 f_s}$ | $\dfrac{\Delta V_o}{V_o} = \dfrac{1-D}{8 L C f_s^2}$ |
| **Boost** | $M = \dfrac{1}{1-D}$ | $L_{\min} = \dfrac{D(1-D)^2 R}{2 f_s}$ | $\dfrac{\Delta V_o}{V_o} = \dfrac{D}{R C f_s}$ |
| **Buck-Boost** | $M = -\dfrac{D}{1-D}$ | $L_{\min} = \dfrac{(1-D)^2 R}{2 f_s}$ | $\dfrac{\Delta V_o}{V_o} = \dfrac{D}{R C f_s}$ |

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $D$ | Duty Cycle (0 < D < 1) | dimensionless |
| $f_s$ | Switching Frequency | Hz (kHz–MHz) |
| $L$ | Inductance | Henry (H) |
| $C$ | Filter Capacitance | Farad (F) |
| $R$ | Load Resistance | Ohm (Ω) |
| $\Delta I_L$ | Inductor Current Ripple | Ampere (A) |
| $\Delta V_o$ | Output Voltage Ripple | Volt (V) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inductor Ripple Current** (a frequently asked parameter):
> $$\Delta I_L = \frac{V_{\text{in}} \cdot D \cdot (1-D)}{L \cdot f_s}$$
> For a Buck converter, the average inductor current equals the average load current: $I_L = I_o$.

## 2.4 Why PWM and Not PFM?

**PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** keeps the frequency fixed and varies $D$ — this gives predictable harmonic spectra, easier filter design, and simpler feedback compensation.

**PFM (Pulse Frequency Modulation)** varies $f_s$ — used in light-load efficiency optimization (e.g., burst-mode in modern biomedical SMPS standby circuits).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Biomedical Imaging (CT, MRI, Ultrasound):** High-voltage SMPS (kV range) for X-ray tubes using Cockcroft-Walton multipliers driven by high-frequency SMPS.
- **Defibrillators:** Capacitor-charging SMPS for energy storage up to 360 J.
- **Patient Monitors:** Isolated 5V/12V/24V rails for digital and analog sections.
- **Implantable Devices:** Ultra-low quiescent-current SMPS for battery longevity.
- **Ventilators & Infusion Pumps:** Reliable, isolated DC rails meeting IEC 60601-1 leakage current limits.

> [!NOTE]
> **EMI Consideration:** The high $di/dt$ and $dv/dt$ during switching generates electromagnetic interference. SMPS therefore require **input EMI filters**, **shielded transformers**, and **snubber circuits** — these are explicit KTU syllabus topics in subsequent modules.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Mandate:** Every algebraic step is explicitly shown. The two derivations below are *high-frequency KTU exam questions* — a student who masters these can confidently attempt any Part B 14-mark question on SMPS principles.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Voltage Conversion Ratio of a Boost Converter

### Assumptions (Standard KTU/Textbook Conventions)
- The converter operates in **steady state**.
- The switch is ideal (zero voltage drop when ON, infinite resistance when OFF).
- The diode is ideal (zero forward drop, infinite reverse blocking).
- The inductor and capacitor are lossless.
- The circuit operates in **Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM)**.
- Output voltage $V_o$ and input current $I_{\text{in}}$ are constant (ripple-free due to large $C$ and $L$).

### Step 1 — Write the Switch ON-State (Interval 1: $0 \le t \le DT_s$)

When the switch S is **closed (ON)**, the diode D is reverse-biased and the output capacitor supplies the load. The inductor is connected directly across the input source.

Applying **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** around the input loop:

$$V_{\text{in}} - V_L = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad V_L = V_{\text{in}}$$

The voltage across the inductor is constant, so the inductor current rises **linearly**. The change in inductor current over the ON-interval is:

$$\Delta I_{L,\text{on}} = \frac{1}{L} \int_0^{DT_s} V_L \, dt = \frac{V_{\text{in}} \cdot D T_s}{L}$$

### Step 2 — Write the Switch OFF-State (Interval 2: $DT_s \le t \le T_s$)

When the switch S is **open (OFF)**, the inductor opposes the change in current and the diode D becomes forward-biased. The inductor now releases its stored energy in series with $V_{\text{in}}$ into the load and the capacitor.

Applying KVL around the loop containing $V_{\text{in}}$, the inductor, and the output:

$$V_{\text{in}} - V_L - V_o = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad V_L = V_{\text{in}} - V_o$$

Since $V_o > V_{\text{in}}$ in a boost converter, $V_L$ is **negative** — meaning the inductor current falls linearly.

The change in inductor current over the OFF-interval is:

$$\Delta I_{L,\text{off}} = \frac{1}{L} \int_{DT_s}^{T_s} (V_{\text{in}} - V_o) \, dt = \frac{(V_{\text{in}} - V_o)(1-D)T_s}{L}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Volt-Second Balance (Inductor Steady-State Condition)

In **steady state**, the net change in inductor current over one complete switching cycle must be zero. This is the fundamental principle for any DC-DC converter in CCM:

$$\Delta I_{L,\text{on}} + \Delta I_{L,\text{off}} = 0$$

Substituting the two expressions derived above:

$$\frac{V_{\text{in}} \cdot D T_s}{L} + \frac{(V_{\text{in}} - V_o)(1-D)T_s}{L} = 0$$

### Step 4 — Simplify Algebraically

Factor out the common term $\frac{T_s}{L}$ (which is non-zero):

$$V_{\text{in}} \cdot D + (V_{\text{in}} - V_o)(1-D) = 0$$

Expand the second term:

$$V_{\text{in}} D + V_{\text{in}}(1-D) - V_o(1-D) = 0$$

The first two terms collapse to $V_{\text{in}}$:

$$V_{\text{in}} D + V_{\text{in}} - V_{\text{in}} D - V_o(1-D) = 0$$

The $V_{\text{in}} D$ terms cancel:

$$V_{\text{in}} - V_o(1-D) = 0$$

### Step 5 — Solve for the Conversion Ratio

Rearrange to isolate $V_o$:

$$V_o(1-D) = V_{\text{in}}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{V_o}{V_{\text{in}}} = \frac{1}{1-D}}$$

This is the celebrated **Boost Converter Voltage Gain Expression**. Observe that as $D \to 1$, the gain $M \to \infty$ in theory (in practice, parasitic resistances limit the gain to about 5–6×).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Minimum Inductance for Continuous Conduction in a Buck Converter

### Step 1 — Boundary Condition Definition

CCM exists as long as the inductor current never drops to zero. The **boundary** between CCM and DCM occurs precisely when the **minimum** inductor current equals zero.

The inductor current waveform is a sawtooth with an average value $I_L$ and a peak-to-peak ripple of $\Delta I_L$. Thus:

$$I_{L,\text{min}} = I_L - \frac{\Delta I_L}{2}$$

For the boundary:

$$I_L = \frac{\Delta I_L}{2}$$

### Step 2 — Express the Average Inductor Current

For an ideal buck converter (lossless), input power equals output power:

$$V_{\text{in}} I_{\text{in}} = V_o I_o$$

From the conversion ratio $V_o = D V_{\text{in}}$:

$$I_{\text{in}} = D I_o$$

The average inductor current in a buck converter equals the input current (since the inductor is in series with the source during the ON-state and carries the full load during freewheeling):

$$I_L = I_o$$

### Step 3 — Express the Inductor Ripple Current

During the ON-state, the voltage across the inductor is $V_L = V_{\text{in}} - V_o = V_{\text{in}}(1-D)$. The ripple is:

$$\Delta I_L = \frac{V_{\text{in}}(1-D) \cdot D T_s}{L} = \frac{V_o(1-D)}{L f_s}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Boundary Condition

Setting $I_L = \Delta I_L / 2$:

$$I_o = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{V_o(1-D)}{L_{\min} f_s}$$

Since $V_o / I_o = R$ (Ohm's law at the load):

$$I_o = \frac{V_o}{R} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{V_o(1-D)}{L_{\min} f_s}$$

Cancel $V_o$ from both sides:

$$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{1-D}{2 L_{\min} f_s}$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $L_{\min}$

$$\boxed{L_{\min} = \frac{(1-D) R}{2 f_s}}$$

This expression tells the designer the **smallest inductor** that still keeps the converter in CCM at the rated load and switching frequency.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Topologies

| Parameter | Buck | Boost | Buck-Boost |
|---|---|---|---|
| Polarity of $V_o$ | Same as $V_{\text{in}}$ | Same as $V_{\text{in}}$ | **Inverted** |
| Magnitude $V_o$ vs $V_{\text{in}}$ | $V_o < V_{\text{in}}$ | $V_o > V_{\text{in}}$ | Either, depending on $D$ |
| Switch position | Series with source | In parallel with load | Series with inductor |
| Diode conduction | During OFF | During OFF | During OFF |
| Stress on switch | $V_{\text{in}}$ | $V_o$ | $V_{\text{in}} + V_o$ |
| Biomedical Example | Step-down for digital logic (5V/3.3V) | Step-up for defibrillator capacitor charging | Battery-powered portable monitors |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Rendering Note:** The following Mermaid block uses strictly alphanumeric node IDs (e.g., `blkA`, `blkB`) and clean uppercase text labels — no markdown bold, italics, or HTML inside the node labels, per the compilation safeguards.

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Complete Isolated SMPS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    blkA["AC MAINS INPUT"] --> blkB["EMI INPUT FILTER"]
    blkB --> blkC["INPUT BRIDGE RECTIFIER"]
    blkC --> blkD["BULK DC LINK CAPACITOR"]
    blkD --> blkE["PWM CONTROLLER IC AND DRIVER"]
    blkE --> blkF["HIGH FREQUENCY SWITCH MOSFET"]
    blkF --> blkG["HIGH FREQUENCY ISOLATION TRANSFORMER"]
    blkG --> blkH["SECONDARY RECTIFIER DIODE"]
    blkH --> blkI["OUTPUT LC FILTER"]
    blkI --> blkJ["REGULATED DC OUTPUT TO LOAD"]
    blkK["OUTPUT VOLTAGE SENSE"] --> blkE
    blkI --> blkK
    blkL["REFERENCE VOLTAGE 2.5V"] --> blkE
    blkM["OPTOCOUPLER ISOLATION"] --> blkE
    blkK --> blkM
```

## 4.2 Operational State Diagram — PWM Control Loop

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> sStart: Power ON
    sStart --> sSense: Controller IC Initialized
    sSense --> sCompare: Read V_out Sample
    sCompare --> sCompute: V_error = V_ref - V_sense
    sCompute --> sPWM: Generate D_new from Error Amplifier
    sPWM --> sDrive: Apply Gate Pulse to MOSFET
    sDrive --> sSense: Next Switching Cycle
    sCompute --> sProtect: If V_error exceeds Limit
    sProtect --> sShutdown: Latch Fault
    sShutdown --> [*]: System OFF
```

## 4.3 Topological Architecture — Three Basic Converters

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph buckA["BUCK CONVERTER"]
        buckIn["V_in"] --> buckSw["SWITCH S"]
        buckSw --> buckNode["NODE X"]
        buckNode --> buckL["INDUCTOR L"]
        buckL --> buckC["CAPACITOR C"]
        buckNode --> buckD["DIODE D"]
        buckD --> buckGnd["GROUND"]
        buckC --> buckOut["V_out"]
    end
    subgraph boostA["BOOST CONVERTER"]
        boostIn["V_in"] --> boostL["INDUCTOR L"]
        boostL --> boostNode["NODE X"]
        boostNode --> boostSw["SWITCH S"]
        boostSw --> boostGnd["GROUND"]
        boostNode --> boostD["DIODE D"]
        boostD --> boostC["CAPACITOR C"]
        boostC --> boostGnd2["GROUND"]
        boostC --> boostOut["V_out"]
    end
    subgraph bbsA["BUCK-BOOST CONVERTER"]
        bbIn["V_in"] --> bbSw["SWITCH S"]
        bbSw --> bbGnd["GROUND"]
        bbIn --> bbL["INDUCTOR L"]
        bbL --> bbNode["NODE X"]
        bbNode --> bbD["DIODE D"]
        bbD --> bbC["CAPACITOR C"]
        bbC --> bbGnd2["GROUND"]
        bbC --> bbOut["V_out inverted"]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The switch S in each converter toggles between ON and OFF states. The inductor $L$ and capacitor $C$ are the **energy-storage and filtering elements** that smooth the chopped waveform. The diode $D$ provides a freewheeling or commutation path. Always identify the inductor current path first when analyzing any SMPS topology — it is the single most important trace to draw during a KTU exam.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Switched-Mode Power Supply (SMPS). List any two advantages of SMPS over linear power supply.**

**Model Answer:**

A Switched-Mode Power Supply (SMPS) is a power electronic converter that regulates the output voltage by varying the **duty cycle** of a high-frequency semiconductor switch (MOSFET/IGBT) operating in the cut-off and saturation regions.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **High Efficiency (70%–95%):** Because the switch is either fully ON (low V, high I → small loss) or fully OFF (low I, high V → small loss), switching losses are minimal, unlike linear regulators which dissipate the excess voltage as heat.
2. **Compact Size & Lightweight:** Operating at high switching frequency (20 kHz–1 MHz) drastically reduces the size of magnetic components (transformer, inductor) and filter capacitors.
3. *(Optional third for full marks)* **Wide Input Voltage Range:** Buck/Boost/Buck-Boost topologies accept a broad AC/DC input.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the meaning of "duty cycle" in an SMPS? State its mathematical relationship with the output voltage of a Buck converter.**

**Model Answer:**

The **duty cycle ($D$)** of a PWM-controlled SMPS is defined as the ratio of the switch ON-time to the total switching period:

$$D = \frac{t_{\text{on}}}{T_s} = t_{\text{on}} \cdot f_s$$

where $T_s$ is the switching period and $f_s$ is the switching frequency.

For an **ideal Buck converter** operating in CCM, the voltage conversion ratio is:

$$\frac{V_o}{V_{\text{in}}} = D \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad V_o = D \cdot V_{\text{in}}$$

Hence, by varying $D$ between 0 and 1, the output voltage can be regulated anywhere between 0 and $V_{\text{in}}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **Note:** Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer ONE question of 14 marks from the choice provided. Each question carries two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a **Boost converter** in Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM). Derive the expression for the output voltage in terms of the input voltage and duty cycle.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Boost converter has an input voltage $V_{\text{in}} = 12\text{ V}$, duty cycle $D = 0.4$, and supplies a load of $R = 50\text{ Ω}$. The switching frequency is $f_s = 25\text{ kHz}$. Determine:
  (i) The output voltage $V_o$.
  (ii) The average output current $I_o$.
  (iii) The average input current $I_{\text{in}}$.
  (iv) The minimum inductance $L_{\min}$ required to maintain CCM.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — Working Principle & Derivation**

**Circuit Description:** A Boost converter consists of an input DC source $V_{\text{in}}$, an inductor $L$ in series with the source, a controlled switch S (MOSFET) connected between the inductor node and ground, a diode D in series with the output, and a filter capacitor $C$ in parallel with the load $R$.

**Mode 1 — Switch ON ($0 \le t \le DT_s$):**
The switch is closed, grounding the node after the inductor. The diode is reverse-biased because the cathode is at the output voltage (higher potential) and the anode is at ground. Current through the inductor rises linearly, and energy $E = \frac{1}{2} L I_L^2$ is stored in the magnetic field. The capacitor $C$ discharges into the load, maintaining $V_o$.

Applying KVL: $V_L = V_{\text{in}}$ → $\Delta I_{L,\text{on}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{in}} D T_s}{L}$

**Mode 2 — Switch OFF ($DT_s \le t \le T_s$):**
The switch opens. The inductor opposes the change in current, driving the node voltage above $V_{\text{in}}$. The diode becomes forward-biased. The inductor releases its stored energy in series with $V_{\text{in}}$ into the load and the capacitor, **adding** to the source voltage.

Applying KVL: $V_L = V_{\text{in}} - V_o$ → $\Delta I_{L,\text{off}} = \dfrac{(V_{\text{in}} - V_o)(1-D)T_s}{L}$

**Steady-State Volt-Second Balance:**
The net change in inductor current over one full cycle is zero:

$$\frac{V_{\text{in}} D T_s}{L} + \frac{(V_{\text{in}} - V_o)(1-D) T_s}{L} = 0$$

Expanding and simplifying:

$$V_{\text{in}} D + V_{\text{in}}(1-D) - V_o(1-D) = 0$$
$$V_{\text{in}} = V_o(1-D)$$

$$\boxed{\frac{V_o}{V_{\text{in}}} = \frac{1}{1-D}}$$

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Problem**

**Given:** $V_{\text{in}} = 12\text{ V}$, $D = 0.4$, $R = 50\text{ Ω}$, $f_s = 25\text{ kHz}$.

**(i) Output Voltage:**

$$V_o = \frac{V_{\text{in}}}{1 - D} = \frac{12}{1 - 0.4} = \frac{12}{0.6} = \boxed{20\text{ V}}$$

**(ii) Average Output Current:**

$$I_o = \frac{V_o}{R} = \frac{20}{50} = \boxed{0.4\text{ A}}$$

**(iii) Average Input Current:**

For an ideal lossless converter, $P_{\text{in}} = P_{\text{out}}$:

$$V_{\text{in}} \cdot I_{\text{in}} = V_o \cdot I_o \quad \Longrightarrow \quad I_{\text{in}} = \frac{V_o \cdot I_o}{V_{\text{in}}} = \frac{20 \times 0.4}{12} = \boxed{0.667\text{ A}}$$

**(iv) Minimum Inductance for CCM:**

$$L_{\min} = \frac{D(1-D)^2 R}{2 f_s} = \frac{0.4 \times (0.6)^2 \times 50}{2 \times 25{,}000}$$

$$L_{\min} = \frac{0.4 \times 0.36 \times 50}{50{,}000} = \frac{7.2}{50{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{L_{\min} = 144\text{ μH}}$$

To ensure CCM in practice, the designer should select a standard inductor value ≥ **180 μH** or **220 μH** with appropriate current rating.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the **Buck, Boost, and Buck-Boost converters** in terms of (i) output voltage polarity, (ii) voltage conversion ratio, (iii) location of the switch, and (iv) one typical biomedical application of each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **Buck converter** is used in a portable patient monitor to step down a 24 V battery to 5 V for the digital logic section. The load draws 2 A. The switching frequency is 50 kHz.
  (i) Determine the duty cycle $D$.
  (ii) Calculate the minimum inductance $L_{\min}$ to ensure CCM operation.
  (iii) If the inductor current ripple $\Delta I_L$ is 20% of the average inductor current, find the value of $L$.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — Comparative Study**

| Criterion | Buck | Boost | Buck-Boost |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Output Polarity** | Same as $V_{\text{in}}$ (positive) | Same as $V_{\text{in}}$ (positive) | **Inverted** (negative) |
| **(ii) Conversion Ratio** | $\dfrac{V_o}{V_{\text{in}}} = D$ | $\dfrac{V_o}{V_{\text{in}}} = \dfrac{1}{1-D}$ | $\dfrac{V_o}{V_{\text{in}}} = -\dfrac{D}{1-D}$ |
| **(iii) Switch Location** | In series between $V_{\text{in}}$ and the LC filter | In parallel with the load (after the inductor) | In series with the source (in shunt-to-ground) |
| **(iv) Biomedical Application** | 5V/3.3V rails in patient monitors | High-voltage rail for defibrillator capacitor charging | Battery-powered portable ECG/infusion pump where input can be above or below output |

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Problem**

**Given:** Buck converter with $V_{\text{in}} = 24\text{ V}$, $V_o = 5\text{ V}$, $I_o = 2\text{ A}$, $f_s = 50\text{ kHz}$.

**Load resistance:**

$$R = \frac{V_o}{I_o} = \frac{5}{2} = 2.5\text{ Ω}$$

**(i) Duty Cycle:**

$$D = \frac{V_o}{V_{\text{in}}} = \frac{5}{24} = \boxed{0.2083 \text{ (or } 20.83\%)}$$

**(ii) Minimum Inductance for CCM:**

$$L_{\min} = \frac{(1-D) R}{2 f_s} = \frac{(1 - 0.2083) \times 2.5}{2 \times 50{,}000} = \frac{0.7917 \times 2.5}{100{,}000}$$

$$L_{\min} = \frac{1.9792}{100{,}000} = \boxed{19.79\text{ μH}}$$

**(iii) Required L for 20% Current Ripple:**

For a Buck converter, $I_L = I_o = 2\text{ A}$.

$$\Delta I_L = 0.20 \times 2 = 0.4\text{ A}$$

Using the ripple formula for a Buck converter:

$$\Delta I_L = \frac{V_{\text{in}}(1-D) D}{L f_s} = \frac{V_o(1-D)}{L f_s}$$

Solving for $L$:

$$L = \frac{V_o(1-D)}{f_s \cdot \Delta I_L} = \frac{5 \times 0.7917}{50{,}000 \times 0.4} = \frac{3.9583}{20{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{L = 197.9\text{ μH}}$$

This value is roughly **10× the minimum** required, ensuring the converter operates well within CCM and produces low output voltage ripple.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Points:**
> 1. **Forgetting volt-second balance derivation steps** (3–4 marks lost). Examiners reward *every* algebraic step — never jump from KVL to the final ratio.
> 2. **Mixing up polarity in Buck-Boost** — the negative sign in $V_o/V_{\text{in}} = -D/(1-D)$ is **mandatory**; omitting it costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Using wrong $L_{\min}$ formula** for the wrong topology — the formula **differs** for Buck, Boost, and Buck-Boost. Memorize the *family* of equations from the table in Section 2.3.
> 4. **Not specifying CCM assumption** — always explicitly state "assuming Continuous Conduction Mode and ideal components" at the start of derivations.
> 5. **Unit conversion errors** in numerical problems — switch frequency is often given in **kHz** but the formula requires **Hz**. Convert first, calculate next.
> 6. **Forgetting the negative sign** when computing output voltage polarity in Buck-Boost converters.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** SMPS regulates by *switching* (ON/OFF) — not by *throttling* (linear dissipation).
- **Efficiency:** SMPS = **70%–95%**; Linear = **30%–50%**.
- **Switching Frequency:** Typically **20 kHz – 1 MHz** (above audible range to avoid noise — important for biomedical use).
- **Three Core Topologies:**
  - **Buck:** $V_o = D \cdot V_{\text{in}}$ (step-down, same polarity).
  - **Boost:** $V_o = V_{\text{in}} / (1-D)$ (step-up, same polarity).
  - **Buck-Boost:** $V_o = -D \cdot V_{\text{in}} / (1-D)$ (inverted output).
- **Duty Cycle:** $D = t_{\text{on}} / T_s$, with $0 < D < 1$.
- **CCM Condition:** $L \ge L_{\min}$, where $L_{\min}$ depends on topology, $D$, $R$, and $f_s$.
- **Volt-Second Balance:** Inductor current ripple over one cycle = 0 in steady state — this is the **master equation** for all SMPS derivations.
- **Energy Storage:** Inductor stores energy when switch is ON; capacitor smooths output.
- **Biomedical Relevance:** SMPS used in defibrillators, patient monitors, MRI, ultrasound, infusion pumps, ventilators.
- **Isolation:** Medical SMPS use high-frequency transformers + optocouplers for **galvanic isolation** (IEC 60601-1).
- **EMI Mitigation:** Input EMI filters, snubbers, shielded magnetics, proper PCB layout.
- **PWM Controller IC Examples:** UC3842, TL494, SG2525 (current-mode / voltage-mode control).
- **Control Strategies:** Voltage-Mode Control (VMC) vs. Current-Mode Control (CMC) — CMC offers better stability and current limiting for medical safety.
- **Power Loss Mechanisms:** Switching losses (proportional to $f_s$), conduction losses ($I^2 R$ in switches and magnetics), core losses (hysteresis + eddy current).
- **Inductor Design Rule of Thumb:** $L \ge 1.5 \times L_{\min}$ for robust CCM operation.
- **Output Ripple Rule of Thumb:** Choose $C$ such that $\Delta V_o / V_o \le 1\%$ for sensitive analog biomedical front-ends.
- **Exam Tip:** Always draw the two current paths (switch ON and switch OFF) **separately** when explaining any SMPS topology — this is a 2-mark certainty.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types (Buck, Boost, Buck-Boost)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT411 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY FOR BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types (Buck, Boost, Buck-Boost)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are DC-DC Converters?

A **DC-DC Converter** is an electronic circuit that converts a source of direct current (DC) from one voltage level to another. They are essential **switch-mode power supplies (SMPS)** that use semiconductor switching devices (typically MOSFETs or BJTs) along with energy storage elements (inductors and capacitors) to regulate voltage.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, the three fundamental non-isolated DC-DC converter topologies are studied:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A DC-DC converter is a power electronic system that uses switching action to transfer energy from input to output, providing regulated DC voltage. The three basic types — **Buck, Boost, and Buck-Boost** — differ in the relationship between input and output voltage magnitudes and polarity.

---

### 1.2 Classification Overview

| Converter Type | Function | Output Voltage | Typical Biomedical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Buck Converter** | Step-Down | $V_{out} < V_{in}$ | Battery management in pacemakers, hearing aids |
| **Boost Converter** | Step-Up | $V_{out} > V_{in}$ | LED drivers in pulse oximeters, sensor biasing |
| **Buck-Boost Converter** | Step-Up or Step-Down | $V_{out} \gtrless V_{in}$ (inverted) | Portable defibrillators, variable voltage supplies |

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tap" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:** Imagine a water tank (input) connected to a delivery pipe (output). A **Buck converter** is like a *pressure-reducing valve* — it lowers the water pressure to a usable level. A **Boost converter** is like a *pressure pump* — it actively increases the pressure. A **Buck-Boost converter** is a *smart valve with a pump* — it can either reduce or boost pressure depending on demand. The "switch" in each converter is the tap that opens/closes rapidly, while the "inductor" stores and releases energy like a flywheel.

### 1.4 Key Terminology

- **Duty Cycle (D):** The fraction of the switching period $T_s$ for which the switch is ON.
$$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_s}, \quad 0 < D < 1$$

- **Switching Frequency ($f_s$):** The rate at which the switch toggles, typically **20 kHz to 1 MHz** in modern biomedical devices.
$$f_s = \frac{1}{T_s}$$

- **Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM):** The inductor current never falls to zero during a switching cycle.
- **Discontinuous Conduction Mode (DCM):** The inductor current falls to zero for some portion of the cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** All three converter analyses in Module 2 assume **Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM) and steady-state operation** unless specified otherwise.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — The Two-Stage Cycle

All three converters operate on a **two-state switching principle**:

**State 1 (Switch ON, Duration $DT_s$):**
- Semiconductor switch is closed (conducting).
- Inductor is connected to the source and **stores energy** (current ramps up linearly).
- Diode is reverse-biased and OFF.

**State 2 (Switch OFF, Duration $(1-D)T_s$):**
- Semiconductor switch is open.
- Inductor **releases stored energy** to the load (current ramps down).
- Diode is forward-biased and provides a freewheeling current path.

The output voltage is **regulated by adjusting the duty cycle D** through a feedback control loop (typically using PWM — Pulse Width Modulation).

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations** — they account for ~70% of marks in converter problems.

| # | Converter | Output Voltage $V_{out}$ | Voltage Gain $M(D)$ | Inductor Voltage Ripple $\Delta I_L$ | Critical Inductance $L_c$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Buck** | $D \cdot V_{in}$ | $D$ | $\dfrac{V_{out}(1-D)}{f_s L}$ | $\dfrac{(1-D)R}{2f_s}$ |
| 2 | **Boost** | $\dfrac{V_{in}}{1-D}$ | $\dfrac{1}{1-D}$ | $\dfrac{V_{in} D}{f_s L (1-D)}$ | $\dfrac{D(1-D)^2 R}{2f_s}$ |
| 3 | **Buck-Boost** | $-\dfrac{D \cdot V_{in}}{1-D}$ | $-\dfrac{D}{1-D}$ | $\dfrac{V_{in} D}{f_s L}$ | $\dfrac{(1-D)^2 R}{2f_s}$ |

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Biomedical Application Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| Input Voltage | $V_{in}$ | Volts (V) | Battery voltage (e.g., 3.7 V Li-ion) |
| Output Voltage | $V_{out}$ | Volts (V) | Circuit supply (e.g., 1.8 V, 5 V, 12 V) |
| Load Resistance | $R$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) | Equivalent resistance of biomedical load |
| Switching Frequency | $f_s$ | Hertz (Hz) | Typically 100 kHz – 1 MHz |
| Inductance | $L$ | Henry (H) | Energy storage element |
| Capacitance | $C$ | Farad (F) | Output ripple filter |
| Duty Cycle | $D$ | Dimensionless | 0 to 1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Negative Sign in Buck-Boost:** The negative sign in the Buck-Boost output equation indicates **output voltage polarity inversion** relative to input. This is a defining characteristic and frequently tested.

---

### 2.3 Boundary Conditions for CCM

For a converter to operate in Continuous Conduction Mode, the inductance $L$ must exceed the **critical inductance** $L_c$:

$$L \geq L_c$$

- If $L < L_c$ → converter enters **DCM** (more complex analysis required).
- If $L \geq L_c$ → converter remains in **CCM** (formulas above apply).

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In biomedical engineering, these converters are critical for:

1. **Implantable devices** (pacemakers, cochlear implants): Buck converters step down the 3.7 V Li-ion battery to power low-voltage digital ICs (1.8 V).
2. **Portable diagnostic equipment**: Boost converters raise battery voltage to drive high-brightness LEDs in pulse oximeters and otoscopes.
3. **Defibrillators and ultrasound**: Buck-Boost converters provide flexible voltage rails regardless of battery discharge state.
4. **Battery life extension**: High switching frequency (100 kHz+) enables small magnetic components → miniaturized, lightweight devices.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why SMPS over Linear Regulators?** SMPS converters achieve efficiencies of **80–95%**, while linear regulators waste energy as heat (often <50% efficiency). For battery-powered biomedical wearables, this efficiency is non-negotiable.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Buck Converter Output Voltage

**Circuit Setup:** Input voltage $V_{in}$ on left, switch in series, then inductor $L$ in series, capacitor $C$ in parallel with load $R$, diode across switch and inductor node to ground.

**Assumptions:**
- Switch and diode are ideal (zero voltage drop when ON).
- Inductor and capacitor are lossless.
- Steady-state CCM operation.

**Step 1: Analyze the OFF state (Switch open, $0 < t < (1-D)T_s$)**

The inductor current must continue to flow (it cannot change instantaneously). The diode becomes forward-biased and clamps the switch side of the inductor to ground.

Applying KVL around the loop containing the inductor, capacitor, and load:

$$V_L = L \frac{di_L}{dt} = -V_{out}$$

This is the voltage across the inductor during OFF state.

**Step 2: Analyze the ON state (Switch closed, $0 < t < DT_s$)**

When the switch closes, the diode becomes reverse-biased. The input voltage is applied across the inductor and load combination:

$$V_L = L \frac{di_L}{dt} = V_{in} - V_{out}$$

**Step 3: Apply the Volt-Second Balance Principle**

In steady-state, the **average voltage across the inductor over one complete switching cycle must be zero** (otherwise the inductor current would continuously rise and saturate).

$$\int_{0}^{T_s} V_L \, dt = 0$$

Breaking this into the two intervals:

$$\int_{0}^{DT_s} (V_{in} - V_{out}) \, dt + \int_{DT_s}^{T_s} (-V_{out}) \, dt = 0$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the integrals**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot DT_s + (-V_{out}) \cdot (1-D)T_s &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Divide through by $T_s$ and simplify**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot D - V_{out} \cdot (1-D) &= 0 \\
D \cdot V_{in} - D \cdot V_{out} - V_{out} + D \cdot V_{out} &= 0 \\
D \cdot V_{in} - V_{out} &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Final Result**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= D \cdot V_{in} \\
M(D) &= D
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification:** For $D = 0.5$, $V_{out} = 0.5 \cdot V_{in}$ — exactly half. For $D = 1$, $V_{out} = V_{in}$ (pass-through). For $D = 0$, $V_{out} = 0$ (fully off). The equation is dimensionally and physically consistent.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Boost Converter Output Voltage

**Circuit Topology:** Input source, then inductor $L$ in series, switch (MOSFET) to ground, diode in series, then output capacitor $C$ and load $R$ in parallel.

**Step 1: ON state analysis ($0 < t < DT_s$)**

When the switch is closed, the input source is shorted across the inductor (diode is reverse-biased because the anode is at ground potential and cathode is at $V_{out}$):

$$V_L = L \frac{di_L}{dt} = V_{in}$$

The inductor current ramps up, storing energy.

**Step 2: OFF state analysis ($DT_s < t < T_s$)**

When the switch opens, the inductor opposes the change in current and reverses its polarity. The diode becomes forward-biased. The inductor is now in series with the input source, and both feed the output:

$$V_L = L \frac{di_L}{dt} = V_{in} - V_{out}$$

Since $V_{in} < V_{out}$ in a Boost converter, $V_L$ is negative — the inductor current ramps down.

**Step 3: Apply Volt-Second Balance**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{DT_s} V_{in} \, dt + \int_{DT_s}^{T_s} (V_{in} - V_{out}) \, dt &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the integrals**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{in} \cdot DT_s + (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot (1-D)T_s &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Divide by $T_s$ and expand**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{in} \cdot D + (V_{in} - V_{out})(1-D) &= 0 \\
D \cdot V_{in} + V_{in} - D \cdot V_{in} - V_{out} + D \cdot V_{out} &= 0 \\
V_{in} - V_{out} + D \cdot V_{out} &= 0 \\
V_{in} &= V_{out}(1-D)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Final Result**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= \frac{V_{in}}{1-D} \\
M(D) &= \frac{1}{1-D}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** As $D \to 1$, $V_{out} \to \infty$ (theoretically). Practically, $D$ is limited to ~0.8 to avoid inductor saturation. For $D = 0.5$, $V_{out} = 2V_{in}$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Buck-Boost Converter Output Voltage

**Circuit Topology:** Input source, then inductor $L$ in series, switch to ground (at the junction of inductor and diode), diode in series pointing toward output, output capacitor and load in parallel. **The output is referenced to the same ground as the input, but the output voltage polarity is inverted** with respect to input.

**Step 1: ON state analysis ($0 < t < DT_s$)**

When the switch is closed, the diode is reverse-biased. The input source is applied across the inductor:

$$V_L = L \frac{di_L}{dt} = V_{in}$$

**Step 2: OFF state analysis ($DT_s < t < T_s$)**

When the switch opens, the inductor current continues and forward-biases the diode. The inductor is now in series with the load (the input is disconnected because the switch is open). The voltage across the inductor is:

$$V_L = L \frac{di_L}{dt} = -V_{out}$$

(negative because the inductor voltage opposes the output voltage, and the output is inverted)

**Step 3: Apply Volt-Second Balance**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{DT_s} V_{in} \, dt + \int_{DT_s}^{T_s} (-V_{out}) \, dt &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the integrals**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{in} \cdot DT_s - V_{out} \cdot (1-D)T_s &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Divide by $T_s$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{in} \cdot D &= V_{out}(1-D)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Final Result**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= -\frac{D \cdot V_{in}}{1-D} \\
M(D) &= -\frac{D}{1-D}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The negative sign is non-negotiable in KTU answers.** The Buck-Boost is also called an **inverting converter** because the output voltage is opposite in polarity to the input. If asked for the magnitude, use $\vert V_{out} \vert = \dfrac{D \cdot V_{in}}{1-D}$.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Problem — Sample Solution (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A Buck converter has $V_{in} = 24$ V, $D = 0.4$, $L = 100$ $\mu$H, $f_s = 50$ kHz, and $R = 10$ $\Omega$. Find $V_{out}$, $\Delta I_L$, and verify CCM operation.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Output voltage
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= D \cdot V_{in} = 0.4 \times 24 = 9.6 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Load current (average)
$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{out} &= \frac{V_{out}}{R} = \frac{9.6}{10} = 0.96 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Inductor current ripple
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta I_L &= \frac{V_{out}(1-D)}{f_s L} = \frac{9.6 \times (1-0.4)}{50{,}000 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= \frac{9.6 \times 0.6}{5} = \frac{5.76}{5} = 1.152 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Critical inductance
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_c &= \frac{(1-D)R}{2 f_s} = \frac{0.6 \times 10}{2 \times 50{,}000} = \frac{6}{100{,}000} = 60 \text{ \mu H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** CCM Verification
Since $L = 100$ $\mu$H $> L_c = 60$ $\mu$H, the converter operates in **CCM** ✓.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Buck Converter — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vin DC Source] --> B[Semiconductor Switch MOSFET]
    B --> C[Inductor L]
    C --> D[Output Node]
    D --> E[Capacitor C Parallel]
    D --> F[Load R]
    D --> G[Diode Freewheeling to GND]
    B -.controls.-> H[PWM Controller Feedback]
    H --> B
    D -.feedback.-> H
```

**Operational States:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start of Cycle] --> B{Switch Position}
    B -->|ON State| C[Inductor Charges from Vin]
    C --> C1[Diode Reverse Biased]
    C1 --> D[Current Ramps Up]
    D --> E[Time DT reached]
    B -->|OFF State| F[Inductor Discharges to Load]
    F --> F1[Diode Forward Biased]
    F1 --> G[Current Ramps Down]
    G --> E
    E --> H{Cycle Complete}
    H -->|Yes| I[Steady State Output]
```

### 4.2 Boost Converter — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vin DC Source] --> B[Inductor L]
    B --> C[Switch Node]
    C --> D[Semiconductor Switch to GND]
    C --> E[Diode Forward to Output]
    E --> F[Output Node]
    F --> G[Capacitor C Filter]
    F --> H[Load R]
    F -.feedback.-> I[PWM Controller]
    I -.controls.-> D
```

**State Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Boost Cycle Start] --> B{Switch Position}
    B -->|CLOSED| C[Inductor Stores Energy from Vin]
    C --> C1[Diode Reverse Biased]
    C1 --> C2[Output Capacitor Powers Load]
    C2 --> D[End of ON time DT]
    B -->|OPEN| E[Inductor Releases Energy]
    E --> E1[Diode Forward Biased]
    E1 --> E2[Vin plus Inductor charges Output]
    E2 --> D
    D --> F[Regulated Vout greater than Vin]
```

### 4.3 Buck-Boost Converter — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vin DC Source] --> B[Inductor L]
    B --> C[Switch Node X]
    C --> D[Switch to GND]
    C --> E[Diode to Output Node Y]
    E --> F[Output Node Y]
    F --> G[Capacitor Filter]
    F --> H[Load R]
    F -.feedback.-> I[PWM Controller]
    I -.controls.-> D
    F --> J[Output is Negative Polarity]
```

### 4.4 Comparative Topology Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DC-DC Converter Family] --> B[Non-Isolated Types]
    A --> C[Isolated Types - not in Module 2]
    B --> D[Buck Step Down]
    B --> E[Boost Step Up]
    B --> F[Buck-Boost Inverting]
    D --> D1[Vout equals D times Vin]
    D --> D2[Vo less than Vi]
    E --> E1[Vout equals Vin over 1 minus D]
    E --> E2[Vo greater than Vi]
    F --> F1[Vout equals minus D Vin over 1 minus D]
    F --> F2[Vo inverted polarity]
```

### 4.5 Key Waveform Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Switching Signal PWM] -->|Triggers| B[Inductor Current Ramp Up]
    A -->|Triggers Off| C[Inductor Current Ramp Down]
    B --> D[Energy Stored in L]
    C --> E[Energy Released to Load]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Output Capacitor Smooths]
    F --> G[Regulated DC Output Vout]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a Buck converter. Derive its output voltage in terms of input voltage and duty cycle.**

**Model Answer:**

A Buck converter is a step-down DC-DC switching converter whose output voltage is less than its input voltage. It uses a controlled switch (MOSFET), a freewheeling diode, an inductor, and a filter capacitor.

Applying volt-second balance across the inductor (average inductor voltage in steady state = 0):

$$
\begin{aligned}
(V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot D \cdot T_s + (-V_{out}) \cdot (1-D) \cdot T_s &= 0 \\
V_{out} &= D \cdot V_{in}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $0 < D < 1$, $V_{out} < V_{in}$, confirming step-down operation.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Why is the output of a Buck-Boost converter inverted? State its voltage gain expression.**

**Model Answer:**

In a Buck-Boost converter, when the switch is ON, the inductor is connected across the input source, storing energy. The diode is reverse-biased, isolating the output. When the switch turns OFF, the inductor voltage reverses polarity to maintain current continuity. The freewheeling diode is now forward-biased, and the inductor delivers its stored energy to the output with **reversed polarity** relative to the input ground reference.

Voltage gain expression:
$$
\begin{aligned}
M(D) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{D}{1-D}
\end{aligned}
$$

The negative sign indicates polarity inversion. This is why it is also called an **inverting converter**.

---

---

### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### ✅ QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a **Boost converter** in Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM). Derive the expression for its output voltage. (7 marks)

**(b)** A Boost converter operates with $V_{in} = 12$ V, duty cycle $D = 0.5$, switching frequency $f_s = 100$ kHz, $L = 200$ $\mu$H, and load resistance $R = 20$ $\Omega$. Calculate:
(i) Output voltage
(ii) Average inductor current
(iii) Inductor current ripple
(iv) Critical inductance (and verify CCM) (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Working Principle and Derivation (7 marks):**

**Circuit Diagram Description:** A Boost converter consists of a DC input source $V_{in}$ in series with an inductor $L$, a controlled switch (MOSFET) connected from the inductor-switch junction to ground, a diode oriented from the switch node to the output, and a parallel combination of capacitor $C$ and load $R$ at the output.

**Mode 1 — Switch ON ($0 < t < DT_s$):**
- The MOSFET is conducting → switch node is grounded.
- Diode is reverse-biased (cathode at $V_{out}$, anode at ~0 V).
- Inductor charges from $V_{in}$: $V_L = V_{in}$, current ramps up linearly.
- Capacitor alone powers the load.

**Mode 2 — Switch OFF ($DT_s < t < T_s$):**
- MOSFET opens → inductor opposes current change → reverses polarity.
- Diode becomes forward-biased.
- Inductor (in series with $V_{in}$) now delivers energy to load and charges capacitor.
- Inductor voltage: $V_L = V_{in} - V_{out}$ (negative since $V_{out} > V_{in}$).

**Derivation using volt-second balance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{DT_s} V_{in} \, dt + \int_{DT_s}^{T_s} (V_{in} - V_{out}) \, dt &= 0 \\
V_{in} D + (V_{in} - V_{out})(1-D) &= 0 \\
V_{in} - V_{out}(1-D) &= 0 \\
V_{out} &= \frac{V_{in}}{1-D}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculations (7 marks):**

**(i) Output Voltage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= \frac{V_{in}}{1-D} = \frac{12}{1-0.5} = \frac{12}{0.5} = 24 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Average Inductor Current:**

Average input current equals average inductor current in a Boost converter:
$$
\begin{aligned}
I_L &= \frac{I_{out}}{1-D} = \frac{V_{out}/R}{1-D} = \frac{24/20}{0.5} = \frac{1.2}{0.5} = 2.4 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Inductor Current Ripple:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta I_L &= \frac{V_{in} \cdot D}{f_s \cdot L \cdot (1-D)} = \frac{12 \times 0.5}{100{,}000 \times 200 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.5} \\
&= \frac{6}{10} = 0.6 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Critical Inductance and CCM Verification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_c &= \frac{D(1-D)^2 R}{2 f_s} = \frac{0.5 \times (0.5)^2 \times 20}{2 \times 100{,}000} = \frac{0.5 \times 0.25 \times 20}{200{,}000} \\
&= \frac{2.5}{200{,}000} = 12.5 \text{ \mu H}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $L = 200$ $\mu$H $\gg L_c = 12.5$ $\mu$H, the converter operates in **CCM** ✓.

---

#### ✅ QUESTION B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Compare the **Buck, Boost, and Buck-Boost** converters based on: (i) output voltage expression, (ii) output polarity, (iii) typical application, and (iv) voltage gain range. (7 marks)

**(b)** A **Buck-Boost converter** has $V_{in} = 15$ V, $D = 0.6$, $R = 30$ $\Omega$, and $L = 150$ $\mu$H with $f_s = 50$ kHz. Calculate: (i) Output voltage (with polarity), (ii) Output current magnitude, (iii) Inductor ripple current, (iv) Critical inductance. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Comparative Table (7 marks):**

| Feature | Buck Converter | Boost Converter | Buck-Boost Converter |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Output Voltage** | $V_{out} = D \cdot V_{in}$ | $V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{1-D}$ | $V_{out} = -\dfrac{D \cdot V_{in}}{1-D}$ |
| **(ii) Output Polarity** | Same as input (non-inverting) | Same as input (non-inverting) | Opposite to input (inverting) |
| **(iii) Typical Application** | Step-down for digital ICs in wearables | LED drivers, sensor excitation | Battery voltage regulators (variable) |
| **(iv) Voltage Gain Range** | $0 < M(D) \leq 1$ | $1 \leq M(D) < \infty$ | $-\infty < M(D) < 0$ |
| **(v) Functional Mode** | Step-down only | Step-up only | Step-up or step-down |

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Output Voltage (with polarity):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= -\frac{D \cdot V_{in}}{1-D} = -\frac{0.6 \times 15}{1-0.6} = -\frac{9}{0.4} = -22.5 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

The negative sign indicates **inverted polarity**.

**(ii) Output Current Magnitude:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert I_{out} \vert &= \frac{\vert V_{out} \vert}{R} = \frac{22.5}{30} = 0.75 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Inductor Ripple Current:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta I_L &= \frac{V_{in} \cdot D}{f_s \cdot L} = \frac{15 \times 0.6}{50{,}000 \times 150 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= \frac{9}{7.5} = 1.2 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Critical Inductance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_c &= \frac{(1-D)^2 R}{2 f_s} = \frac{(0.4)^2 \times 30}{2 \times 50{,}000} = \frac{0.16 \times 30}{100{,}000} \\
&= \frac{4.8}{100{,}000} = 48 \text{ \mu H}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $L = 150$ $\mu$H $> L_c = 48$ $\mu$H, the converter operates in **CCM** ✓.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in Buck-Boost:** Many students write $V_{out} = \dfrac{D \cdot V_{in}}{1-D}$ without the negative sign. This is a **1-mark direct cut** and sometimes 2 marks if the polarity is asked explicitly. **Always include the negative sign** unless the question asks for magnitude.
>
> 2. **Confusing Boost and Buck-Boost formulas:** Boost has $V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{1-D}$ (positive). Buck-Boost has $V_{out} = -\dfrac{D \cdot V_{in}}{1-D}$ (negative). Mixing these up loses 3–4 marks.
>
> 3. **Not verifying CCM:** Numerical problems almost always require you to check $L \geq L_c$. Skipping this loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 4. **Unit errors in ripple current:** $\Delta I_L$ must come out in Amperes. Watch out for $L$ in $\mu$H (multiply by $10^{-6}$) and $f_s$ in kHz (multiply by $10^{3}$).
>
> 5. **Inductor ripple formula misuse:** Buck: $\Delta I_L = \dfrac{V_{out}(1-D)}{f_s L}$ (uses $V_{out}$). Boost and Buck-Boost: use $V_{in}$ in the numerator. Mixing these is a frequent error.
>
> 6. **Writing "Buck" when asked about "Buck-Boost":** Always read the question twice before writing the formula.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **Buck converter** steps DOWN voltage: $V_{out} = D \cdot V_{in}$, where $0 < D < 1$. Non-inverting.
- ✅ A **Boost converter** steps UP voltage: $V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{1-D}$. Non-inverting.
- ✅ A **Buck-Boost converter** can step up or down: $V_{out} = -\dfrac{D \cdot V_{in}}{1-D}$. **Output is INVERTED in polarity.**
- ✅ **Volt-Second Balance** is the key principle for deriving all three converter equations: average inductor voltage over one cycle = 0 in steady state.
- ✅ **Amp-Second Balance** (charge balance on capacitor) gives output voltage ripple: $\Delta V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{out}(1-D)}{8 f_s^2 L C}$ (Buck example).
- ✅ **Critical inductance $L_c$** must be computed to verify Continuous Conduction Mode. Different formulas for each converter type.
- ✅ For **CCM**: $L \geq L_c$ → use simple formulas. For **DCM**: $L < L_c$ → more complex analysis (not in Module 2 scope).
- ✅ **Biomedical relevance**: Buck → implantable device power management; Boost → LED drivers in diagnostic equipment; Buck-Boost → portable devices with variable battery voltage.
- ✅ **Efficiency**: SMPS converters are 80–95% efficient, far superior to linear regulators — critical for battery-powered biomedical wearables.
- ✅ **Switching frequency $f_s$** is typically 20 kHz to 1 MHz. Higher $f_s$ → smaller inductors and capacitors → smaller device footprint.

---

> **END OF NOTES — MODULE 2: DC-DC CONVERTER TYPES (BUCK, BOOST, BUCK-BOOST)**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech (PEEBT411) Board Examination Standards*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advantages and Applications in Medical Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT411 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY FOR BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Advantages and Applications in Medical Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Medical Device (as per IEC 60601-1 & US FDA 21 CFR 801.4):** Any instrument, apparatus, implement, machine, appliance, implant, in vitro reagent, software, material, or other similar or related article, intended by the manufacturer to be used, alone or in combination, for human beings for one or more of the specific medical purposes of:
> - **Diagnosis, prevention, monitoring, prediction, prognosis, treatment, or alleviation of disease**
> - **Diagnosis, monitoring, treatment, alleviation of, or compensation for, an injury or disability**
> - **Investigation, replacement, or modification of the anatomy or of a physiological or pathological process or state**

When the device achieves its principal intended action **by means other than pharmacological, immunological, or metabolic means** (though these may assist), it qualifies as an **Electrical Medical Device** when it incorporates an electrical energy source.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Doctor's Universal Translator" Analogy**
>
> Imagine a doctor from the 1850s trying to listen to the heart, lungs, and electrical activity of internal organs with only a wooden stethoscope and bare hands. The human body is essentially a **biochemical and bioelectrical machine** — but its signals (microvolt-level heart potentials, millivolt-level brain waves, megahertz ultrasound echoes) are completely invisible and inaudible to our natural senses.
>
> An electrical medical device acts like a **universal translator and amplifier**:
> - It **picks up** weak bio-electric signals (ECG, EEG, EMG)
> - It **translates** them into visible/audible formats (graphs, tones, images)
> - It **delivers** controlled energy back into the body (defibrillation, pacing, ablation)
> - It **monitors** continuously without fatigue, unlike a human observer
>
> In short, electrical technology extends the doctor's senses and surgical reach into realms the human body cannot directly access.

### 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics & Safety Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Leakage Current Limits (IEC 60601-1, 3rd Edition — Mandatory for Board Exam):**
> - **Earth Leakage Current:** **5 mA (NC)** and **10 mA (SFC)**
> - **Enclosure/Touch Leakage Current:** **0.1 mA (NC)** and **0.5 mA (SFC)**
> - **Patient Auxiliary Current (Type B/BF):** **100 µA (NC)** and **500 µA (SFC)**
> - **Patient Auxiliary Current (Type CF — Cardiac):** **10 µA (NC)** and **50 µA (SFC)**
>
> *(NC = Normal Condition, SFC = Single Fault Condition)*

### 1.4 Classification of Medical Devices (FDA Risk-Based)

| **Class** | **Risk Level** | **Description** | **Example Devices** |
|:---------:|:--------------:|-----------------|---------------------|
| Class I | **Low** | General controls; minimal electrical risk | Tongue depressors, stethoscopes, elastic bandages |
| Class II | **Moderate** | Special controls + performance standards | Infusion pumps, surgical drapes, pregnancy test kits, **ECG machines, pulse oximeters** |
| Class III | **High** | Premarket approval (PMA); life-sustaining/supporting | **Pacemakers, defibrillators, heart valves, implantable neurostimulators** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Insulation Type Symbols (Mandatory in KTU Board Exams):**
> - **Type B (Body):** Earthed patient connection; standard protection.
> - **Type BF (Body Floating):** Floating (isolated) patient connection; higher protection.
> - **Type CF (Cardiac Floating):** Highest protection; for **direct cardiac contact** (e.g., catheter electrodes).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Advantages of Electrical Technology in Medical Devices

The integration of electrical engineering principles into biomedical applications provides six fundamental advantages that have revolutionized modern healthcare:

1. **Non-Invasiveness and Minimally Invasive Access**
   - Electrical sensors (surface electrodes, optical sensors) can acquire physiological signals **without breaking the skin barrier**, drastically reducing infection risk and patient recovery time.
   - Example: A 12-lead ECG captures complete cardiac electrical activity using only 10 surface electrodes.

2. **High Sensitivity and Selectivity**
   - Modern bio-amplifiers detect signals as low as **1 µV (microvolt)** — the typical amplitude range of fetal ECG at abdominal surface.
   - Frequency-selective filtering isolates the desired bio-signal (e.g., 0.5 Hz to 150 Hz for ECG) from ambient noise.

3. **Real-Time Monitoring and Closed-Loop Control**
   - Continuous, fatigue-free monitoring enables closed-loop systems (e.g., **artificial pancreas** for Type-1 diabetes, **anaesthesia depth controllers**).
   - Sampling rates up to **100 kHz** are achievable for high-fidelity signal acquisition.

4. **Quantitative and Reproducible Measurements**
   - Replaces subjective qualitative assessment with standardized, repeatable numerical values.
   - Enables statistical analysis, longitudinal patient tracking, and machine-learning diagnostics.

5. **Miniaturization and Portability**
   - Advances in semiconductor technology (Moore's Law) have enabled wearable/implantable devices: **Holter monitors (50 g), insulin pumps (40 g), cochlear implants (~5 g)**.

6. **Therapeutic Energy Delivery**
   - Controlled application of electrical energy for tissue stimulation, ablation, defibrillation, and neuromodulation.

### 2.2 Major Application Categories (KTU 2024 Module 2 Mapping)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5 Functional Categories of Biomedical Devices:**
>
> 1. **Diagnostic Devices** — Identify pathology (e.g., MRI, CT, ultrasound, ECG, blood glucose meter)
> 2. **Monitoring Devices** — Continuous/periodic surveillance of vital signs (e.g., ICU multi-parameter monitor, pulse oximeter, fetal monitor)
> 3. **Therapeutic Devices** — Treat or cure disease (e.g., defibrillator, pacemaker, infusion pump, dialysis machine, TENS unit)
> 4. **Imaging Devices** — Visualize internal anatomy/physiology (e.g., X-ray, CT, MRI, PET, ultrasound)
> 5. **Assistive & Prosthetic Devices** — Restore lost function (e.g., cochlear implant, bionic limb, retinal prosthesis, exoskeleton)

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter / Concept** | **Equation / Value** | **Units** | **Application Context** |
|-------------------------|---------------------|:---------:|--------------------------|
| Patient Auxiliary Current Limit (CF) | $I_{aux,CF} \leq 10 \text{ \mu A (NC)}$ | µA | Direct cardiac contact devices |
| Patient Auxiliary Current Limit (BF/B) | $I_{aux,BF} \leq 100 \text{ \mu A (NC)}$ | µA | Body-surface devices |
| Enclosure Leakage (NC) | $I_{enc} \leq 0.1 \text{ mA}$ | mA | Touch current limit |
| Earth Leakage (NC) | $I_{earth} \leq 5 \text{ mA}$ | mA | Ground fault protection |
| Signal Amplitude — ECG (R-peak) | $1.0 \text{ to } 2.0$ | mV | Standard adult ECG |
| Signal Amplitude — EEG | $10 \text{ to } 100$ | µV | Brain wave detection |
| Signal Amplitude — EMG | $50 \text{ \mu V to } 5$ | mV | Muscle action potentials |
| Defibrillation Energy (Adult) | $E = 150 \text{ to } 360$ | Joules | Biphasic shock delivery |
| Power (Electrical to Thermal) | $P = I^{2} \cdot R$ | Watts | Surgical diathermy/cautery |
| Heart Rate (from R-R interval) | $HR = \frac{60}{RR_{\text{sec}}}$ | bpm | Cardiotachometer |
| Mean Arterial Pressure | $MAP = DBP + \frac{1}{3}(SBP - DBP)$ | mmHg | Hemodynamic monitoring |
| Oxygen Saturation (Beer-Lambert) | $SpO_2 = f\!\left(\frac{AC_{660}}{DC_{660}} \div \frac{AC_{940}}{DC_{940}}\right)$ | % | Pulse oximetry |
| SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio) | $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | dB | Bio-amplifier design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Board Exam:** "**CF is for Cardiac, 10 µA is the key**" — Type CF devices have the **10× tighter** leakage limit than Type BF because even 100 µA can induce ventricular fibrillation when applied directly to the myocardium.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In contemporary healthcare infrastructure, electrical medical devices constitute the **backbone of every modern ICU, OT, and diagnostic suite**. The global medical device market (USD 600+ billion in 2024) is fundamentally driven by the convergence of:

- **Semiconductor miniaturization** (enabling implants)
- **Wireless telemetry** (enabling remote patient monitoring, IoMT — Internet of Medical Things)
- **AI/ML edge processing** (enabling real-time arrhythmia detection in wearables)
- **Energy harvesting** (enabling battery-less implants)

A KTU biomedical engineer must understand both the **electronic operating principles** and the **clinical application context** to design safe, effective, and regulatory-compliant devices.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Framework: How to Evaluate a Medical Device Application

Since the topic is conceptual, we construct a **rigorous analytical framework** that KTU examiners expect students to demonstrate in 14-mark answers. The following worked analysis shows how to **classify a device, justify its safety class, and map it to application category**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample Worked Analysis: Pulse Oximeter**
>
> We demonstrate the step-by-step analytical process for any biomedical device evaluation.

**Step 1 — Identify the Physiological Variable Measured**

A pulse oximeter measures **arterial oxygen saturation ($SpO_2$)** and **pulse rate (PR)** non-invasively at the fingertip, earlobe, or toe using **photoplethysmography (PPG)**.

**Step 2 — Classify the Energy Interaction with the Body**

The device emits low-intensity red (660 nm) and infrared (940 nm) light into tissue. Light is partially absorbed by oxyhemoglobin and deoxyhemoglobin. The device is therefore **non-invasive** and **passive in its therapeutic intent** (it does not deliver energy for treatment).

**Step 3 — Determine the Risk Classification**

Because the device does **not** contact the heart directly and is **not life-sustaining** (interruption does not cause immediate harm), it falls under **Class II** (moderate risk) per FDA and **Class IIa** per EU MDR. Insulation type is **Type BF** (patient-isolated floating circuit).

**Step 4 — Apply the Leakage Current Safety Bounds**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Type BF Patient Auxiliary Current Limit (NC)} &= 100 \text{ µA} \\
\text{Type BF Patient Auxiliary Current Limit (SFC)} &= 500 \text{ µA} \\
\text{Enclosure Leakage (NC)} &= 0.1 \text{ mA} \\
\text{Earth Leakage (NC)} &= 5 \text{ mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

The pulse oximeter's design must ensure that under **any single fault** (e.g., broken earth wire), the current through the patient remains **below 500 µA**.

**Step 5 — Application Domain Mapping**

Pulse oximeter fits the **Monitoring Devices** category (continuous surveillance of oxygenation in ICU, OT, post-operative care, home sleep apnea screening, COVID-19 monitoring, high-altitude medicine).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Heart Rate Calculation from ECG R-R Interval

This is a frequently-asked 7-mark derivation in KTU board exams.

> **Given:** A patient's ECG trace shows successive R-peaks at the following time positions (in seconds): 0.00, 0.82, 1.60, 2.45, 3.25.
> **Find:** Heart rate in beats per minute (bpm).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Method: Average R-R Interval} \\
RR_1 &= 0.82 - 0.00 = 0.82 \text{ s} \\
RR_2 &= 1.60 - 0.82 = 0.78 \text{ s} \\
RR_3 &= 2.45 - 1.60 = 0.85 \text{ s} \\
RR_4 &= 3.25 - 2.45 = 0.80 \text{ s} \\
\overline{RR} &= \frac{0.82 + 0.78 + 0.85 + 0.80}{4} \text{ s} \\
\overline{RR} &= \frac{3.25}{4} \text{ s} \\
\overline{RR} &= 0.8125 \text{ s} \\
HR &= \frac{60}{\overline{RR}} = \frac{60}{0.8125} \\
HR &= 73.85 \text{ bpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A heart rate of **~74 bpm** lies within the normal adult resting range (**60–100 bpm**). The device would classify this as a **Normal Sinus Rhythm** and not trigger an alarm.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Diagnostic vs. Therapeutic vs. Monitoring Devices

| **Attribute** | **Diagnostic Devices** | **Therapeutic Devices** | **Monitoring Devices** |
|---------------|-----------------------|-------------------------|------------------------|
| Primary Purpose | Identify pathology | Treat/manage disease | Track physiological state |
| Energy Direction | Into the device (passive) | Into the patient (active) | Bi-directional or passive |
| Risk Class (typical) | Class I–II | Class II–III | Class II |
| Contact Type | Type BF (surface) | Type BF or CF | Type BF |
| Examples | ECG, EEG, glucometer | Defibrillator, pacemaker, TENS | Pulse oximeter, fetal monitor, multi-parameter monitor |
| Output | Waveform/image/reading | Energy (electrical, thermal, mechanical) | Continuous numerical display + alarms |
| Regulatory Bar | Moderate | **High** (PMA required for Class III) | Moderate |

### 3.4 Worked Example: Defibrillator Energy Calculation

A monophasic defibrillator delivers a damped sinusoidal pulse through a 50 Ω patient chest impedance. The capacitor is charged to **2000 V** and has a capacitance of **32 µF**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{stored}} &= \frac{1}{2} C V^{2} \\
E_{\text{stored}} &= \frac{1}{2} \times 32 \times 10^{-6} \times (2000)^{2} \\
E_{\text{stored}} &= \frac{1}{2} \times 32 \times 10^{-6} \times 4 \times 10^{6} \\
E_{\text{stored}} &= \frac{1}{2} \times 128 \text{ J} \\
E_{\text{stored}} &= 64 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is below the **150–360 J** standard adult defibrillation dose. A clinician would typically charge to higher voltage (~2750 V → ~120 J delivered) for an adult patient.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification Flowchart of Medical Devices

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Medical Device] --> B{Energy Interaction}
    B -- Non-energy delivering --> C[Non-Active]
    B -- Energy delivering --> D[Active]
    C --> E[Diagnostic]
    C --> F[Monitoring]
    D --> G[Therapeutic]
    D --> H[Imaging]
    D --> I[Assistive Implant]
    E --> E1[ECG Machine]
    E --> E2[EEG Machine]
    E --> E3[Blood Glucose Meter]
    F --> F1[Pulse Oximeter]
    F --> F2[ICU Multi-Parameter Monitor]
    F --> F3[Fetal Heart Monitor]
    G --> G1[Defibrillator]
    G --> G2[Pacemaker]
    G --> G3[Infusion Pump]
    G --> G4[Dialysis Machine]
    H --> H1[X-Ray System]
    H --> H2[MRI Scanner]
    H --> H3[Ultrasound Scanner]
    H --> H4[PET Scanner]
    I --> I1[Cochlear Implant]
    I --> I2[Retinal Prosthesis]
    I --> I3[Deep Brain Stimulator]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Generic Architecture of an Electrical Medical Device

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Patient / Body] --> S1[Sensor / Electrode]
    S1 --> A1[Preamplifier]
    A1 --> F1[Filter Stage]
    F1 --> A2[Main Amplifier]
    A2 --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter]
    ADC --> MCU[Microcontroller / DSP]
    MCU --> DSP[Signal Processing Algorithm]
    DSP --> D1[Display / Monitor]
    DSP --> AL[Alarm Module]
    DSP --> TX[Wireless Transmitter]
    TX --> CLOUD[Cloud / Hospital Network]
    MCU --> ACT[Actuator / Therapy Delivery]
    ACT --> P
    PWR[Isolated Power Supply IEC 60601] --> A1
    PWR --> A2
    PWR --> ADC
    PWR --> MCU
    PWR --> ACT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice the dedicated **Isolated Power Supply** block — this is the cornerstone of **patient electrical safety**. It provides **galvanic isolation** (typically > 4000 V RMS creepage/clearance) between the mains earth and any patient-connected circuit, preventing dangerous earth-loops and macro-shock hazards.

### 4.3 Application Mapping Diagram: Medical Specialties and Their Core Devices

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MED[Medical Specialties] --> CARD[Cardiology]
    MED --> NEURO[Neurology]
    MED --> RESP[Respiratory]
    MED --> ENDO[Endocrinology]
    MED --> ORTHO[Orthopedics]
    MED --> OPHTH[Ophthalmology]
    CARD --> CD1[ECG]
    CARD --> CD2[Pacemaker]
    CARD --> CD3[Defibrillator]
    CARD --> CD4[Cardiac Catheter Lab]
    NEURO --> NR1[EEG]
    NEURO --> NR2[EMG]
    NEURO --> NR3[Deep Brain Stimulator]
    NEURO --> NR4[MRI]
    RESP --> RP1[Pulse Oximeter]
    RESP --> RP2[Spirometer]
    RESP --> RP3[Ventilator]
    ENDO --> EN1[Insulin Pump]
    ENDO --> EN2[CGM Sensor]
    ORTHO --> OR1[Exoskeleton]
    ORTHO --> OR2[Bionic Limb]
    OPHTH --> OP1[Retinal Prosthesis]
    OPHTH --> OP2[OCT Scanner]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Define a medical device. List any four advantages of electrical technology in medical devices.**

**Model Answer:**

A medical device is any instrument, apparatus, appliance, implant, or software intended by the manufacturer to be used for human beings for the diagnosis, prevention, monitoring, treatment, or alleviation of disease, injury, or disability, achieving its principal intended action by means other than pharmacological, immunological, or metabolic means (as per IEC 60601-1 and US FDA 21 CFR 801.4).

**Four advantages of electrical technology in medical devices:**

1. **High sensitivity** — Detects microvolt-level bio-signals (e.g., fetal ECG at 1 µV).
2. **Non-invasive measurement** — Acquires vital signs without breaking the skin (e.g., pulse oximeter).
3. **Real-time continuous monitoring** — Enables fatigue-free, 24/7 surveillance in ICU.
4. **Miniaturization and portability** — Modern implants weigh as little as 5 g (e.g., cochlear implant).

*(Any four from: non-invasiveness, sensitivity, real-time operation, reproducibility, miniaturization, therapeutic energy delivery)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Differentiate between Type BF and Type CF medical devices with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Feature** | **Type BF (Body Floating)** | **Type CF (Cardiac Floating)** |
|-------------|------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Patient Connection | Floating (isolated from earth) | Floating (isolated from earth) |
| Level of Protection | Higher than Type B | **Highest** among all types |
| Intended Application | Body surface / external contact | **Direct cardiac contact** |
| Patient Aux. Current Limit (NC) | 100 µA | **10 µA (10× stricter)** |
| Patient Aux. Current Limit (SFC) | 500 µA | **50 µA (10× stricter)** |
| Defibrillation Proof | Optional (suffix "defibrillation-proof") | **Mandatory** |
| Example | Pulse oximeter, ECG machine, TENS unit | **Intracardiac catheter, pacemaker lead, intracardiac defibrillator** |

**Key Point:** Type CF is mandatory for any device whose applied part can make conductive contact with the heart, because even currents as low as 10 µA reaching the myocardium can trigger ventricular fibrillation during the vulnerable T-wave period.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

### **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Discuss in detail the various applications of electrical technology in medical devices, with at least one example for each application category.

### **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a generic electrical medical device and identify the safety-critical blocks.

---

### **MODEL ANSWER — Question 3 (a)**

Electrical technology has revolutionized modern medicine by enabling five major categories of applications:

**1. Diagnostic Applications**
- Detect and identify pathology through bio-signal acquisition and imaging.
- *Examples:* **Electrocardiograph (ECG)** records the 1–2 mV electrical activity of the heart using 10 surface electrodes and detects arrhythmias, MI, and ischemia. **Electroencephalograph (EEG)** measures 10–100 µV brain potentials via scalp electrodes for epilepsy and sleep disorder diagnosis.

**2. Monitoring Applications**
- Continuous/periodic surveillance of vital signs with alarm generation.
- *Examples:* **Multi-parameter ICU monitor** tracks ECG, SpO₂, NIBP, respiration, and temperature simultaneously. **Pulse oximeter** uses 660 nm and 940 nm PPG to measure oxygen saturation at the fingertip.

**3. Therapeutic Applications**
- Delivers controlled energy (electrical, thermal, mechanical) to treat disease.
- *Examples:* **Defibrillator** delivers 150–360 J biphasic shock to depolarize the myocardium during VF/VT. **Pacemaker** delivers 0.5–5 V, 0.5 ms pulses at 60–100 bpm to maintain heart rhythm. **TENS unit** delivers low-frequency currents for chronic pain relief.

**4. Imaging Applications**
- Visualize internal anatomy and physiology.
- *Examples:* **MRI** uses 1.5 T or 3 T static magnetic field plus RF pulses for soft-tissue imaging. **Ultrasound scanner** uses 2–15 MHz piezoelectric transducers for real-time fetal and cardiac imaging. **X-ray system** uses 30–150 kVp tube for skeletal imaging.

**5. Assistive / Prosthetic Applications**
- Restore lost sensory or motor function.
- *Examples:* **Cochlear implant** electrically stimulates the auditory nerve in profoundly deaf patients. **Bionic limb** uses EMG signals from residual muscles to actuate motorized prosthetic joints. **Retinal prosthesis (Argus II)** stimulates retinal ganglion cells in retinitis pigmentosa patients.

---

### **MODEL ANSWER — Question 3 (b)**

**Block Diagram of a Generic Electrical Medical Device:**

```
[Patient/Bio-source] → [Sensor/Electrode] → [Preamplifier] → 
[Filter] → [Main Amplifier] → [ADC] → [Microcontroller/DSP] → 
[Display/Alarm/Wireless Tx] → [User/Clinician]
                                       ↘
                                 [Actuator/Therapy] → [Patient]
                                  (powered by Isolated Power Supply)
```

**Description of each block:**

1. **Sensor / Electrode** — Converts physiological variable (biopotential, pressure, temperature, optical density) into electrical signal. Example: Ag/AgCl electrode for ECG.

2. **Preamplifier** — High-input-impedance (≥ 10 MΩ), low-noise instrumentation amplifier (e.g., AD620, INA128) with CMRR > 80 dB to reject 50/60 Hz mains interference.

3. **Filter Stage** — Bandpass filter (e.g., 0.5–150 Hz for ECG) to remove DC offset, baseline wander, and high-frequency noise.

4. **Main Amplifier** — Provides variable gain (×100 to ×10000) to bring signal to ADC input range.

5. **ADC** — Samples signal at appropriate rate (≥ 500 Hz for ECG) with 12–24 bit resolution.

6. **Microcontroller / DSP** — Executes signal processing, feature extraction, alarm algorithms, and display driving.

7. **Display / Alarm / Wireless** — Real-time waveform, numerical readouts, threshold-based alerts, and IoMT connectivity.

8. **Isolated Power Supply (Safety-Critical Block)** — Provides medical-grade isolation per **IEC 60601-1**: ≥ 4000 V RMS isolation, < 100 µA patient leakage (Type BF), Class II double insulation, and battery-backed operation.

**Safety-Critical Blocks Identification:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Isolated Power Supply** — Primary defence against macro-shock (current through body from mains earth).
> - **Preamplifier Isolation Barrier** — Prevents patient from becoming a current path to ground.
> - **Defibrillation Protection Circuitry** — Diverts the high-voltage shock away from sensitive electronics when used alongside a defibrillator.
> - **Alarm and Watchdog System** — Ensures failure modes fail-safely (ISO 14971 risk management).

---

### **ALTERNATIVE CHOICE — Question 3B**

**Q4. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

### **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Classify medical devices based on risk and patient-contact type. Explain the leakage current safety standards as per IEC 60601-1.

### **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Explain in detail the working principle of a defibrillator with a circuit diagram, and explain why a biphasic waveform is preferred over a monophasic one.

---

### **MODEL ANSWER — Question 4 (a)**

**Classification of Medical Devices:**

**1. Risk-Based Classification (FDA):**

- **Class I (Low Risk):** General controls. Examples — stethoscope, tongue depressor.
- **Class II (Moderate Risk):** Special controls + 510(k) clearance. Examples — ECG machine, infusion pump, pulse oximeter.
- **Class III (High Risk):** Premarket approval (PMA) required. Examples — pacemaker, implantable defibrillator, heart valve.

**2. Patient-Contact Type Classification (IEC 60601-1):**

- **Type B (Body):** Earthed patient connection. Example — EEG machine.
- **Type BF (Body Floating):** Floating patient connection; higher protection. Example — pulse oximeter, TENS unit.
- **Type CF (Cardiac Floating):** Highest protection; for direct cardiac contact. Example — pacemaker, intracardiac catheter.

**3. Applied Part Symbol (Marking on Device):**

- **B** — Type B (no patient isolation)
- **BF** — Type BF (floating)
- **CF** — Type CF (cardiac floating, highest)
- **⊘** with **CF** — Defibrillation-proof CF

**Leakage Current Safety Standards (IEC 60601-1):**

| **Leakage Type** | **NC Limit** | **SFC Limit** | **Applies To** |
|------------------|-------------:|--------------:|----------------|
| Earth Leakage Current | 5 mA | 10 mA | All classes |
| Enclosure / Touch Current | 0.1 mA | 0.5 mA | All classes |
| Patient Auxiliary Current (Type B/BF) | 100 µA | 500 µA | Type B, BF |
| Patient Auxiliary Current (Type CF) | **10 µA** | **50 µA** | Type CF only |

> **Engineering Note:** The "10 µA" limit for Type CF is critical because the body's natural ventricular fibrillation threshold is approximately **10 µA at 50 Hz** when applied directly to the myocardium during the T-wave vulnerable period (R-on-T phenomenon). Hence, any device with a conductive path to the heart must restrict leakage to a value below this threshold even under fault conditions.

---

### **MODEL ANSWER — Question 4 (b)**

**Working Principle of a Defibrillator:**

A defibrillator is a **therapeutic Class III** device that delivers a controlled high-energy electrical shock (150–360 J in adults) across the patient's chest (via paddle electrodes or adhesive pads) to **simultaneously depolarize a critical mass of myocardial cells**, thereby terminating a chaotic re-entrant arrhythmia (ventricular fibrillation — VF, or pulseless ventricular tachycardia — pVT) and allowing the sinoatrial (SA) node to resume normal sinus rhythm.

**Block Diagram:**

```
[AC Mains] → [High-Voltage Transformer] → [Rectifier] → 
[High-Voltage Capacitor C (e.g., 32 µF)] → [Charging Switch] → 
[Inductor L + Patient Electrodes (Rpatient ≈ 50 Ω)] → 
[Patient Chest] → [Discharge Control / Trigger]
```

**Working Phases:**

1. **Charge Phase:** The high-voltage capacitor is charged to a target voltage (e.g., 2000–3600 V) determined by the selected energy dose. The energy stored follows $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^{2}$.

2. **Discharge Phase:** When the operator presses the discharge button, the capacitor discharges through an inductor (L) and the patient (R) in a damped LCR circuit, delivering a controlled pulse.

3. **Damped Sinusoidal Monophasic:** The current flows in **one direction only** through the chest, decaying over ~4 ms.

4. **Biphasic Truncated Exponential (BTE):** After ~4 ms, the circuit **reverses polarity**, allowing the second phase to flow for another ~4 ms in the opposite direction.

**Why Biphasic is Preferred:**

1. **Lower energy required:** Biphasic achieves equivalent defibrillation success at ~150 J compared to ~200–360 J for monophasic — reducing myocardial injury.
2. **Higher first-shock success rate:** ~90% for biphasic vs. ~60% for monophasic in clinical trials.
3. **Reduced post-shock ST-segment elevation** and less myocardial damage.
4. **Better for high-impedance patients** (obese, elderly) due to adaptive impedance compensation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Type B" and "Type BF":** Students often write "Type B = body, Type BF = body floating" but forget to mention that **Type CF is mandatory for any direct cardiac contact** — this is the most common mark-deduction point.
>
