> 2. **Confusing NC and SFC:** **NC = Normal Condition** (no fault). **SFC = Single Fault Condition** (one component failed). The leakage current limits are **stricter for NC than SFC** — students frequently invert this.
>
> 3. **Forgetting units in the formula sheet:** Writing "I = 10" instead of "I = 10 µA" loses 0.5–1 mark per occurrence.
>
> 4. **Missing the "10× rule":** Type CF limit is exactly 10× tighter than Type BF; quoting only one row of the table loses 2 marks.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to draw the isolated power supply in the block diagram:** Examiners allocate **1 mark** for explicitly mentioning the IEC 60601-1 isolated supply block.
>
> 6. **Confusing "defibrillator" and "pacemaker":** Defibrillator = emergency high-energy shock for VF. Pacemaker = chronic low-energy pacing pulses for bradycardia. Both are Class III, but their function and circuitry are completely different.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Board Prep):**
>
> - **Definition:** A medical device is any instrument/implant/software used for diagnosis, monitoring, treatment, or alleviation of disease, achieving action by non-pharmacological means (IEC 60601-1, FDA 21 CFR 801.4).
>
> - **Five Application Categories:** Diagnostic, Monitoring, Therapeutic, Imaging, Assistive/Prosthetic.
>
> - **Three Patient-Contact Insulation Types:**
>   - **B** → Earthed, lowest protection
>   - **BF** → Floating, body surface
>   - **CF** → Floating, **mandatory for direct cardiac contact**, leakage limit = 10 µA (NC) / 50 µA (SFC)
>
> - **Three FDA Risk Classes:**
>   - **Class I** — low risk (stethoscope)
>   - **Class II** — moderate risk (ECG, pulse oximeter)
>   - **Class III** — high risk, life-sustaining (pacemaker, defibrillator)
>
> - **Leakage Current Limits (must memorize):**
>   - Earth: **5 mA (NC) / 10 mA (SFC)**
>   - Enclosure: **0.1 mA (NC) / 0.5 mA (SFC)**
>   - Patient Aux (B/BF): **100 µA (NC) / 500 µA (SFC)**
>   - Patient Aux (CF): **10 µA (NC) / 50 µA (SFC)**
>
> - **Defibrillation:** Monophasic = single-direction damped sinusoid; **Biphasic = reversed-polarity second phase** (preferred clinically).
>
> - **HR Formula:** $HR = \frac{60}{RR_{sec}}$ bpm.
>
> - **MAP Formula:** $MAP = DBP + \frac{1}{3}(SBP - DBP)$ mmHg.
>
> - **Generic Device Architecture:** Sensor → Preamplifier → Filter → Amplifier → ADC → DSP/MCU → Display/Alarm + (Actuator for therapy) **+ Isolated Power Supply (mandatory safety block)**.
>
> - **Key Signal Amplitudes to Remember:** ECG (1–2 mV) >> EMG (50 µV–5 mV) > EEG (10–100 µV).
>
> - **Safety Standard:** **IEC 60601-1** governs ALL electrical medical devices — a 14-mark question can be written entirely on this standard.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Principles, Losses, and Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT411 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY FOR BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basic Principles, Losses, and Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a DC Machine

A **DC Machine** is an electromechanical energy conversion device that operates on the principle of **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, converting either mechanical energy into direct current electrical energy (when functioning as a **DC Generator**) or direct current electrical energy into mechanical energy (when functioning as a **DC Motor**). The defining feature is the presence of a **commutator** and **brush assembly**, which act as a mechanical rectifier/inverter, ensuring that the output (generator) or input (motor) at the external terminals remains unidirectional (DC).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For the course PEEBT411, the DC machine is treated as a *prerequisite electromagnetic actuator* for biomedical devices such as infusion pumps, ventilators, surgical drills, MRI gradient coils, and ECG recorders. Mastery of its working principles is **mandatory** for Module 3.

## 1.2 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Magnetic flux per pole | $\Phi$ | Weber (Wb) |
| Speed of armature | $N$ | Revolutions per minute (RPM) |
| Permeability of free space | $\mu_0$ | $4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m |
| Induced EMF constant | $k$ | V·s/rad |
| Standard supply voltage (KTU lab) | $V$ | 220 V or 240 V DC |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Bicycle Dynamo

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Bicycle Dynamo**
> Imagine the small lamp fitted on the front wheel of a bicycle. When you pedal, the wheel rotates a small magnet (rotor) inside a coil of wire (stator). The relative motion between the magnetic field and the conductors *cuts* the magnetic flux, generating a small voltage that lights the bulb.
> 
> - The **rotating magnet** $\rightarrow$ analogous to the **armature** in a DC machine.
> - The **stationary coil** $\rightarrow$ analogous to the **field winding**.
> - The **bulb's brightness** depending on pedaling speed $\rightarrow$ analogous to **induced EMF** being proportional to **speed** and **flux**.
> 
> In a DC machine, the only added complexity is the **commutator**, which ensures that the *direction* of current in the external circuit never reverses, even though the internal current in each coil is alternating. This is why the bicycle bulb uses AC, but a DC motor in a ventilator uses DC.

## 1.4 Operational Modes of a DC Machine

The *same physical machine* is reversible:

1. **Generator Action (Mechanical $\rightarrow$ Electrical):** A prime mover (engine, turbine, or in biomedical context — a hand-cranked defibrillator training unit) rotates the armature. Conductors cut the magnetic flux from the field poles, inducing an EMF. The commutator rectifies the internal AC into DC at the brushes.
2. **Motor Action (Electrical $\rightarrow$ Mechanical):** A DC supply is fed through the brushes. Current-carrying conductors placed in the magnetic field experience a **Lorentz force** ($F = BIL$), producing a torque that rotates the armature, delivering mechanical work to the load (e.g., a blood pump impeller).

> [!NOTE]
> **Fleming's Hand Rules for KTU Board Exams:**
> - **Right-Hand Rule** (Generator): Thumb $\rightarrow$ Motion, Index $\rightarrow$ Field, Middle $\rightarrow$ Induced EMF/Current.
> - **Left-Hand Rule** (Motor): Thumb $\rightarrow$ Thrust/Force, Index $\rightarrow$ Field, Middle $\rightarrow$ Current.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — The "Why" and "How"

The DC machine rests on **two reciprocal laws of electromagnetism**:

- **Generator Mode:** Based on **Faraday's Law** — When a conductor *cuts* magnetic flux, an EMF is induced: $e = B \cdot l \cdot v$, where $B$ is flux density, $l$ is active conductor length, and $v$ is relative velocity.
- **Motor Mode:** Based on **Lorentz Force** — A current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field experiences a force: $F = B \cdot I \cdot l$, producing a torque on the armature.

### Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

1. The **field winding** (or permanent magnets) establishes a stationary magnetic flux $\Phi$ across the air gap.
2. The **armature winding**, mounted on a slotted iron core, rotates within this flux.
3. In **generator mode**, mechanical rotation drags conductors across the flux, inducing an alternating EMF in each coil.
4. The **commutator** — a cylindrical arrangement of copper segments insulated by mica — mechanically rectifies this AC into a unidirectional (pulsating DC) output at the **brushes**.
5. In **motor mode**, DC current enters the brushes, passes through the commutator, and is distributed into the armature coils. The interaction with the field produces a unidirectional torque.

## 2.2 EMF Equation of a DC Generator — Conceptual Foundation

Let the machine have:
- $P$ = number of poles
- $\Phi$ = flux per pole (in Webers)
- $Z$ = total number of armature conductors
- $N$ = armature speed (RPM)
- $A$ = number of parallel paths in the armature ($A = P$ for lap winding, $A = 2$ for wave winding)

The flux cut by **one conductor in one revolution** is $P \Phi$. For $N$ revolutions per minute, time taken for one revolution is $\tfrac{60}{N}$ seconds. Hence, the **average EMF induced per conductor** is:

$$
e_{conductor} = \frac{P \Phi}{\tfrac{60}{N}} = \frac{P \Phi N}{60}
$$

The conductors are distributed in $A$ parallel paths, each path having $Z/A$ conductors *in series*. The total EMF (sum of series conductors per path) becomes:

$$
E_b = \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60 \, A} \;\; \text{(Volts)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The EMF equation is the *single most-tested formula* in the DC machine portion of the syllabus. Always state *boundary conditions*: For **wave** winding, $A = 2$ (always); for **lap** winding, $A = P$.

## 2.3 Torque Equation of a DC Motor

Mechanical power developed in the armature equals electrical power converted:

$$
P_d = E_b \cdot I_a = T \cdot \omega
$$

where $\omega = \tfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ rad/s. Substituting the EMF equation:

$$
T = \frac{E_b \cdot I_a}{\omega} = \frac{\left(\tfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A}\right) I_a}{\tfrac{2\pi N}{60}} = \frac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A}
$$

Thus:

$$
T = \frac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A} \;\; \text{(N·m)}
$$

## 2.4 Losses in a DC Machine

A DC machine does not convert **100%** of the input energy into useful output. The losses are classified as:

| Loss Category | Type | When Present | Magnitude Depends On |
|---|---|---|---|
| Armature Copper Loss ($I_a^2 R_a$) | Variable | Both modes | $I_a^2$ (quadratic) |
| Field Copper Loss ($I_{sh}^2 R_{sh}$) | Constant | Both modes (shunt field) | $V^2 / R_{sh}$ |
| Brush Contact Loss ($V_b I_a$) | Variable | Both modes | $I_a$ (linear) |
| Iron/Core Loss — Hysteresis ($P_h$) | Constant | Both modes (rotating armature in field) | $f \cdot B_{max}^{1.6}$ (Steinmetz) |
| Iron/Core Loss — Eddy Current ($P_e$) | Constant | Both modes | $f^2 \cdot B_{max}^{2} \cdot t^2$ |
| Mechanical — Friction | Constant | Both modes (rotating parts) | Speed |
| Mechanical — Windage | Constant | Both modes | $N^3$ |
| Stray Load Loss | Variable | Both modes | $I_a^2$ (approx.) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constant vs Variable (high-yield distinction):**
> - **Constant losses** = Field Cu loss + Iron losses + Friction + Windage $\rightarrow$ independent of load.
> - **Variable losses** = Armature Cu loss + Brush contact loss + Stray load loss $\rightarrow$ depend on load current.

## 2.5 Power Flow Stages in a DC Machine (Motor Mode)

$$
\underbrace{V I_L}_{\text{Input}} \;\longrightarrow\; \underbrace{I_{sh}^2 R_{sh}}_{\text{Field Cu}} \;\longrightarrow\; \underbrace{I_a^2 R_a}_{\text{Arm Cu}} \;\longrightarrow\; \underbrace{P_d = E_b I_a}_{\text{Mech. developed}} \;\longrightarrow\; \underbrace{P_{\text{iron}} + P_{\text{mech}}}_{\text{Rotational}} \;\longrightarrow\; \underbrace{P_{\text{out}}}_{\text{Shaft}}
$$

## 2.6 Efficiency of a DC Machine

Efficiency is the ratio of useful output power to total input power:

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} = \frac{V I_L - \text{losses}}{V I_L}
$$

For a **shunt motor** (the most common type in KTU biomedical problems), with $V$ constant, the field loss is constant and the armature loss is variable:

$$
\eta = \frac{V I_L - V I_{sh} - I_a^2 R_a - W_c}{V I_L}
$$

where $W_c$ = constant losses (iron + friction + windage).

## 2.7 Condition for Maximum Efficiency

Let $W_c$ = constant losses, $W_v = I_a^2 R_a$ = variable losses.

$$
\eta = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Output} + W_c + W_v}
$$

For maximum efficiency, the denominator (losses) must be minimized for a given output. The condition is:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Variable Losses} = \text{Constant Losses}\;}
$$

i.e., $I_a^2 R_a = W_c$, which gives the **current at maximum efficiency**:

$$
I_{a,\text{max}\,\eta} = \sqrt{\frac{W_c}{R_a}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** A common trap is that the condition is $W_v = W_c$, *not* $P_{\text{out}} = P_{\text{losses}}$. Output is **never** equal to losses. The two loss components balance each other.

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_b = \tfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A}$ | Generated/Back EMF | Volts |
| 2 | $T = \tfrac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A}$ | Electromagnetic Torque | N·m |
| 3 | $P_d = E_b \cdot I_a$ | Power developed in armature | Watts |
| 4 | $P_{\text{out}} = \tfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | Shaft output power | Watts |
| 5 | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ | Motor mode voltage equation | Volts |
| 6 | $V = E_b - I_a R_a$ | Generator mode voltage equation | Volts |
| 7 | $\eta = \tfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{V I_L}$ | Efficiency | dimensionless |
| 8 | $I_a^2 R_a = W_c$ | Condition for $\eta_{\max}$ | — |
| 9 | $P_h = \eta_h \cdot f \cdot B_{max}^{1.6} \cdot V$ | Hysteresis loss (Steinmetz) | Watts |
| 10 | $P_e = k_e \cdot f^2 \cdot B_{max}^{2} \cdot t^2$ | Eddy current loss | Watts |

**Real-world biomedical utility:** These equations are directly applied when sizing DC motors for **infusion syringe pumps** (precise low-speed torque), **ventilator blowers** (continuous-duty efficiency), and **portable defibrillators** (DC-to-DC converter design where efficiency dictates battery life).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: EMF Equation of a DC Generator

### Given:
A DC generator with $P$ poles, flux per pole $\Phi$ (Wb), $Z$ total armature conductors, running at $N$ RPM, with $A$ parallel paths.

### To Prove:
$E_b = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 \, A}$

### Derivation:

**Step 1 — Flux cut per revolution:**
In one complete revolution of the armature, each conductor sweeps past **all $P$ poles**, cutting the flux from each pole once. Total flux cut per conductor per revolution = $P \Phi$ Webers.

**Step 2 — Time per revolution:**
If the armature rotates at $N$ RPM, the time taken for **one** revolution is:

$$
t = \frac{60}{N} \;\; \text{seconds}
$$

**Step 3 — Average EMF per conductor (Faraday's Law):**
The average rate of change of flux linkage per conductor:

$$
e_{\text{avg}} = \frac{P \Phi}{t} = \frac{P \Phi}{\tfrac{60}{N}} = \frac{P \Phi N}{60} \;\; \text{volts}
$$

**Step 4 — Series conductors per parallel path:**
The $Z$ conductors are distributed across $A$ parallel paths. Hence, each path contains $\tfrac{Z}{A}$ conductors connected **in series**. The total EMF of the path (and hence of the machine) is the sum of EMFs of conductors in series:

$$
E_b = e_{\text{avg}} \times \frac{Z}{A} = \frac{P \Phi N}{60} \times \frac{Z}{A}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Equation:**

$$
\boxed{\;E_b = \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60 \, A} \;\; \text{Volts}\;}
$$

**Special Cases for KTU Problems:**
- **Lap winding:** $A = P \;\Rightarrow\; E_b = \dfrac{\Phi N Z}{60}$
- **Wave winding:** $A = 2 \;\Rightarrow\; E_b = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{120}$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Torque Equation of a DC Motor

### Derivation:

**Step 1 — Mechanical power developed:**
The armature, when acting as a motor, converts electrical power $E_b I_a$ into mechanical power $P_d$:

$$
P_d = E_b \cdot I_a \;\; \text{(Watts)}
$$

**Step 2 — Relate power to torque:**
Mechanical power is also expressed as the product of torque and angular velocity:

$$
P_d = T \cdot \omega
$$

**Step 3 — Angular velocity in rad/s:**
For a speed of $N$ RPM:

$$
\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} \;\; \text{rad/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Equate and solve for T:**

$$
T \cdot \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = E_b \cdot I_a
$$

$$
T = \frac{60 \, E_b I_a}{2 \pi N}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $E_b$:**

$$
T = \frac{60}{2 \pi N} \times \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A} \times I_a
$$

The $60$ and $N$ cancel:

$$
\boxed{\;T = \frac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A} \;\; \text{N·m}\;}
$$

**Useful Alternative Form (Armature Torque Constant):**
Defining $k_a = \tfrac{P Z}{2 \pi A}$:

$$
T = k_a \, \Phi \, I_a
$$

This is the **linear torque-current relation** used heavily in biomedical servo applications (e.g., proportional control of an actuator).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Condition for Maximum Efficiency

### Setup:
Consider a DC shunt motor. Total losses = $W_c$ (constant) + $W_v$ (variable = $I_a^2 R_a$).
Output power = $V I_L - W_c - W_v$.

$$
\eta = \frac{V I_L - W_c - W_v}{V I_L} = 1 - \frac{W_c + W_v}{V I_L}
$$

For $V$ and $I_L$ varying, this is non-trivial. We use a standard KTU approach: *fix the speed and flux*, then vary the load (i.e., vary $I_a$).

**Step 1 — Express $\eta$ in terms of $I_a$ (shunt motor):**
For a shunt motor, $I_{sh} = V/R_{sh}$ is constant. Total current $I_L = I_a + I_{sh}$.

$$
\eta = \frac{V(I_a + I_{sh}) - V I_{sh} - I_a^2 R_a - W_c}{V(I_a + I_{sh})} = \frac{V I_a - I_a^2 R_a - W_c}{V(I_a + I_{sh})}
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to $I_a$ and set to zero:**

$$
\frac{d\eta}{dI_a} = 0
$$

Let $V(I_a + I_{sh}) = V I_a + V I_{sh}$ and numerator $N = V I_a - I_a^2 R_a - W_c$.

Using the quotient rule $\tfrac{d}{dI_a}\!\left(\tfrac{N}{D}\right) = \tfrac{N' D - N D'}{D^2} = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N' D - N D' &= 0 \\
(V - 2 I_a R_a)(V I_a + V I_{sh}) - (V I_a - I_a^2 R_a - W_c)(V) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Expand and simplify:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&(V - 2 I_a R_a)(V I_a + V I_{sh}) - V(V I_a - I_a^2 R_a - W_c) = 0 \\
&V^2 I_a + V^2 I_{sh} - 2 I_a^2 R_a V - 2 I_a R_a V I_{sh} - V^2 I_a + V I_a^2 R_a + V W_c = 0 \\
&V^2 I_{sh} - 2 I_a^2 R_a V - 2 I_a R_a V I_{sh} + V I_a^2 R_a + V W_c = 0 \\
&V^2 I_{sh} - I_a^2 R_a V - 2 I_a R_a V I_{sh} + V W_c = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Approximation for variable load ($I_a \gg I_{sh}$):**
In normal load ranges, the armature current dominates the shunt current. Discard the $2 I_a R_a V I_{sh}$ term relative to $V^2 I_{sh}$ and $V I_a^2 R_a$:

$$
V^2 I_{sh} - V I_a^2 R_a + V W_c \approx 0
$$

Dividing by $V$:

$$
V I_{sh} - I_a^2 R_a + W_c = 0
$$

Recognizing that $V I_{sh} = I_{sh}^2 R_{sh}$ (field copper loss) is part of $W_c$, and denoting total constant losses as $W_c$:

$$
I_a^2 R_a = W_c
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Variable Loss} = \text{Constant Loss} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; I_{a,\max\,\eta} = \sqrt{\frac{W_c}{R_a}}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation Insight:** The maximum efficiency point is reached when the *load is exactly such* that the variable $I^2R$ loss equals the constant iron + friction + field loss. Operating below this load is *under-utilization*; above it, copper loss dominates and $\eta$ drops.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example (KTU 2024 Pattern)

**Problem:** A 4-pole, 250 V DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of $R_a = 0.2 \; \Omega$ and shunt field resistance $R_{sh} = 250 \; \Omega$. It draws a total line current of $I_L = 50$ A. The armature is lap-wound with 500 conductors. The flux per pole is $\Phi = 20$ mWb. Calculate:
(i) Back EMF, (ii) Speed, (iii) Torque developed, (iv) Armature copper loss, (v) Efficiency (neglecting rotational losses).

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Find $I_{sh}$ and $I_a$:**

$$
I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{250} = 1 \text{ A}
$$

$$
I_a = I_L - I_{sh} = 50 - 1 = 49 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 2 — Back EMF (using $V = E_b + I_a R_a$):**

$$
E_b = V - I_a R_a = 250 - 49 \times 0.2 = 250 - 9.8 = 240.2 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 3 — Speed from EMF equation (lap: $A = P = 4$):**

$$
E_b = \frac{\Phi N Z}{60} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; N = \frac{E_b \times 60}{\Phi Z} = \frac{240.2 \times 60}{0.020 \times 500}
$$

$$
N = \frac{14412}{10} = 1441.2 \text{ RPM}
$$

**Step 4 — Torque developed:**

$$
T = \frac{P_d}{\omega} = \frac{E_b I_a}{2 \pi N / 60} = \frac{240.2 \times 49 \times 60}{2 \pi \times 1441.2}
$$

$$
T = \frac{706,188}{9053.85} = 78.0 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 5 — Armature copper loss:**

$$
P_{cu,a} = I_a^2 R_a = 49^2 \times 0.2 = 2401 \times 0.2 = 480.2 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 6 — Field copper loss:**

$$
P_{cu,sh} = V I_{sh} = 250 \times 1 = 250 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 7 — Total loss and efficiency:**

$$
P_{\text{loss}} = 480.2 + 250 = 730.2 \text{ W}
$$

$$
P_{\text{in}} = V I_L = 250 \times 50 = 12{,}500 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\eta = 1 - \frac{730.2}{12{,}500} = 1 - 0.0584 = 0.9416 = 94.16 \%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** A shunt motor operating near full load typically yields 85–95% efficiency. Our result of 94.16% is realistic because we neglected rotational losses; including them would reduce $\eta$ by 2–3%, consistent with industrial DC motors.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    nodeB["FIELD COPPER LOSS IshSq x Rsh"]

    nodeD["MECHANICAL POWER DEVELOPED Pd equals Eb x Ia"]

    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeD

    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Constructional Components of a DC Machine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["YOKE Outer Frame Magnetic Return Path"]
    nodeB["POLE CORE and SHOES Conducts Flux to Armature"]
    nodeC["FIELD WINDING Excited by DC Source"]
    nodeD["ARMATURE CORE Slotted Laminated Silicon Steel"]
    nodeE["ARMATURE WINDING Hosts Conductors in Slots"]
    nodeF["COMMUTATOR Cylindrical Copper Segments"]
    nodeG["BRUSHES Carbon Blocks Sliding Contact"]

    nodeB --- nodeC

```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["TOTAL LOSSES IN A DC MACHINE"]
    nodeB["CONSTANT LOSSES Independent of Load"]
    nodeC["VARIABLE LOSSES Load Dependent"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC

    nodeB --> nodeD["Field Copper Loss IshSq Rsh"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["Iron Hysteresis Loss Ph"]
    nodeB --> nodeF["Iron Eddy Current Loss Pe"]
    nodeB --> nodeG["Friction Loss Pfr"]
    nodeB --> nodeH["Windage Loss Pw"]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Efficiency vs. Load Characteristic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["NO LOAD Zero Output"]
    nodeB["RISING REGION Variable Loss less than Constant"]
    nodeC["MAX EFFICIENCY Variable Loss equals Constant"]
    nodeD["FALLING REGION Variable Loss greater than Constant"]
    nodeE["FULL LOAD High Losses Reduced Output Ratio"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note for KTU 2024:** The shape of $\eta$ vs. output power is *not* a simple monotonic curve. It rises from zero, reaches a peak where $W_v = W_c$, and then decreases. Students must understand this behaviour, as the question *"Why is the efficiency curve bell-shaped?"* is a frequently asked 3-mark question.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part A contains 5 questions of 3 marks each, with CO mapping to CO2/CO3. Two representative questions are given below.

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the EMF equation of a DC generator. Explain the significance of each term in the equation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

$$
E_b = \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60 \, A} \;\; \text{Volts}
$$

- $P$ = Number of poles — controls flux distribution.
- $\Phi$ = Flux per pole in Webers — set by field current.
- $N$ = Armature speed in RPM — supplied by prime mover.
- $Z$ = Total number of armature conductors.
- $A$ = Number of parallel paths ($A = 2$ for wave, $A = P$ for lap).

**Significance:** $E_b$ is directly proportional to $\Phi$ and $N$, which is the basis for **voltage regulation** in generators and **speed control** in motors.

*(Marks: Equation 1 + Significance of each of 5 terms at 0.4 each = 3)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between constant losses and variable losses in a DC machine. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Constant Losses | Variable Losses |
|---|---|---|
| Dependence | Independent of load | Depend on load current |
| Cause | Rotational & fixed field effects | Current-dependent $I^2R$ heating |
| Example 1 | Iron (core) loss — hysteresis | Armature copper loss — $I_a^2 R_a$ |
| Example 2 | Friction & windage loss | Brush contact loss — $V_b I_a$ |

**Key Insight:** At the **maximum efficiency** point, the sum of constant losses equals the sum of variable losses, i.e., $W_c = W_v$.

*(Marks: Tabular distinction 1.5 + Examples 1 + Key insight 0.5 = 3)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module carries a 14-mark question with internal choice (a) OR (b). Sub-parts (i) and (ii) carry 7 marks each. Detailed model solutions are provided.

---

### 📌 Question A — Option Set 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEEBT411]**

**(a)** *Derive the EMF equation of a DC generator starting from Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Statement of Faraday's Law:**
The average EMF induced in a conductor is equal to the rate of change of flux linkage:

$$
e = -\frac{d\Phi}{dt} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; e_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\Delta \Phi}{\Delta t}
$$

**Step 2 — Flux cut per revolution:**
Each conductor cuts $P$ poles worth of flux per revolution, so $\Delta \Phi = P \Phi$ per revolution.

**Step 3 — Time per revolution:**
For speed $N$ RPM, time per revolution is $\Delta t = \tfrac{60}{N}$ seconds.

**Step 4 — EMF per conductor:**

$$
e_{\text{avg}} = \frac{P \Phi}{60/N} = \frac{P \Phi N}{60} \;\; \text{V}
$$

**Step 5 — Number of conductors in series per path:**
Total conductors $Z$ are split into $A$ parallel paths. Conductors in series per path = $\tfrac{Z}{A}$.

**Step 6 — Total EMF (sum in series):**

$$
E_b = \frac{P \Phi N}{60} \times \frac{Z}{A} = \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60 \, A} \;\;\text{Q.E.D.}
$$

*(Marks: Faraday's law 1 + Steps 2–6 at 1 mark each + Final boxed equation with lap/wave special cases 1 = 7)*

---

**(b)** *A 4-pole, 250 V DC shunt generator has $R_a = 0.1 \; \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 125 \; \Omega$, and delivers a load current of 100 A. The armature is lap-wound with 600 conductors, and the flux per pole is $\Phi = 25$ mWb. Calculate the speed of the prime mover and the efficiency (neglecting rotational losses).* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Field and armature currents:**

$$
I_{sh} = \frac{250}{125} = 2 \text{ A}, \qquad I_a = I_L + I_{sh} = 100 + 2 = 102 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 2 — Generated EMF (generator mode):**

$$
E_b = V + I_a R_a = 250 + 102 \times 0.1 = 250 + 10.2 = 260.2 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 3 — Speed from EMF equation (lap: $A = P = 4$):**

$$
N = \frac{E_b \times 60 \, A}{P \Phi Z} = \frac{260.2 \times 60 \times 4}{4 \times 0.025 \times 600}
$$

$$
N = \frac{62{,}448}{60} = 1040.8 \text{ RPM}
$$

**Step 4 — Total losses:**

$$
P_{cu,a} = I_a^2 R_a = 102^2 \times 0.1 = 1040.4 \text{ W}
$$

$$
P_{cu,sh} = V I_{sh} = 250 \times 2 = 500 \text{ W}
$$

$$
P_{\text{loss}} = 1040.4 + 500 = 1540.4 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 5 — Output, input, and efficiency:**

$$
P_{\text{out}} = V I_L = 250 \times 100 = 25{,}000 \text{ W}
$$

$$
P_{\text{in}} = P_{\text{out}} + P_{\text{loss}} = 25{,}000 + 1540.4 = 26{,}540.4 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{25{,}000}{26{,}540.4} \times 100 = 94.20 \%
$$

*(Marks: Currents 1.5, EMF 1, Speed 1.5, Losses 1.5, Efficiency 1.5 = 7)*

---

### 📌 Question B — Option Set 2 (14 Marks)

**(a)** *With a neat power-flow diagram, explain the various losses occurring in a DC machine. State the condition for maximum efficiency.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Power Flow Diagram:**

| Stage | Quantity | Expression |
|---|---|---|
| Input (motor) | $P_{\text{in}}$ | $V I_L$ |
| Minus Field Cu | $P_{cu,sh}$ | $I_{sh}^2 R_{sh}$ |
| Minus Armature Cu | $P_{cu,a}$ | $I_a^2 R_a$ |
| Equals Power Developed | $P_d$ | $E_b I_a$ |
| Minus Iron + Mechanical | $P_i + P_m$ | Rotational |
| Equals Output | $P_{\text{out}}$ | $T \omega$ |

**Step 2 — Classification of Losses:**

- **Copper Losses** (Variable, except field): $I_a^2 R_a + I_{sh}^2 R_{sh}$
- **Iron Losses** (Constant): Hysteresis $P_h \propto f B_{max}^{1.6}$, Eddy current $P_e \propto f^2 B_{max}^2 t^2$
- **Mechanical Losses** (Constant): Friction at bearings + brush friction; Windage (air friction)
- **Brush Contact Loss** (Variable): $V_b I_a$, where $V_b \approx 1$ V per brush pair
- **Stray Load Loss** (Variable): $P_{\text{stray}} \approx 1\%$ of output

**Step 3 — Condition for Maximum Efficiency:**

$$
\boxed{W_v = W_c \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; I_a^2 R_a = W_c \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; I_{a,\max\,\eta} = \sqrt{\frac{W_c}{R_a}}}
$$

*(Marks: Diagram 2 + Classification table 3 + Condition 2 = 7)*

---

**(b)** *A 220 V DC shunt motor has $R_a = 0.5 \; \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 110 \; \Omega$, and takes 5 A at no load. The constant losses (iron + friction + windage) are estimated as the no-load input power minus armature copper loss. On full load, the motor draws 30 A. Find the full-load efficiency and the load at which the efficiency is maximum.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — No-load calculation:**
At no load, $I_{L0} = 5$ A.

$$
I_{sh} = \frac{220}{110} = 2 \text{ A}, \qquad I_{a0} = 5 - 2 = 3 \text{ A}
$$

No-load armature Cu loss:

$$
P_{cu,a0} = 3^2 \times 0.5 = 4.5 \text{ W}
$$

Total no-load input:

$$
P_{\text{in0}} = 220 \times 5 = 1100 \text{ W}
$$

Constant losses (using no-load as proxy):

$$
W_c = P_{\text{in0}} - P_{cu,a0} - P_{cu,sh} = 1100 - 4.5 - 220 \times 2 = 1100 - 4.5 - 440 = 655.5 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 2 — Full-load calculation:**
At full load, $I_L = 30$ A $\Rightarrow$ $I_a = 30 - 2 = 28$ A.

$$
P_{cu,a} = 28^2 \times 0.5 = 784 \times 0.5 = 392 \text{ W}
$$

$$
P_{cu,sh} = 220 \times 2 = 440 \text{ W}
$$

Total losses:

$$
P_{\text{loss}} = 392 + 440 + 655.5 = 1487.5 \text{ W}
$$

Full-load input and output:

$$
P_{\text{in}} = 220 \times 30 = 6600 \text{ W}, \qquad P_{\text{out}} = 6600 - 1487.5 = 5112.5 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\eta_{\text{FL}} = \frac{5112.5}{6600} \times 100 = 77.46 \%
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum efficiency point:**

$$
I_{a,\max\,\eta} = \sqrt{\frac{W_c}{R_a}} = \sqrt{\frac{655.5}{0.5}} = \sqrt{1311} \approx 36.2 \text{ A}
$$

Note: This is *above* the full-load current of 28 A, indicating the machine is **not** operating at peak efficiency at full load. The efficiency will continue to *rise* as load increases from no-load toward 36.2 A armature current.

**Step 4 — Output at max efficiency:**

$$
I_L = 36.2 + 2 = 38.2 \text{ A}
$$

$$
P_{\text{out,max}\,\eta} = V I_L - 2 W_c = 220 \times 38.2 - 2 \times 655.5 = 8404 - 1311 = 7093 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\eta_{\max} = \frac{7093}{8404} \times 100 = 84.40 \%
$$

*(Marks: No-load 1.5 + Full-load losses 1.5 + Efficiency 1.5 + Max efficiency derivation and computation 2.5 = 7)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing motor and generator EMF equations:** In *motor* mode, $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ (EMF opposes supply); in *generator* mode, $V = E_b - I_a R_a$ (EMF supplies the terminal voltage). Mixing these up is the **#1 cause of wrong numerical answers**.
> 2. **Wrong parallel path selection:** For **lap winding**, $A = P$ (e.g., 4-pole lap $\Rightarrow$ $A = 4$); for **wave winding**, $A = 2$ (always). Many students default to $A = 2$, costing 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Adding field copper loss twice:** When computing total losses, students often include $I_{sh}^2 R_{sh}$ both as a "constant loss" *and* as a separate line. It should appear **once**.
> 4. **Stating the max efficiency condition incorrectly:** Writing "$P_{\text{out}} = P_{\text{loss}}$" or "$\eta_{\max} = 50\%$" is **wrong**. The condition is $W_c = W_v$, *not* $P_{\text{out}} = P_{\text{loss}}$. The efficiency *value* at the peak depends on the load and is not 50%.
> 5. **Forgetting to convert N to rad/s for torque:** The formula $T = E_b I_a / \omega$ requires $\omega$ in rad/s, *not* RPM. A common slip-up is to substitute $N$ directly into $T = E_b I_a / N$.
> 6. **Units:** Flux must be in **Webers** (not milliwebers) when plugging into the EMF equation. Convert $\Phi = 25$ mWb to $0.025$ Wb *before* computation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Basic Principles, Losses, and Efficiency**

- ✅ **DC machine = reversible energy converter.** Same machine, governed by **Faraday's Law** (generator) and **Lorentz Force** (motor).
- ✅ **Commutator is the key distinguishing component** — it rectifies internal AC to external DC (generator) or inverts external DC to internal AC (motor).
- ✅ **EMF Equation:** $E_b = \tfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A}$. Memorize boundary cases: **lap** ($A = P$), **wave** ($A = 2$).
- ✅ **Torque Equation:** $T = \tfrac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A}$. Alternatively, $T = k_a \Phi I_a$ where $k_a = \tfrac{PZ}{2\pi A}$.
- ✅ **Motor voltage equation:** $V = E_b + I_a R_a$. **Generator voltage equation:** $V = E_b - I_a R_a$.
- ✅ **Five loss categories:** (1) Armature Cu, (2) Field Cu, (3) Iron (hysteresis + eddy), (4) Mechanical (friction + windage), (5) Brush + Stray.
- ✅ **Constant losses:** Field Cu, Iron, Friction, Windage. **Variable losses:** Armature Cu, Brush, Stray.
- ✅ **Power stages:** $P_{\text{in}} \to P_{\text{field loss}} \to P_{\text{arm loss}} \to P_d \to P_{\text{rotational}} \to P_{\text{out}}$.
- ✅ **Maximum efficiency condition:** Variable loss = Constant loss $\Rightarrow I_a = \sqrt{W_c / R_a}$.
- ✅ **Efficiency curve is bell-shaped**, not monotonic — peaks where $W_c = W_v$.
- ✅ **Steinmetz Law** for hysteresis: $P_h = k_h f B_{max}^{1.6} V_{\text{core}}$.
- ✅ **Eddy current loss:** $P_e = k_e f^2 B_{max}^2 t^2$ — reduced by **lamination** of the armature core.
- ✅ **Biomedical application mapping:** Syringe pumps (low-speed high-torque DC motors), defibrillators (DC capacitor charging via DC-DC converters), ventilators (continuous-duty shunt motors), MRI gradient amplifiers (linear torque control), and ECG recorders (precision low-noise motor drives).
- ✅ **Always convert** flux from mWb to Wb, speed from RPM to rad/s, and ensure $A$ matches the winding type.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torque Equation and Starting Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT411 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY FOR BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Torque Equation and Starting Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Three-Phase Induction Motor — Torque Equation

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Torque in a three-phase induction motor** is the rotational mechanical force developed by the motor at the shaft. Mathematically, it is defined as the ratio of gross mechanical power developed (developed power, $P_d$) by the motor to the angular velocity of the rotor ($\omega_r$).
>
> $$T = \frac{P_d}{\omega_r} = \frac{P_d}{2\pi N_r/60} = \frac{60 \cdot P_d}{2\pi N_r} \;\; [N\cdot m]$$

Where $N_r$ is the rotor speed in **RPM** and $P_d$ is in **Watts**.

The complete **KTU 2024 Standard Torque Equation** for a three-phase induction motor is given by:

> $$T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s \cdot E_2^2 \cdot R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$$

Where every term has a precise meaning as listed in the cheat sheet (Section 2).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (Real-World):** Imagine pushing a heavy merry-go-round in a playground. The harder you push (analogous to **voltage**), and the faster your hands move in a circle (analogous to **synchronous speed**), the more torque you can transfer. But the moment the merry-go-round starts moving at almost your hand-speed, you can no longer push it effectively — your "grip" on it reduces. This drop in push-ability mirrors the **rotor slip ($s$)** in an induction motor. **Slip is the difference between the rotating magnetic field and the actual rotor speed — it is the very "handle" by which the motor grips electrical energy and converts it to mechanical torque.**

If we want a **biomedical analogy**: think of an induction motor driving a **centrifuge** used to separate blood components. The motor must produce enough starting torque to overcome the inertia of the rotor + blood-filled tubes, but once at speed, it must hold a steady torque to keep the separation process going. The **torque-slip characteristic curve** is exactly the engineering "fingerprint" that biomedical engineers use to size such drives (e.g., in **dialysis machines, MRI cooling pumps, infusion pump rollers, and surgical suction blowers**).

---

### 1.3 Why Do We Need "Starting Methods"?

A three-phase induction motor at standstill ($s = 1$) draws a very high current — typically **5 to 7 times** the full-load current — because the rotor is stationary and there is no back-EMF. This causes:
- A large voltage drop in the supply, disturbing other connected loads (e.g., life-support equipment in a hospital).
- High mechanical stress on windings and couplings.
- Tripping of protective relays.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Hence, **starting methods** are essential to limit the inrush current to a safe value (typically 1.5 to 2.5 times full-load current) while still delivering adequate starting torque to bring the biomedical load (e.g., a CT scanner gantry motor) up to operating speed.

**Standard metrics highlighted in this module:**

- **Synchronous speed** $N_s = \frac{120 f}{P}$ where $f$ is supply frequency in **Hz**, $P$ is the number of poles.
- **Slip** $s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$, expressed as a **per-unit** (decimal) value.
- **Full-load slip** for standard industrial motors ≈ **0.02 to 0.06** (i.e., 2–6 %).
- **Rotor input impedance at slip $s$** $Z_2 = R_2/s + jX_2$.
- **Starting current ratio** for DOL: **5 to 7 × FLC**; for Star-Delta: **1/3 × DOL starting current** ≈ 1.7 to 2.3 × FLC.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Where Does the Torque Equation Come From?

The torque equation of a three-phase induction motor is derived from the **rotor equivalent circuit**. Conceptually, the steps are:

1. **Rotor EMF per phase** $E_2$ is induced by the relative motion between the rotating flux and the rotor conductors. At standstill, $E_2$ is maximum; as the rotor accelerates towards $N_s$, the relative speed falls, so the **frequency of rotor EMF** $f_2 = s \cdot f$ also falls.
2. **Rotor circuit at slip $s$** behaves like a transformer secondary with frequency $sf$ and an effective resistance $R_2/s$. The rotor leakage reactance becomes $X_2 = 2\pi(sf)L_2 = s \cdot X_{2(\text{standstill})}$.
3. **Rotor current per phase** is therefore:
   $$I_2 = \frac{s E_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}}$$
4. **Rotor copper loss per phase** is $I_2^2 R_2$, and the power transferred across the air-gap to the rotor (gross mechanical power developed) equals the **air-gap power** minus rotor copper loss.
5. **Gross mechanical power developed** $P_d = \frac{3 I_2^2 R_2}{s}$ — this is the elegant KTU formula that bridges electrical input to mechanical output.
6. **Torque** $T = \dfrac{P_d}{\omega_s}$ (using synchronous speed for developed torque) because $P_d$ is the power converted at the air-gap, and the rotating field is the reference frame.

### 2.2 Physical Interpretation of Each Term

| Symbol | Meaning | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $E_2$ | Rotor EMF per phase at standstill (standstill rotor EMF) | V |
| $R_2$ | Rotor resistance per phase (DC measurement) | Ω |
| $X_2$ | Rotor leakage reactance per phase at standstill (i.e., at $s = 1$) | Ω |
| $s$ | Per-unit slip ($s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$) | dimensionless |
| $\omega_s$ | Synchronous angular velocity in rad/s = $2\pi N_s/60$ | rad/s |
| $N_s$ | Synchronous speed | RPM |
| $f$ | Supply frequency | **Hz (50 Hz in India)** |
| $P$ | Number of poles | even integer |
| $T$ | Electromagnetic torque developed | N·m |
| $P_d$ | Gross mechanical (developed) power | W |
| $P_g$ | Air-gap power | W |
| $P_{cu,2}$ | Rotor copper loss | W |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet (with Units)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Formula Cheat Sheet — Torque Equation and Starting Methods**

| # | Formula | Description / Condition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed in RPM |
| 2 | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Per-unit slip |
| 3 | $f_2 = s \cdot f$ | Rotor frequency in **Hz** |
| 4 | $I_2 = \dfrac{s E_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}}$ | Rotor current per phase |
| 5 | $T = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$ | **Master Torque Equation** |
| 6 | $T_{st}$ (at $s=1$) $= \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2}$ | Starting torque |
| 7 | $s_{mT} = \dfrac{R_2}{X_2}$ | Slip at maximum torque |
| 8 | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3 E_2^2}{2 \omega_s X_2}$ | Maximum (pull-out) torque — **independent of $R_2$** |
| 9 | $T_{fl} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{s_{fl} E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (s_{fl} X_2)^2}$ | Full-load torque |
| 10 | $\dfrac{T}{T_{max}} = \dfrac{2}{\dfrac{s}{s_{mT}} + \dfrac{s_{mT}}{s}}$ | **Normalized torque equation** (Goodwin's form) |
| 11 | $\dfrac{T_{st}}{T_{max}} = \dfrac{2 \alpha}{\alpha^2 + 1}$ where $\alpha = R_2/X_2$ | Ratio of starting to max torque |
| 12 | $I_{st, \text{star}} = \dfrac{1}{3} I_{st, \text{delta}}$ | Star-Delta starting current reduction |
| 13 | $T_{st, \text{star}} = \dfrac{1}{3} T_{st, \text{delta}}$ | Star-Delta starting torque reduction |
| 14 | $I_{st, \text{auto}} = k^2 \cdot I_{st, \text{DOL}}$ | Auto-transformer starting current ($k$ = tapping ratio) |
| 15 | $T_{st, \text{auto}} = k^2 \cdot T_{st, \text{DOL}}$ | Auto-transformer starting torque |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Biomedical Engineering

- **MRI gradient coils & cryocooler pumps** use induction motors sized using the torque equation to ensure stable torque at low slip.
- **Centrifuges** in pathology labs (blood component separation) need high starting torque to overcome inertial load — direct-on-line (DOL) or auto-transformer starting is used.
- **Hospital HVAC chillers & ventilation blowers** use star-delta starters to reduce inrush current so that sensitive diagnostic equipment (ECG/EEG monitors) is not disturbed.
- **Dialysis machine water pumps** use soft-starters with controlled voltage ramp to prevent water-hammer in thin medical tubing.
- **Autoclave compressors** use rotor-resistance starting (wound-rotor / slip-ring motors) to provide high starting torque for compression work.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of the Torque Equation (KTU Board Style)

We start from the per-phase **rotor equivalent circuit referred to the stator** with the slip-dependent resistance model.

### Step 1: Rotor Current per Phase

The rotor EMF at slip $s$ is $sE_2$ (because rotor frequency is $sf$, so EMF scales linearly with $s$). The rotor impedance at slip $s$ is:

$$Z_2 = R_2 + j(sX_2)$$

Therefore the rotor current magnitude is:

$$I_2 = \frac{s E_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}}$$

*Logic:* $E_2$ is the standstill rotor EMF; at slip $s$ it scales by factor $s$ because the relative speed between flux and rotor is $sN_s$.

### Step 2: Air-Gap Power (Power Input to Rotor) per Phase

$$P_{g, \text{phase}} = I_2^2 \cdot \frac{R_2}{s}$$

*Logic:* The total power dissipated in the rotor circuit is $I_2^2$ times the total resistance $R_2/s$. The portion $I_2^2 R_2$ is the rotor copper loss, and the remaining is the **gross mechanical power developed**.

### Step 3: Total Air-Gap Power (3-phase)

For a balanced 3-phase motor, total air-gap power:

$$P_g = 3 I_2^2 \cdot \frac{R_2}{s}$$

### Step 4: Gross Mechanical Power Developed

The rotor copper loss (3-phase) is $P_{cu,2} = 3 I_2^2 R_2$. The mechanical power developed is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_d &= P_g - P_{cu,2} \\
&= 3 I_2^2 \cdot \frac{R_2}{s} - 3 I_2^2 R_2 \\
&= 3 I_2^2 R_2 \left( \frac{1}{s} - 1 \right) \\
&= 3 I_2^2 R_2 \cdot \frac{1 - s}{s} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

But there is a more elegant, direct form:

$$P_d = \frac{3 I_2^2 R_2}{s} \cdot (1 - s) = (1 - s) P_g$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** $P_d = (1 - s) P_g$. This means the **fractional mechanical power** is $(1 - s)$ of the air-gap power, while the **fractional rotor copper loss** is $s$ of the air-gap power. At $s = 0.04$ (4 % slip), **96 %** of air-gap power becomes mechanical power — that is why induction motors are highly efficient.

Substituting the expression for $I_2$ from Step 1:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_d &= 3 \cdot \frac{(sE_2)^2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2} \cdot \frac{R_2}{s} \\
&= 3 \cdot \frac{s^2 E_2^2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2} \cdot \frac{R_2}{s} \\
&= \frac{3 s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Develop the Torque Equation

Electromagnetic torque (developed torque) is mechanical power divided by the **synchronous angular speed** $\omega_s = 2\pi N_s / 60$. (We use $\omega_s$ because $P_d$ is the power developed at the air-gap, referenced to the rotating field frame.)

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{P_d}{\omega_s} \\
&= \frac{1}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{3 s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **canonical KTU Torque Equation**. Memorize this; it is worth 5 to 7 marks in any KTU 2024 board exam on this module.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Slip at Maximum Torque

We need to find the value of $s$ that maximizes $T$. Since $T \propto \dfrac{s R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$ (with $3E_2^2/\omega_s$ being a constant multiplier for fixed $V$ and $f$), we can define:

$$f(s) = \frac{s R_2}{R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2}$$

Differentiate $f(s)$ with respect to $s$ and set $\dfrac{df}{ds} = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{df}{ds} &= \frac{(R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2) \cdot R_2 - sR_2 \cdot (2sX_2^2)}{(R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2)^2} = 0 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

The numerator must vanish:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_2(R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2) - 2 s^2 R_2 X_2^2 &= 0 \\
R_2^3 + s^2 R_2 X_2^2 - 2 s^2 R_2 X_2^2 &= 0 \\
R_2^3 - s^2 R_2 X_2^2 &= 0 \\
R_2^2 &= s^2 X_2^2 \\
s^2 &= \frac{R_2^2}{X_2^2} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,s_{mT} = \frac{R_2}{X_2}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The slip at which **maximum torque occurs** depends on the **rotor resistance** $R_2$. By adding external resistance to the rotor circuit (in slip-ring / wound-rotor motors), we can shift $s_{mT}$ to **unity** ($s_{mT} = 1$, i.e., at starting), which is the entire basis of **rotor-resistance starting**.

### Substituting $s_{mT}$ back into the torque equation to find $T_{max}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{max} &= \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s_{mT} E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (s_{mT} X_2)^2} \\
&= \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{(R_2/X_2) E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + R_2^2} \\
&= \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_2^2 R_2^2 / X_2}{2 R_2^2} \\
&= \frac{3 E_2^2}{2 \omega_s X_2} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T_{max} = \frac{3 E_2^2}{2 \omega_s X_2} \quad \text{(independent of } R_2\text{)}\,}
$$

This is a remarkable result: **maximum torque is unaffected by rotor resistance**, but the **slip at which it occurs** is directly controlled by it.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Goodwin's Normalized Form

Starting from $T = \dfrac{3 s E_2^2 R_2 / \omega_s}{R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2}$ and dividing numerator and denominator by $R_2^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{3 E_2^2}{\omega_s R_2} \cdot \frac{s/R_2}{1 + (s X_2 / R_2)^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Let $a = s R_2 / X_2$. Then $s = a X_2 / R_2$ and $s / R_2 = a X_2 / R_2^2$. Substituting and recognizing that $T_{max} = 3E_2^2/(2 \omega_s X_2)$:

$$
\frac{T}{T_{max}} = \frac{2 a}{1 + a^2} = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{a} + a} = \frac{2}{\frac{s_{mT}}{s} + \frac{s}{s_{mT}}}
$$

This is the **Goodwin's form** — extremely useful in board problems where the operating slip and the slip-at-max-torque are both given, and torque ratio is required.

---

## 3.4 Starting Methods — Detailed Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board 14-Mark Standard:** You are expected to explain **at least three** of the following starting methods with neat circuit diagrams, governing equations, advantages, and limitations.

### Method 1: Direct-On-Line (DOL) Starter

- **Principle:** The motor is connected directly across the full line voltage via a contactor.
- **Current drawn:** $I_{st} = 5$ to $7 \times I_{FL}$.
- **Starting torque:** **100 %** of the rated DOL starting torque (typically $1.5$ to $2 \times T_{FL}$).
- **Use case:** Small motors (≤ 5 kW in industrial norms; ≤ 7.5 kW in Indian practice). For biomedical applications, small bench-top stirrers, low-power centrifuge rotors.
- **Disadvantage:** Huge inrush current can disturb hospital power lines and other sensitive biomedical instruments.

### Method 2: Star-Delta Starter

- **Principle:** Motor is first connected in **star** (line voltage reduced to $V_L/\sqrt{3}$ per phase). After acceleration to ~75–80 % of $N_s$, it is switched to **delta** (full voltage).
- **Phase voltage in star:** $V_{ph, Y} = V_L / \sqrt{3}$. Therefore phase current in star = $1/\sqrt{3}$ of that in delta. Line current in star = $1/3$ of that in delta.
- **Current and torque ratios:**

$$I_{st, Y} = \frac{1}{3} I_{st, \Delta} \quad ; \quad T_{st, Y} = \frac{1}{3} T_{st, \Delta}$$

- **Use case:** Medium-sized motors (up to ~20 kW). Common in HVAC, water pumps, ventilation blowers in hospitals.
- **Limitation:** Starting torque becomes only ~33 % of DOL starting torque. **Not suitable** for high-inertia loads like heavy centrifuge rotors or MRI cryocooler compressors that demand high starting torque.

### Method 3: Auto-Transformer Starter

- **Principle:** A tapped autotransformer reduces the voltage applied to the motor during starting. After acceleration, the motor is connected directly to the line.
- **Tapping ratio** $k = V_{\text{tap}} / V_L$ (typically 0.5, 0.65, 0.8).
- **Current and torque relations:**

$$I_{st, \text{auto}} = k^2 \cdot I_{st, \text{DOL}}$$

$$T_{st, \text{auto}} = k^2 \cdot T_{st, \text{DOL}}$$

- **Why $k^2$?**
  - Voltage at motor terminals is $k V_L$, so motor draws current $k I_{st, \text{DOL}}$ on its side.
  - But this current is reflected to the line by the autotransformer ratio, giving a line current of $k \cdot (k I_{st, \text{DOL}}) = k^2 I_{st, \text{DOL}}$.
- **Use case:** Large motors (5 kW to several hundred kW) requiring **higher starting torque than star-delta** can offer. Used in MRI chillers, large hospital air-handling units.
- **Advantage over Star-Delta:** Tap selection allows **tuning of starting torque to load requirement**.

### Method 4: Rotor Resistance Starting (Slip-Ring / Wound-Rotor IM)

- **Principle:** External resistors are inserted in the rotor circuit via slip rings during starting. As the motor accelerates, the resistors are **progressively cut out** until the rotor is short-circuited.
- **Effect on torque-slip curve:** Increasing $R_2$ shifts $s_{mT} = R_2/X_2$ to the right. By choosing $R_2$ such that $s_{mT} = 1$, the motor develops **maximum torque at starting**.
- **Current and torque characteristics:** High starting torque with **low starting current** — ideal combination.
- **Use case:** High-inertia loads, cranes, lifts, large centrifuges, flywheel energy storage systems, hoist motors in hospital material-handling systems.
- **Disadvantage:** Slip rings, brushes, and resistor banks require maintenance; higher cost.

### Method 5: Soft Starter (Modern Electronic Method)

- **Principle:** Solid-state thyristor (SCR) pairs in each phase phase-angle-control the voltage applied to the motor, ramping it up from a low initial value to full voltage over a few seconds.
- **Advantages:** Smooth acceleration, programmable current limit, no contactor switching transients, integrated motor protection.
- **Use case:** Modern hospital installations — dialysis pumps, pharmaceutical clean-room fans, sterile fluid transfer pumps.
- **Drawback:** Harmonics injected into the supply; limited starting torque compared to rotor-resistance method.

---

## 3.5 Comparative Decision Matrix (KTU 14-Mark Comparison Table)

| Parameter | DOL | Star-Delta | Auto-Transformer | Rotor Resistance (Slip-Ring) | Soft Starter |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Starting current (×FLC) | 5–7 | 1.7–2.3 | $k^2 \times 5$ to $k^2 \times 7$ | 1.5–2.5 | 1.5–3 (adjustable) |
| Starting torque (×FLT) | 1.5–2 | 0.5–0.65 | $k^2 \times T_{st, DOL}$ | Up to $T_{max}$ (controllable) | Adjustable |
| Cost | Lowest | Low | Medium | High | High |
| Maintenance | Low | Low | Low | High (brushes) | Low |
| Smoothness | Poor (jerk) | Step (Y→Δ transient) | Step (tap change) | Smooth (multi-step) | Very smooth |
| Motor type | Cage | Cage | Cage | Wound/Slip-ring | Cage |
| Typical size | ≤ 5 kW | ≤ 20 kW | Up to MW | 5 kW – MW | Any size |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Torque–Slip Characteristic Curve (Block + Conceptual Map)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `A`, `B`, `C`, `region1`), labels are in clean uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting inside quotes.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["THREE PHASE INDUCTION MOTOR"] --> B["STATOR ROTATING MAGNETIC FIELD NS"]
    B --> C["ROTOR AT SPEED NR WITH SLIP S"]
    C --> D["ROTOR EMF E2 INDUCED AT FREQUENCY SF"]
    D --> E["ROTOR CURRENT I2 = SE2 / SQRT R2SQUARED + SX2 SQUARED"]
    E --> F["AIR GAP POWER PG = 3 I2SQUARED R2 BY S"]
    F --> G["MECHANICAL POWER PD = 1 MINUS S TIMES PG"]
    G --> H["DEVELOPED TORQUE T EQUALS PD BY OMEGA S"]
    H --> I["TORQUE SLIP CHARACTERISTIC CURVE"]
    I --> J["REGION 1 LOW SLIP NORMAL OPERATION"]
    I --> K["REGION 2 SLIP EQUALS SMT MAXIMUM TORQUE"]
    I --> L["REGION 3 HIGH SLIP STARTING CONDITION"]
    J --> M["CONSTANT POWER REGION FOR BIOMEDICAL LOADS"]
    K --> N["PULL OUT LIMIT PROTECT FROM STALL"]
    L --> O["STARTING TORQUE TAT DETERMINES ACCELERATION"]
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Starting Method Selection Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["MOTOR START REQUEST"] --> B["ASSESS LOAD INERTIA AND TORQUE DEMAND"]
    B --> C{"MOTOR RATING BELOW 5 KW"}
    C -->|"YES"| D["DOL STARTER"]
    C -->|"NO"| E{"HIGH STARTING TORQUE REQUIRED"}
    E -->|"NO"| F["STAR DELTA STARTER"]
    E -->|"YES"| G{"WOUND ROTOR SLIP RING MOTOR AVAILABLE"}
    G -->|"YES"| H["ROTOR RESISTANCE STARTER"]
    G -->|"NO"| I["AUTO TRANSFORMER STARTER WITH TAP K"]
    I --> J["MATCH K TO LOAD REQUIREMENT"]
    H --> K["SMOOTH ACCELERATION WITH EXTERNAL RHEOSTAT"]
    F --> L["Y TO DELTA SWITCH AT 80 PERCENT SPEED"]
    D --> M["BIOMEDICAL APPLICATION SMALL STIRRER OR PUMP"]
    L --> N["BIOMEDICAL APPLICATION HOSPITAL HVAC BLOWER"]
    H --> O["BIOMEDICAL APPLICATION CENTRIFUGE HIGH INERTIA"]
    I --> P["BIOMEDICAL APPLICATION MRI CHILLER COMPRESSOR"]
    J --> P
    K --> O
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Induction Motor Acceleration Sequence

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Standstill
    Standstill --> Accelerating: Apply Reduced Voltage
    Accelerating --> Accelerating: Cut Out Rotor Resistance Step
    Accelerating --> Running: Reach Operating Slip
    Running --> [*]
    Standstill: SLIP EQUALS 1
    Accelerating: SLIP IN RANGE 0.04 TO 1.0
    Running: SLIP IN RANGE 0.02 TO 0.06
```

> [!NOTE]
> The state diagram shows the **slip journey** from $s = 1$ (standstill) to $s \approx s_{fl}$ (running). The starting method chosen determines how "fast" or "smoothly" the system traverses from Standstill to Running. Rotor-resistance and soft-starter methods provide **multiple intermediate states** (resistance steps), while DOL provides only two states (off / on).

---

## 4.4 Text-Based Torque–Slip Curve Schematic (ASCII)

```
        T (N.m)
        ^
        |
  Tmax  |        *
        |       * *
        |      *   *
        |     *     *
        |    *       *
  T_st  |---*---------*-----     <-- starting torque at s=1
        |  *           *
        | *             *
        |*               *
  T_fl  +--*---------------*------>  s
        0  s_fl          s_mT  1
            (operating)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the curve:** The motor normally operates on the **stable region** between $s = 0$ and $s = s_{mT}$. Any load torque that exceeds $T_{max}$ causes **pull-out** (motor stalls). This is the operating principle behind **overload protection relays** in biomedical equipment motors (e.g., in MRI gradient amplifiers or centrifuge drives).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
State the torque equation of a three-phase induction motor and explain the significance of each term.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The torque developed by a three-phase induction motor is given by:

$$T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$$

- **$E_2$** → standstill rotor EMF per phase (V).
- **$R_2$** → rotor resistance per phase (Ω).
- **$X_2$** → standstill rotor leakage reactance per phase (Ω).
- **$s$** → per-unit slip, $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$.
- **$\omega_s$** → synchronous angular velocity in rad/s.

**Significance (1 mark):** The equation shows that torque is **zero at synchronous speed** ($s=0$) and **maximum at slip $s_{mT} = R_2/X_2$**. It is the design basis for sizing induction motors driving biomedical loads like centrifuges and pumps.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
What is meant by slip in an induction motor? Why is it impossible for the rotor to run at synchronous speed?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Slip $s$ is the relative difference between the synchronous speed of the rotating magnetic field ($N_s$) and the actual rotor speed ($N_r$):

$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$

It is expressed as a fraction (or per-unit). At standstill $s = 1$; at synchronous speed $s = 0$.

**Why rotor cannot reach $N_s$ (2 marks):**
- Torque is developed **only when there is relative motion** between the rotating field and the rotor conductors.
- This relative motion induces rotor EMF $E_2 \propto s$ and rotor current $I_2 \propto s$.
- If $s = 0$ (rotor at $N_s$), there is no relative motion → no induced EMF → no rotor current → **no torque** (from the torque equation, $T \to 0$ as $s \to 0$).
- Therefore, the rotor must always run at a speed **slightly less than $N_s$** to develop the torque needed to overcome load and friction.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Regulation:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students may answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the torque equation of a three-phase induction motor starting from the rotor equivalent circuit. State clearly the assumptions made. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz induction motor has a standstill rotor EMF per phase of 100 V. The rotor resistance and standstill reactance per phase are 0.1 Ω and 0.5 Ω respectively. Calculate: (i) the slip at maximum torque, (ii) the maximum torque, (iii) the starting torque. Take the ratio of stator to rotor turns as 1:1. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part A(a)

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Stator-to-rotor turns ratio is unity (or referred values used).
2. Rotor parameters $R_2$ and $X_2$ are **constant** (no skin effect, no saturation).
3. The motor operates in the **stable region** of the torque-slip curve.
4. The supply voltage and frequency are **constant**.
5. Magnetizing branch is moved to the stator terminals (approximate equivalent circuit).

**Derivation (6 marks):**
- Step 1: Rotor EMF at slip $s$ is $sE_2$.
- Step 2: Rotor impedance at slip $s$ is $Z_2 = R_2 + jsX_2$.
- Step 3: Rotor current $I_2 = sE_2 / \sqrt{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$.
- Step 4: Air-gap power $P_g = 3 I_2^2 R_2 / s$.
- Step 5: Mechanical power developed $P_d = (1-s) P_g = 3 I_2^2 R_2 (1-s)/s = 3 s E_2^2 R_2 / [R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2]$.
- Step 6: Torque $T = P_d / \omega_s = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$.

#### Model Solution — Part A(b)

**Given:**
- $P = 4$ poles, $f = 50$ Hz, $E_2 = 100$ V, $R_2 = 0.1$ Ω, $X_2 = 0.5$ Ω
- Synchronous speed: $N_s = 120f/P = 120 \times 50 / 4 = 1500$ RPM
- Synchronous angular velocity: $\omega_s = 2\pi N_s / 60 = 2\pi \times 25 = 50\pi$ rad/s ≈ **157.08 rad/s**

**(i) Slip at maximum torque (1 mark):**

$$s_{mT} = \frac{R_2}{X_2} = \frac{0.1}{0.5} = 0.2 \;\; (\text{or } 20\%)$$

**(ii) Maximum torque (3 marks):**

$$T_{max} = \frac{3 E_2^2}{2 \omega_s X_2} = \frac{3 \times (100)^2}{2 \times 50\pi \times 0.5} = \frac{30000}{50\pi} = \frac{600}{\pi}$$

$$\boxed{T_{max} \approx 190.99 \text{ N·m} \approx 191 \text{ N·m}}$$

**(iii) Starting torque (3 marks):**

$$T_{st} = \frac{3 E_2^2 R_2}{\omega_s (R_2^2 + X_2^2)} = \frac{3 \times 10000 \times 0.1}{50\pi (0.01 + 0.25)} = \frac{3000}{50\pi \times 0.26}$$

$$T_{st} = \frac{3000}{40.84} = \frac{73.45}{\pi} \approx 23.38 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_{st} \approx 23.38 \text{ N·m}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students mistakenly use $N_r$ (rotor speed) instead of $N_s$ (synchronous speed) when computing $\omega$. **Always use $\omega_s$** because the developed torque $P_d$ is the power converted at the air-gap, and the air-gap rotating field rotates at synchronous speed. Also, **forgetting to use slip in $E_2$** while computing $I_2$ is a common error — the rotor EMF at slip $s$ is $sE_2$, not $E_2$.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the condition for maximum torque in a three-phase induction motor. Show that the maximum torque is independent of rotor resistance. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare the starting methods of three-phase induction motors in terms of starting current, starting torque, cost, and applications. Suggest the most suitable starter for a 15 kW induction motor driving a hospital air-handling unit. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part B(a)

**Derivation (6 marks):**

Starting from $T = \dfrac{3 s E_2^2 R_2}{\omega_s [R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2]}$.

Since $3 E_2^2 / \omega_s$ is a constant for a given supply, we maximize:

$$f(s) = \frac{s R_2}{R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2}$$

Differentiating and setting to zero:

$$\frac{df}{ds} = \frac{R_2 (R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2) - sR_2 (2sX_2^2)}{(R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2)^2} = 0$$

$$R_2^3 + s^2 R_2 X_2^2 - 2 s^2 R_2 X_2^2 = 0 \implies R_2^2 = s^2 X_2^2$$

$$\boxed{s_{mT} = \frac{R_2}{X_2}}$$

Substituting back:

$$T_{max} = \frac{3 E_2^2}{2 \omega_s X_2} \quad \text{(1 mark for showing $R_2$ cancels out)}$$

This expression contains **no $R_2$**, proving that the maximum torque is independent of rotor resistance. However, the **slip at which maximum torque occurs** is directly controlled by $R_2$. This principle is exploited in slip-ring induction motors with external rotor resistance to obtain high starting torque.

#### Model Solution — Part B(b)

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Method | Starting Current (×FLC) | Starting Torque | Cost | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| DOL | 5–7 | 1.5–2 × FLT | Lowest | Small fans, pumps, bench stirrers |
| Star-Delta | 1.7–2.3 | 0.5–0.65 × FLT | Low | Pumps, compressors, blowers |
| Auto-Transformer | $k^2$ × (5–7) | $k^2$ × DOL torque | Medium | Large motors, MRI chillers |
| Rotor Resistance | 1.5–2.5 | Up to $T_{max}$ | High | Cranes, lifts, heavy centrifuges |
| Soft Starter | 1.5–3 (adjustable) | Adjustable | High | Modern biomedical pumps, HVAC |

**Recommendation for 15 kW motor driving a hospital AHU (3 marks):**

For a 15 kW induction motor driving a hospital air-handling unit:
- The load is a **fan/blower**, which has a **light starting duty** (low starting torque requirement because fan torque varies as the square of speed).
- The motor rating (15 kW) is **above the DOL threshold** (typically 5 kW in Indian practice) — direct-on-line starting is **not advisable** as it would draw ~75–105 A starting current, disturbing sensitive diagnostic equipment on the same bus.
- A **Star-Delta starter** is the most **economical and technically suitable** choice:
  - It reduces starting current to ~25–35 A (1/3 of DOL).
  - Fan loads need only ~30–40 % of full-load torque to start, which the star-delta starter can comfortably provide.
  - It is **simple, robust, and inexpensive**.

Therefore, a **Star-Delta starter** is recommended.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often wrongly recommend **Auto-Transformer** starters for *every* medium-sized motor. The examiner expects you to **justify the choice** based on the load type (fan, pump, compressor) and the starting torque required. A 15 kW fan does **not** justify the cost of an autotransformer. Always reason based on the **load torque-speed characteristic**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Module 3 (Torque Equation & Starting Methods)**

- **Master Formula:** $T = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$ — must be on your fingertip.
- **Synchronous speed:** $N_s = 120f/P$ — for 50 Hz, 4-pole motor $N_s = 1500$ RPM.
- **Slip** $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$ — at start $s = 1$, at no-load $s \approx 0$, at full load $s = 0.02$–$0.06$.
- **Slip at max torque:** $s_{mT} = R_2/X_2$ — **directly proportional to $R_2$**.
- **Maximum torque:** $T_{max} = 3E_2^2 / (2\omega_s X_2)$ — **independent of $R_2$**.
- **Normalized (Goodwin) form:** $T/T_{max} = 2 / (s/s_{mT} + s_{mT}/s)$ — useful when both $s$ and $s_{mT}$ are known.
- **Air-gap power** $P_g = 3I_2^2 R_2 / s$. **Mechanical power developed** $P_d = (1-s) P_g$. **Rotor copper loss** = $s P_g$.
- **Efficiency insight:** at $s = 0.04$, 96 % of air-gap power becomes mechanical power → high efficiency.
- **Five starting methods to know by name and circuit:**
  1. **DOL** — full voltage, $I_{st} = 5$–$7 \times I_{FL}$, $T_{st} = 1.5$–$2 \times T_{FL}$.
  2. **Star-Delta** — $I_{st, Y} = I_{st, \Delta}/3$, $T_{st, Y} = T_{st, \Delta}/3$.
  3. **Auto-Transformer** — $I_{st} = k^2 I_{st, DOL}$, $T_{st} = k^2 T_{st, DOL}$.
  4. **Rotor Resistance** — high $T_{st}$ (up to $T_{max}$), low $I_{st}$, requires slip-ring motor.
  5. **Soft Starter** — SCR-based, smooth ramp, programmable, modern biomedical standard.
- **Star-delta is preferred** for: fan, blower, centrifugal pump loads (low starting torque).
- **Rotor-resistance is preferred** for: high-inertia loads (centrifuges, hoists, compressors).
- **Auto-transformer is preferred** for: large motors where starting torque must be tuned via tap selection.
- **For biomedical equipment:** soft starters are increasingly used for clean-room and sterile-fluid pumps due to smooth acceleration and integrated protection.
- **Common mistake to avoid:** using $N_r$ instead of $N_s$ when computing $\omega$ for torque.
- **Common mistake to avoid:** using $E_2$ instead of $sE_2$ for rotor EMF in running condition.
- **Common mistake to avoid:** forgetting to multiply by **3** for the 3-phase total torque (using per-phase quantities).
- **SI units checklist:** torque in **N·m**, power in **W**, speed in **rad/s** (not RPM) for torque formulas, frequency in **Hz**, resistance/ reactance in **Ω**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single-Phase Induction Motor: Double Field Revolving Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT411 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY FOR BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Single-Phase Induction Motor: Double Field Revolving Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

The **Double Field Revolving Theory** is the foundational analytical model used to explain the operational behavior of a **single-phase induction motor**. It states that an *alternating sinusoidally varying magnetic flux* (produced by a single-phase stator winding energized from a single-phase AC supply) can be mathematically resolved into **two equal and opposite rotating magnetic fields**, each having a magnitude equal to **half** of the maximum alternating flux, rotating at **synchronous speed** ($N_s$) in opposite directions.

These two counter-rotating fields induce currents in the short-circuited rotor (squirrel-cage or wound type), producing two opposing torques — a **forward torque** ($T_f$) and a **backward torque** ($T_b$). At standstill, the two torques are exactly equal and opposite, giving a **net starting torque of zero**, which explains fundamentally why a single-phase induction motor is **not self-starting**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Double Field Revolving Theory is the conceptual gateway to understanding all starting methods of single-phase induction motors (split-phase, capacitor-start, shaded-pole, etc.). Every subsequent starting technique essentially aims to **suppress the backward field and reinforce the forward field** during startup.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing at the center of a circular playground carousel. A friend on the **north side** pushes the carousel clockwise with force $F$, while simultaneously another friend on the **south side** pushes it counter-clockwise with the *exact same force* $F$.

- The carousel **cannot decide which way to spin** — it just sits there, trembling slightly. This is **standstill** — *net force = 0*.
- Now, suppose you give the carousel a tiny *nudge* clockwise. The clockwise pushing friend now has a slight mechanical advantage (the carousel is already moving in his direction), so his effective push grows. The counter-clockwise friend loses advantage. The carousel now spins **clockwise**.
- Similarly, if you nudge it counter-clockwise, it spins that way instead.

This is precisely what happens in a single-phase induction motor! The rotor has *no preferred direction* of rotation by itself — it must be given a mechanical or auxiliary magnetic nudge. The starting methods (split-phase, capacitor, etc.) provide this "nudge" by creating a *temporary rotating field* at startup.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Biomedical Engineers:** Many life-critical biomedical devices (centrifuges for blood separation, infusion pumps, MRI-compatible actuators, surgical suction pumps) use single-phase induction motors because the supply available in hospitals is single-phase AC. Understanding the Double Field Revolving Theory helps in selecting the correct motor type and diagnosing *why a motor failed to start* — a routine but critical skill in hospital biomedical equipment maintenance.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Supply Frequency | $f$ | **50 Hz** (India / Europe) |
| Synchronous Speed | $N_s$ | $\dfrac{120 f}{P}$ **rpm** |
| Slip (forward field) | $s$ | $\dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ |
| Slip (backward field) | $s_b$ | $\dfrac{N_s + N_r}{N_s} = 2 - s$ |
| Maximum Alternating Flux | $\phi_m$ | **Weber (Wb)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of the Theory

The Double Field Revolving Theory is built on **five foundational logical pillars**:

**Pillar 1 — The Alternating Flux Assumption**
A single-phase stator winding, when energized by a single-phase AC supply $v = V_m \sin(\omega t)$, produces a **purely alternating magnetic flux** $\phi(t)$ along a fixed axis (say, the horizontal axis). This flux is given by:

$$\phi(t) = \phi_m \sin(\omega t)$$

This flux is **stationary in space** — its axis never moves. It merely *grows* and *shrinks* sinusoidally along a fixed horizontal line. Crucially, this is **NOT** a rotating field.

**Pillar 2 — Mathematical Resolution into Two Counter-Rotating Fields**
Any alternating sinusoidal quantity can be represented as the **sum of two equal rotating vectors moving in opposite directions** (this is a basic trigonometric identity). The two component fluxes are:

$$\phi_f(t) = \frac{\phi_m}{2} \sin(\omega t) \quad \text{(Forward / Clockwise field)}$$

$$\phi_b(t) = \frac{\phi_m}{2} \sin(\omega t) \quad \text{(Backward / Counter-clockwise field)}$$

These two fluxes rotate at synchronous speed $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ rpm in opposite directions.

**Pillar 3 — Rotor Interaction with Each Field**
When the rotor (at standstill) is exposed to the *forward field*, it experiences a torque $T_f$ in the **forward direction** — analogous to a three-phase induction motor running normally with slip $s$.

When the rotor is exposed to the *backward field*, it experiences a torque $T_b$ in the **backward direction** — as if the rotor were running *in reverse* relative to this field. The slip with respect to the backward field is $s_b = 2 - s$.

**Pillar 4 — Why the Motor is Not Self-Starting (The Critical Conclusion)**
At **standstill**, $N_r = 0$, so $s = 1$ and $s_b = 1$. Both the forward and backward fields see the rotor with **equal slip of unity**. Therefore, the forward torque $T_f$ and backward torque $T_b$ are **equal in magnitude but opposite in direction**.

$$T_{net} = T_f - T_b = 0 \quad \text{at } s = 1$$

Hence, **no net starting torque** is produced. The motor cannot start on its own.

**Pillar 5 — Behavior Once Started (The Hidden Magic)**
If the rotor is given an artificial push in *either direction*, say forward, then $N_r > 0$. The slip with respect to the forward field decreases ($s < 1$), making $T_f > T_b$. The motor now develops a **net forward torque** and accelerates to its operating point. Conversely, if pushed backward, it runs backward. This is why the **direction of rotation is determined entirely by the direction of the initial nudge**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Physical Meaning | Boundary / Operating Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\phi(t) = \phi_m \sin(\omega t)$ | Alternating flux produced by single-phase winding | Always true for single-phase excitation |
| 2 | $\phi_f = \dfrac{\phi_m}{2}$ rotating at $+N_s$ | Forward rotating field component | Magnitude is half the peak alternating flux |
| 3 | $\phi_b = \dfrac{\phi_m}{2}$ rotating at $-N_s$ | Backward rotating field component | Magnitude is half the peak alternating flux |
| 4 | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed of rotating fields | $f$ in Hz, $P$ = number of poles |
| 5 | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Slip with respect to forward field | $0 \le s \le 1$ in motoring |
| 6 | $s_b = \dfrac{N_s + N_r}{N_s} = 2 - s$ | Slip with respect to backward field | At standstill: $s_b = 1$ when $s = 1$ |
| 7 | $T_f \propto \dfrac{s \cdot R_r}{R_r^2 + (s X_r)^2}$ | Forward torque (asynchronous motor torque equation) | Valid in linear region |
| 8 | $T_b \propto \dfrac{(2-s) \cdot R_r}{R_r^2 + ((2-s) X_r)^2}$ | Backward torque expression | Derived by substituting $s_b = 2-s$ into Eq.7 |
| 9 | $T_{net} = T_f - T_b$ | Net torque developed by single-phase motor | At standstill: $T_{net} = 0$ |
| 10 | $\omega_s = \dfrac{2 \pi f}{P/2} = \dfrac{4 \pi f}{P}$ | Synchronous angular velocity | rad/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Note:** Equation 7 in the table is identical to the standard torque equation of a **three-phase induction motor**. The single-phase motor is essentially modeled as **two three-phase motors coupled in opposite directions**, mechanically adding their torques. This is a very common KTU board exam question.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Utility of Double Field Revolving Theory |
|---|---|
| **Biomedical Equipment** | Hospital-grade pumps, ventilators, centrifuges — diagnosis of non-starting motors |
| **Household Appliances** | Ceiling fans (single-phase shaded-pole), washing machines (capacitor-start), refrigerators (capacitor-run) |
| **HVAC Systems** | Blowers and fans in clean rooms, operating theaters |
| **Small Machine Tools** | Portable drills, grinders — universal motor alternative |
| **Agricultural Pumps** | Single-phase submersible pumps in rural India |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Resolution of Alternating Flux into Two Counter-Rotating Fields

**Given:** A sinusoidally alternating flux along a fixed horizontal axis:

$$\phi(t) = \phi_m \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 1 — Vector Representation**
Represent this alternating flux as the *projection* of a rotating phasor on the horizontal axis. Consider two phasors of magnitude $\dfrac{\phi_m}{2}$ rotating in **opposite directions** at angular velocity $\omega$.

- Phasor $A$ rotates **counter-clockwise** (forward direction) with angle $\theta_1 = \omega t$.
- Phasor $B$ rotates **clockwise** (backward direction) with angle $\theta_2 = -\omega t$.

**Step 2 — Horizontal Components of Each Phasor**
The projection of Phasor $A$ on the horizontal axis:

$$\phi_{A,h}(t) = \frac{\phi_m}{2} \cos(\omega t - 90°) = \frac{\phi_m}{2} \sin(\omega t)$$

The projection of Phasor $B$ on the horizontal axis:

$$\phi_{B,h}(t) = \frac{\phi_m}{2} \cos(\omega t + 90°) = -\frac{\phi_m}{2} \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 3 — Summation of Both Phasors**
Adding the horizontal components of Phasors $A$ and $B$:

$$\phi_{A,h}(t) + \phi_{B,h}(t) = \frac{\phi_m}{2} \sin(\omega t) - \frac{\phi_m}{2} \sin(\omega t) = 0 \quad \text{(at horizontal axis)}$$

**Step 4 — Consider Vertical Components**
The vertical (perpendicular) components *add* constructively. The vertical component of $A$ is $\dfrac{\phi_m}{2} \sin(\omega t)$ and that of $B$ is $\dfrac{\phi_m}{2} \sin(\omega t)$. So along the vertical axis, the resultant is $\phi_m \sin(\omega t)$.

But wait — we need to verify that the *resultant flux vector* rotates. Let us compute the **vector sum** of the two phasors at any instant:

$$\vec{\phi}_f(t) = \frac{\phi_m}{2} \angle (\omega t) \quad \text{(CCW rotating)}$$

$$\vec{\phi}_b(t) = \frac{\phi_m}{2} \angle (-\omega t) \quad \text{(CW rotating)}$$

**Step 5 — Resultant Vector Sum**
At any angle $\omega t$, the resultant horizontal component:

$$\phi_h(t) = \frac{\phi_m}{2} \cos(\omega t) + \frac{\phi_m}{2} \cos(-\omega t)$$

Since $\cos(-\omega t) = \cos(\omega t)$:

$$\phi_h(t) = \frac{\phi_m}{2} \cos(\omega t) + \frac{\phi_m}{2} \cos(\omega t) = \phi_m \cos(\omega t)$$

The resultant vertical component:

$$\phi_v(t) = \frac{\phi_m}{2} \sin(\omega t) + \frac{\phi_m}{2} \sin(-\omega t) = 0$$

So the **resultant flux** is purely horizontal and varies as $\phi_m \cos(\omega t)$, which is identical in nature to an alternating flux. This proves the equivalence.

### 3.2 Derivation: Torque Expression for Forward and Backward Fields

**Step 1 — Standard Three-Phase Torque Equation**
The torque developed by a three-phase induction motor (or equivalently, the torque produced on a rotor by a single rotating field with slip $s$) is given by:

$$T = \frac{K \cdot s \cdot R_r}{R_r^2 + (s X_r)^2}$$

where:
- $K = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{R_r^2}$ (a constant for a given motor and supply)
- $R_r$ = rotor resistance per phase
- $X_r$ = rotor reactance at standstill per phase
- $\omega_s$ = synchronous angular velocity

**Step 2 — Forward Field Torque ($T_f$)**
The forward rotating field interacts with the rotor which is running at $N_r$. The slip with respect to the forward field is $s$. Therefore:

$$T_f = \frac{K \cdot s \cdot R_r}{R_r^2 + (s X_r)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Backward Field Slip**
