The backward field rotates at $-N_s$ (opposite to forward direction). The rotor rotates at $+N_r$ (forward direction). The *relative speed* between the backward field and the rotor is:

$$N_{rel,b} = N_s + N_r$$

Therefore, the slip with respect to the backward field:

$$s_b = \frac{N_s + N_r}{N_s} = 1 + \frac{N_r}{N_s} = 2 - s$$

This is because $\dfrac{N_r}{N_s} = 1 - s$, so $s_b = 1 + (1 - s) = 2 - s$.

**Step 4 — Backward Field Torque ($T_b$)**
Substituting $s_b = 2 - s$ into the standard torque equation:

$$T_b = \frac{K \cdot s_b \cdot R_r}{R_r^2 + (s_b X_r)^2} = \frac{K \cdot (2 - s) \cdot R_r}{R_r^2 + ((2 - s) X_r)^2}$$

**Step 5 — Net Torque Developed**

$$\boxed{T_{net} = T_f - T_b = \frac{K \cdot s \cdot R_r}{R_r^2 + (s X_r)^2} - \frac{K \cdot (2 - s) \cdot R_r}{R_r^2 + ((2 - s) X_r)^2}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Proof of Zero Starting Torque at Standstill

**At Standstill:** $N_r = 0$, therefore $s = 1$ and $s_b = 2 - 1 = 1$.

Substituting $s = 1$ in the forward torque expression:

$$T_f\big|_{s=1} = \frac{K \cdot (1) \cdot R_r}{R_r^2 + (1 \cdot X_r)^2} = \frac{K \cdot R_r}{R_r^2 + X_r^2}$$

Substituting $s_b = 1$ in the backward torque expression:

$$T_b\big|_{s_b=1} = \frac{K \cdot (1) \cdot R_r}{R_r^2 + (1 \cdot X_r)^2} = \frac{K \cdot R_r}{R_r^2 + X_r^2}$$

Therefore, the net torque at standstill:

$$T_{net}\big|_{s=1} = T_f - T_b = \frac{K \cdot R_r}{R_r^2 + X_r^2} - \frac{K \cdot R_r}{R_r^2 + X_r^2} = 0$$

**Conclusion:** The single-phase induction motor has **zero starting torque** and is therefore **not self-starting**.

### 3.4 Derivation: Proof of Non-Zero Torque at Running Condition

**At a typical running speed** (say, $N_r = 0.95 N_s$, so $s = 0.05$ and $s_b = 1.95$):

Substituting $s = 0.05$:

$$T_f = \frac{K \cdot 0.05 \cdot R_r}{R_r^2 + (0.05 X_r)^2} \quad (\text{large value})$$

Substituting $s_b = 1.95$:

$$T_b = \frac{K \cdot 1.95 \cdot R_r}{R_r^2 + (1.95 X_r)^2} \quad (\text{much smaller value})$$

Therefore:

$$T_{net} = T_f - T_b > 0$$

This positive net torque sustains the rotation. **Once started, the motor continues to run.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** A very common KTU question asks: *"Why does a single-phase induction motor, though not self-starting, continue to run once started?"* The answer lies precisely in this derivation — when $s \neq 1$, the forward and backward slips are unequal, making $T_f \neq T_b$, yielding a non-zero net torque.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Concept of Double Field Revolving Theory

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["AC Single Phase Supply V sin wt"] --> B["Single Phase Stator Winding"]
    B --> C["Alternating Flux phi t = phi_m sin wt"]
    C --> D["Mathematical Resolution via Trigonometric Identity"]
    D --> E1["Forward Rotating Flux phi_f magnitude phi_m over 2 at +Ns"]
    D --> E2["Backward Rotating Flux phi_b magnitude phi_m over 2 at -Ns"]
    E1 --> F1["Rotor Slip s = Ns minus Nr over Ns"]
    E2 --> F2["Rotor Slip sb = Ns plus Nr over Ns = 2 minus s"]
    F1 --> G1["Forward Torque Tf proportional to s Rr over Rr squared plus s Xr squared"]
    F2 --> G2["Backward Torque Tb proportional to sb Rr over Rr squared plus sb Xr squared"]
    G1 --> H["Net Torque Tnet = Tf minus Tb"]
    G2 --> H
    H --> I{"Is rotor speed Nr equal to 0?"}
    I -->|"Yes standstill"| J["Tnet = 0  Motor NOT self starting"]
    I -->|"No running condition"| K["Tnet greater than 0  Motor runs in direction of initial nudge"]
    K --> L["Biomedical Application Pumps Fans Centrifuges Ventilators"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Rotor Slip Variation with Speed

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Rotor Speed Nr"] --> B1{"Slip Calculation"}
    B1 --> C1["Forward Slip s = Ns minus Nr over Ns"]
    B1 --> C2["Backward Slip sb = 2 minus s"]
    C1 --> D1["At Nr equals 0  s equals 1"]
    C2 --> D2["At Nr equals 0  sb equals 1"]
    D1 --> E1["Tf and Tb equal  Tnet equals 0"]
    D2 --> E1
    C1 --> F1["At Nr equals 0.95 Ns  s equals 0.05"]
    C2 --> F2["At Nr equals 0.95 Ns  sb equals 1.95"]
    F1 --> G1["Tf is large positive"]
    F2 --> G2["Tb is small positive"]
    G1 --> H1["Tnet greater than 0  Motor accelerates"]
    G2 --> H1
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Forward and Backward Field Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Alternating Flux along Fixed Horizontal Axis"] --> B["Resolved into Two Rotating Components"]
    B --> C1["Forward Field phi_f rotating CCW at Ns"]
    B --> C2["Backward Field phi_b rotating CW at Ns"]
    C1 --> D1["Induces EMF in rotor at frequency sf"]
    C2 --> D2["Induces EMF in rotor at frequency sb times f"]
    D1 --> E1["Produces Forward Torque Tf"]
    D2 --> E2["Produces Backward Torque Tb"]
    E1 --> F1["Net Torque Tnet"]
    E2 --> F1
    F1 --> G1["At Standstill  Tnet equals 0"]
    F1 --> G2["At Running  Tnet greater than 0 in direction of rotation"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State the Double Field Revolving Theory as applied to a single-phase induction motor.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Double Field Revolving Theory states that an **alternating sinusoidal magnetic flux** produced by a single-phase stator winding can be mathematically resolved into **two rotating magnetic fields** of **equal magnitude** (each equal to *half* the maximum alternating flux, i.e., $\dfrac{\phi_m}{2}$), rotating at **synchronous speed** $N_s = \dfrac{120f}{P}$ in **opposite directions**. These are:

1. **Forward rotating field** ($\phi_f$) rotating clockwise at $+N_s$.
2. **Backward rotating field** ($\phi_b$) rotating counter-clockwise at $-N_s$.

The theory is used to explain why a single-phase induction motor is **not self-starting** — at standstill, both fields produce equal and opposite torques, giving a net starting torque of zero.

**(Mark Distribution:** *Statement of theory — 1 mark; Two components with magnitude — 1 mark; Synchronous speed mention — 1 mark* **)**

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Why is a single-phase induction motor not self-starting?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A single-phase induction motor is not self-starting because:

1. The single-phase stator winding produces only an **alternating flux**, not a rotating flux. **(1 mark)**
2. By the Double Field Revolving Theory, this alternating flux splits into two **equal and opposite rotating fields** of magnitude $\dfrac{\phi_m}{2}$, each inducing equal and opposite torques on the rotor at standstill. **(1 mark)**
3. At standstill ($N_r = 0$, $s = 1$, $s_b = 1$), the forward torque $T_f$ equals the backward torque $T_b$, giving a **net starting torque $T_{net} = T_f - T_b = 0$**. Hence, the rotor cannot start on its own. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students often write only $\phi_f = \phi_b = \phi_m$ instead of $\phi_f = \phi_b = \dfrac{\phi_m}{2}$. **Each missing factor of 2 costs 1 mark.**
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing the *rotor resistance* $R_r$ and *rotor reactance* $X_r$ with stator values. Always specify *rotor* parameters referred to stator.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** When asked to "derive", students skip the trigonometric identity step. Examiners **explicitly allocate 2 marks** for stating $\sin\theta = \dfrac{e^{j\theta} - e^{-j\theta}}{2j}$ or the phasor-resolution logic. Do not skip this.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Forgetting units of $N_s$ (rpm) vs. $\omega_s$ (rad/s). Mixing units is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction**.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Writing slip as $\dfrac{N_s + N_r}{N_s}$ for the forward field — it should be $\dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$. This is the **single most common slip-direction error**.

---

#### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – July 2024 – Modified]`

**(a)** Explain the **Double Field Revolving Theory** for a single-phase induction motor with necessary mathematical derivation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for **net torque** developed by a single-phase induction motor. Show that the **starting torque is zero** at standstill. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B-A**

### **Part (a) — Explanation and Derivation of Double Field Revolving Theory (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Statement of the Theory (2 Marks)**

A single-phase stator winding, when energized by a single-phase AC supply $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$, produces an alternating flux along a fixed axis:

$$\phi(t) = \phi_m \sin(\omega t)$$

This alternating flux can be mathematically resolved into **two rotating fluxes** of equal magnitude ($\phi_m / 2$) rotating at synchronous speed $N_s$ in **opposite directions**.

**Step 2 — Mathematical Derivation (4 Marks)**

Using the trigonometric identity for $\sin(\omega t)$:

$$\phi(t) = \phi_m \sin(\omega t) = \frac{\phi_m}{2} \cdot 2 \sin(\omega t)$$

Represent each component as a rotating phasor:

- Phasor $\vec{\phi}_f = \dfrac{\phi_m}{2} \angle \omega t$ (counter-clockwise rotation — **forward field**)
- Phasor $\vec{\phi}_b = \dfrac{\phi_m}{2} \angle (-\omega t)$ (clockwise rotation — **backward field**)

The projection of the forward phasor on the reference horizontal axis:

$$\phi_{f,h}(t) = \frac{\phi_m}{2} \cos(\omega t - 90°) = \frac{\phi_m}{2} \sin(\omega t)$$

The projection of the backward phasor on the reference horizontal axis:

$$\phi_{b,h}(t) = \frac{\phi_m}{2} \cos(\omega t + 90°) = -\frac{\phi_m}{2} \sin(\omega t)$$

For the resultant to equal $\phi(t)$, consider *vertical* components also. The vertical components *add* to give $\phi_m \sin(\omega t)$. This proves the equivalence between an alternating flux and the sum of two counter-rotating fluxes of half magnitude. **(4 marks for derivation steps)**

**Step 3 — Significance (1 Mark)**

At synchronous speed $N_s = \dfrac{120f}{P}$, both rotating fields induce EMFs in the rotor, producing forward torque $T_f$ and backward torque $T_b$. This decomposition forms the basis for explaining the self-starting problem.

---

### **Part (b) — Derivation of Net Torque and Proof of Zero Starting Torque (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Forward and Backward Torque Expressions (3 Marks)**

The torque produced by a rotating magnetic field on an induction motor rotor is given by the standard asynchronous motor torque equation:

$$T = \frac{K \cdot s \cdot R_r}{R_r^2 + (s X_r)^2}$$

where $K$ is a motor constant. The forward field sees slip $s$, so:

$$T_f = \frac{K \cdot s \cdot R_r}{R_r^2 + (s X_r)^2}$$

The backward field rotates at $-N_s$, while the rotor rotates at $+N_r$. The relative speed is $N_s + N_r$, giving backward slip:

$$s_b = \frac{N_s + N_r}{N_s} = 2 - s$$

Therefore, the backward torque is:

$$T_b = \frac{K \cdot (2 - s) \cdot R_r}{R_r^2 + ((2 - s) X_r)^2}$$

**Step 2 — Net Torque Expression (2 Marks)**

$$T_{net} = T_f - T_b = \frac{K \cdot s \cdot R_r}{R_r^2 + (s X_r)^2} - \frac{K \cdot (2 - s) \cdot R_r}{R_r^2 + ((2 - s) X_r)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Proof of Zero Starting Torque (2 Marks)**

At standstill, $N_r = 0$, so $s = 1$ and $s_b = 2 - 1 = 1$. Substituting:

$$T_f\big|_{s=1} = \frac{K \cdot R_r}{R_r^2 + X_r^2}$$

$$T_b\big|_{s_b=1} = \frac{K \cdot R_r}{R_r^2 + X_r^2}$$

$$T_{net}\big|_{s=1} = T_f - T_b = 0$$

Hence, **the starting torque of a single-phase induction motor is zero**, confirming that it is **not self-starting**.

---

#### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Modified]`

**(a)** With a neat **phasor diagram**, explain how an alternating flux can be resolved into two counter-rotating fluxes of half magnitude each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A single-phase induction motor is running at a slip of **0.04** with respect to the forward field. Calculate:
   1. The slip with respect to the backward field.
   2. The relative speed between the rotor and the forward field.
   3. The relative speed between the rotor and the backward field.

Assume: $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$, $P = 4$ poles. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B-B**

### **Part (a) — Phasor Resolution (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Reference Alternating Flux (1 Mark)**

Consider an alternating flux along the reference axis (x-axis):

$$\phi(t) = \phi_m \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 2 — Two Counter-Rotating Phasors (2 Marks)**

Draw two phasors $\vec{\phi}_f$ and $\vec{\phi}_b$, each of magnitude $\dfrac{\phi_m}{2}$:

- $\vec{\phi}_f$ rotates **counter-clockwise** at angular velocity $\omega$.
- $\vec{\phi}_b$ rotates **clockwise** at angular velocity $\omega$.

**Step 3 — Phasor Diagram Construction (2 Marks)**

At time $t$:
- $\vec{\phi}_f$ is at angle $+\omega t$ from the x-axis.
- $\vec{\phi}_b$ is at angle $-\omega t$ from the x-axis.

**Step 4 — Resultant Verification (2 Marks)**

The vector sum of $\vec{\phi}_f$ and $\vec{\phi}_b$:

- Horizontal component: $\dfrac{\phi_m}{2}\cos(\omega t) + \dfrac{\phi_m}{2}\cos(\omega t) = \phi_m \cos(\omega t)$
- Vertical component: $\dfrac{\phi_m}{2}\sin(\omega t) + \dfrac{\phi_m}{2}\sin(-\omega t) = 0$

The resultant is a stationary vector along the x-axis with magnitude $\phi_m \cos(\omega t)$, which is the alternating flux. **Hence proved.**

---

### **Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- Slip with respect to forward field: $s = 0.04$
- Frequency: $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$
- Number of poles: $P = 4$

**Step 1 — Synchronous Speed (1 Mark)**

$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 2 — Backward Field Slip (2 Marks)**

$$s_b = 2 - s = 2 - 0.04 = 1.96$$

**Step 3 — Rotor Speed (1 Mark)**

$$N_r = N_s (1 - s) = 1500 \times (1 - 0.04) = 1500 \times 0.96 = 1440 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 4 — Relative Speed between Rotor and Forward Field (1.5 Marks)**

$$N_{rel,f} = N_s - N_r = 1500 - 1440 = 60 \text{ rpm}$$

(This is the speed at which the forward field "cuts" the rotor — also equal to $s \cdot N_s = 0.04 \times 1500 = 60$ rpm.)

**Step 5 — Relative Speed between Rotor and Backward Field (1.5 Marks)**

$$N_{rel,b} = N_s + N_r = 1500 + 1440 = 2940 \text{ rpm}$$

(Alternatively, $N_{rel,b} = s_b \cdot N_s = 1.96 \times 1500 = 2940$ rpm.)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **The Core Theorem:** An alternating flux $\phi_m \sin(\omega t)$ is mathematically equivalent to **two counter-rotating fluxes**, each of magnitude $\dfrac{\phi_m}{2}$, rotating at $N_s = \dfrac{120f}{P}$ rpm in opposite directions.

- **Starting Torque = Zero:** At standstill ($N_r = 0$), both $s = 1$ and $s_b = 1$, making $T_f = T_b$ and $T_{net} = 0$. **Hence not self-starting.**

- **Backward Field Slip Formula:** $s_b = 2 - s$ — this is the **single most important formula** for this entire topic. Memorize it cold.

- **Net Torque Equation:** $T_{net} = \dfrac{K s R_r}{R_r^2 + (s X_r)^2} - \dfrac{K (2-s) R_r}{R_r^2 + ((2-s) X_r)^2}$

- **Direction Ambiguity:** A single-phase motor can run in *either direction* once started — direction is determined by the **initial mechanical nudge** or by the **auxiliary starting winding's phase-shift**.

- **Trigonometric Basis:** The decomposition uses $\sin(\omega t) = \dfrac{e^{j\omega t} - e^{-j\omega t}}{2j}$, which represents the imaginary parts of two counter-rotating complex exponentials.

- **Engineering Takeaway for Biomedical Engineers:** Every starting method (split-phase, capacitor-start, capacitor-run, shaded-pole) works by **breaking the symmetry** between $T_f$ and $T_b$ during startup — essentially adding an auxiliary phase-shifted flux to make the net field *rotate* instead of merely alternate.

- **Common Slip Values to Remember:** At standstill: $s = 1$, $s_b = 1$; At full-load (typical): $s \approx 0.04$ to $0.06$, so $s_b \approx 1.94$ to $1.96$.

- **Exam Hot-Spots:** Drawing the phasor diagram (3 marks reserved); proving $T_{net} = 0$ at $s = 1$ (2 marks); writing the net torque equation (3 marks); numerical slip calculation (2 marks).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Making Single-Phase Induction Motor Self-Starting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT411 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY FOR BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Making Single-Phase Induction Motor Self-Starting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Single-Phase Induction Motor (SPIM)** is an alternating current (AC) motor that operates on a **single-phase sinusoidal supply** to produce mechanical rotation. However, a single-phase induction motor is **NOT inherently self-starting** because the alternating flux produced by a single-phase stator winding is **purely pulsating** (oscillates along a fixed axis) and does not constitute a true **rotating magnetic field (RMF)**.

To make a single-phase induction motor self-starting, an auxiliary mechanism must be employed to **convert the pulsating magnetic field into a rotating magnetic field** during starting. This is achieved by introducing a **second (auxiliary) winding** spatially displaced from the main winding, supplied with a **phase-displaced current**, creating a **two-phase system** that produces a rotating magnetic field and develops a **starting torque**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The core concept of "making SPIM self-starting" rests on **phase splitting** — producing a **time-displaced (phase-shifted) alternating flux** in a spatially displaced auxiliary winding such that the resultant becomes a rotating field.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine pushing a child on a swing. If you push **only once, in a single direction**, the swing oscillates back and forth but never completes a full circle — it just swings on the same arc. Similarly, a single-phase alternating current produces a flux that "swings" back and forth along one axis (pulsates) — it cannot, by itself, start a rotor to rotate continuously.

Now, imagine **two people pushing the swing alternately** from two different angles (one pushing forward, the other pushing sideways, with a slight delay between them). The combined effect of these two pushes, occurring at slightly different times and from different directions, creates a **circular motion** of the swing. This is exactly how a **rotating magnetic field** is produced — by combining **two alternating fluxes** that are **spatially displaced** (at different physical locations on the stator) and **temporally phase-shifted** (currents reaching their peaks at different times).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The two ingredients for a rotating magnetic field are:
> 1. **Spatial Displacement** — windings placed at an angle (typically **90 electrical degrees** apart).
> 2. **Temporal Phase Displacement** — currents having a phase difference (ideally **90 electrical degrees** in time).

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Supply Frequency (f):** **50 Hz** (India, as per KTU Kerala context) or **60 Hz** (other regions).
- **Synchronous Speed:** $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ where $P$ = number of poles.
- **Electrical Degrees:** 360° electrical = 180° mechanical for a **2-pole** machine.
- **Phase Split Angle (Ideal):** $\phi = 90°$ between main and auxiliary winding currents.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why a Single-Phase Motor Fails to Self-Start

When a single-phase AC supply is applied to the main (running) winding of a stator:

1. It produces an **alternating (pulsating) flux** $\phi(t) = \phi_m \sin(\omega t)$ that is **stationary in space** (axis-fixed) but **varying in magnitude and reversing direction** with time.
2. According to the **Double Revolving Field Theory** (proposed by Leblanc), this pulsating field can be mathematically resolved into **two counter-rotating magnetic fields** of **equal magnitude** rotating at synchronous speed $N_s$ in **opposite directions**.
3. The **forward component** ($B_f$) and **backward component** ($B_b$) produce **equal and opposite torques** at standstill: $T_f = -T_b$, hence **net starting torque = 0**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Double Revolving Field Theory (Leblanc, 1896):**
> $$\phi(t) = \phi_m \sin(\omega t) \cdot \cos(\theta) = \frac{\phi_m}{2}\sin(\omega t - \theta) + \frac{\phi_m}{2}\sin(\omega t + \theta)$$
> where the first term represents the **forward rotating field** and the second term represents the **backward rotating field**.

### 2.2 The Principle of Self-Starting

To break this torque symmetry, we must ensure that **at standstill**, the forward torque $T_f$ exceeds the backward torque $T_b$. This is achieved by:

- Adding an **auxiliary winding** (also called starting or auxiliary winding) physically displaced by **90 electrical degrees** from the main winding.
- Feeding this winding with a current **phase-shifted** from the main winding current.
- The phase shift is created by adding an **impedance** (resistor, capacitor, or inductor) in series with the auxiliary winding.

### 2.3 Methods of Making SPIM Self-Starting

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following methods:

| S.No. | Method | Phase-Splitting Element | Starting Torque | Applications |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | **Split-Phase (Resistance-Start) Motor** | High resistance in auxiliary winding | **1.5 to 2 times** full-load torque | Fans, blowers, washing machines, small pumps |
| 2 | **Capacitor-Start Motor** | Capacitor in series with auxiliary winding | **3 to 4.5 times** full-load torque | Compressors, conveyors, pumps |
| 3 | **Capacitor-Run (Permanent Capacitor) Motor** | Capacitor permanently in auxiliary circuit | **0.5 to 1.0 times** full-load torque | Fans, ceiling fans (older designs) |
| 4 | **Capacitor-Start Capacitor-Run Motor** | Two capacitors (start + run) | **3 to 4.5 times** full-load torque | Compressors, high-inertia loads |
| 5 | **Shaded-Pole Motor** | Copper shading rings (no auxiliary winding) | **0.5 to 1.0 times** full-load torque | Small fans, record players, hair dryers |
| 6 | **Repulsion-Start Motor** | Commutator + brushes (DC-like starting) | **3 to 5 times** full-load torque | Industrial drives (mostly obsolete) |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULAS FOR KTU EXAMS — Memorize these for ESE & University papers.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | **Synchronous Speed** | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | rpm; $P$ = number of poles |
| 2 | **Angular Synchronous Speed** | $\omega_s = \dfrac{2 \pi f}{P} = \dfrac{2 \pi N_s}{60}$ | rad/s |
| 3 | **Slip** | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 4 | **Rotor Speed** | $N_r = N_s (1 - s)$ | rpm |
| 5 | **Rotor Frequency** | $f_r = s \cdot f$ | Hz |
| 6 | **Forward Field Flux Magnitude** | $\phi_f = \dfrac{\phi_m}{2}$ | Wb |
| 7 | **Backward Field Flux Magnitude** | $\phi_b = \dfrac{\phi_m}{2}$ | Wb |
| 8 | **Torque at Standstill (Pure Pulsating Field)** | $T_{net} = 0$ | N·m (no rotation) |
| 9 | **Net Torque with Auxiliary Winding** | $T_{net} = T_f - T_b > 0$ | N·m (motor starts) |
| 10 | **Phase Displacement (Ideal)** | $\alpha = 90°$ | Electrical degrees |
| 11 | **Auxiliary Winding Impedance** | $Z_{aux} = R_{aux} + jX_{aux}$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 12 | **Phase Angle of Aux Current** | $\theta_{aux} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{X_{aux}}{R_{aux}}\right)$ | Radians or degrees |
| 13 | **Torque Equation (Approximate)** | $T \propto \phi \cdot I_r \cdot \cos(\psi)$ | N·m |
| 14 | **Starting Torque (Capacitor-Start)** | $T_{st} = k \cdot \phi_m \cdot I_{aux} \cdot \sin(\alpha)$ | N·m |
| 15 | **Centrifugal Switch Operates At** | $N \approx 70\% \text{ to } 80\%$ of $N_s$ | rpm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Self-starting SPIMs power **biomedical equipment** like **centrifuges, blood pumps, ventilators, surgical suction pumps, dialysis machines, infusion pumps, and autoclave stirrers** — making this topic directly relevant to the biomedical engineering curriculum.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Double Revolving Field Theory Applied to a Pulsating Flux

**Step 1 — Express the single-phase pulsating flux:**

Let the main winding be oriented along the horizontal axis ($\theta = 0$). The flux at any point in space (angle $\theta$) and time $t$ is:

$$
\phi(\theta, t) = \phi_m \sin(\omega t) \cdot \cos(\theta)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the trigonometric product-to-sum identity:**

We use the identity:
$$
\sin(A)\cos(B) = \tfrac{1}{2}\left[\sin(A-B) + \sin(A+B)\right]
$$

Substituting $A = \omega t$ and $B = \theta$:

$$
\phi(\theta, t) = \frac{\phi_m}{2}\sin(\omega t - \theta) + \frac{\phi_m}{2}\sin(\omega t + \theta)
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret each term as a rotating flux wave:**

- The first term $\dfrac{\phi_m}{2}\sin(\omega t - \theta)$ represents a **forward (counter-clockwise) rotating field** with peak magnitude $\phi_f = \dfrac{\phi_m}{2}$ rotating at angular speed $\omega$.
- The second term $\dfrac{\phi_m}{2}\sin(\omega t + \theta)$ represents a **backward (clockwise) rotating field** with peak magnitude $\phi_b = \dfrac{\phi_m}{2}$ rotating at angular speed $\omega$ in the opposite direction.

**Step 4 — Compute the torque contributions at standstill ($s = 1$):**

At standstill, the forward slip = 1 and the backward slip = 1 (relative to the opposite direction). The torques are:

$$
T_f = k \cdot \phi_f^2 \cdot \frac{R_r / s}{(R_r / s)^2 + X_r^2}\bigg|_{s=1} = k \cdot \phi_f^2 \cdot \frac{R_r}{R_r^2 + X_r^2}
$$

$$
T_b = k \cdot \phi_b^2 \cdot \frac{R_r / s}{(R_r / s)^2 + X_r^2}\bigg|_{s=1} = k \cdot \phi_b^2 \cdot \frac{R_r}{R_r^2 + X_r^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Equate the torques at standstill:**

Since $\phi_f = \phi_b = \dfrac{\phi_m}{2}$:

$$
T_f = T_b \quad \Longrightarrow \quad T_{net} = T_f - T_b = 0
$$

**Conclusion:** A purely single-phase pulsating field produces **zero net starting torque** — the motor cannot self-start.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Torque Production When an Auxiliary Winding is Added

**Step 1 — Define the two currents and their fluxes:**

Let the main winding current be:
$$
i_m(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)
$$

This produces a flux along the main axis (let's say the **X-axis**):
$$
\phi_m(t) = \phi_m \sin(\omega t)
$$

Let the auxiliary winding current (phase-shifted by angle $\alpha$) be:
$$
i_a(t) = I_a \sin(\omega t - \alpha)
$$

This produces a flux along the auxiliary axis (the **Y-axis**, displaced by 90 electrical degrees):
$$
\phi_a(t) = \phi_a \sin(\omega t - \alpha)
$$

**Step 2 — Combine to form a rotating field:**

The resultant flux at any time $t$ at the spatial angle $\theta$ is:

$$
\phi_{res}(\theta, t) = \phi_m \sin(\omega t)\cos(\theta) + \phi_a \sin(\omega t - \alpha)\sin(\theta)
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the special condition $\alpha = 90°$ and $\phi_m = \phi_a = \phi_0$:**

Substituting $\alpha = 90°$:

$$
\phi_{res}(\theta, t) = \phi_0 \sin(\omega t)\cos(\theta) + \phi_0 \sin(\omega t - 90°)\sin(\theta)
$$

Using $\sin(\omega t - 90°) = -\cos(\omega t)$:

$$
\phi_{res}(\theta, t) = \phi_0 \sin(\omega t)\cos(\theta) - \phi_0 \cos(\omega t)\sin(\theta)
$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the rotating field identity:**

Apply the identity $\sin(A - B) = \sin A \cos B - \cos A \sin B$:

$$
\phi_{res}(\theta, t) = \phi_0 \sin(\omega t - \theta)
$$

**Conclusion:** This is a flux wave of constant magnitude $\phi_0$ rotating in the positive $\theta$ direction at angular velocity $\omega$. The motor is now **self-starting**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Starting Methods

| Parameter | Split-Phase | Capacitor-Start | Capacitor-Run | Shaded-Pole |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Phase-splitting device** | Resistor (R) | Capacitor (C) | Capacitor (C, permanent) | Copper shading ring |
| **Phase angle (α) achieved** | **20° to 30°** | **80° to 90°** (can be made >90°) | **80° to 90°** | Very small (≈10° to 30°) |
| **Starting Torque** | Low (1.5–2 × F.L.) | Very High (3–4.5 × F.L.) | Moderate (0.5–1 × F.L.) | Very Low (0.5–1 × F.L.) |
| **Starting Current** | Moderate | Low (good!) | Low | Low |
| **Aux Winding Disconnected?** | **Yes** (centrifugal switch at 75% Ns) | **Yes** (centrifugal switch) | **No** (stays in circuit) | **No** auxiliary winding |
| **Power Factor at Start** | Poor | **Good** | **Excellent** | Poor |
| **Cost** | Low | Moderate | Moderate | **Very Low** (cheapest) |
| **Reversibility** | Easy | Easy | Difficult | Difficult |
| **Typical Rating** | Up to **0.5 HP** (≈ 373 W) | **0.5 to 10 HP** (≈ 373 W to 7.5 kW) | Up to **1 HP** (≈ 746 W) | < **50 W** (tiny loads) |
| **Typical Biomedical Use** | Bench-top stirrers, small fans | Compressors in ventilators, suction pumps | Ceiling/table fans, blowers | Small instrument cooling fans |

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Phase Angle in a Capacitor-Start Motor

The main winding has impedance:
$$
Z_m = R_m + jX_m
$$

The auxiliary (starting) winding has impedance in series with a capacitor $C$:
$$
Z_a = R_a + j\left(X_a - X_C\right)
$$

where $X_C = \dfrac{1}{\omega C} = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi f C}$.

The phase angle of the auxiliary current relative to the supply voltage is:
$$
\theta_a = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{X_a - X_C}{R_a}\right)
$$

The phase angle of the main current is:
$$
\theta_m = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{X_m}{R_m}\right)
$$

The net phase displacement $\alpha$ between the two currents is:

$$
\alpha = \theta_m - \theta_a
$$

For **maximum starting torque**, we require $\alpha \approx 90°$, which is achieved by appropriately sizing the capacitor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Design Tip:** A properly designed capacitor-start motor can have $\alpha$ slightly greater than 90°, producing a **revolving field with a reverse-rotating component**, but the **forward component dominates**, giving excellent starting torque.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Self-Starting SPIM System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[AC Mains Supply - 230V, 50Hz Single Phase] --> B[Centrifugal Switch - Normally Closed at Standstill]
    A --> C[Main Winding - High Inductance, Low Resistance]
    A --> B
    B --> D[Auxiliary Winding - Low Inductance]
    D --> E[Phase-Splitting Element: Resistor or Capacitor]
    E --> F[Rotor - Squirrel Cage Type]
    C --> F
    F --> G[Mechanical Shaft Output]
    F --> H[Sensor: Speed Increases towards 75 percent of Ns]
    H --> I[Centrifugal Mechanism Trips - Switch Opens]
    I --> J[Auxiliary Winding Disconnected from Supply]
    J --> K[Motor Continues Running on Main Winding Only]
```

### 4.2 Circuit Schematic (Mermaid Block Diagram) — Capacitor-Start Motor

```mermaid
graph LR
    L[Line - L] --> M1[Main Winding]
    L --> M2[Centrifugal Switch CS]
    M2 --> A1[Auxiliary Winding]
    A1 --> C1[Starting Capacitor]
    C1 --> N[Neutral - N]
    M1 --> N
    style M1 fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4,color:#000
    style A1 fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#FF7F0E,color:#000
    style C1 fill:#E8F8E8,stroke:#2CA02C,color:#000
    style M2 fill:#FDE8E8,stroke:#D62728,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Rotating Magnetic Field Formation — Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Main Winding Flux: phi_m sin wt along X axis] --> C[Vector Summation in Stator Air Gap]
    B[Aux Winding Flux: phi_a sin wt minus alpha along Y axis] --> C
    C --> D{Phase Displacement alpha = 90 degrees?}
    D -->|YES| E[Perfect Circular Rotating Field - Motor Self-Starts]
    D -->|NO - elliptical| F[Elliptical Rotating Field - Reduced Starting Torque]
    E --> G[Rotor Experiences Net Forward Torque]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Rotor Accelerates and Reaches Steady State Speed]
```

### 4.4 Decision Matrix for Selecting a Self-Starting Method

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start[Need: Make SPIM Self-Starting] --> Q1{Load Type?}
    Q1 -->|Very Small - lt 50W| P1[Shaded Pole Motor - cheap, low torque]
    Q1 -->|Small - up to 0.5 HP| Q2{Required Starting Torque?}
    Q1 -->|Medium - 0.5 to 10 HP| Q3{Starting Torque gt 3x FL Torque?}
    Q2 -->|Low - 1.5x to 2x| P2[Split Phase Resistance Start Motor]
    Q2 -->|Moderate| P3[Capacitor Run Motor - Permanent Capacitor]
    Q3 -->|YES - High Starting Torque Needed| P4[Capacitor Start Motor - Centrifugal Switch]
    Q3 -->|YES - High Starting and Run Performance| P5[Capacitor Start Capacitor Run Motor]
    P1 --> End[Selection Complete]
    P2 --> End
    P3 --> End
    P4 --> End
    P5 --> End
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Biomedical Context Note:** The shaded pole motor is used in **small medical device cooling fans** and **nebulizer air pumps** due to its extremely low cost and maintenance-free operation.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Why is a single-phase induction motor not self-starting? Explain briefly using the double revolving field theory.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A single-phase induction motor is not self-starting because the alternating flux produced by a single stator winding is **pulsating** (alternates in magnitude and reverses direction) but does **not rotate** in space.

According to the **Double Revolving Field Theory (Leblanc)**, this pulsating flux can be resolved into **two equal and opposite rotating magnetic fields**, each of magnitude $\dfrac{\phi_m}{2}$, rotating at synchronous speed $N_s$ in **opposite directions**. At standstill, these two fields produce **equal and opposite torques** ($T_f = T_b$), resulting in a **net starting torque of zero**. Hence, the rotor cannot start on its own. **(3 marks)**

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**List any three methods of making a single-phase induction motor self-starting.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three commonly used methods are:

1. **Split-phase (Resistance-start) motor** — A high-resistance auxiliary winding is used to create a small phase shift.
2. **Capacitor-start motor** — A capacitor is connected in series with the auxiliary winding to produce a near-90° phase shift, giving high starting torque.
3. **Shaded-pole motor** — Copper shading rings on the stator poles create a phase-shifted flux in a portion of the pole face. **(3 marks)**

*(Other valid methods: Capacitor-run, Capacitor-start Capacitor-run, Repulsion-start)*

---

### 📗 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

#### **OPTION A: Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Adaptation]**

**(a)** Describe the **construction and working of a Capacitor-Start single-phase induction motor** with a neat schematic. Explain the role of the centrifugal switch. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression showing that a **pulsating single-phase flux** can be resolved into **two counter-rotating fields** of equal magnitude. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A capacitor-start SPIM consists of:
- **Stator:** Laminated silicon steel core with **two windings** embedded in the slots:
  - **Main winding (M):** Highly inductive, low resistance, occupies the lower part of the slots. It remains in the circuit continuously.
  - **Auxiliary (starting) winding (A):** Physically displaced by **90 electrical degrees** from the main winding. It has low inductance and is connected in series with a **starting capacitor** $C_s$ and a **centrifugal switch (CS)**.
- **Rotor:** Squirrel-cage rotor — copper or aluminum bars short-circuited by end rings.
- **Centrifugal switch:** Mounted on the rotor shaft, **normally closed** at standstill.

**Working (3 marks):**
When the motor is energized:
- The main winding current $I_m$ lags the supply voltage by a large angle (highly inductive).
- The auxiliary winding current $I_a$ leads the supply voltage (capacitive circuit) or lags by a small angle.
- The **phase difference** $\alpha$ between $I_m$ and $I_a$ approaches **80° to 90°**, producing a strong **rotating magnetic field**.
- This RMF cuts the squirrel-cage rotor bars, inducing currents and producing a **high starting torque** (3 to 4.5 times full-load torque).
- The rotor accelerates. When the speed reaches **70% to 80%** of $N_s$, the centrifugal switch **opens**, disconnecting the auxiliary winding and capacitor.
- The motor then continues to run on the **main winding alone**, operating on the cross-field theory principle.

**Role of Centrifugal Switch (1 mark):**
The centrifugal switch ensures the **auxiliary winding and capacitor are disconnected** once the motor has started. This is necessary because:
- The auxiliary winding is **not designed for continuous operation** (it would overheat).
- The starting capacitor is **electrolytic** and would be damaged by prolonged use.
- Removing the auxiliary winding improves the running performance and power factor.

---

##### **Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Derivation: Double Revolving Field Theory**

Consider a single-phase winding producing a pulsating flux along the X-axis:
$$
\phi(\theta, t) = \phi_m \sin(\omega t) \cos(\theta) \quad \text{...(1)}
$$

Apply the product-to-sum identity:
$$
\sin A \cos B = \tfrac{1}{2}[\sin(A - B) + \sin(A + B)]
$$

Substituting $A = \omega t$ and $B = \theta$:
$$
\phi(\theta, t) = \frac{\phi_m}{2}\sin(\omega t - \theta) + \frac{\phi_m}{2}\sin(\omega t + \theta) \quad \text{...(2)}
$$

The first term $\dfrac{\phi_m}{2}\sin(\omega t - \theta)$ represents a **forward (CCW) rotating field** $\phi_f = \dfrac{\phi_m}{2}$.

The second term $\dfrac{\phi_m}{2}\sin(\omega t + \theta)$ represents a **backward (CW) rotating field** $\phi_b = \dfrac{\phi_m}{2}$.

Both fields rotate at angular velocity $\omega = 2\pi f$ rad/s (synchronous speed).

**Torque at standstill:**
The torque produced by a rotating field is proportional to the square of its flux magnitude. At standstill ($s = 1$ for both):
$$
T_f = T_b \quad \Longrightarrow \quad T_{net} = T_f - T_b = 0
$$

**Conclusion:** The single-phase pulsating flux is equivalent to **two equal, oppositely rotating fields** that produce **zero net starting torque**. This is why the motor needs auxiliary mechanisms to self-start. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **OPTION B: Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Adaptation]**

**(a)** With the help of a **phasor diagram**, explain the working of a **split-phase (resistance-start) induction motor**. Why is its starting torque low compared to a capacitor-start motor? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **construction, working and applications of a shaded-pole motor** with a clear diagram. Discuss why it is suitable for biomedical instrument fans. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Split-Phase Motor Construction (2 marks):**
- **Main winding (M):** Low resistance, high inductance, placed in the lower portion of the slots.
- **Auxiliary winding (A):** High resistance (often made of thin wire or with fewer turns), displaced 90° electrically from the main winding.
- **Centrifugal switch (CS):** In series with the auxiliary winding.

**Working with Phasor Analysis (3 marks):**
- Supply voltage $V$ is applied to both windings in parallel.
- **Main winding current $I_m$** lags $V$ by a large angle (highly inductive → high $X_m$). Let $\theta_m \approx 70°$ to $80°$.
- **Auxiliary winding current $I_a$** lags $V$ by a smaller angle (less inductive due to higher $R_a$). Let $\theta_a \approx 30°$ to $40°$.
- The **phase difference** $\alpha = \theta_m - \theta_a \approx 20°$ to $30°$.

```
       V (Reference)
       |
       |--- I_a (lags by ~30°)
       |
       |------ I_m (lags by ~75°)
       |
       Phase difference α ≈ 45°
```

This **small phase difference** creates a **weak elliptical rotating field**, producing a **low starting torque (1.5 to 2 × full-load torque)**.

**Why starting torque is low (2 marks):**
1. The phase displacement $\alpha$ is only **20° to 30°** (far from ideal 90°), resulting in a poorly rotating (elliptical) field.
2. The auxiliary winding has high resistance, leading to **high $I^2R$ losses** during starting.
3. The rotating field is not circular but **elliptical**, with a significant reverse-rotating component that reduces net torque.

---

##### **Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Construction of Shaded-Pole Motor (3 marks):**
- **Stator:** Salient-pole type (not distributed windings). Each pole has a slot cut across its pole face.
- **Shading ring (shading coil):** A short-circuited copper ring (also called a **shading band**) is embedded in the slot, encircling a small portion of each pole (the **shaded portion**).
- **Rotor:** Squirrel-cage rotor.
- **Single winding** on the entire pole, supplied from single-phase AC mains.

**Working (3 marks):**
- When AC is applied, an alternating flux $\phi$ is produced in each pole.
- This changing flux links the **shading ring**, inducing an EMF and a current in it.
- By **Lenz's law**, the induced current in the shading ring **opposes the change** in flux, **delaying** the flux in the shaded portion.
- This creates a **phase difference** between the flux in the **unshaded portion** ($\phi_u$) and the **shaded portion** ($\phi_s$): $\phi_s$ lags $\phi_u$ by a small angle.
- The result is a **shifting flux wave** that moves from the unshaded region to the shaded region across the pole face — equivalent to a **weak rotating magnetic field**.
- This produces a small **starting torque** (0.5 to 1.0 × full-load torque).

**Applications & Biomedical Relevance (1 mark):**
- Small cooling fans in medical instruments (X-ray machines, ultrasound probes, blood analyzers).
- Hair dryers, small pumps, record players, toys, instrument blowers.
- **Why suitable for biomedical fans:** Extremely cheap, maintenance-free (no centrifugal switch, no auxiliary winding, no capacitor), very small size, and operates silently. Ideal where high torque is not required and reliability is critical.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that COST MARKS in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing the direction of the two rotating fields** in the double revolving theory. Always state: forward field = $\sin(\omega t - \theta)$, backward field = $\sin(\omega t + \theta)$.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the centrifugal switch** in capacitor-start / split-phase answers — it is a **frequently asked 2-3 mark sub-part** and skipping it costs easy marks.
> 3. **Writing "90° phase difference" for split-phase motors** — this is WRONG. Split-phase achieves only **20° to 30°**; capacitor-start achieves **80° to 90°**.
> 4. **Omitting the phasor diagram** in split-phase / capacitor-start questions — KTU examiners explicitly award marks for the **phasor diagram** (typically 2 marks).
> 5. **Not stating the slip at starting** ($s = 1$) when deriving the torque expressions.
> 6. **Confusing the rotor frequency formula** $f_r = s \cdot f$ with the supply frequency.
> 7. **In biomedical applications:** avoid generic statements like "used in industries" — be specific to **medical equipment** (ventilators, centrifuges, infusion pumps) to demonstrate application-level understanding.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **single-phase induction motor** has a **single-phase stator winding** that produces a **pulsating (non-rotating) flux**.
- ✅ The **Double Revolving Field Theory** (Leblanc) resolves the pulsating flux into **two equal, counter-rotating fields** of magnitude $\dfrac{\phi_m}{2}$, each rotating at $N_s$.
- ✅ At standstill: $T_f = T_b$, so **net starting torque = 0** → **not self-starting**.
- ✅ Self-starting requires: **(i) spatial displacement of 90° electrical** between main and auxiliary windings, **AND** **(ii) temporal phase displacement** of currents.
- ✅ **Split-phase motor** uses **high resistance** in auxiliary winding → α ≈ 20° to 30° → **low starting torque** (1.5–2 × F.L.).
- ✅ **Capacitor-start motor** uses a **capacitor** in series with auxiliary winding → α ≈ 80° to 90° → **high starting torque** (3–4.5 × F.L.).
- ✅ **Capacitor-run motor** has the capacitor **permanently** connected → better power factor, smoother operation.
- ✅ **Capacitor-start Capacitor-run motor** uses two capacitors (one centrifugal-switched, one permanent) → best of both worlds.
- ✅ **Shaded-pole motor** uses **copper shading rings** → cheapest, smallest, lowest torque (0.5–1 × F.L.), used in tiny biomedical fans.
- ✅ **Centrifugal switch** operates at **70%–80% of $N_s$** to disconnect the auxiliary winding and starting capacitor.
- ✅ Synchronous speed: $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ ; Slip: $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ ; Rotor frequency: $f_r = s \cdot f$.
- ✅ For a **50 Hz, 4-pole** motor: $N_s = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500$ rpm.
- ✅ For a **60 Hz, 2-pole** motor: $N_s = \dfrac{120 \times 60}{2} = 3600$ rpm.
- ✅ **Biomedical applications:** ventilators (capacitor-start), blood centrifuges (capacitor-start/run), infusion pumps (capacitor-run), small instrument cooling fans (shaded-pole), surgical suction pumps (capacitor-start), nebulizer compressors (capacitor-start).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Split Phase and Capacitor Start Induction Run
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT411 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY FOR BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Split Phase and Capacitor Start Induction Run

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Single-Phase Induction Motors — The Foundation

A **single-phase induction motor** is an alternating current (AC) motor that operates on a single-phase supply. Unlike three-phase induction motors, a single-phase induction motor is **not self-starting** because the single-phase alternating current produces a pulsating magnetic field rather than a rotating magnetic field.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The pulsating magnetic field of single-phase AC can be resolved into two counter-rotating magnetic fields of equal magnitude. The resultant torque is zero at starting, which is why auxiliary starting mechanisms are mandatory.

### 1.2 Split-Phase Induction Motor — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A **Split-Phase Induction Motor** is a single-phase induction motor that uses two windings — a **main (running) winding** and an **auxiliary (starting) winding** — which are spatially displaced by **90 electrical degrees** in space. The two windings are supplied by the same single-phase AC source, but the auxiliary winding has a **high resistance-to-reactance (R/X) ratio**, causing a phase displacement of approximately **25° to 30°** between the currents of the two windings. This phase difference produces a weak rotating magnetic field, sufficient to provide starting torque.

### 1.3 Capacitor Start Induction Run Motor — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A **Capacitor Start Capacitor Run (CSCR) motor** (also called **Capacitor Start Induction Run** in simpler configurations) is a single-phase induction motor in which a **capacitor is connected in series with the auxiliary starting winding** to create a much larger phase shift (typically **70° to 90°**) between the main and auxiliary winding currents. This produces a near-circular rotating magnetic field, yielding **high starting torque**. The capacitor is disconnected from the circuit by a centrifugal switch once the motor reaches approximately **70% to 80%** of synchronous speed.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Two-Person Swing:**
> Imagine pushing a child on a swing. If you push the swing only when it is at the *exact same position* every time (single-phase, pulsating force), the swing will not start moving — it just wobbles. However, if **two people push alternately** at the right moments, the swing begins rotating (split-phase concept). Now, if you place a **well-timed mechanical spring** (the capacitor) to give an extra "boost" at the right moment, the swing starts smoothly with a strong push (capacitor start concept). Once the swing is going, the second pusher (auxiliary winding) can step away, and the swing continues with just one person (running mode).

### 1.5 Key Physical Quantities

- **Synchronous Speed:** $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$, where $f$ is the supply frequency (**50 Hz** in India) and $P$ is the number of poles.
- **Slip:** $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$, typically **2% to 5%** at full load.
- **Phase Displacement (Split-Phase):** **25° to 30°**
- **Phase Displacement (Capacitor Start):** **70° to 90°**
- **Typical Starting Torque (Split-Phase):** **1.5 to 2 times** full-load torque.
- **Typical Starting Torque (Capacitor Start):** **3 to 4.5 times** full-load torque.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Single-Phase Motors Are Not Self-Starting

A single-phase AC supply produces a **pulsating magnetic field**, which can be mathematically resolved into **two counter-rotating magnetic fields of equal magnitude** (Forward Field $B_f$ and Backward Field $B_b$) using **Fresnel's Double Revolving Field Theory**.

- At **standstill (s = 1 for forward, s = 1 for backward)**, the torques produced by both fields are equal and opposite → **Net starting torque = 0**.
- Once the rotor is manually rotated in either direction, one field dominates, and the motor continues to run in that direction.

### 2.2 Constructional Details of Split-Phase Motor

| Component | Description | Function |
|-----------|-------------|----------|
| **Stator** | Laminated silicon steel core with slots | Houses main and auxiliary windings |
| **Main Winding (Running)** | Heavy gauge copper wire, low resistance, high inductance | Carries current during running operation |
| **Auxiliary Winding (Starting)** | Thin gauge copper wire, high resistance, low inductance | Creates phase shift for starting torque |
| **Centrifugal Switch** | Mechanical switch mounted on rotor shaft | Disconnects auxiliary winding at ~75% of synchronous speed |
| **Rotor** | Squirrel-cage type (cast aluminum bars in iron slots) | Carries induced current, produces torque |
| **End Shields** | Cast iron frames with bearings | Mechanical support |

### 2.3 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **AC supply** is applied simultaneously to the **main winding** and **auxiliary winding** through the centrifugal switch (which is initially closed).
2. The **main winding current $I_m$** lags the voltage by a large angle (highly inductive, low R/X).
3. The **auxiliary winding current $I_a$** lags the voltage by a small angle (highly resistive, high R/X).
4. The **phase difference** $\alpha = \angle I_m - \angle I_a$ is between **25° to 30°**.
5. Two currents that are **temporally displaced** and **spatially displaced by 90°** (winding axis) produce an **elliptical rotating magnetic field**.
6. This elliptical field induces current in the **squirrel-cage rotor**, producing **starting torque** ($T_{st} \approx 1.5$ to $2 \times T_{fl}$).
7. As the rotor accelerates to about **70–80% of $N_s$**, the **centrifugal switch opens**, disconnecting the auxiliary winding.
8. The motor continues to run on the **main winding alone** using the pulsating field (now with a dominant forward component).

### 2.4 Capacitor Start Induction Run — Operating Principle

1. A **capacitor $C$** is connected in series with the **auxiliary winding** via the centrifugal switch.
2. The capacitor causes the auxiliary winding current $I_a$ to **lead** the main winding current $I_m$ by approximately **70° to 90°**.
3. This near-quadrature phase shift produces a **near-circular rotating magnetic field**.
4. **Starting torque is dramatically increased** ($T_{st} \approx 3$ to $4.5 \times T_{fl}$).
5. At **70–80% of synchronous speed**, the centrifugal switch opens, **disconnecting both the capacitor and the auxiliary winding**.
6. The motor runs on the main winding alone, identical to a split-phase motor in running mode.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does the capacitor increase starting torque?** Because the starting torque in a single-phase motor is proportional to $\sin(\alpha)$, where $\alpha$ is the phase angle between the two winding currents. For split-phase, $\sin(30°) = 0.5$, whereas for capacitor start, $\sin(80°) = 0.985$ — nearly **double the torque production capability**.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Units |
|---|----------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed | rpm |
| 2 | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Slip (per unit) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $T \propto \phi \cdot I_r \cdot \cos(\theta_r)$ | General torque equation (analogous to DC motor) | N·m |
| 4 | $T_{st} \propto I_m \cdot I_a \cdot \sin(\alpha)$ | Starting torque in split-phase and capacitor motors | N·m |
| 5 | $\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{X_m}{R_m}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{X_a}{R_a}\right)$ | Phase angle between $I_m$ and $I_a$ | radians / degrees |
| 6 | $X_c = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi f C}$ | Capacitive reactance of starting capacitor | ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 7 | $Z_a = \sqrt{R_a^2 + (X_a - X_c)^2}$ | Impedance of auxiliary branch with capacitor | ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 8 | $I_a = \dfrac{V}{Z_a}$ | Auxiliary winding current | Amperes (A) |
| 9 | $\cos\phi_m = \dfrac{R_m}{Z_m}$, $\cos\phi_a = \dfrac{R_a}{Z_a}$ | Power factors of main and auxiliary windings | dimensionless |
| 10 | $P_f = \cos\phi = \cos\!\left(\tan^{-1}\!\dfrac{R}{X}\right)$ | Standard power factor expression | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Capacitor start induction run motors are the **workhorse of biomedical and laboratory equipment** — used in **centrifuges, blood bank refrigerators, autoclaves, dental drills, and peristaltic pumps** — where high starting torque is critical to overcome static friction and load inertia.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Starting Torque in Terms of Phase Angle $\alpha$

The starting torque of a single-phase motor with two windings is derived from the interaction of the forward and backward rotating fields. The simplified empirical formula is:

$$
T_{st} = k \cdot I_m \cdot I_a \cdot \sin(\alpha)
$$

where:
- $k$ = machine constant (depends on design)
- $I_m$ = main winding current magnitude
- $I_a$ = auxiliary winding current magnitude
- $\alpha$ = phase angle between $I_m$ and $I_a$

**Step-by-Step Interpretation:**

1. The **main winding** has impedance $\vec{Z_m} = R_m + jX_m$ → current $\vec{I_m} = \dfrac{V}{\vec{Z_m}}$ lagging by $\phi_m = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{X_m}{R_m}\right)$.

2. The **auxiliary winding** has impedance $\vec{Z_a} = R_a + jX_a$ → current $\vec{I_a} = \dfrac{V}{\vec{Z_a}}$ lagging by $\phi_a = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{X_a}{R_a}\right)$.

3. The **phase angle between currents** is:

$$
\alpha = \phi_m - \phi_a
$$

4. With a **capacitor $C$** in series with the auxiliary winding, the new auxiliary branch impedance becomes $\vec{Z_a'} = R_a + j(X_a - X_c)$, where $X_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$.

5. The new auxiliary current leads the supply voltage by $\phi_a' = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{X_c - X_a}{R_a}\right)$.

6. The new phase displacement becomes:

$$
\alpha' = \phi_m + \phi_a'
$$

7. For **split-phase**: $\alpha \approx 30°$, hence $T_{st} \propto \sin(30°) = 0.5$.

8. For **capacitor start**: $\alpha' \approx 80°$, hence $T_{st} \propto \sin(80°) = 0.985$.

9. **Torque improvement ratio:**

$$
\dfrac{T_{st, \text{cap}}}{T_{st, \text{split}}} = \dfrac{\sin(80°)}{\sin(30°)} = \dfrac{0.985}{0.5} \approx 1.97
$$

**Conclusion:** The capacitor-start motor provides **approximately twice the starting torque** of a comparable split-phase motor.

### 3.2 Derivation: Synchronous Speed and Slip for a 4-Pole, 50 Hz Motor

Given: $P = 4$ poles, $f = 50$ Hz.

$$
N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P} = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}
$$

Assuming the rotor runs at $N_r = 1440$ rpm at full load:

$$
s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = \dfrac{1500 - 1440}{1500} = \dfrac{60}{1500} = 0.04 = 4\%
$$

This **4% slip** is typical for standard single-phase induction motors used in biomedical applications.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Split-Phase vs. Capacitor Start

| Parameter | Split-Phase Motor | Capacitor Start Induction Run |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Auxiliary winding resistance** | High (deliberately designed) | Moderate (with capacitor compensation) |
| **Phase displacement $\alpha$** | 25° to 30° | 70° to 90° |
| **Starting torque ($T_{st}/T_{fl}$)** | 1.5 to 2.0 | 3.0 to 4.5 |
| **Starting current** | Moderate (6 to 8 × full load) | High (high torque per ampere) |
| **Power factor at starting** | Low (0.4 to 0.5) | High (0.7 to 0.9) |
| **Cost** | Low | Moderate (additional capacitor + switch) |
| **Reliability** | High (simple construction) | Moderate (capacitor failure possible) |
| **Applications** | Fans, blowers, small pumps, washing machines (low-torque) | Compressors, refrigerators, air-conditioners, biomedical centrifuges (high-torque) |
| **Direction reversal** | Easy (swap auxiliary winding terminals) | Easy (swap auxiliary winding terminals) |
| **Running mode** | Main winding only | Main winding only (auxiliary disconnected) |

### 3.4 Derivation: Centrifugal Switch Activation Speed

Let the centrifugal switch activation speed be $N_{sw} = 0.75 \times N_s$ (typical design).

For a 4-pole, 50 Hz motor:

$$
N_{sw} = 0.75 \times 1500 = 1125 \text{ rpm}
$$

The slip at switching point:

$$
s_{sw} = \dfrac{1500 - 1125}{1500} = 0.25 = 25\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Note:** The centrifugal switch must **never open at standstill** (otherwise the motor will not start) and must **always open below synchronous speed** (otherwise the auxiliary winding will overheat and burn out within 15–30 seconds). A typical safe design ensures opening at **70% to 80% of $N_s$**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Split-Phase Motor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ACSupply[AC Single Phase Supply 230V 50Hz] --> MainBranch
    ACSupply --> AuxBranch
    MainBranch --> MainWinding[Main Running Winding High X Low R]
    AuxBranch --> CentrifugalSwitch[Centrifugal Switch Normally Closed]
    CentrifugalSwitch --> AuxWinding[Auxiliary Starting Winding High R Low X]
    MainWinding --> StatorCore[Stator Magnetic Field Elliptical RMF]
    AuxWinding --> StatorCore
    StatorCore --> RotorInduction[Induced EMF in Squirrel Cage Rotor]
    RotorInduction --> RotorTorque[Rotor Develops Starting Torque]
    RotorTorque --> Acceleration[Rotor Accelerates to 75 percent of Ns]
    Acceleration --> SwitchOpens[Centrifugal Switch Opens]
    SwitchOpens --> AuxDisconnected[Auxiliary Winding Disconnected]
    AuxDisconnected --> RunningMode[Motor Runs on Main Winding Only]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Capacitor Start Induction Run Motor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ACSupply[AC Single Phase Supply 230V 50Hz] --> MainBranch
    ACSupply --> AuxBranch
    MainBranch --> MainWinding[Main Running Winding Inductive Lagging]
    AuxBranch --> CentrifugalSwitch[Centrifugal Switch NC]
    CentrifugalSwitch --> Capacitor[AC Start Capacitor 80 to 300 microF]
    Capacitor --> AuxWinding[Auxiliary Starting Winding]
    MainWinding --> StatorCore[Stator Near Circular RMF]
    AuxWinding --> StatorCore
    StatorCore --> RotorInduction[Induced Current in Squirrel Cage]
    RotorInduction --> HighTorque[High Starting Torque 3 to 4.5 x Full Load]
    HighTorque --> Acceleration[Rotor Accelerates Rapidly]
    Acceleration --> SwitchOpens[Centrifugal Switch Opens at 75 percent Ns]
    SwitchOpens --> CapDisconnected[Capacitor and Auxiliary Winding Disconnected]
    CapDisconnected --> RunningMode[Single Winding Operation Main Winding Only]
```

### 4.3 Torque–Speed Characteristic Comparison (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SP[Split Phase Motor Curve]
        SP1[At Standstill Tst about 1.5 to 2 times Tfl] --> SP2[Linear Rise to 75 percent Ns]
        SP2 --> SP3[Switch Opens Slight Torque Dip]
        SP3 --> SP4[Steady State at Near Synchronous Speed]
    end
    subgraph CS[Capacitor Start Motor Curve]
        CS1[At Standstill Tst about 3 to 4.5 times Tfl] --> CS2[Rapid Rise to 75 percent Ns]
        CS2 --> CS3[Switch Opens Minor Torque Dip]
        CS3 --> CS4[Steady State at Near Synchronous Speed]
    end
    SP4 --> Comparison[Capacitor Start Provides Higher Tst but Similar Running Performance]
    CS4 --> Comparison
```

### 4.4 Phasor Diagram: Currents in Split-Phase and Capacitor Start Motors

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Vref[Reference Voltage V along X axis] --> ImVector[Im Lags V by phi m about 60 to 70 deg]
    Vref --> IaVectorSplit[Ia Lags V by phi a about 30 to 40 deg Split Phase]
    Vref --> IaVectorCap[Ia Leads V by phi a prime about 20 to 30 deg Capacitor Start]
    ImVector --> AlphaSplit[Phase Angle alpha about 30 deg]
    ImVector --> AlphaCap[Phase Angle alpha about 80 to 90 deg]
    IaVectorSplit --> AlphaSplit
    IaVectorCap --> AlphaCap
    AlphaSplit --> TstSplit[Tst proportional to sin 30 deg about 0.5]
    AlphaCap --> TstCap[Tst proportional to sin 80 deg about 0.985]
```

### 4.5 Centrifugal Switch Mechanism (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    RotorShaft[Rotor Shaft Rotating] --> GovernorWeights[Two Governor Weights Spring Loaded]
    GovernorWeights --> CentrifugalForce[Outward Centrifugal Force at High Speed]
    CentrifugalForce --> LeverMovement[Weights Move Outward and Push Lever]
    LeverMovement --> ContactOpen[Electrical Contacts Open]
    ContactOpen --> AuxCircuitBreak[Auxiliary Winding Circuit Breaks]
    AuxCircuitBreak --> SafeRunning[Safe Single Winding Running]
    LowSpeed[Low Speed or Standstill] --> SpringReturn[Spring Pulls Weights Inward]
    SpringReturn --> ContactClose[Electrical Contacts Close Ready for Next Start]
```
