- Must be connected to the **Protective Earth (PE) conductor** in Class I equipment to safely dissipate the fault current.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In biomedical engineering, the ECP concept is the cornerstone of *patient and operator safety* against **macroshock** and **microshock**. It directly governs the leakage current limits prescribed by **IEC 60601-1** (the "General Standard" for medical electrical equipment).

### 1.2 Related Conductive Parts (Distinguishing ECPs)

| Term | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Live Part (L)** | A conductor intended to carry current in normal service (line, neutral) | Copper conductor inside cable |
| **Exposed-Conductive-Part (ECP)** | A touchable conductive part that becomes live only under fault | Metal body of a defibrillator, X-ray tube housing |
| **Extraneous Conductive part (ExCP)** | A conductive part **not forming part of the electrical installation** that can introduce a potential | Building steel, water pipes, gas pipes |
| **Protective Earth (PE)** | Earth conductor used for safety, not a functional current path | Green-yellow wire |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Water Bucket Analogy"** 🪣
>
> Imagine a **steel water bucket** (the ECP) sitting on a wooden table. Normally, the bucket is dry and safe. However, a pipe (the live wire) passes through its wall. The insulation (rubber gasket) keeps water from leaking out under normal pressure.
>
> If the **gasket cracks (insulation fails)**, water (fault current) suddenly gushes into the bucket. If you touch the bucket, you get "shocked."
>
> Now, attach a **drain pipe (Protective Earth conductor)** from the bucket to the ground. The moment the gasket cracks, water rushes out through the drain to earth *before* it can harm anyone holding the bucket. This is exactly how ECP earthing works!

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Body Resistance (R_body, dry skin):** **~ 2,000 Ω to 100,000 Ω** (typical 2 kΩ hand-to-hand).
- **Body Resistance (wet/medical gel):** drops to **~ 300 Ω to 1,000 Ω**.
- **Let-go Threshold Current:** **~ 10 mA (AC, 50/60 Hz), 30 mA (DC)**.
- **Ventricular Fibrillation Threshold (Macroshock):** **~ 100 mA for > 0.2 s**.
- **Microshock Threshold (Direct cardiac contact):** **~ 10 µA (0.01 mA)**.
- **Standard Earth-Fault Loop Impedance (Zs):** typically **< 0.5 Ω** for hospital-grade TN systems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two-Way Classification of Equipment (Based on ECP Treatment)

**A. Class I Equipment (Earthed ECP)**
- ECP is connected to the **Protective Earth (PE)** conductor.
- A single fault (insulation failure) triggers a **high fault current** that operates the overcurrent protection (fuse/MCB) within milliseconds.
- Used for most stationary medical equipment (X-ray units, MRI, anesthesia machines).

**B. Class II Equipment (Double-Insulated)**
- ECP is isolated from live parts by **both basic AND supplementary insulation**.
- No PE conductor is required. A single fault does **not** create a hazardous voltage on the ECP.
- Marked with the **double-square symbol** `□`.
- Used in portable patient-connected devices where earthing continuity cannot be guaranteed.

**C. Class III Equipment (Battery / SELV)**
- Powered by **Safety Extra-Low Voltage (SELV ≤ 25 V AC or 60 V DC)**.
- No ECPs exist in the traditional sense.

### 2.2 Protective Earthing — The "Why" Behind It

1. **Step 1 — Fault Initiation:** A breakdown of basic insulation causes the live conductor to contact the ECP.
2. **Step 2 — Fault Current Path:** A low-impedance path is created: Line → ECP → PE conductor → Earth → Source neutral.
3. **Step 3 — Protective Device Operation:** The high fault current (typically hundreds of amps) instantly trips the MCB or blows the fuse.
4. **Step 4 — Touch Voltage Elimination:** Power is removed before the operator/patient can be exposed to a dangerous touch voltage for a harmful duration.
5. **Step 5 — Why this saves lives:** Even if a person touches the ECP, the parallel path to earth via PE has a much lower impedance, and protective devices disconnect the circuit within **≤ 0.4 s (medical IT systems)** or **≤ 0.2 s (high-current circuits)**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{touch} = I_{body} \times R_{body}$ | Touch voltage vs body current | V, A, Ω |
| 2 | $I_{f} = \dfrac{V_{phase}}{Z_{S}}$ | Earth-fault loop current | A |
| 3 | $Z_{S} = Z_{source} + Z_{line} + Z_{PE}$ | Total earth-fault loop impedance | Ω |
| 4 | $t_{disc} = \dfrac{k^{2} \cdot S^{2}}{I_{f}^{2}}$ | Adiabatic disconnection time (fuse) | s |
| 5 | $I_{touch} = \dfrac{V_{touch}}{R_{body} \vert\vert Z_{PE}}$ | Current through body when PE exists | A |
| 6 | $E_{lim} = I_{f}^{2} \cdot t \le k^{2} S^{2}$ | Energy limit of PE conductor | J |
| 7 | $I_{leak} = \omega C V$ | Capacitive leakage current | A |
| 8 | $P_{dissipated} = I_{f}^{2} \times Z_{PE}$ | Power dissipated in PE during fault | W |

> [!NOTE]
> In every formula above, **$\vert$ is rendered as `\vert` (LaTeX absolute-value bar)** to keep markdown table syntax intact. In handwritten solutions, use the vertical bar `|` for the parallel impedance symbol.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hospital Earthing Systems:** ECP classification directly drives the choice between **TN-S, TN-C-S, IT, or TT** systems. Modern ICUs use the **Medical IT (Isolated Terra) system** to limit touch voltages.
- **Medical Device Compliance:** IEC 60601-1 limits **Earth Leakage Current to ≤ 0.5 mA (NC)** and **≤ 1 mA (SFC)** for Class I devices.
- **Patient Applied Parts:** Type B, BF, and CF classifications depend on ECP design and insulation levels.
- **Defibrillation Proofing:** ECPs of patient monitors must withstand **5 kV defibrillator pulses** without becoming hazardous.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Touch Voltage Across an Earthed ECP During a Single Fault

**Problem Setup:** A Class I medical device is powered by 230 V, 50 Hz single-phase AC. The ECP is earthed via a PE conductor. Basic insulation fails, making the ECP live. The operator touches it.

**Given:**
- Source voltage: $V_{phase} = 230 \, \text{V (rms)}$
- PE conductor resistance: $R_{PE} = 0.3 \, \Omega$
- Earth-fault loop impedance (line + PE): $Z_{S} = 0.5 \, \Omega$
- Operator's body resistance (dry): $R_{body} = 2{,}000 \, \Omega$
- Operator's foot resistance: $R_{foot} = 1{,}000 \, \Omega$

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Compute the fault current through the PE conductor} \\[4pt]
I_{f} &= \frac{V_{phase}}{Z_{S}} \\[4pt]
I_{f} &= \frac{230 \, \text{V}}{0.5 \, \Omega} \\[4pt]
I_{f} &= 460 \, \text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 2: Compute the touch voltage on the ECP} \\[4pt]
V_{touch} &= I_{f} \times R_{PE} \\[4pt]
V_{touch} &= 460 \, \text{A} \times 0.3 \, \Omega \\[4pt]
V_{touch} &= 138 \, \text{V (rms)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 3: Compute the actual current through the operator} \\[4pt]
I_{body} &= \frac{V_{touch}}{R_{body} + R_{foot}} \\[4pt]
I_{body} &= \frac{138 \, \text{V}}{2{,}000 \, \Omega + 1{,}000 \, \Omega} \\[4pt]
I_{body} &= 46 \, \text{mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Commentary on Step 1:** The fault current of 460 A is huge, far above the let-go threshold. This is intentional — it instantly trips the 16 A MCB within a few milliseconds.

> **Commentary on Step 2:** Even though the line is 230 V, the operator only "sees" 138 V because the PE conductor drops the majority of the voltage.

> **Commentary on Step 3:** 46 mA exceeds the let-go threshold (10 mA) and is dangerously close to the ventricular-fibrillation threshold. Hence, fast disconnection (≤ 0.4 s) is critical.

**Step 4 — Disconnection Time Verification (Adiabatic Equation for a 2.5 mm² PE copper conductor):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{disc} &= \frac{k^{2} \, S^{2}}{I_{f}^{2}} \\[4pt]
t_{disc} &= \frac{(143)^{2} \times (2.5)^{2}}{(460)^{2}} \\[4pt]
t_{disc} &= \frac{20{,}449 \times 6.25}{211{,}600} \\[4pt]
t_{disc} &= \frac{127{,}806}{211{,}600} \\[4pt]
t_{disc} &= 0.604 \, \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Conclusion:** The protective device disconnects in **0.604 s**, limiting exposure well below IEC 60601-1 limits. Without PE, the touch voltage would have been **230 V** — lethal.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Leakage Current Through a Patient Connected to an ECP

A patient is connected to a monitoring device whose ECP has degraded insulation resistance $R_{ins} = 10 \, \text{M}\Omega$ to the patient applied part.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{leak} &= \frac{V_{phase}}{R_{ins}} \\[4pt]
I_{leak} &= \frac{230 \, \text{V}}{10 \times 10^{6} \, \Omega} \\[4pt]
I_{leak} &= 23 \, \mu\text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Biomedical Insight:** At $23 \, \mu\text{A}$, this current is **23× the microshock threshold (10 µA)** for direct cardiac contact. Hence, even seemingly "insignificant" ECP leakage can be fatal in a patient with an intracardiac catheter (CF-type applied part).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — ECP vs Equipment Class

| Equipment Class | ECP Present? | PE Connection | Insulation | Safe Under 1st Fault? | Safe Under 2nd Fault? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Class I** | Yes | Yes | Basic | ✅ Yes (via PE) | ❌ No |
| **Class II** | Yes | No | Basic + Supplementary | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Class III** | No (SELV) | No | None required | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Internally Powered** | Varies | Varies | Varies | Depends on design | Depends on design |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — ECP in a Class I Medical Device

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SourceA["230V AC Mains Source"] --> MCB["Overcurrent Protection MCB 16A"]
    MCB --> BasicIns["Basic Insulation Between L and ECP"]
    BasicIns --> ECP["Exposed Conductive Part ECP Metal Chassis"]
    ECP -.->|"Fault Path Live to ECP"| FaultArc["Single Fault Insulation Failure"]
    FaultArc --> PECond["Protective Earth PE Conductor 2.5 sq mm"]
    PECond --> EarthBus["Hospital Earth Busbar"]
    EarthBus --> SourceN["Source Neutral Earthed Point"]
    ECP --> Operator["Operator or Patient Hand Contact"]
    Operator --> BodyRes["Body Resistance 2 kOhm Dry"]
    BodyRes --> Floor["Floor / Return Path"]
    Floor --> EarthBus

    classDef safetyCritical fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#b30000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef normalPart fill:#d6f0ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef faultPath fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#806600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class ECP,Operator safetyCritical
    class SourceA,MCB,BasicIns,BodyRes,Floor,PECond,EarthBus,SourceN normalPart
    class FaultArc faultPath
```

### 4.2 Hierarchical Classification of Conductive Parts

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Conductive Parts in Electrical Installation"] --> B["Live Parts L"]
    A --> C["Exposed Conductive Parts ECP"]
    A --> D["Extraneous Conductive Parts ExCP"]

    B --> B1["Line Conductor Phase"]
    B --> B2["Neutral Conductor N"]

    C --> C1["Metal Chassis of Class I Device"]
    C --> C2["X Ray Tube Housing"]
    C --> C3["Defibrillator Handle"]
    C --> C4["Surgical Lamp Reflector"]

    D --> D1["Water Pipe in Building"]
    D --> D2["Structural Steel Beam"]
    D --> D3["Gas Pipeline"]
    D --> D4["Radiator"]

    C1 --> E["Must be Bonded to PE Conductor"]
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> E
    C4 --> E

    D1 --> F["Must be Equipotentially Bonded"]
    D2 --> F
    D3 --> F
    D4 --> F

    classDef liveStyle fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ecpStyle fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef excpStyle fill:#e0ccff,stroke:#330099,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef actionStyle fill:#d9f2d9,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class B,B1,B2 liveStyle
    class C,C1,C2,C3,C4 ecpStyle
    class D,D1,D2,D3,D4 excpStyle
    class E,F actionStyle
```

### 4.3 Insulation Hierarchy Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Live Conductor L"] -->|"Basic Insulation"| B["Intermediate Conductor"]
    B -->|"Supplementary Insulation"| C["ECP Metal Chassis"]

    A2["Live Conductor L"] -->|"Double Insulation Basic plus Supplementary"| C2["ECP Metal Chassis Class II"]

    A3["SELV Source ≤ 25V AC"] --> C3["Touchable Part Class III No ECP Concept"]

    classDef insul fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ecpBox fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef safe fill:#d9f2d9,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class A,B,A2,C2,A3,C3 ecpBox
    class C insul
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]**
**Define an Exposed-Conductive-Part (ECP). Why must it be connected to a Protective Earth (PE) conductor in Class I medical equipment?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) **Definition:** An Exposed-Conductive-Part is a touchable conductive part of electrical equipment that is not normally live but can become live under a single fault condition (e.g., insulation failure).
- (1 mark) **Why PE connection is required:** When basic insulation fails, the ECP becomes live. If connected to PE, a low-impedance fault path is created, allowing a large fault current to flow that instantly trips the protective device.
- (1 mark) **Biomedical relevance:** This prevents dangerous touch voltages and limits exposure to **macroshock** (operators) and **microshock** (patients with applied parts) in the hospital environment.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]**
**Distinguish between an Exposed-Conductive-Part (ECP) and an Extraneous Conductive Part (ExCP) with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | ECP | ExCP |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Part of the electrical equipment that becomes live on insulation failure | Conductive part **not** part of the electrical installation |
| Examples (1 mark each) | Metal chassis of an ECG machine, X-ray tube housing | Hospital water pipe, structural steel beam |
| Treatment | Bonded to PE conductor | Equipotentially bonded to main earth busbar |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain the role of Protective Earthing in ensuring the safety of an Exposed-Conductive-Part (ECP) in a Class I medical device. Derive the expression for touch voltage during a single insulation fault. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, classify the various conductive parts encountered in a hospital electrical installation. Discuss how each is treated for safety. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Protective Earthing and Touch Voltage Derivation (7 marks):**

- **(2 marks) Concept:** Protective Earthing connects all ECPs of Class I equipment to the hospital earth busbar via the PE conductor. Its purpose is to provide a **low-impedance fault path** so that the resulting high fault current operates the overcurrent protective device (MCB/fuse) within a stipulated disconnection time (≤ 0.4 s in medical IT systems, ≤ 5 s in TN systems for final circuits).

- **(3 marks) Derivation:** Refer to the derivation in Section 3.1 above. The touch voltage on the ECP is $V_{touch} = I_{f} \times R_{PE}$. With $I_{f} = \dfrac{V_{phase}}{Z_{S}}$ and $Z_{S} = Z_{source} + Z_{line} + Z_{PE}$, we obtain $V_{touch} = \dfrac{V_{phase} \cdot R_{PE}}{Z_{S}}$. For a 230 V system with $R_{PE} = 0.3 \, \Omega$ and $Z_{S} = 0.5 \, \Omega$, $V_{touch} = 138 \, \text{V}$, which would otherwise have been the full 230 V.

- **(2 marks) Biomedical relevance:** Even with PE, the disconnection time must be verified using the adiabatic equation $t = \dfrac{k^{2} S^{2}}{I_{f}^{2}}$ to ensure energy through the body stays below the let-go and fibrillation thresholds, especially when patients are connected to applied parts (Type B/BF/CF).

**(b) — Classification of Conductive Parts (7 marks):**

- **(2 marks) Live Parts (L):** Conductors intentionally carrying current (Line, Neutral). Always insulated; never touchable.
- **(2 marks) Exposed-Conductive-Part (ECP):** Touchable parts of equipment that become live under fault. Examples: ECG chassis, X-ray housing. **Treatment:** Bonded to PE conductor.
- **(2 marks) Extraneous Conductive Parts (ExCP):** Conductive parts not part of the electrical installation (water pipes, structural steel). **Treatment:** Equipotentially bonded to hospital main earth to prevent potential differences.
- **(1 mark) Conclusion:** Correct identification and bonding of ECPs and ExCPs is the foundation of equipotential bonding in operating rooms (OR), ICU, and CCU as mandated by **IEC 60364-7-710** and **IS 732**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** What are Class I, Class II, and Class III medical electrical devices? Compare them in terms of ECP handling, protective earthing, and insulation levels. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Class I medical device is fed from a 230 V, 50 Hz single-phase source. The earth-fault loop impedance is 0.4 Ω and the PE conductor resistance is 0.25 Ω. Calculate: (i) the fault current, (ii) the touch voltage on the ECP, (iii) the body current assuming $R_{body} = 1.5 \, \text{k}\Omega$, and (iv) comment on whether the operator is safe. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Equipment Classification (7 marks):**

| Class | ECP Treatment | PE Required? | Insulation | Safety |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Class I** (2 marks) | Yes — chassis is ECP | Yes (Mandatory) | Basic only | Safe on 1st fault (PE clears) |
| **Class II** (2 marks) | Yes — but double insulated | No (Forbidden) | Basic + Supplementary | Safe even on 1st fault |
| **Class III** (2 marks) | No (SELV ≤ 25 V AC) | No | None (source is SELV) | Intrinsically safe |
| (1 mark) | Examples: Class I → X-ray unit; Class II → infusion pump; Class III → handheld thermometer | | | |

**(b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

- **(i) Fault Current (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{f} &= \frac{V_{phase}}{Z_{S}} = \frac{230 \, \text{V}}{0.4 \, \Omega} = 575 \, \text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **(ii) Touch Voltage (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{touch} &= I_{f} \times R_{PE} = 575 \, \text{A} \times 0.25 \, \Omega = 143.75 \, \text{V (rms)}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **(iii) Body Current (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{body} &= \frac{V_{touch}}{R_{body}} = \frac{143.75 \, \text{V}}{1{,}500 \, \Omega} = 95.8 \, \text{mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **(iv) Safety Comment (1 mark):** An 95.8 mA body current is **at the ventricular-fibrillation threshold** (100 mA for > 0.2 s) and **10× the let-go threshold**. The operator is **NOT safe** unless the protective device disconnects within the prescribed adiabatic time. With 575 A fault current, a 16 A MCB trips in **< 0.1 s**, which limits electrocution risk to acceptable biomedical limits.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Forgetting to write the units (V, A, Ω)** in the final numerical answer — costs 0.5 marks each.
> 2. **Conflating ECP with Live Part** — examiners deduct up to 1 mark. Always clarify "touchable + normally not live + live on fault."
> 3. **In derivation, skipping the adiabatic time step** — without it, the touch-voltage calculation is incomplete.
> 4. **Drawing diagrams without proper labels** — ECP and PE labels are mandatory for the 2-mark block-diagram sub-question.
> 5. **Mixing up Class I and Class II definitions** — Class II uses **supplementary + basic**, NOT just supplementary.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **ECP = touchable, normally not live, live on single fault** — core definition.
- [x] ECP must be **bonded to PE** in Class I devices.
- [x] **Class I** = PE + Basic Insulation. **Class II** = Double Insulation. **Class III** = SELV.
- [x] **Macroshock threshold ≈ 100 mA**, **Microshock threshold ≈ 10 µA** (cardiac).
- [x] **Touch voltage formula:** $V_{touch} = I_{f} \cdot R_{PE}$.
- [x] **Fault loop equation:** $I_{f} = \dfrac{V_{phase}}{Z_{S}}$.
- [x] **Adiabatic disconnection:** $t = \dfrac{k^{2} S^{2}}{I_{f}^{2}}$.
- [x] **Medical IT system** is used in OR/ICU to limit first-fault touch voltage.
- [x] **IEC 60601-1** limits Earth Leakage Current: **≤ 0.5 mA (NC), ≤ 1 mA (SFC)**.
- [x] **Type B, BF, CF** applied parts differ in F-type isolated (CF) for direct cardiac use.
- [x] Always distinguish **ECP (equipment part)** from **ExCP (building part)** in equipotential bonding.
- [x] Patient safety depends on **PE continuity**, **low leakage**, and **defibrillator-proof insulation** of ECPs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Extra-Low Voltage and Extraneous-Conductive-Part (EXCP)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT411 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY FOR BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Extra-Low Voltage and Extraneous-Conductive-Part (EXCP)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Extra-Low Voltage (ELV) — Formal Definition

**Extra-Low Voltage (ELV)** is defined under **IEC 60364** and adopted in the **National Electrical Code (NEC) of India (IS 732)** as a voltage whose steady-state open-circuit value does **not exceed**:

- **50 V AC (rms)** at 50/60 Hz, **or**
- **120 V DC (ripple-free)**

between conductors, or between any conductor and earth, in a circuit which is separated from the supply by a safety isolating transformer or equivalent means.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In a hospital/medical location context, ELV is the **first line of patient safety** because under physiological conditions the human body (especially a patient with breached skin resistance due to electrode gel, intravenous lines, or surgical incisions) can experience ventricular fibrillation at currents as low as **10 µA (10 microamperes) directly through the heart** — the so-called **microshock** threshold.

ELV is sub-classified into three protective categories:

| Sub-type | Abbreviation | Earthing of Secondary | Key Feature |
|----------|--------------|----------------------|-------------|
| Safety Extra-Low Voltage | **SELV** | **NOT** earthed | Double/Reinforced insulation; safest |
| Protective Extra-Low Voltage | **PELV** | **Earthed** | Same voltage limits, but exposed parts earthed |
| Functional Extra-Low Voltage | **FELV** | Earthing depends on system | Functional only, **NOT for protection** |

### 1.2 Extraneous-Conductive-Part (EXCP) — Formal Definition

An **Extraneous-Conductive-Part (EXCP)** is defined by **IEC 60364-4-41** and **IS 732** as:

> A conductive part **not forming part of the electrical installation** but liable to introduce an **electric potential** (usually the earth potential of the location) to which it is connected.

Common examples in a hospital/clinical setting:

- **Metal water pipes** (cold/hot water risers)
- **Structural steelwork** of the building (rebar, I-beams)
- **Metallic ventilation ducts** and air-conditioning trunks
- **Metal sink/trough frames**
- **Reinforced concrete flooring with exposed rebar**
- **Radiators, gas pipes, central heating systems**

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Story)

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Water Tap Trap" Analogy:** Imagine you are in an old hospital bathroom. The metal water tap is connected to the city's underground metallic water main. The electrical earth rod of the building is also buried in the same soil. Due to a fault in a neighboring building, the water pipe rises to **230 V** relative to true earth. You, standing barefoot on a wet tile, touch the tap. **The pipe is an EXCP** — it is *not* part of your hospital's wiring, but it *introduced* a dangerous potential to your hand. Current flows: **Tap → Hand → Body → Wet Feet → Earth → Back to source.** Your ordinary MCB will **not** trip because the fault current is bypassing your installation. This is why **equipotential bonding** of all EXCPs is mandated in medical locations.

### 1.4 Exposed-Conductive-Part (ECP) — The "Twin" Concept

For contrast, an **Exposed-Conductive-Part (ECP)** is a conductive part of electrical equipment that **can be touched** and is **not normally live**, but may become live under fault conditions (e.g., metal chassis of a defibrillator, motor housing). The ECP must be connected to the **protective earth (PE) conductor**; the EXCP must be connected to the **equipotential bonding conductor**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic:** *ECP is "Electrical-Circuit-Part" — connect to Earth. EXCP is "eXternal-Construction-Part" — connect by eXtra bonding (equipotential).*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of ELV Protection

The safety of an ELV system rests on **three independent barriers** — if any one fails, the next must still protect the patient/operator:

1. **Voltage limitation (≤ 50 V AC / 120 V DC):** Reduces the *available driving voltage* below the ventricular fibrillation threshold for the worst-case body resistance path.
2. **Protective separation (galvanic isolation):** The secondary of the isolating transformer has **no direct electrical connection** to the primary. A primary-to-secondary short cannot raise the ELV circuit to mains potential.
3. **Basic insulation on the primary + supplementary/reinforced insulation OR double insulation:** Two independent layers of insulation so a single fault does not energize the ELV side.

### 2.2 Why 50 V AC? — The Physiological Basis

The **let-go threshold** (the current at which a person can no longer release a grasped conductor) is approximately:

- **10 mA AC (50/60 Hz)** for adult males
- **20 mA DC**

Using the IEEE/BSI body impedance model, a current of 10 mA through a body-hand-to-hand resistance of **5 kΩ** (worst case for dry skin) requires a driving voltage of:

$$
V_{touch, \text{let-go}} = I_{let-go} \times R_{body} = 10 \text{ mA} \times 5000 \text{ }\Omega = 50 \text{ V}
$$

Therefore, **50 V AC** is the internationally adopted "safe upper limit" assuming reasonable skin integrity. For a **patient with microshock risk** (catheter, pacing lead), this drops to microampere levels — hence SELV with **< 5 mA** available current is mandated for patient-applied parts (per **IEC 60601-1**).

### 2.3 The Equipotential Bonding Principle for EXCPs

**Equipotential bonding** ties all EXCPs to a common equipotential bonding busbar (often called the "supplementary equipotential bonding point" in medical locations). This ensures that **under any single fault**, the touch voltage between simultaneously accessible EXCPs and ECPs is **reduced to ≤ 25 V AC or ≤ 60 V DC** in standard locations, and **≤ 10 mV** across the patient environment in medical Group 1/2 rooms.

The general expression for **body current under a touch voltage** is:

$$
I_{body} = \frac{V_{touch}}{Z_{body}}
$$

where $Z_{body}$ is the **body impedance** (function of voltage, frequency, skin moisture, current path). IEC 60479-1 tabulates $Z_{body}$:

| Touch Voltage $V_{touch}$ (AC rms) | Body Impedance $Z_{body}$ (≈ 5th percentile) |
|:-----------------------------------:|:---------------------------------------------:|
| **25 V** | **≈ 3 250 Ω** |
| **50 V** | **≈ 2 000 Ω** |
| **100 V** | **≈ 1 200 Ω** |
| **230 V** | **≈ 800 Ω** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice the impedance is **not** linear — it drops as voltage rises due to skin breakdown. This is why "doubling the safety margin" of voltage is more effective than doubling conductor cross-section.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **one-stop cheat sheet** for numerical problems in this topic. **Commit to memory** before the ESE.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Units | Boundary / Notes |
|:-:|:------------------|:-------------:|:-----:|:-----------------|
| 1 | **ELV limit (AC)** | $V_{ELV,AC} \le 50$ | V rms | 50/60 Hz, sinusoidal |
| 2 | **ELV limit (DC)** | $V_{ELV,DC} \le 120$ | V DC | Ripple-free (≤ 10% ripple) |
| 3 | **Body current (Ohm's law for body)** | $I_{body} = V_{touch} / Z_{body}$ | A | $Z_{body}$ from IEC 60479-1 |
| 4 | **Touch voltage in faulted installation** | $V_{touch} = I_{fault} \times Z_{bonding}$ | V | $Z_{bonding}$ = impedance of bonding loop |
| 5 | **Let-go current threshold (AC)** | $I_{let\text{-}go} \approx 10$ | mA | Adult, hand-grasp |
| 6 | **Let-go current threshold (DC)** | $I_{let\text{-}go} \approx 20$ | mA | Adult |
| 7 | **Ventricular fibrillation threshold** | $I_{fib} \approx 100$ | mA | For ≥ 1 s through chest, 50 Hz |
| 8 | **Microshock threshold (patient)** | $I_{micro} \approx 10$ | µA | Directly to myocardium via catheter |
| 9 | **Microshock limit (medical SELV)** | $I_{patient} \le 10$ | µA | IEC 60601-1 patient auxiliary current |
| 10 | **Touch voltage limit (medical equipotential)** | $\Delta V_{touch} \le 10$ | mV | Across patient environment, Group 1/2 |
| 11 | **Resistance of ELV source (max)** | $R_{source} \le 5 \text{ mA} / V_{rated}$ | Ω | Limits available fault current |
| 12 | **Cross-sectional area of bonding conductor (Cu)** | $A \ge 6$ | mm² | Medical equipotential bonding, IS 732 |
| 13 | **ELV isolation test voltage (primary-secondary)** | $V_{test} = 4 \text{ kV}$ | V rms | 1 min dielectric test, IEC 61558-1 |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Biomedical equipment design:** Patient monitors, infusion pumps, and ECG machines use SELV internally so a single insulation failure cannot energize the patient leads.
- **Medical-IT (isolated power) systems in operation theatres:** Use isolation transformers with unearthed secondary, monitor **line-isolation monitors (LIM)** to detect first-fault currents, paired with **equipotential bonding of every EXCP** in the room.
- **Wet locations (hydrotherapy, dialysis):** ELV is mandatory for any hand-held or patient-contact equipment.
- **Hospital-grade outlets:** Marked with green dot, but the *circuit* they feed may still be 230 V — true ELV devices always carry a **transformer** nearby.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Why 50 V AC is the Universal ELV Limit

We derive the **maximum permissible touch voltage** from the *let-go threshold* and the *body impedance* model.

**Step 1:** State the safety criterion.
The body current $I_{body}$ must remain **below the let-go threshold** $I_{L} = 10 \text{ mA}$ so a person who grasps a live conductor can still release it.

**Step 2:** Apply Ohm's law across the body.
The body acts as a non-linear impedance $Z_{body}$ between the contact points.

$$
I_{body} = \frac{V_{touch}}{Z_{body}}
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for the maximum permissible $V_{touch}$.
Re-arranging for $V_{touch}$ and substituting the safety limit $I_{body} = I_L$:

$$
V_{touch, \text{max}} = I_L \times Z_{body}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute standard tabulated values.
At 50 V AC, the 5th-percentile body impedance (hand-to-hand, dry skin) is approximately $Z_{body} = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$.

$$
V_{touch, \text{max}} = (10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A}) \times (5 \times 10^{3} \text{ }\Omega)
$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate.

$$
V_{touch, \text{max}} = 50 \text{ V}
$$

This confirms that **50 V AC** is the harmonised upper limit. **Above this voltage**, the body impedance drops (skin puncture) and current rises faster than voltage, so a 100 V or 230 V touch is disproportionately dangerous.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Touch Voltage in a Faulted Circuit with EXCP

Consider a simplified single-line scenario. A line-to-PE fault occurs inside a piece of equipment in a hospital room, energising its metal chassis. The room has **two EXCPs**: a metal water pipe and a metal sink. Both are connected to the supplementary equipotential bonding busbar.

**Step 1: Define the circuit variables.**
- $I_f$ = fault current from line to chassis
- $Z_{bond}$ = impedance of the bonding conductor from chassis to busbar
- $Z_{EXCP}$ = impedance of the EXCP-to-busbar conductor
- $R_{body}$ = resistance of patient between hand (on chassis) and foot (on floor touching sink)

**Step 2: Write the loop equation for touch voltage.**
The touch voltage is the potential difference between the chassis and the EXCP accessible to the patient.

$$
V_{touch} = V_{chassis} - V_{EXCP}
$$

Under the fault, $V_{chassis}$ rises to $I_f \times Z_{bond}$, and the EXCP voltage $V_{EXCP}$ is held close to true earth by the bonding. The *residual* touch voltage is the **IR drop along the bonding loop**:

$$
V_{touch} = I_f \times (Z_{bond} + Z_{EXCP})
$$

**Step 3: Substitute numerical values for a hospital OR.**
Assume $I_f = 100 \text{ mA}$ (limited by a 30 mA RCD that fails to clear in time), $Z_{bond} = 0.1 \text{ }\Omega$, $Z_{EXCP} = 0.1 \text{ }\Omega$ (short Cu conductor).

$$
V_{touch} = (0.1 \text{ A}) \times (0.1 + 0.1) \text{ }\Omega
$$

**Step 4: Evaluate.**

$$
V_{touch} = 0.02 \text{ V} = 20 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 5: Compare to the medical equipotential limit.**
The medical-IT standard (IEC 60364-7-710) requires $V_{touch} \le 10 \text{ mV}$ in patient environment Group 2. Our $20 \text{ mV}$ **exceeds the limit** — meaning the bond impedance must be reduced (heavier conductor, $\ge 6 \text{ mm}^2$ Cu, and shorter path).

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Available Current from an SELV Source

IEC 61558-2-6 requires that the **internal impedance of an SELV source** be high enough to limit the output current under a single-fault short-circuit.

**Step 1:** Safety criterion.
The short-circuit current $I_{sc}$ of the SELV source must not exceed **5 mA** for voltages up to 50 V AC (or **12 mA** for DC).

**Step 2:** Apply Ohm's law to the worst case.
The maximum source voltage is $V_{ELV}$, so the maximum permissible source impedance is:

$$
R_{source, \text{max}} = \frac{V_{ELV}}{I_{sc, \text{max}}}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $V_{ELV} = 50 \text{ V}$, $I_{sc, \text{max}} = 5 \text{ mA}$.

$$
R_{source, \text{max}} = \frac{50 \text{ V}}{5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A}} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

**Step 4: Interpret.** An SELV source must inherently limit its output to $\le 5 \text{ mA}$ under any single fault. A small step-down transformer with high secondary impedance, or an electronic current-limiter circuit, achieves this.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — ELV Sub-types vs. EXCP Bonding

| Scenario | Is it ELV? | Earth on secondary? | Touch current limit | EXCP bonding required? | Patient-safe? |
|:---------|:----------:|:-------------------:|:------------------:|:----------------------:|:-------------:|
| Battery-powered torch (< 12 V) | ✅ | N/A (DC) | I ≤ 5 mA | ❌ | ✅ |
| Mains-fed SELV (laptop charger) | ✅ | ❌ | I ≤ 5 mA | ❌ (if SELV) | ✅ |
| Mains-fed PELV (industrial sensor) | ✅ | ✅ | I ≤ 5 mA | ✅ (if earthed) | ⚠️ Limited |
| FELV from autotransformer | ❌ Not protected | Depends | I ≥ 30 mA | ✅ Mandatory | ❌ |
| Operating theatre main supply (Medical-IT) | ❌ 230 V isolated | ❌ N/A | Monitored | ✅ + Reference earth | ✅ (with LIM) |
| Bathroom shaver socket (BS EN 61558-2-23) | ✅ | ❌ | 20 mA | ❌ | ✅ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — SELV Power Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    mains[Mains 230V AC] --> pri[Primary Winding]
    pri --> core[Isolation Transformer]
    core --> sec[Secondary Winding]
    sec --> elvcirc[ELV Circuit less than 50V AC]
    elvcirc --> devi1[Patient Electrode ECG]
    elvcirc --> devi2[Blood Pressure Cuff Pump]
    sec -.->.->|Double Insulation| barrier[Safety Barrier 4kV]
    barrier -.->.-> elvcirc
    elvcirc -.->.->|No Earth| noearth[Secondary NOT Earthed]
    devi1 --> patient[Patient Body Microshock Path]
    devi2 --> patient
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**
- The **isolation transformer** (centre) provides **galvanic separation** between mains and the patient side.
- The **double insulation barrier** (dotted line, 4 kV rated) ensures even a primary-to-secondary breakdown cannot energise the patient side.
- The **secondary is deliberately NOT earthed** (dashed line labelled "noearth") — this is the defining feature of SELV.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — EXCP Equipotential Bonding in a Medical Room

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    busbar[Supplementary Equipotential Bonding Busbar]
    busbar --> pipe[Metal Water Pipe EXCP]
    busbar --> rebar[Structural Steel Rebar EXCP]
    busbar --> duct[Metal AC Duct EXCP]
    busbar --> rad[Radiator EXCP]
    busbar --> gnd[Hospital Earth Electrode]
    ecp1[Defibrillator Chassis ECP] --> pe[Protective Earth Conductor]
    ecp2[Monitor Chassis ECP] --> pe
    pe --> busbar
    patient[Patient on Couch] -->|Touches monitor| ecp2
    patient -->|Touches radiator| rad
    busbar -.->.->|All EXCPs and ECPs at same potential| patient
```

**Interpretation:**
- Every **ECP** (equipment chassis) returns its fault current to the **busbar** via the protective earth (PE).
- Every **EXCP** (water pipe, rebar, duct, radiator) is bonded to the same busbar via a separate **equipotential bonding conductor** of at least **6 mm² Cu**.
- The patient, who may simultaneously touch a monitor chassis and a radiator, experiences **virtually zero potential difference** because both points are at the same potential.

### 4.3 Decision Flowchart — Is a Part an ECP or EXCP?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Is it a conductive part?] --> q1{Is it part of the electrical installation?}
    q1 -->|Yes, e.g. equipment chassis| ecp[It is an ECP]
    q1 -->|No, e.g. water pipe| q2{Is it liable to introduce a potential?}
    q2 -->|Yes, e.g. structural metalwork in soil| excp[It is an EXCP]
    q2 -->|No, isolated| notlive[Not a safety concern]
    ecp --> action1[Connect to PE conductor]
    excp --> action2[Connect to Equipotential Bonding Busbar]
    notlive --> action3[No special action required]
```

### 4.4 Circuit Schematic — Fault Current Path with Bonding

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L[Line 230V] --> X[Equipment Internal Fault]
    X --> chassis[Equipment Chassis ECP]
    chassis -->|PE conductor, Z_bond| busbar[Equipotential Busbar]
    busbar -->|Bonding, Z_EXCP| pipe[Water Pipe EXCP]
    pipe --> earth[Hospital Earth]
    L --> earth
    busbar --> patient[Patient Touch Path]
    patient --> pipe
```

**Interpretation:** A line-to-chassis fault current flows: L → chassis → PE → busbar → bonding conductor → EXCP → earth → back to source. The patient, who bridges the chassis and the EXCP, only "sees" the **voltage drop along the bonding loop** (which we computed as 20 mV in Derivation 2, reducible to < 10 mV with proper design).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** Define **Extra-Low Voltage (ELV)** as per IEC 60364. List its three sub-classifications.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Extra-Low Voltage is a voltage whose steady-state open-circuit value does not exceed **50 V AC (rms)** or **120 V DC (ripple-free)** in a circuit isolated from the supply mains by a safety isolating transformer or equivalent means. *(2 marks for definition)*
The three sub-classifications are:
1. **Safety Extra-Low Voltage (SELV)** — secondary not earthed; safest.
2. **Protective Extra-Low Voltage (PELV)** — secondary earthed for operational reasons.
3. **Functional Extra-Low Voltage (FELV)** — functional use only, not a safety measure. *(1 mark for the three names)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** Distinguish between an **Exposed-Conductive-Part (ECP)** and an **Extraneous-Conductive-Part (EXCP)** with one example each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | ECP | EXCP |
|--------|-----|------|
| Definition | Conductive part of electrical equipment that may become live under fault | Conductive part **not** forming part of the electrical installation, liable to introduce a potential |
| Example | Metal chassis of a monitor | Metal water pipe, structural steel |
| Connection | Protective Earth (PE) conductor | Equipotential bonding conductor |

*(2 marks for distinction, 1 mark for examples)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **Module Choice — [KTU ESE Model Paper Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **Q A.** *(a)* Explain the **three essential requirements** for an ELV system to be classified as **Safety Extra-Low Voltage (SELV)**. *(7 marks)*
> 
> *(b)* With a neat diagram, describe the **equipotential bonding of EXCPs in a medical location (Group 2)**. Why is the touch voltage limit across the patient environment restricted to **10 mV**? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Three Requirements for SELV** *(7 marks)*

1. **Voltage Limitation (2 marks):** The nominal voltage of the SELV circuit must not exceed **50 V AC (rms)** or **120 V DC** between any two conductors or between a conductor and earth. This is the "extra-low" part of the definition.

2. **Protective Separation (Galvanic Isolation) (2 marks):** The SELV circuit must be supplied from a **safety isolating transformer** (or equivalent device like a motor-generator set, electronic converter with double insulation) such that there is **no direct electrical connection** between the primary and secondary windings. This ensures a primary-to-secondary insulation breakdown cannot raise the SELV side to mains potential.

3. **Insulation of the SELV Circuit from Earth and from Other Circuits (2 marks):** The SELV circuit must be insulated from both the **protective earth** and from any other higher-voltage circuit. The conductors of the SELV circuit must have **basic insulation** and be physically separated from those of other circuits, OR be enclosed in a multi-core cable with each conductor individually insulated.

*(1 mark for a small working diagram or final summary statement: "SELV = Voltage limit + Isolation + Insulation-from-earth")*

**(b) Equipotential Bonding in Medical Group 2 Locations** *(7 marks)*

**Diagram (3 marks):** Provide a labelled sketch showing:
- An operating table with a patient
- The supplementary equipotential bonding busbar (a copper bar on the wall)
- Connections from the busbar to: the OT table frame, the operating light housing, the pendant-mounted medical gas columns, the metal door frames, and the floor grid mesh.

**Explanation (3 marks):** In Group 2 locations (e.g., operating theatres, cardiac catheterization labs), an **additional supplementary equipotential bonding network** is mandated. This network interconnects all **ECPs** (via PE) and **EXCPs** (via dedicated bonding conductors of $\ge 6 \text{ mm}^2$ Cu). The network also includes the **anti-static floor grid** and the **OT table frame**. The intent is to ensure that during a first insulation fault in the medical-IT system, the patient lying on the table never experiences a touch voltage above the limit.

**Why 10 mV limit (1 mark):** A 10 mV limit corresponds (with body resistance $\sim 1 \text{ k}\Omega$ in this scenario) to a body current of **10 µA** — which is the **microshock threshold** below which ventricular fibrillation via a catheter or pacing lead is essentially avoided. Thus the equipotential bonding is sized to keep the patient current below the microshock hazard level.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**  *(Alternative choice)*

> **Q B.** *(a)* With the help of a labelled block diagram, describe the architecture of a **medical isolated power system (Medical-IT system)**. How does it enhance patient safety compared to a TN-S system? *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* A single-fault leakage of **30 mA** is detected on a piece of equipment in a hospital. The patient, lying on a trolley, simultaneously touches the equipment chassis and a bonded water pipe. The bonding-conductor impedance between the chassis and the water-pipe busbar is measured as **0.3 Ω**. Calculate the **touch voltage experienced by the patient** and comment on whether it meets the **Group 2 medical equipotential limit of 10 mV**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Medical Isolated Power System (Medical-IT) Block Diagram** *(7 marks)*

A **medical-IT system** is an **isolated power system** (IT = Isolé Terre) in which the secondary of an isolation transformer is **deliberately not earthed** (no neutral-to-earth connection on the load side).

**Block diagram components (3 marks):**
- Mains supply (TN-S) → 230 V/230 V **isolation transformer** (with monitoring winding) → IT secondary busbars (L1, L2) → distribution to OT sockets.
- **Line Isolation Monitor (LIM):** connected between the two IT lines and earth; continuously measures the **insulation resistance** of the IT system; alarms (does not trip) on first fault.
- **Equipotential bonding busbar** in the OT, connected to all EXCPs and to the earth reference point of the LIM.
- **Overload and overcurrent protection** in the form of insulation monitoring devices (not RCDs — RCDs would trip and cut power mid-surgery).

**Comparison with TN-S (4 marks):**

| Feature | TN-S System | Medical-IT System |
|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| Neutral-earth connection | Solid (TN) | **No neutral-earth on load side** |
| First-fault behaviour | Fault current flows → protective device trips → **power lost** | Fault current is **tiny** (limited by system impedance) → LIM alarms → **power continues** |
| Patient safety under first fault | Patient on table exposed until RCD trips (~30 ms); microshock possible | Patient experiences **< 10 mV** because the IT system limits the fault current to mA range |
| Continuity of supply | Lost on first fault | Maintained on first fault — **critical during surgery** |

**(b) Touch Voltage Calculation** *(7 marks)*

**Given:** $I_{fault} = 30 \text{ mA} = 0.030 \text{ A}$, $Z_{bond} = 0.3 \text{ }\Omega$, medical Group 2 limit $= 10 \text{ mV} = 0.010 \text{ V}$.

**Step 1: Touch voltage across the patient (Ohm's law on the bonding loop).** *(2 marks)*

$$
V_{touch} = I_{fault} \times Z_{bond} = 0.030 \text{ A} \times 0.3 \text{ }\Omega
$$

**Step 2: Evaluate.** *(2 marks)*

$$
V_{touch} = 0.009 \text{ V} = 9 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 3: Compare to the medical limit.** *(1 mark)*

$$
V_{touch} = 9 \text{ mV} \le 10 \text{ mV} \quad \textbf{✓ PASSES the Group 2 limit}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the resulting patient current and comment.** *(2 marks)*

With patient body impedance $R_{body} \approx 1 \text{ k}\Omega$ in this dry-skin scenario:

$$
I_{patient} = \frac{V_{touch}}{R_{body}} = \frac{9 \text{ mV}}{1000 \text{ }\Omega} = 9 \text{ µA}
$$

This is **just below the 10 µA microshock threshold** — a marginal but acceptable result. In practice, the bonding impedance is reduced to **< 0.1 Ω** by using shorter and heavier ($\ge 6 \text{ mm}^2$ Cu) conductors, ensuring the touch voltage remains well below 10 mV even for larger fault currents.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse ECP and EXCP.** Many students write "EXCP" when they mean "ECP". Remember: ECP is *Electrical-Circuit Part → Earth (PE)*. EXCP is *eXternal-Construction Part → eXtra bonding (equipotential)*.
> 2. **The 10 mV limit is a Group 2 medical limit, not a domestic limit.** Domestic TN-S systems accept 50 V touch; medical Group 2 insists on 10 mV. Writing "50 V" here will cost full marks.
> 3. **Microshock = 10 µA, macroshock = 10 mA** — do not interchange. Examiners specifically test whether you know the patient is *orders of magnitude* more sensitive.
> 4. **In Medical-IT, the first fault does NOT trip** — write "alarms" or "raises alert", not "trips the breaker". The whole point of IT is to *maintain* supply during surgery.
> 5. **In calculations, show units at every step.** KTU examiners deduct for unitless numerical answers.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] **ELV limit:** 50 V AC rms / 120 V DC (ripple-free), defined in **IEC 60364** and adopted in **IS 732**.
- [ ] **ELV sub-types:** **SELV** (no earth, safest), **PELV** (earthed), **FELV** (functional only, not protective).
- [ ] **Three pillars of SELV:** voltage limit + protective separation (isolation transformer) + insulation from earth and other circuits.
- [ ] **Let-go threshold:** 10 mA AC, 20 mA DC — this is the physiological basis for the 50 V AC limit.
- [ ] **Microshock threshold:** 10 µA directly to the heart (patient with catheter/pacing lead) — basis for the 10 mV medical equipotential limit.
- [ ] **Macroshock threshold:** 100 mA through the chest for ≥ 1 s → ventricular fibrillation.
- [ ] **ECP** = Exposed-Conductive-Part (e.g., equipment chassis) → connect to **PE**.
- [ ] **EXCP** = Extraneous-Conductive-Part (e.g., water pipe, rebar) → connect to **equipotential bonding busbar** (≥ 6 mm² Cu).
- [ ] **Medical Group 2 touch voltage limit:** **10 mV** across the patient environment.
- [ ] **Medical-IT system:** isolation transformer with **unearthed** secondary, LIM alarms (does not trip) on first fault — supplies operating theatres.
- [ ] **SELV source impedance:** must limit $I_{sc} \le 5 \text{ mA}$ → internal resistance ≥ 10 kΩ for 50 V sources.
- [ ] **Bonding conductor sizing (medical):** ≥ **6 mm² Cu**, short run to keep impedance low.
- [ ] **Key formula:** $V_{touch} = I_{fault} \times Z_{bonding}$ — minimize the loop impedance to stay under the limit.
- [ ] **Body impedance is non-linear:** drops from ~5 kΩ at 25 V to ~800 Ω at 230 V due to skin puncture.
- [ ] **Real-world example:** Shaver socket in a hospital bathroom is SELV (per BS EN 61558-2-23) — patient's wet skin + mains = lethal; SELV prevents it.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Grounding System - IT, TN-C, TN-C-S, TN-S, and TT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT411 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY FOR BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Grounding System - IT, TN-C, TN-C-S, TN-S, and TT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ⚡ MODULE 4: Grounding Systems in Biomedical Installations

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition — What is a Grounding / Earthing System?

A **grounding (earthing) system** is the deliberate electrical interconnection between an electrical installation and the general mass of earth, designed to ensure **personnel safety**, **equipment protection**, **fault current dissipation**, and **reference voltage stabilization**. The international standard **IEC 60364** classifies low-voltage distribution systems using a **three-letter code** that describes how the source, the neutral, and the exposed conductive parts (metal enclosures, chassis, patient beds) are connected to earth.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (IEC 60364 Nomenclature)**
> - **1st Letter** – Relationship of the **power system (source)** to earth: **T** (Terre = direct earth connection) or **I** (Isolated from earth).
> - **2nd Letter** – Relationship of the **exposed conductive parts (load frames)** to earth: **T** (directly earthed, independent of source earth) or **N** (connected to the earthed neutral of the source).
> - **3rd Letter** – Arrangement of **Neutral (N) and Protective Earth (PE)** conductors: **C** (Combined into a single PEN conductor) or **S** (Separated as distinct N and PE conductors).

## 1.2 The Five Standard Configurations

The five recognized configurations are: **IT, TN-S, TN-C, TN-C-S, and TT**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Building's Safety Net"

Imagine a **high-rise hospital** where every electrical wire is a tightrope walker.
- The **grounding system** is the **safety net** stretched beneath.
- The **first letter** tells you how the pole (the **source transformer**) is anchored: tied to the net (**T**) or floating in the air (**I**).
- The **second letter** tells you how the **patient bed** (the exposed conductive part) is anchored: tied to the net directly (**T**) or tied to the tightrope walker via a rope (**N**).
- The **third letter** tells you whether the safety rope and the safety net are **one combined rope** (**C**) or **two separate ropes** (**S**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Grounding is CRITICAL in Biomedical Engineering**
> - In **Group 2 medical locations** (operating theatres, ICUs, cardiac catheterization labs), a **single line-to-chassis fault must NOT disconnect supply** — continuity of power can be life-critical.
> - Medical **applied parts** (Type CF) may contact the patient's heart directly — leakage current must be limited to **< 10 µA**.
> - The **medical IT system (isolated power system)** is mandatory in such areas per **IEC 60364-7-710**.

## 1.4 Conceptual Overview Table of the Five Systems

| System | Source-Earth | Exposed Part-Earth | N & PE Arrangement | Typical Biomedical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **IT** | Isolated (or via high Z) | Directly earthed (T) | N/A | Operating rooms, ICU (Group 2) |
| **TN-S** | Directly earthed | Connected to source neutral | Separate PE & N | Hospitals general wards, Group 1 |
| **TN-C** | Directly earthed | Connected to source neutral | Combined PEN | **Not recommended** for medical |
| **TN-C-S** | Directly earthed | Connected to source neutral | Part combined, part separate | Utility supply side → Hospital intake |
| **TT** | Directly earthed | Independently earthed (separate electrode) | N/A | Rural clinics, outdoor equipment |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Resistance Values (must memorize):**
> - Maximum allowable **earth electrode resistance** for installations: **≤ 5 Ω** (medical locations may require **≤ 1 Ω**).
> - Standard **mains voltage** $U_0$ (line-to-neutral): **230 V** (single-phase) / **415 V** (three-phase).
> - Conventional disconnection time for final circuits ≤ **32 A**: **0.4 s** (TN) and **0.2 s** (TT with RCD).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 System-by-System Detailed Breakdown

### 🔹 2.1.1 The TN System Family (T-N)

**Operating Principle:** The source neutral is **directly earthed (T)** at the transformer. All exposed conductive parts (chassis, enclosures, medical equipment casings) are connected to this same earthed neutral point via a protective conductor (**N**). A **short circuit between line and exposed part** creates a high fault current (since it is essentially a line-to-neutral short), causing the overcurrent protective device (MCB/fuse) to trip almost instantaneously.

#### (a) TN-S System — Fully Separated N and PE

**Architecture:** The neutral (N) and protective earth (PE) are run as **two physically separate conductors** from the transformer all the way to every load. The PE is connected to earth at the source and at multiple points along the distribution, but **never carries load current**.

**Why It Matters for Biomedical:** This is the **gold standard** for hospital distribution downstream of the main intake. Because the PE conductor never carries load return current, there is **no standing potential** on equipment chassis — eliminating a major source of patient microshock risk.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Features of TN-S:**
> - **No PEN conductor** anywhere in the system.
> - PE conductor remains at near-zero potential under normal operation.
> - Suitable for installations with **RCDs (Residual Current Devices)** — no nuisance tripping.
> - **Mandatory** for Group 1 medical locations per IS 732 / IEC 60364.

#### (b) TN-C System — Combined PEN Conductor

**Architecture:** A **single conductor (PEN)** performs **both** the neutral function (return path for load current) **and** the protective earth function throughout the entire installation.

**Why It Is Discouraged in Hospitals:** The PEN conductor **continuously carries load return current**. This means the protective earth at every outlet is at a **small but non-zero potential** above true earth (typically 1–3 V). For sensitive biomedical equipment (ECG, EEG, defibrillators), this small potential can create **common-mode noise** and is a hazard for **patient auxiliary currents**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Alert:** TN-C systems are **prohibited in medical locations** with intracardiac procedures because the PEN conductor can carry hazardous voltages during faults in other parts of the building.

#### (c) TN-C-S System — Partially Combined (PME — Protective Multiple Earthing)

**Architecture:** The PEN conductor is used **on the supply side** (utility network) up to the building's main distribution board (MEN link), after which it is **split into separate N and PE** conductors throughout the installation.

**Why It Is the Utility Standard:** It is the most common arrangement supplied by electricity utilities (called **PME** in the UK / Ireland, **MEN** in Australia). It provides good safety at low cost because the utility only needs to run 4 wires instead of 5 for three-phase supply.

### 🔹 2.1.2 The TT System — Independent Earths

**Operating Principle:** The source neutral is earthed at the **transformer** (via the utility earth electrode), but each consumer installation has its **own independent earth electrode** (typically a driven rod or plate). The exposed conductive parts are bonded to **this local electrode**, not to the utility neutral.

**Fault Loop in TT:** When a line-to-chassis fault occurs, current must travel: Line → fault → chassis → consumer earth electrode ($R_A$) → soil → utility earth electrode ($R_B$) → transformer neutral. This is a **high-impedance loop**, so fault current is **small (typically < 100 A)** — **insufficient to trip an MCB**. Therefore, **RCDs (ELCBs)** are mandatory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RCD Sizing Rule for TT Systems:**
> The earth-fault loop impedance in TT is dominated by **two earth electrodes in series**, so MCBs cannot detect the fault reliably. A **30 mA RCD** is mandatory for socket outlets and **medical IT socket outlets**.

### 🔹 2.1.3 The IT System — Isolated (Medical IT)

**Operating Principle:** The power source (typically an **isolation transformer**) is **completely isolated from earth** — or connected via a very high monitoring impedance. The exposed conductive parts are earthed locally (T).

**The Defining Behavior:** A **single line-to-earth fault does NOT cause disconnection**. The system continues to operate, but an **Insulation Monitoring Device (IMD)** sounds an alarm when insulation drops below the threshold (typically 50 kΩ). Only a **second simultaneous fault** triggers disconnection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Medical IT is Mandatory for Group 2 Locations:**
> - **Continuity of supply** during a single fault — life-supporting equipment must not lose power.
> - **First-fault leakage current is limited** to < 1 mA (limited by insulation impedance and IMD impedance).
> - Provides a **"clean" reference potential** for sensitive patient monitoring equipment.
> - Required per **IEC 60364-7-710** and **IS 732** for operating theatres, ICU, cardiac catheterization labs.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Earth-Fault Loop Impedance & Disconnection Calculations**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbols / Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Earth-fault loop impedance (TT) | $Z_s = Z_E + R_A + Z_T$ | $Z_E$ = source earth Z (Ω), $R_A$ = consumer electrode R (Ω), $Z_T$ = line conductor Z (Ω) | TT loop calculation |
| 2 | Earth-fault loop impedance (TN) | $Z_s = Z_{line} + Z_{PE}$ | External + internal impedance | TN-S, TN-C-S |
| 3 | Prospective earth-fault current | $I_f = \dfrac{U_0}{Z_s}$ | $U_0$ = nominal line-to-neutral V (**230 V**), $Z_s$ = loop impedance (Ω) | All systems |
| 4 | Touch voltage (TT) | $U_t = I_f \cdot R_A$ | Volts | Patient contact safety |
| 5 | Disconnection condition (RCD) | $I_{\Delta n} \le \dfrac{U_0}{Z_s}$ | $I_{\Delta n}$ = RCD rated residual current (A) | Selecting RCD rating |
| 6 | Medical IT alarm threshold | $R_{ins} \le 50\,\text{k}\Omega$ | Insulation resistance | IMD alarm setting |
| 7 | IMD internal impedance | $Z_i \ge 100\,\text{k}\Omega$ | Per IEC 60364-7-710 | Limits first-fault current |
| 8 | Maximum first-fault leakage (medical IT) | $I_{leak,max} = \dfrac{U_0}{Z_i}$ | Amperes | Should be < 1 mA typically |
| 9 | Conventional disconnection time (final circuit ≤ 32 A) | $t_{disc} \le 0.4\,\text{s}$ (TN), $0.2\,\text{s}$ (TT) | Seconds | Safety limit |
| 10 | Earth electrode resistance target | $R_{earth} \le 5\,\text{Ω}$ (general), $\le 1\,\text{Ω}$ (medical) | Ohms | Design verification |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Earth-Fault Current in a TT System

**Problem Statement:** A hospital ward has a TT system. The source (utility) earth electrode resistance is **$R_B = 2\,\Omega$**. The consumer (hospital) earth electrode resistance is **$R_A = 5\,\Omega$**. The impedance of the line conductor from transformer to the fault point is **$Z_L = 0.4\,\Omega$**. The nominal mains voltage is **$U_0 = 230\,\text{V}$**. A line-to-chassis fault occurs on a piece of equipment.

**Step 1 — Identify the fault loop path.**

The current path is: **Transformer secondary winding → line conductor → fault point → equipment chassis → PE conductor → consumer earth electrode ($R_A$) → soil → utility earth electrode ($R_B$) → transformer neutral (earthed).**

**Step 2 — Apply KVL around the fault loop.**

Let $I_f$ be the prospective earth-fault current. Summing voltages around the closed loop:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_0 &= I_f \cdot Z_L + I_f \cdot R_A + I_f \cdot R_B \\
U_0 &= I_f \cdot (Z_L + R_A + R_B)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
230 &= I_f \cdot (0.4 + 5 + 2) \\
230 &= I_f \cdot 7.4 \\
I_f &= \frac{230}{7.4} \\
I_f &= 31.08\,\text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the touch voltage at the chassis.**

The touch voltage is the voltage developed across the consumer's earth electrode — this is what a patient would experience if touching the faulty chassis while standing on the floor (assumed at true earth potential):

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_t &= I_f \cdot R_A \\
U_t &= 31.08 \times 5 \\
U_t &= 155.4\,\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify against disconnection limits.**

The standard **50 V AC** is the maximum permissible touch voltage for prolonged contact (medical environments may demand **25 V AC**). Since $U_t = 155.4\,\text{V} \gg 50\,\text{V}$, an **MCB alone will NOT protect** — an **RCD is mandatory**.

**Step 6 — Size the RCD.**

For an RCD with $I_{\Delta n} = 30\,\text{mA}$ to operate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_f &= 31.08\,\text{A} = 31{,}080\,\text{mA} \\
\frac{I_f}{I_{\Delta n}} &= \frac{31{,}080}{30} = 1036 \gg 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $I_f \gg I_{\Delta n}$, the **30 mA RCD will trip well within 0.2 s** — patient is protected.

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation — First-Fault Current in Medical IT System

**Problem Statement:** A medical IT system uses an isolation transformer with secondary **$U_0 = 230\,\text{V}$**, **50 Hz**. The IMD has an internal monitoring impedance **$Z_i = 200\,\text{k}\Omega$**. A single line-to-earth fault occurs on the secondary. Compute the first-fault leakage current and the touch voltage.

**Step 1 — Recognize that with no direct earth on the secondary, the only return path for the first fault current is through the IMD's internal impedance to earth (and back through the distributed insulation capacitance).**

**Step 2 — Apply Ohm's law for the worst-case fault.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{leak} &= \frac{U_0}{Z_i} \\
I_{leak} &= \frac{230}{200{,}000} \\
I_{leak} &= 1.15 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{A} \\
I_{leak} &= 1.15\,\text{mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with IEC 60601 patient leakage limits.**

- **Type B applied parts:** 100 µA normal, 500 µA single-fault.
- **Type BF applied parts:** 100 µA normal, 500 µA single-fault.
- **Type CF (cardiac):** 10 µA normal, 50 µA single-fault.

The 1.15 mA is the **total system leakage** (not through the patient applied part) — it is well below the threshold for ventricular fibrillation.

**Step 4 — Touch voltage on the faulty chassis.**

With the chassis earthed to the hospital's protective earth electrode ($R_A = 5\,\Omega$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_t &= I_{leak} \cdot R_A \\
U_t &= 1.15 \times 10^{-3} \times 5 \\
U_t &= 5.75 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V} \\
U_t &= 5.75\,\text{mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**

A touch voltage of **5.75 mV** is utterly negligible — patient microshock is impossible. The IMD continues to monitor and would **alarm the staff** so they can fix the fault, but **the supply remains ON**.

## 3.3 KTU Analytical Comparison Matrix — All Five Systems

> [!NOTE]
> **This matrix is the single most important table for KTU 2024 examination — memorize the qualitative comparisons.**

| Parameter | IT | TN-S | TN-C | TN-C-S | TT |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Source connection to earth | Isolated / via high Z | Direct (T) | Direct (T) | Direct (T) | Direct (T) |
| Exposed parts connection | Local earth (T) | To neutral (N) | To neutral (N) | To neutral (N) | Local earth (T) |
| Neutral & PE arrangement | N/A | Separate (S) | Combined (C) | Part-C, Part-S | N/A |
| Earth-fault loop impedance | Very high | Low | Lowest | Low | High (two electrodes) |
| Prospective fault current | < 1 mA (first fault) | High (kA) | Highest (kA) | High (kA) | Low (tens of A) |
| Disconnection on first fault | **NO** (alarm only) | **YES** (MCB trips) | **YES** (MCB trips) | **YES** (MCB trips) | **YES** (RCD trips) |
| RCD required? | Optional (IMD replaces) | Recommended | Avoid (nuisance trip) | Recommended | **Mandatory** |
| Continuity of supply on first fault | **YES** | NO | NO | NO | NO |
| Touch voltage on faulted chassis | < 50 mV | Tens of V | Tens of V | Tens of V | Up to $U_0$ |
| Suitability for Group 2 medical | **Excellent** (mandatory) | **Good** | **Poor (avoid)** | **Good** (utility side) | **Fair** (with RCD) |
| Cost of installation | High (transformer + IMD) | Medium | Lowest | Low–Medium | Medium |
| Susceptibility to EMC noise | Low (isolated) | Low | High (PEN carries current) | Medium | Low |
| Standard reference | IEC 60364-7-710 | IEC 60364 | IEC 60364 | IEC 60364 | IEC 60364 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🖼️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

> [!NOTE]
> **The following Mermaid block diagrams illustrate the topology of each grounding system. They are functional architecture diagrams, not circuit schematics, but they correctly represent the conductor arrangement and earth connections.**

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcIT["ISOLATION TRANSFORMER\nSecondary NOT earthed"]
    L1IT["L LINE"]
    L2IT["N LINE"]
    loadIT["MEDICAL EQUIPMENT\nChassis"]
    earthIT["LOCAL EARTH\nElectrode"]
    imdIT["INSULATION MONITORING\nDEVICE IMD"]
    paIT["PATIENT\nApplied Part"]
    
    srcIT --- L1IT
    srcIT --- L2IT
    L1IT --- loadIT
    L2IT --- loadIT
    loadIT --- earthIT
    loadIT --- paIT
    imdIT -.monitors.-> L1IT
    imdIT -.monitors.-> L2IT
    imdIT --- earthIT
    
    classDef source fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#d84315
    classDef load fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    classDef monitor fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    classDef earth fill:#d7ccc8,stroke:#3e2723
    class srcIT source
    class loadIT,paIT load
    class imdIT monitor
    class earthIT earth
```

> **Figure M.4-A — Medical IT System (Isolated Power System):** Note the **floating secondary** of the isolation transformer, the **dedicated IMD** continuously monitoring insulation between live conductors and earth, and the **local earth electrode** for exposed parts. First fault = alarm only.

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcTNS["DISTRIBUTION\nTRANSFORMER\nNeutral EARTHED"]
    L1S["L"]
    L2S["N"]
    PES["PE Separate"]
    loadS["MEDICAL EQUIPMENT\nChassis bonded to PE"]
    earthS["EARTH BUS\nat transformer"]
    
    srcTNS --- L1S
    srcTNS --- L2S
    srcTNS --- earthS
    L1S --- loadS
    L2S --- loadS
    PES --- loadS
    earthS --- PES
    
    classDef source fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#d84315
    classDef load fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20

    class L1S,L2S,PES conductor
```

> **Figure M.4-B — TN-S System (Separate PE and N throughout):** N and PE run as **two completely separate conductors** from the transformer. PE carries no load current and is held at near-zero potential.


> **Figure M.4-C — TN-C System (Combined PEN conductor):** A **single PEN conductor** carries load return current AND serves as protective earth. **Prohibited in critical medical locations** because chassis potential is not at true earth.
