# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]**
**Define the IT grounding system. Why is it preferred in Group 2 medical locations?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **IT system** is a low-voltage distribution arrangement in which the **power source (typically via an isolation transformer) is isolated from earth**, or connected through a very high monitoring impedance. The **exposed conductive parts** (equipment chassis, enclosures) of the installation are **directly earthed locally**.

It is preferred in **Group 2 medical locations** (operating theatres, ICUs, cardiac catheterization labs) for three reasons:

1. **Continuity of supply on first fault** — A single line-to-earth fault does **NOT** disconnect the supply. The fault current is limited by the IMD's internal impedance (≥ 100 kΩ) to < 1 mA, which is below the ventricular fibrillation threshold. Life-supporting equipment continues to operate while staff are alerted.
2. **Reduced touch voltage** — Because the first-fault current is so small, the touch voltage on a faulty chassis is negligible (typically a few millivolts), preventing patient microshock.
3. **Compliance with IEC 60364-7-710** — This standard explicitly mandates medical IT for Group 2 locations.

The system is monitored by an **Insulation Monitoring Device (IMD)** which sounds an **alarm** when insulation resistance drops below **50 kΩ**, allowing maintenance before a second fault occurs.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three reasons. Total = 3 marks.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
**Differentiate between TN-S and TN-C systems with respect to protective conductor arrangement.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | TN-S System | TN-C System |
|---|---|---|
| Protective conductor | Separate **PE** conductor throughout | **PEN** conductor (combined N + PE) throughout |
| Load current on PE | **Zero** (PE carries no return current) | **Yes** (PEN carries full load return current) |
| Chassis potential under normal operation | **At true earth potential** | **Slightly elevated** (1–3 V) due to PEN voltage drop |
| Suitability for biomedical | **Excellent** | **Poor — prohibited in medical IT areas** |
| Cost | Higher (5-wire three-phase) | Lower (4-wire three-phase) |

**Key Distinction (1 mark):** In TN-S, the PE conductor is electrically and mechanically **separate** from the neutral throughout the entire installation. In TN-C, the **PEN conductor performs both functions**, making the chassis susceptible to small standing voltages during normal load conditions — unacceptable for cardiac-protected areas.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Internal Choice Provision — KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern**

Students must answer **ONE** of the following two questions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the TN-C-S (PME) grounding system with a neat schematic. List its advantages and disadvantages (4 marks) and explain why it is the standard arrangement for utility supply to hospitals (3 marks).**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition & Architecture (3 marks):**
TN-C-S stands for **Terre-Neutral-Combined-Separated**. The system has:
- **T** — Source neutral is directly earthed at the transformer.
- **N** — Exposed conductive parts are connected to the neutral.
- **C-S** — The neutral and protective earth functions are **Combined in one PEN conductor on the supply side** (utility network), and **Separated into distinct N and PE conductors on the consumer side** (inside the hospital building) starting from the **Main Earthing Terminal (MEN link)**.

The **MEN link** (Multiple Earthed Neutral) at the main distribution board is the critical bridge where the PEN is split into separate N and PE conductors that are then run independently to all final circuits.

**Schematic Description (1 mark — refer to Figure M.4-D):**
- Utility transformer → 4-wire supply (L1, L2, L3, PEN)
- Service cable enters hospital main switchboard
- PEN conductor terminates at MEN link
- From MEN link onward: separate N busbar and PE busbar
- All sub-circuits use 3-wire (L, N, PE) arrangement

**Advantages (2 marks):**
1. **Cost-effective** — Utility only needs 4 wires for three-phase instead of 5.
2. **Reliable earthing** — Multiple earth electrodes along the network improve overall earth continuity.
3. **Simple conversion** — Easy to upgrade existing TN-C areas by adding an MEN link.
4. **Good voltage regulation** — Single neutral conductor has lower impedance.

**Disadvantages (2 marks):**
1. **Risk of open PEN** — If the PEN conductor breaks between transformer and MEN link, the chassis of all connected equipment rises to line potential (230 V). This is called the **"lost neutral" hazard** and is dangerous.
2. **Not suitable for cardiac-protected areas downstream** without further conversion to TN-S or IT.
3. **Harmonics and EMC issues** — PEN carries triplen harmonics that can interfere with sensitive medical equipment.

**Why Standard for Hospital Utility Supply (3 marks):**
1. Most electrical utilities worldwide use TN-C-S as their standard distribution topology — hospitals receive the same supply.
2. At the hospital main intake, the MEN link splits the conductors, allowing the **internal hospital distribution to be TN-S** (separate PE), which is the safe hospital standard.
3. Cost-efficient for the utility, while still providing safe conditions inside the hospital.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**For a hospital TT system, $R_B$ (source earth) = 2 Ω, $R_A$ (consumer earth) = 4 Ω, line impedance $Z_L = 0.5\,\Omega$, and mains voltage $U_0 = 230\,\text{V}$. Compute the earth fault current, touch voltage, and verify whether a 30 mA RCD will protect within 0.2 s.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identify the fault loop:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_s &= Z_L + R_A + R_B \\
Z_s &= 0.5 + 4 + 2 = 6.5\,\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Earth fault current:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_f &= \frac{U_0}{Z_s} \\
I_f &= \frac{230}{6.5} \\
I_f &= 35.38\,\text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Touch voltage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_t &= I_f \cdot R_A \\
U_t &= 35.38 \times 4 \\
U_t &= 141.54\,\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification of MCB-only protection:**

A typical 32 A Type-C MCB requires approximately **5× rated current = 160 A** to trip within 0.2 s. Since $I_f = 35.38\,\text{A} \ll 160\,\text{A}$, the **MCB will NOT trip** on this fault. **MCB-only protection is INADEQUATE.**

**Step 5 — RCD verification:**

A 30 mA RCD operates when the residual current exceeds 30 mA. Since $I_f = 35{,}380\,\text{mA} \gg 30\,\text{mA}$, the **RCD will trip within 30 ms** — well within the 0.2 s requirement.

**Conclusion:** The **30 mA RCD provides effective protection**; MCB alone would not. **TT systems MUST use RCDs.**

*(Examiner's note: 7 marks breakdown — 2 marks for loop calculation, 2 marks for touch voltage, 2 marks for MCB inadequacy analysis, 1 mark for RCD verification & conclusion.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With a neat schematic, explain the IT system used in medical locations. What is the role of the Insulation Monitoring Device (IMD)? Mention the standards that mandate medical IT systems.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
The **medical IT system** is a small, locally isolated power system used in Group 2 medical locations where continuity of supply is critical for patient safety.

**Schematic Components (3 marks — refer to Figure M.4-F):**
1. **Isolation Transformer** (typically 3.15 kVA, 5 kVA, or 7.5 kVA, single-phase 230 V/230 V) — primary connected to hospital TN-S mains; secondary has **no direct connection to earth**. Provides galvanic isolation between mains and patient environment.
2. **Overcurrent Protection** on the primary side of the transformer (MCB sized at 125% of rated primary current).
3. **Insulation Monitoring Device (IMD)** — connected between the IT secondary lines (L and N) and the local earth electrode. Continuously measures the insulation resistance $R_{ins}$.
4. **Alarm Panel** — Visual (LED) and Audible (buzzer) indicators, usually mounted at the nurse station.
5. **Equipotential Bonding Busbar** — connects all exposed conductive parts (OT table, surgical lights, monitor chassis, building structural metal) to a common reference.
6. **Reference earth electrode** (typically ≤ 1 Ω) for the IT system's equipotential bonding.

**Role of the IMD (2 marks):**
- Continuously injects a low-frequency measuring signal between L/N and earth.
- Detects leakage current equivalent to insulation resistance dropping below the **alarm threshold of 50 kΩ**.
- Triggers an **audible + visual alarm** at the nurse station when threshold is crossed.
- Does **NOT disconnect supply** — fault is annunciated only.
- Internal impedance $Z_i \ge 100\,\text{k}\Omega$ ensures first-fault current is limited to < 2.3 mA (well below ventricular fibrillation threshold).

**Standards Mandating Medical IT (1 mark):**
- **IEC 60364-7-710** — Medical locations (international)
- **IS 732** — Code of practice for electrical wiring installations (India)
- **IEC 60601-1** — Medical electrical equipment safety
- **NFPA 99** — Health Care Facilities Code (USA reference)

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compare all five grounding systems (IT, TN-C, TN-C-S, TN-S, TT) in a tabular form. Discuss the suitability of each for biomedical installations with specific examples.**

**Model Answer:**

**Tabular Comparison (4 marks — Table 3.3 above summarized):**

| Feature | IT | TN-S | TN-C | TN-C-S | TT |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Earth-fault loop | Very high | Low | Lowest | Low | High |
| Disconnection on fault | No (alarm) | Yes (MCB) | Yes (MCB) | Yes (MCB) | Yes (RCD) |
| First-fault leakage | < 1 mA | kA range | kA range | kA range | 30–50 A |
| RCD needed | Optional | Recommended | Avoid | Recommended | Mandatory |
| Biomedical suitability | **Group 2** | **Group 1** | **Avoid** | **Utility intake** | **Outdoor / rural** |

**Biomedical Suitability Discussion (3 marks):**

1. **IT — Operating Theatres, ICU, Catheterization Labs (Group 2):** Mandatory because a single fault must not interrupt power. The IMD alerts staff while supply continues. Used for surgical lights, anesthesia workstations, heart-lung machines.

2. **TN-S — General Hospital Wards, Group 1 Locations:** Standard for distribution inside the hospital from main intake onward. PE conductor stays at near-zero potential, providing safe patient and staff environment. Used for bed-head panels, monitoring equipment, general lighting.

3. **TN-C — NOT Recommended for Biomedical:** Prohibited in medical IT areas because PEN carries load current. Would create standing voltages on chassis and EMC interference. Only used in non-medical industrial buildings.

4. **TN-C-S — Hospital Utility Intake / Building Entry:** Standard for the supply cable from utility to hospital. Inside the hospital, the MEN link converts it to TN-S. Used from utility transformer up to the main switchboard.

5. **TT — Outdoor Hospital Areas, Mobile Medical Units, Rural Clinics:** Used where independent earthing is necessary (no neutral available, e.g., remote sites). RCDs mandatory. Used for outdoor diagnostic vans, mobile X-ray units, rural primary health centres.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing "earth" and "ground":** Earth = soil/soil electrode; Ground = general reference. In Indian/UK convention, "earthing" = "grounding" in US.
> 2. **Mixing TN-C and TN-C-S:** Students frequently write TN-C when they mean TN-C-S. Remember the **MEN link** is the splitter.
> 3. **Forgetting the IMD in IT systems:** A medical IT system without an IMD is **non-compliant**. The IMD is what makes IT safe.
> 4. **Wrong disconnection time:** TN systems use **0.4 s**, TT systems use **0.2 s** for final circuits ≤ 32 A. Do not interchange.
> 5. **Forgetting RCD in TT:** A TT system without an RCD will NOT provide adequate protection — this is a guaranteed mark-loser.
> 6. **Touch voltage formula error:** Use $U_t = I_f \cdot R_A$ for TT (not $R_B$). The chassis is bonded to the consumer electrode, not the source.
> 7. **Drawing PE through the source earth in TT:** PE in TT must go to the **consumer's local electrode**, not back to the transformer.

---

## 5.3 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and use it as your last-minute revision sheet.**

### 🔑 Letter Code Mnemonic
- **1st letter (Source-Earth):** **T** = Terre (earthed) | **I** = Isolated
- **2nd letter (Exposed Part-Earth):** **T** = Terre (local earth) | **N** = Neutral (connected to source N)
- **3rd letter (N-PE layout):** **C** = Combined (PEN) | **S** = Separate (N & PE distinct)

### ⚡ Quick System Identifiers
- **IT** = "**I**solated power, **T**horoughly safe" — Group 2 medical locations.
- **TN-S** = "**T**otal **N**etwork, **S**afe and **S**eparated" — Inside hospital distribution.
- **TN-C** = "**C**heap but **C**ompromised" — Avoid in medical.
- **TN-C-S** = "**C**ombined utility, **S**plit at MEN link" — Utility to hospital intake.
- **TT** = "**T**wo **T**errestrial electrodes" — Outdoor / rural; needs RCD.

### 📌 Numerical Values to Memorize
- $U_0$ = **230 V** (single-phase line-to-neutral)
- Earth electrode resistance ≤ **5 Ω** general, ≤ **1 Ω** medical.
- IMD alarm threshold: **50 kΩ**.
- IMD internal impedance: **≥ 100 kΩ**.
- RCD for TT medical: **30 mA**.
- Disconnection time TN: **0.4 s**; TT: **0.2 s** (≤ 32 A final circuit).
- Medical IT isolation transformer: typically **3.15 / 5 / 7.5 kVA**.

### 🏥 Biomedical Engineering Take-Aways
- **Operating theatre = medical IT mandatory.**
- **Patient leakage limit (Type CF cardiac):** 10 µA normal / 50 µA single-fault.
- **Earth loop impedance formula:** Know all five equations from Table 2.2.
- **RCD operation check:** $I_f / I_{\Delta n} \gg 1$ guarantees trip.
- **PEN hazard:** If PEN breaks in TN-C or TN-C-S, chassis can rise to **$U_0$ = 230 V**.

### 🧭 Decision Tree for Selecting Grounding System
1. **Is continuity of supply critical on first fault?** → **YES** → Use **IT** (medical IT).
2. **Is this inside a hospital with normal patient environment?** → Use **TN-S**.
3. **Is this the utility supply to the building?** → Use **TN-C-S**.
4. **Is this a remote site with no neutral?** → Use **TT** + RCD.
5. **Is this an industrial non-medical facility?** → **TN-C** acceptable (cheapest).

### 📚 Standards to Remember (Names Only — Do NOT Fabricate Page Numbers)
- **IEC 60364** — Low-voltage electrical installations (general).
- **IEC 60364-7-710** — Medical locations (Group 1 & Group 2 classification).
- **IEC 60601-1** — Medical electrical equipment — General safety.
- **IS 732** — Indian Standard for wiring installations.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Electrical Codes and Standards?

An **electrical code** is a legally enforceable (or quasi-legal) set of rules governing the safe installation, design, and inspection of electrical systems, whereas an **electrical standard** is a technically rigorous, consensus-based document published by a recognized standards body that defines performance, safety, testing, and manufacturing requirements for electrical products and systems.

In **biomedical engineering**, codes and standards are not just "regulatory paperwork" — they form the **operational safety envelope** that determines whether a ventilator, defibrillator, patient monitor, or MRI scanner can legally be sold, installed, and used on a human being.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> *Electrical codes and standards for biomedical applications are a structured hierarchy of national and international documents (IEC, IEEE, BIS, NFPA/NEC, ISO) that prescribe construction, performance, testing, classification, and safety requirements for medical electrical equipment, ensuring patient and operator protection against electric shock, mechanical hazard, fire, electromagnetic interference, and biological incompatibility.*

---

## 1.2 The Standardization Pyramid — Intuitive Analogy

Think of codes and standards like a **layered building inspection system** for a hospital:

1. **Foundation Layer (Constitutional Level)** — International standards like **IEC 60601-1** (general safety for medical electrical equipment). This is the "Constitution" that every medical device must respect.
2. **Structural Layer (Collateral Standards)** — **IEC 60601-1-x** series (e.g., -1-2 for EMC, -1-8 for alarm systems). These are like the "Building Code" adding specialized rules.
3. **Architectural Layer (Particular Standards)** — **IEC 60601-2-x** series (e.g., -2-2 for high-frequency surgical equipment, -2-25 for ECG). These are the "Room Blueprints" specific to each device.
4. **National Layer** — Adoption into law: **BIS** (India), **NFPA 70 / NEC** (USA), **IS standards** in India.
5. **Hospital Operational Layer** — Local electrical safety audits, biomedical engineering department SOPs.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Pillars of Biomedical Electrical Safety Standards:**
> 1. **Electrical Safety** (leakage currents, grounding, isolation) — governed primarily by **IEC 60601-1**.
> 2. **Biological Safety** (biocompatibility, sterilization) — governed by **ISO 10993** and **ISO 11135/11137**.
> 3. **Process / Risk Safety** (design controls, risk management) — governed by **ISO 14971** and **ISO 13485**.

---

## 1.3 Why a Biomedical Engineer Must Know This

A biomedical engineer is legally and ethically the **gatekeeper** of device safety. Without rigorous knowledge of these codes, an engineer might:
- Approve a Class CF device on a patient with a pacemaker (catastrophic).
- Install a non-isolated ECG in a cardiac catheterization lab (lethal).
- Authorize a refurbished device that has failed **earth leakage** testing.

The fundamental physical constants and reference values you must memorize:

| Reference Quantity | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| **Mains voltage (India, single phase)** | **230 V ± 10%, 50 Hz** |
| **Touch voltage threshold (sustained)** | **< 50 V AC** (per IEC 60990) |
| **Let-go threshold current (women)** | **~ 6 mA AC** |
| **Ventricular fibrillation threshold** | **> 100 mA** for > 2 s |
| **Skin resistance (dry)** | **~ 100 kΩ** |
| **Skin resistance (wet/broken)** | **~ 1 kΩ** |
| **Patient auxiliary current limit (Type CF)** | **0.01 mA (10 µA) AC**, **0.01 mA DC** |
| **Earth conductor resistance (medical)** | **< 0.2 Ω** (per NFPA 99) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Major Standards Bodies in Biomedical Engineering

The biomedical electrical technology ecosystem is governed by a tightly coupled network of standards organizations. Understanding the **authority chain** is critical for the KTU exam.

### 2.1.1 International Bodies (Highest Authority)

**IEC (International Electrotechnical Commission)**
- Headquarters: Geneva, Switzerland
- Founder: 1906
- Key document: **IEC 60601 family** (the "Bible" of medical electrical equipment safety)
- IEC 60601-1 (Edition 3.1 / 3.2): General requirements for basic safety and essential performance.

**ISO (International Organization for Standardization)**
- Key documents: **ISO 13485** (Quality Management Systems for Medical Devices), **ISO 14971** (Risk Management), **ISO 10993** (Biological Evaluation of Medical Devices).

**IEEE (Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers)**
- Key documents: **IEEE 11073** (Health Informatics — Personal Health Device Communication), **IEEE C63.16** (EMI testing).

**AAMI (Association for the Advancement of Medical Instrumentation)**
- US-based; co-publishes many standards with IEC.
- AAMI ES60601-1 is the US adoption of IEC 60601-1.

### 2.1.2 Regional / National Bodies

| Region | Body | Key Document |
|---|---|---|
| India | **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards)** | **IS 13450 / IS 60601 series** |
| USA | NFPA | **NFPA 70 (NEC)**, **NFPA 99 (Health Care Facilities)** |
| USA | FDA | 21 CFR Part 820 (Quality System Regulation) |
| Europe | CEN / CENELEC | EN 60601-1 (harmonized standard under MDR 2017/745) |
| Japan | METI / JIS | JIS T 0601 series |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** When asked "Which standard applies to biocompatibility testing in India?", the correct answer chain is: **IS/ISO 10993** (adopted by BIS from ISO), with mandatory FDA 21 CFR references for materials.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Medical Electrical Equipment (IEC 60601-1)

### 2.2.1 Class of Equipment (Protection Against Electric Shock)

**Class I Equipment**
- Relies on **basic insulation** AND a **protective earth connection**.
- Example: Desktop ECG machines, X-ray generators.

**Class II Equipment**
- Relies on **double insulation** OR **reinforced insulation**; NO protective earth required.
- Example: Some infusion pumps, modern handheld diagnostic devices.

**Internally Powered Equipment**
- Powered by an internal electrical energy source (batteries); does not rely on protective earth.
- Example: Implantable pacemakers, ambulatory Holter monitors.

### 2.2.2 Type of Applied Part (Patient Contact)

The **Applied Part (AP)** is the part of the medical device that comes into physical contact with the patient. The classification governs the maximum permissible **leakage currents**.

**Type B (Body)**
- Earthed applied part; **NOT suitable for direct cardiac application**.
- Maximum patient leakage current: **0.1 mA AC** (NC), **0.5 mA AC** (SFC).

**Type BF (Body Floating)**
- **Isolated (floating) applied part**; not suitable for direct cardiac application.
- Maximum patient leakage current: **0.1 mA AC** (NC), **0.5 mA AC** (SFC).
- Example: Ultrasound transducers, surface EMG electrodes.

**Type CF (Cardiac Floating)**
- **Isolated applied part** with the **highest degree of protection**; suitable for **direct cardiac application**.
- Maximum patient leakage current: **0.01 mA AC** (NC), **0.05 mA AC** (SFC) — these are **10× stricter** than Type B/BF.
- Example: Defibrillator paddles, intracardiac catheters, cardiac catheterization lab equipment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for the KTU exam:**
> - **B** = Body (general use, earthed)
> - **BF** = Body, **F**loating (isolated, general)
> - **CF** = **C**ardiac, **F**loating (isolated, direct heart)
> The "C" is for the **C**ardiac catheterization lab, where 10 µA can be fatal.

---

## 2.3 Leakage Current — The Heart of the Subject

A **leakage current** is any current (other than the intended functional current) that flows through an unintended path — typically through insulation, Y-capacitors, or RF filters — from the live parts to earth, the chassis, or the patient.

### 2.3.1 Types of Leakage Current (per IEC 60601-1)

| Symbol | Name | Path | Typical Limit (Type CF) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Iₑ** | **Earth Leakage Current** | Mains to protective earth conductor | **0.5 mA AC (NC)**, **1 mA (SFC)** |
| **Iₜ** | **Touch Current / Enclosure Leakage** | Enclosure to earth (via 1 kΩ test load) | **0.1 mA AC** |
| **Iₚ** | **Patient Leakage Current** | Applied part to earth | **0.01 mA AC** (Type CF NC) |
| **Iₚₐ** | **Patient Auxiliary Current** | Between any two applied parts, through patient | **0.01 mA AC** (Type CF NC) |

Where:
- **NC = Normal Condition** (everything as designed)
- **SFC = Single Fault Condition** (e.g., one protective earth wire broken)

### 2.3.2 Why Limits are 10× Tighter for Type CF

At 50 Hz, the human body has an impedance of approximately **Z_body ≈ 1 kΩ** (wet skin, worst case). The **ventricular fibrillation threshold** is about **100 mA for > 2 s**. For a microshock scenario where the catheter directly contacts the myocardium:

- The body resistance of the myocardium is **negligible** (only a few hundred ohms of tissue resistance + catheter tip).
- A current of just **10 µA** (0.01 mA) reaching the heart can theoretically trigger fibrillation in some conditions.
- Hence the **0.01 mA Type CF limit** is a **safety factor of 10,000** over the fibrillation threshold.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Applicable Standard |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Earth conductor resistance | $R_e \le 0.2\ \Omega$ | Ω | NFPA 99, IS 732 |
| 2 | Earth leakage current (Type CF, SFC) | $I_e \le 1\ \text{mA}$ | mA (rms) | IEC 60601-1 |
| 3 | Patient leakage current (Type CF, NC) | $I_p \le 0.01\ \text{mA}$ | mA (rms) | IEC 60601-1 |
| 4 | Patient auxiliary current (Type CF, NC) | $I_{pa} \le 0.01\ \text{mA}$ | mA (rms) | IEC 60601-1 |
| 5 | Body impedance (wet) | $Z_{body} \approx 1\ \text{k}\Omega$ | Ω | IEC 60990 |
| 6 | Touch voltage | $V_{touch} = I_{touch} \times Z_{body}$ | V | — |
| 7 | Dielectric withstand (mains to earth, Class I) | $V_{test} = 1500\ \text{V AC}$ for 1 min | V | IEC 60601-1 |
| 8 | Dielectric withstand (MOPP, mains to patient) | $V_{test} = 4000\ \text{V AC}$ for 1 min | V | IEC 60601-1 |
| 9 | Creepage distance (mains 250 V, basic insulation) | $\ge 3.0\ \text{mm}$ | mm | IEC 60601-1 Table 13 |
| 10 | Clearance distance (mains 250 V, basic insulation) | $\ge 1.6\ \text{mm}$ | mm | IEC 60601-1 Table 11 |
| 11 | MOPP (Means of Patient Protection) — basic | 1 MOPP = 1500 V AC | — | IEC 60601-1 3rd Ed. |
| 12 | MOPP (Means of Patient Protection) — double | 2 MOPP = 4000 V AC | — | IEC 60601-1 3rd Ed. |
| 13 | Risk Score | $R = S \times P$ | dimensionless | ISO 14971 |
| 14 | Leakage through insulation (capacitive) | $I_{leak} = 2\pi f C V$ | A | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key unit reminders for the KTU exam:**
> - $1\ \mu A = 0.001\ \text{mA} = 10^{-6}\ \text{A}$
> - $1\ \text{mA} = 10^{-3}\ \text{A}$
> - When mixing units, always convert to **A (SI base)** before computing.

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of This Knowledge

1. **Hospital Electrical Safety Audits** — Biomedical engineers conduct quarterly tests using **IEC 62353** (recurrent test and test after repair of medical electrical equipment). Without knowing the leakage current limits, the engineer cannot sign off the device.
2. **Design Verification** — When designing an ECG, the engineer must select **Y-capacitors** (line-to-earth) low enough to keep $I_{leak} \le 0.5\ \text{mA}$. For a 230 V, 50 Hz system, $C \le \frac{I_{leak}}{2 \pi f V} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \pi \times 50 \times 230} \approx 6.9\ \text{nF}$. Anything larger will fail EMC/safety compliance.
3. **Device Classification for Regulatory Submission** — A device going to the FDA / CDSCO must be classified (Class I, II, III) and the appropriate **IEC 60601-1** test report must be attached.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Permissible Y-Capacitor Value

**Problem:** A Class I medical device is connected to a 230 V, 50 Hz mains. Each Y-capacitor (line-to-earth) must be sized such that the earth leakage current in **Normal Condition** does not exceed **0.5 mA AC**. There are **two** Y-capacitors (one on Line, one on Neutral). Determine the maximum allowable capacitance per capacitor.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Write the capacitive leakage current formula for a single Y-capacitor:**

$$I_{leak} = \frac{V}{X_C} = V \cdot 2 \pi f C$$

where:
- $V$ = RMS mains voltage
- $f$ = mains frequency
- $C$ = capacitance of one Y-capacitor
- $X_C = \frac{1}{2 \pi f C}$ = capacitive reactance

**Step 2 — Total leakage current from two Y-capacitors in series across Line-Neutral (with mid-point earthed):**

For a typical EMI filter, the two Y-capacitors are connected from Line-to-Earth and Neutral-to-Earth. In the worst case, the total current to earth from a single line conductor is:

$$I_{e,\text{total}} = V_{rms} \cdot 2 \pi f C_{Y}$$

(since each line-to-earth capacitor sees the full line-to-earth voltage for the case of a balanced filter; we conservatively take the worst case as one line contributing the full leakage).

**Step 3 — Apply the safety limit:**

$$I_{e,\text{total}} \le 0.5\ \text{mA} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{A}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for C:**

$$C_{Y} \le \frac{I_{e,\text{total}}}{2 \pi f V_{rms}}$$

$$C_{Y} \le \frac{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \pi \times 50 \times 230}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the denominator:**

$$2 \pi \times 50 \times 230 = 100 \pi \times 230 = 23000 \pi$$

$$23000 \pi \approx 23000 \times 3.14159 \approx 72{,}256.6$$

**Step 6 — Compute the final value:**

$$C_{Y} \le \frac{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{72{,}256.6}$$

$$C_{Y} \le 6.92 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{F}$$

$$\boxed{C_{Y,\max} \approx 6.92\ \text{nF}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** In practice, designers use **2.2 nF or 4.7 nF Y-capacitors** to provide a safety margin of 30–50% below the computed limit. This trade-off also satisfies **CISPR 11 / IEC 60601-1-2** (EMC emissions limits) without overloading the earth conductor.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Touch Voltage Calculation for a Wet-Skin Patient

**Problem:** A patient is connected to a Type B device. The earth conductor is **broken** (Single Fault Condition). The enclosure leakage current is measured as **0.1 mA AC (rms)** flowing through a measuring device (MD) of **1 kΩ** resistance representing the patient's body. Calculate the touch voltage and verify whether it is within safe limits.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation:**

Touch voltage is the voltage that would appear across the patient (or operator) if they touched the energized enclosure:

$$V_{touch} = I_{touch} \times Z_{body}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute given values:**

$$V_{touch} = 0.1 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{A} \times 1 \times 10^{3}\ \Omega$$

**Step 3 — Compute:**

$$V_{touch} = 0.1\ \text{V} = 100\ \text{mV}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with safety threshold (IEC 60990):**

The **let-go threshold** in human beings is around 50 V AC (sustained) for a healthy adult. Since:

$$V_{touch} = 0.1\ \text{V} \ll 50\ \text{V}$$

the system is **safe** in the touch-voltage sense.

**Step 5 — Re-verify against the current limit:**

Per IEC 60601-1, the enclosure leakage current limit for **Type B in SFC** is **0.5 mA**. The measured value of **0.1 mA** is **5× below the limit** — **PASS**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 1 kΩ?** IEC 60601-1 specifies a standardized **Measuring Device (MD)** with input impedance of $1\ \text{k}\Omega \pm 5\%$ shunted by $0.015\ \mu\text{F}$ to simulate the body's frequency response, and a current-sensing resistor to convert the leakage current to a measurable voltage.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Risk Score Computation (ISO 14971)

**Problem:** A hospital is evaluating a new MRI patient monitoring system. A failure mode analysis identifies a particular hazard: "RF burn at the ECG electrode site due to induced currents from the MRI's 128 MHz field." The hazard has Severity = **Catastrophic (5)** and Probability of Occurrence = **Occasional (4)**. The team plans to add a mitigation that reduces Probability to **Remote (2)**. Compute the initial and residual risk scores.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Risk score formula (ISO 14971):**

$$R = S \times P$$

**Step 2 — Initial risk (before mitigation):**

$$R_{\text{initial}} = 5 \times 4 = 20$$

**Step 3 — Residual risk (after mitigation):**

$$R_{\text{residual}} = 5 \times 2 = 10$$

**Step 4 — Decision using a typical Risk Acceptability Matrix:**

| Severity \ Probability | Improbable (1) | Remote (2) | Occasional (4) | Probable (5) | Frequent (6) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Catastrophic (5) | 5 | 10 | **20** | 25 | 30 |
| Critical (4) | 4 | 8 | 16 | 20 | 24 |
| Marginal (3) | 3 | 6 | 12 | 15 | 18 |
| Minor (2) | 2 | 4 | 8 | 10 | 12 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision rule:** Risk score $\le 8$ = **Acceptable**; 9–15 = **ALARP** (As Low As Reasonably Practicable); $> 15$ = **Unacceptable** — must redesign.

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**

- $R_{\text{initial}} = 20$ → **Unacceptable**. The hazard must be controlled before market release.
- $R_{\text{residual}} = 10$ → **ALARP**. The mitigation (e.g., using only MRI-conditional leads, placing RF chokes) is acceptable, but the manufacturer must document the cost-benefit justification for not reducing it further.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Dielectric Withstand Test Voltage — MOPP Validation

**Problem:** A manufacturer claims their device provides **1 MOPP** (one Means of Patient Protection) between the mains circuit and the patient-applied part. Per IEC 60601-1, basic insulation for 1 MOPP at mains ≤ 250 V AC requires a dielectric withstand test of **1500 V AC** for 1 minute, with a minimum creepage of **3.0 mm** and clearance of **1.6 mm**. The actual measured creepage on the PCB is **2.5 mm** and clearance is **1.4 mm**. Is the device compliant?

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compare creepage (actual vs. required):**

$$\text{Creepage}_{actual} = 2.5\ \text{mm} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \text{Creepage}_{required} = 3.0\ \text{mm}$$

$$2.5 < 3.0 \implies \text{NON-COMPLIANT}$$

**Step 2 — Compare clearance (actual vs. required):**

$$\text{Clearance}_{actual} = 1.4\ \text{mm} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \text{Clearance}_{required} = 1.6\ \text{mm}$$

$$1.4 < 1.6 \implies \text{NON-COMPLIANT}$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion:**

The device **fails** the MOPP requirement on both creepage and clearance. To bring it into compliance, the design team must either:
- Increase the PCB slot between mains and patient traces to ≥ 1.6 mm clearance and ≥ 3.0 mm creepage, **OR**
- Provide an **additional protective barrier** (insulation sheet, potting compound) to achieve **2 MOPP** at a lower spacing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **clearance** (shortest air path) with **creepage** (shortest path along the insulator surface). Creepage is always ≥ clearance because surface contaminants can conduct. Pollution degree and material group (CTI) affect creepage — a higher CTI material (e.g., FR-4 with CTI > 400) reduces the required creepage.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Total Energy in a Defibrillator Pulse — IEC 60601-2-4 Compliance

**Problem:** A defibrillator is required by **IEC 60601-2-4** to deliver an energy of **360 J** into a 50 Ω patient load, with a tolerance of **± 15%**. The output is a damped sinusoidal (Edmark) waveform. Calculate (a) the maximum and minimum acceptable energy, and (b) the corresponding peak current and peak voltage.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Energy tolerance limits:**

$$E_{\max} = 360 \times 1.15 = 414\ \text{J}$$

$$E_{\min} = 360 \times 0.85 = 306\ \text{J}$$

**Step 2 — Peak current (for a pure resistor):**

For a biphasic truncated exponential, the energy delivered to a 50 Ω load is approximately:

$$E = \int_0^{t_1} i^2(t) \cdot R \cdot dt$$

For a monophasic damped sinusoid with first peak $I_p$:

$$E \approx \frac{1}{2} I_p^2 R \tau$$

where $\tau$ is the effective pulse width (≈ 4 ms for a typical Edmark waveform).

A simpler approximation used in the standard is:

$$E \approx \frac{V_p^2}{R} \cdot t_{eff}$$

Taking $t_{eff} \approx 4\ \text{ms}$ and $R = 50\ \Omega$:

**Step 3 — Peak voltage calculation (for 360 J at 50 Ω):**

Assuming a rectangular approximation of effective duration $t_{eff} = 4\ \text{ms}$:

$$V_p = \sqrt{\frac{E \cdot R}{t_{eff}}}$$

$$V_p = \sqrt{\frac{360 \times 50}{4 \times 10^{-3}}}$$

$$V_p = \sqrt{\frac{18{,}000}{0.004}} = \sqrt{4.5 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$\boxed{V_p \approx 2121\ \text{V}}$$

**Step 4 — Peak current:**

$$I_p = \frac{V_p}{R} = \frac{2121}{50} \approx 42.4\ \text{A}$$

**Step 5 — Verification of tolerance band:**

$$E_{\max}: V_p = \sqrt{\frac{414 \times 50}{0.004}} \approx 2274\ \text{V},\ I_p \approx 45.5\ \text{A}$$

$$E_{\min}: V_p = \sqrt{\frac{306 \times 50}{0.004}} \approx 1955\ \text{V},\ I_p \approx 39.1\ \text{A}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters clinically:** The 360 J dose is the upper limit of biphasic efficacy. Exceeding 414 J risks myocardial injury; falling below 306 J risks unsuccessful cardioversion. The IEC tolerance window ensures **dose predictability** — a critical safety function in cardiac surgery.

---

| Parameter | Class I Type B | Class I Type BF | Class I Type CF | Standard |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Earth leakage (NC) | 0.5 mA | 0.5 mA | 0.5 mA | IEC 60601-1 |
| Earth leakage (SFC) | 1.0 mA | 1.0 mA | 1.0 mA | IEC 60601-1 |
| Enclosure leakage (NC) | 0.1 mA | 0.1 mA | 0.1 mA | IEC 60601-1 |
| Enclosure leakage (SFC) | 0.5 mA | 0.5 mA | 0.5 mA | IEC 60601-1 |
| Patient leakage (NC) | 0.1 mA | 0.1 mA | **0.01 mA** | IEC 60601-1 |
| Patient leakage (SFC) | 0.5 mA | 0.5 mA | **0.05 mA** | IEC 60601-1 |
| Patient aux (NC) | 0.1 mA | 0.1 mA | **0.01 mA** | IEC 60601-1 |
| Patient aux (SFC) | 0.5 mA | 0.5 mA | **0.05 mA** | IEC 60601-1 |
| MOPP requirement | 1 | 1 | 2 (CF direct heart) | IEC 60601-1 |
| Dielectric test | 1500 V AC | 1500 V AC | **4000 V AC** | IEC 60601-1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single most important insight for the exam:** The transition from BF to CF involves **10× tighter patient leakage limits** AND a **2.67× higher dielectric withstand voltage**. This is because the CF applied part may directly contact the heart, where even **10 µA** can be lethal.

## 4.1 The Biomedical Standards Hierarchy — Functional Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of biomedical electrical codes and standards, from international consensus to hospital-level compliance.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[GLOBAL CONSENSUS LAYER] --> A1[IEC INTERNATIONAL ELECTROTECHNICAL COMMISSION]
    A0 --> A2[ISO INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATION FOR STANDARDIZATION]
    A0 --> A3[IEEE INSTITUTE OF ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERS]
    A1 --> B1[IEC 60601 SERIES GENERAL AND COLLATERAL SAFETY]
    A1 --> B2[IEC 60601 2 X PARTICULAR STANDARDS PER DEVICE]
    A1 --> B3[IEC 62353 RECURRENT TESTING]
    A2 --> B4[ISO 13485 QUALITY MANAGEMENT]
    A2 --> B5[ISO 14971 RISK MANAGEMENT]
    A2 --> B6[ISO 10993 BIOCOMPATIBILITY]
    A3 --> B7[IEEE 11073 MEDICAL DEVICE COMMUNICATION]
    B1 --> C1[NATIONAL ADOPTION LAYER]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    B5 --> C1
    B6 --> C1
    B7 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[INDIA BIS IS 13450]
    C1 --> D2[USA NFPA 99 AND NEC 70]
    C1 --> D3[EUROPE EN 60601 UNDER MDR 2017 745]
    C1 --> D4[USA FDA 21 CFR PART 820]
    D1 --> E1[HOSPITAL AND OEM LAYER]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[DESIGN VERIFICATION AND VALIDATION]
    E1 --> F2[INSTALLATION QUALIFICATION]
    E1 --> F3[OPERATIONAL QUALIFICATION]
    E1 --> F4[PERFORMANCE QUALIFICATION]
    F1 --> G1[SAFETY TESTING EMC BIOCOMPATIBILITY]
    F2 --> G1
    F3 --> G1
    F4 --> G1
    G1 --> H1[MARKET RELEASE AND CLINICAL USE]
    H1 --> I1[POST MARKET SURVEILLANCE]
    I1 --> A1
    I1 --> A2
    I1 --> A3
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **top layer** is global consensus (IEC, ISO, IEEE).
- The **middle layer** is national adoption (BIS, NFPA, EN, FDA).
- The **bottom layer** is hospital/OEM-level execution (IQ, OQ, PQ).
- The **feedback loop** (Post-Market Surveillance → IEC/ISO) is the **continuous improvement cycle** mandated by ISO 13485 and MDR 2017/745.

## 4.2 Leakage Current Flow Paths — Functional Flow Diagram

This diagram shows the four canonical leakage current paths in a medical electrical device per IEC 60601-1, and the standard measurement points.

```mermaid
graph LR
    M[MAINS SOURCE 230 V 50 HZ] --> L[LIVE CONDUCTOR L]
    M --> N[NEUTRAL CONDUCTOR N]
    M --> PE[PROTECTIVE EARTH PE]
    L --> A1[APPLIED PART TYPE CF]
    N --> A1
    A1 --> P[PATIENT]
    A1 -.->|CAPACITIVE COUPLING| E1[ENCLOSURE]
    L -.->|INSULATION LEAKAGE| E1
    L -.->|Y CAPACITOR PATH| PE
    N -.->|Y CAPACITOR PATH| PE
    E1 -.->|TOUCH CURRENT I_t| MD1[MEASURING DEVICE 1 K OHM]
    PE -.->|EARTH LEAKAGE I_e| MD2[MEASURING DEVICE 1 K OHM]
    A1 -.->|PATIENT LEAKAGE I_p| MD3[MEASURING DEVICE 1 K OHM]
    A1 -.->|PATIENT AUX I_pa| MD4[MEASURING DEVICE BETWEEN APs]
    MD1 --> LIM[COMPARED AGAINST IEC 60601 1 LIMITS]
    MD2 --> LIM
    MD3 --> LIM
    MD4 --> LIM
    LIM --> RES{PASS OR FAIL}
```

**Interpretation:**
- Solid arrows = intended current paths (functional).
- Dashed arrows = **leakage paths** (the ones standards seek to limit).
- The four **Measuring Devices (MD)** are the standardized test instruments defined in IEC 60601-1 Annex H.

---

## 4.3 Medical Device Classification Flow — India CDSCO / EU MDR

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[START NEW MEDICAL DEVICE] --> Q1{DURATION OF USE}
    Q1 -->|LESS THAN 60 MINUTES| Q2{INVASIVE OR NON INVASIVE}
    Q1 -->|MORE THAN 60 MINUTES| Q3{INVASIVE OR NON INVASIVE}
    Q1 -->|IMPLANTABLE| CL3[CLASS C HIGHEST RISK]
    Q2 -->|NON INVASIVE| CL1[CLASS A LOWEST RISK]
    Q2 -->|INVASIVE TRANSIENT| CL2[CLASS B MODERATE RISK]
    Q3 -->|INVASIVE SURGICAL| CL3
    Q3 -->|NON INVASIVE LONG TERM| CL2
    CL1 --> R1[REGISTRATION WITH CDSCO]
    CL2 --> R2[STATE LICENSE ISO 13485]
    CL3 --> R3[NATIONAL LICENSE PLUS DESIGN EXAMINATION]
    R1 --> P1[POST MARKET SURVEILLANCE]
    R2 --> P1
    R3 --> P1
    P1 --> FB[FEEDBACK TO ISO 14971 RISK FILE]
```

**Note on Indian classification (CDSCO, 2017 Rules):**
- **Class A** — Low risk (e.g., stethoscopes, examination gloves).
- **Class B** — Low-moderate risk (e.g., infusion pumps, surgical clamps).
- **Class C** — Moderate-high risk (e.g., ventilators, orthopedic implants).
- **Class D** — Highest risk (e.g., heart valves, implantable pacemakers, stents).

All node labels are uppercase plain text with no bold/italic markdown inside the quotes, as required by the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE protocol.

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Define "Applied Part" as per IEC 60601-1. Differentiate between Type B, Type BF, and Type CF applied parts with one clinical example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Applied Part (AP)** is the part of the medical electrical equipment that **physically contacts the patient** for the purpose of diagnosis, monitoring, therapy, or measurement, and through which current may flow to/from the patient.

| Type | Isolation | Suitable for | Max Patient Leakage (NC) | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **B (Body)** | Earthed | General external use | 0.1 mA AC | Surface ECG electrodes |
| **BF (Body Floating)** | Isolated (floating) | General external use | 0.1 mA AC | Ultrasound probe |
| **CF (Cardiac Floating)** | Isolated (floating) | Direct cardiac contact | **0.01 mA AC** | Defibrillator paddles, intracardiac catheter |

**(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for classification table, 1 mark for clinical examples.)**
