topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT411 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY FOR BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Protection Against Indirect Contact

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Protection Against Indirect Contact** is defined (per **IEC 60601-1** and **IS 8437 / IEC 60364**) as the set of preventive measures designed to safeguard persons, patients, and medical staff from electric shock hazards that arise **not from direct contact with live parts**, but from **touching exposed conductive parts (ECPs)** of electrical equipment that have become energized due to an **insulation failure** (i.e., a fault condition).

In biomedical environments, this protection is absolutely critical because the patient may be in a state of reduced physiological resistance (intact skin may be bypassed via catheters, electrodes, or surgical incisions), and any hazardous touch voltage — even as low as **50 V AC** — can result in **ventricular fibrillation** or **microshock**.

**Classification of Electric Shock Hazards in Medical Practice:**

| Hazard Type | Current Path | Typical Threshold | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Direct Contact** | Body ↔ Live Part (Line/Neutral) | > 1 mA (perception) | Module 3 — touched upon |
| **Indirect Contact** | Body ↔ Exposed Conductive Part (faulty casing) | > 10 mA (let-go) | **Module 4 — current topic** |
| **Microshock** | Direct to heart via catheter/electrode | **10 μA** | Module 4 — ICU/CCU focus |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Leaking Water Pipe**
>
> Imagine your home water tap is normally safe (the "live parts" are inside the pipe). But suppose a crack develops *inside* the wall — water starts seeping out of the **outer metal handle** of the tap. You are NOT touching the water directly; you are touching the **handle that has become wet** due to internal damage.
>
> - **Direct contact** = putting your hand directly into flowing water inside the pipe.
> - **Indirect contact** = touching the wet metal handle (an "exposed conductive part" energized by an internal **insulation failure**).
> - **Protection against indirect contact** = the safety systems (the pipe's outer insulation, an automatic shut-off valve like an **RCD**, or a **protective earth wire** that drains the leakage safely to ground).
>
> In a hospital, the "tap" is a medical device (e.g., a defibrillator, ECG monitor, or electrosurgical unit) and the "patient" is the most vulnerable person in the room.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in Biomedical Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Patient Safety:** A patient under anesthesia or in ICU has conductive media (saline, blood) directly interfacing with internal organs.
> - **Equipment Reliability:** Defibrillators and electrosurgical units generate high voltages internally; insulation failure can energize chassis.
> - **Standards Compliance:** Medical electrical equipment is governed by **IEC 60601-1** (general safety), **IEC 60601-1-1** (system safety), and **IEC 60364-7-710** (medical locations).
> - **KTU 2024 Outcome Mapping:** This topic maps directly to **CO4** — "Apply electrical safety concepts in biomedical equipment design and hospital wiring."

## 1.4 Exposed Conductive Part (ECP) vs. Extraneous Conductive Part

> [!NOTE]
> - **Exposed Conductive Part (ECP):** A conductive part of equipment that can be touched and is not normally live, but **may become live under fault conditions** (e.g., metal chassis of a monitor, screws, knobs).
> - **Extraneous Conductive Part (EtCP):** A conductive part **not forming part of the electrical installation**, liable to introduce a potential (e.g., water pipes, structural steel, gas pipes). EtCPs must be equipotentially bonded to the protective earth bar in medical locations.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Philosophy of Protection

The principle of protection against indirect contact is based on **three pillars**:

1. **Prevent Touch Voltage from reaching hazardous levels** — by design, insulation, or equipotential bonding.
2. **Limit the duration of any hazardous touch voltage** — by automatic disconnection of supply via protective devices.
3. **Drain the fault current safely to earth** — via a low-impedance protective earth (PE) conductor.

## 2.2 Classification of Protection Methods (KTU High-Yield)

### A. **Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS)**

The most common method. Used in **TN, TT, and IT** systems (defined by IEC 60364).

- **Operation Principle:** When a fault occurs (live-to-casing insulation breakdown), a high fault current flows through the **PE conductor** back to the source, triggering a protective device (MCB / fuse / RCCB) that disconnects the supply.
- **Disconnect Time Limits** (per IEC 60364-4-41):

| System Type | Touch Voltage (V) | Max Disconnect Time (s) |
|---|---|---|
| TN system | $\leq 50 \text{ V}$ | $\leq 0.4 \text{ s}$ (final circuits $\leq 32 \text{ A}$) |
| TN system | $\leq 50 \text{ V}$ | $\leq 5 \text{ s}$ (distribution circuits) |
| TT system | $\leq 50 \text{ V}$ | $\leq 0.2 \text{ s}$ (medical: 0.1 s) |
| **Medical IT (isolated power)** | $\leq 25 \text{ V}$ | No automatic disconnection on first fault |

### B. **Double or Reinforced Insulation (Class II Equipment)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Equipment marked with the **square-within-a-square** symbol $\square$ relies on:
> - **Basic Insulation** (between live parts and accessible parts) **PLUS**
> - **Supplementary Insulation** (an independent second layer).
>
> If both fail simultaneously, protective earthing is unnecessary. Common in patient-connected accessories like ECG cables, SpO₂ probes.

### C. **Electrical Separation (Class III / Isolated Power System — IT)**

- The supply is isolated from earth using an **isolating transformer** with a turns ratio of 1:1.
- A **first fault does not produce a touch voltage** (since there is no return path to source).
- Mandatory in **Group 2 medical locations** (operating theatres, cardiac catheterization labs, intensive care units) per **IEC 60364-7-710**.
- Equipped with an **Insulation Monitoring Device (IMD)** that continuously measures insulation resistance and alarms if it falls below **50 kΩ**.

### D. **Equipotential Bonding**

All exposed and extraneous conductive parts in a patient environment are connected to a common **Potential Equalization Busbar (PEB)**, ensuring **no significant potential difference** can exist between simultaneously accessible parts.

> The maximum allowable resistance between any two points in the patient environment bonding network: **$R \leq 0.1 \, \Omega$** (per IEC 60601-1-1, verified with 25 A test current at 6 V AC).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are EXAM-CRITICAL and appear frequently in KTU End-Semester Evaluation (ESE).**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables | Units | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Touch Voltage (prospective)** | $U_T = I_f \cdot Z_{PE}$ | $I_f$ = fault current, $Z_{PE}$ = PE impedance | V, A, Ω | Calculate hazardous voltage on casing |
| 2 | **Fault Loop Impedance (TN)** | $Z_s = Z_{\text{source}} + Z_{\text{line}} + Z_{PE}$ | Sum of source, line, PE impedances | Ω | Verify disconnection |
| 3 | **Earth Fault Current (TT)** | $I_f = \dfrac{U_0}{R_A + R_B}$ | $U_0$ = nominal phase voltage (230 V), $R_A$ = earth electrode, $R_B$ = equipment earth | A | RCD sizing |
| 4 | **Disconnection Time (MCB)** | $t_d = f(I_f / I_n)$ | $I_n$ = rated current | s | Read from MCB curve (B, C, D) |
| 5 | **RCD Trip Current** | $I_{\Delta n} \leq \dfrac{U_L}{R_A}$ | $U_L$ = permissible touch voltage limit (50 V), $R_A$ = earth electrode resistance | A, V, Ω | Standard values: 30 mA (medical), 6 mA (ICU special) |
| 6 | **PE Conductor Sizing (adiabatic)** | $S = \dfrac{\sqrt{I^2 t}}{k}$ | $I$ = fault current, $t$ = disconnect time, $k$ = material factor (Cu=143, Al=95) | mm² | Sizing of protective earth |
| 7 | **Touch Current Limit (medical)** | $I_{\text{touch}} \leq 100 \, \mu\text{A}$ (CF-type, normal) | Patient Auxiliary Current (per IEC 60601-1) | μA | Patient-connected equipment |
| 8 | **Microshock Threshold** | $I \leq 10 \, \mu\text{A}$ | 50/60 Hz current through heart | μA | Catheter/electrode paths |
| 9 | **Insulation Resistance (medical IT)** | $R_{\text{ins}} \geq 50 \, \text{k}\Omega$ | Minimum alarm threshold (IMD) | Ω | Per IEC 60364-7-710 |
| 10 | **Equipotential Bonding Resistance** | $R_{\text{bond}} \leq 0.1 \, \Omega$ | Measured between any two bonded parts | Ω | Patient environment verification |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Phase Voltage used in KTU problems:** $U_0 = 230 \, \text{V AC}$ (Indian standard) — students often mistakenly use 220 V which leads to minor mark deductions.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hospital Wiring Design:** Electrical consultants must specify **Medical IT (isolated power) systems** in OTs/ICUs to ensure continuity of supply even during a first earth fault.
- **Equipment Manufacturing:** Manufacturers select Class I (earthed), Class II (double-insulated), or Class III (SELV/PELV) based on use environment.
- **Preventive Maintenance:** Periodic earth loop impedance testing (using a loop impedance tester) and RCD trip-time testing are mandatory under **NABH (National Accreditation Board for Hospitals)** standards.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Touch Voltage in a TN System During Earth Fault

**Given Scenario:** A Class I medical device is connected to a 230 V, 50 Hz TN-C-S system. The fault loop impedance $Z_s$ is measured to be **0.35 Ω**. Compute the touch voltage appearing on the casing if the live conductor faults to the metal chassis.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the fault current path.**

When insulation between line conductor and chassis fails, current flows from the source, through the line conductor, into the chassis, then back through the combined **PEN / PE conductor** to the transformer neutral.

**Step 2 — Apply Ohm's Law to the fault loop.**

The prospective fault current is:

$$
I_f = \frac{U_0}{Z_s}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the known values.**

$$
I_f = \frac{230 \text{ V}}{0.35 \, \Omega}
$$

$$
I_f = 657.14 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the touch voltage on the casing.**

The touch voltage $U_T$ is the potential drop across the PE conductor portion of the loop. Assuming PE impedance $Z_{PE}$ is approximately **0.05 Ω** (a typical small fraction of total loop):

$$
U_T = I_f \cdot Z_{PE}
$$

$$
U_T = 657.14 \times 0.05
$$

$$
U_T = 32.86 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**

Since $U_T = 32.86 \, \text{V} \lt 50 \, \text{V}$ (the IEC 60364 touch voltage limit), the condition is **safe from a touch-voltage perspective**, provided that the **disconnection time** requirement is also satisfied (i.e., protective device operates within 0.4 s for this 32 A final circuit).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always show the substitution step with units — marks are awarded for correct unit handling.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — RCD Sizing for a TT System in a Biomedical Lab

**Given:** A TT-system feeds a biomedical research lab. The earth electrode resistance at the equipment $R_A = 166.7 \, \Omega$. The permissible touch voltage limit $U_L = 50 \, \text{V}$. Compute the maximum allowable RCD trip current.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Recall the disconnection condition for a TT system.**

For automatic disconnection in a TT system, the protective device must operate such that:

$$
I_a \cdot R_A \leq U_L
$$

where $I_a$ is the current causing automatic operation of the protective device.

**Step 2 — Solve for the required operating current.**

$$
I_a \leq \frac{U_L}{R_A}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values.**

$$
I_a \leq \frac{50}{166.7}
$$

$$
I_a \leq 0.3 \text{ A}
$$

$$
I_a \leq 300 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 4 — Select a standard RCD.**

Standard RCD ratings are **6 mA, 10 mA, 30 mA, 100 mA, 300 mA, 500 mA**. A **30 mA RCD** is the standard choice for personnel protection in biomedical environments (sensitive enough to prevent ventricular fibrillation, which requires currents > 30 mA through the body for > 0.5 s).

**Step 5 — Verify that the 30 mA RCD satisfies the constraint.**

The 30 mA RCD will trip at $I_{\Delta n} = 30 \text{ mA}$, which is well below the 300 mA required — hence it **automatically satisfies** the disconnection requirement. ✔

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 30 mA?** IEC 60479-1 / IEC 479 establishes that currents of 30 mA for durations > 0.5 s can cause ventricular fibrillation in healthy adults. For medical applications, **6 mA RCDs** are used in some special applications (e.g., cardiac catheterization labs in some national standards).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Adiabatic Sizing of Protective Earth Conductor

**Given:** A circuit has a fault current $I = 1500 \, \text{A}$ and the upstream MCB has a disconnection time $t = 0.4 \, \text{s}$. The PE conductor is copper (k = 143) with PVC insulation. Compute the minimum PE conductor cross-section.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Recall the adiabatic equation (per IEC 60364-5-54, BS 7671).**

$$
S = \frac{\sqrt{I^2 \, t}}{k}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
S = \frac{\sqrt{(1500)^2 \times 0.4}}{143}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the numerator.**

$$
S = \frac{\sqrt{2.25 \times 10^6 \times 0.4}}{143}
$$

$$
S = \frac{\sqrt{9 \times 10^5}}{143}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the square root.**

$$
S = \frac{948.68}{143}
$$

$$
S = 6.63 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the standard minimum sizing rule.**

Per IEC 60364-5-54, the PE conductor must be at least:
- $2.5 \, \text{mm}^2$ Cu (if mechanically protected), or
- $4 \, \text{mm}^2$ Cu (if not mechanically protected).

Since $6.63 \, \text{mm}^2 \gt 4 \, \text{mm}^2$, we select the **next standard size** = **$10 \, \text{mm}^2$ Cu**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always round UP to the next standard size — selecting 6 mm² when the calculation gives 6.63 mm² is a common mark-losing mistake.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Protection Schemes

| Feature | Class I (PE) | Class II (Double Ins.) | Class III (SELV) | Medical IT (Isolated) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Symbol** | $\oplus$ | $\square$ | $\text{III}$ | IT, IMD |
| **Protection Mechanism** | Earth + ADS | Reinforced insulation | Extra-low voltage (< 25 V AC) | Isolation + insulation monitoring |
| **Voltage on First Fault** | Hazardous (must disconnect) | None (insulation holds) | None (below threshold) | None (no return path) |
| **Max Touch Voltage** | 50 V AC | N/A (no fault current) | 25 V AC | 50 V AC (limit) |
| **Disconnection Time** | 0.4 s (TN) / 0.1 s (medical TT) | Not required | Not required | Not required on first fault |
| **Patient Environment Use** | Allowed (with TN-S + RCD) | Patient-connected cables | Battery-powered devices | **Mandatory in Group 2 (OT, ICU, CCU)** |
| **Monitoring** | Loop impedance testing | Insulation resistance test | Voltage measurement | **Continuous IMD** |

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the hierarchical decision logic and components involved in a **Medical IT (Isolated Power) System** — the gold standard for protection against indirect contact in Group 2 medical locations.

```mermaid

    B --> C[Hospital Main Distribution Board]
    C --> D[Sub Distribution Board for OT Block]

    F --> G2[Operating Theatre 2 Circuit]
    F --> G3[Intensive Care Unit Circuit]
    F --> G4[Cardiac Cath Lab Circuit]

    H[Insulation Monitoring Device IMD] --> F
    I[Line Isolation Monitor LIM] --> F
    J[Equipotential Bonding Busbar PEB] --> F

    K1[ECG Monitor] --> G1
    K2[Defibrillator] --> G2
    K3[Ventilator] --> G3
    K4[Anesthesia Workstation] --> G1

    L[Overload and Short Circuit Protection MCB] --> F
    M[Residual Current Monitor RCM] --> F

    N[Continuous Insulation Resistance Measurement] --> H
    O[Alarm if Rins below 50 kiloOhm] --> H
    P[Visual and Audible Indication in OT] --> H

    style A fill:#fbb,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#bbf,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#bfb,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#bfb,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#ff9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px

    style I fill:#f9f,stroke:#333

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The **Medical Isolation Transformer** (highlighted yellow) is the centerpiece. It **decouples the secondary side from earth**, meaning a single line-to-chassis fault on any device **does not create a closed circuit** — hence no hazardous touch voltage develops. The **IMD** continuously measures insulation resistance and alerts staff before a second fault occurs.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Earth Fault Loop in a TN-S System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Transformer Secondary Winding] -->|Line Conductor L| B[Load Equipment Metal Chassis]
    B -->|Protective Earth PE| C[Consumer Earth Bar]
    C -->|Earth Conductor| D[Source Neutral and Earth]
    D -->|Neutral N| A
    E[Fault Insulation Failure] -.-> B

    F[MCB or Fuse Trips within 0.4s] --> G[Supply Disconnected Automatically]

    style E fill:#f55,stroke:#900,color:#fff
    style F fill:#9f9,stroke:#060
    style G fill:#9f9,stroke:#060
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The fault loop in a TN-S system is **L → chassis (fault) → PE → source**. The current is large (hundreds of amps) because the loop impedance is very low. This large current ensures **fast disconnection** by the MCB, limiting the time the touch voltage is present on the chassis.

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Selection of Protection Class

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Medical Equipment] --> B{Is it patient-connected?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Is it in Group 2 location OT, ICU, CCU?}
    B -- No --> D[Class I with PE acceptable]
    C -- Yes --> E[Medical IT system mandatory - Class I or II on IT supply]
    C -- No --> F[Class I with TN-S and 30mA RCD]
    E --> G{Does equipment have applied part type CF?}
    G -- Yes CF --> H[Additional patient auxiliary current limit 10uA DC and 100uA AC]
    G -- No BF --> I[Standard Class I or II]
    H --> J[Verify defibrillator proof and leakage per IEC 60601]
    I --> J
    D --> K[Verify PE continuity 0.1 ohm max]
    F --> K
    J --> L[Equipment Approved for Clinical Use]
    K --> L
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield:** The above decision tree captures the **IEC 60601-1 applied part classification**:
> - **Type B (Body):** No patient connection to heart. Earth permitted.
> - **Type BF (Body Floating):** Has F-type (floating) applied part; no direct cardiac connection.
> - **Type CF (Cardiac Floating):** Direct connection to heart — **highest safety class** (defibrillators, intracardiac catheters).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Before You Write!**
> 1. **Mixing up Class I / II / III symbols** — $\oplus$ (earth), $\square$ (double insulated), Roman III (SELV) — students frequently interchange them, losing **2 marks** per occurrence.
> 2. **Using 220 V instead of 230 V** — the Indian standard nominal phase voltage since 2005 is **230 V**; using 220 V is a minor deduction.
> 3. **Forgetting the equipotential bonding resistance** limit of **0.1 Ω** in medical patient environments — students often write 1 Ω (which is the non-medical limit).
> 4. **Confusing microshock (10 μA) with macroshock thresholds** — ventricular fibrillation threshold through intact skin is ~ 100 mA, but **direct cardiac** path is 10 μA.
> 5. **Not specifying IMD + LIM distinction** — IMD monitors insulation; LIM computes hazard current. Both are required in IT systems but are **not the same device**.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`

**Define protection against indirect contact. How does it differ from protection against direct contact?**

**Model Answer:**

Protection against indirect contact refers to the safety measures applied to prevent electric shock when a person touches an **exposed conductive part (ECP)** of electrical equipment that has become **live due to an insulation failure**. It does NOT involve touching a normally-live conductor.

| Aspect | Direct Contact | Indirect Contact |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Touching a live part under normal conditions | Touching a normally dead part that became live due to fault |
| **Example** | Touching a bare wire | Touching a metal ECG chassis that is internally faulted |
| **Primary Protection** | Insulation, IP rating, barriers | Earthing, double insulation, RCD, equipotential bonding |
| **IEC Reference** | IEC 60364-4-41 (basic protection) | IEC 60364-4-41 (fault protection) |
| **KTU Standard Mark Scheme** | — | (3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for distinction, 1 for example) |

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**What is a Medical IT system? Why is it mandatory in Group 2 medical locations?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Medical IT (Isolated Terre) System** is an ungrounded electrical distribution system used in hospitals, where the secondary of an isolation transformer is **floating** (not connected to earth) and is continuously monitored by an **Insulation Monitoring Device (IMD)**.

**Why mandatory in Group 2 locations (OT, ICU, CCU):**
- A **single line-to-chassis fault does not trip the supply**, ensuring **continuity of power** to life-support equipment during surgery.
- The fault current is limited to the small leakage through the insulation capacitance (typically < 1 mA), so **no hazardous touch voltage** appears on the chassis.
- The IMD provides **early warning** of insulation degradation before a second fault can occur.
- Governed by **IEC 60364-7-710** and **IS 732**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A — Choice Option 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**a)** Explain the various methods of protection against indirect contact with suitable diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A TT-system is installed in a biomedical research laboratory. The earth electrode resistance is $R_A = 200 \, \Omega$ and the equipment has $R_B = 50 \, \Omega$. Calculate: (i) the prospective touch voltage, (ii) the required RCD trip current, and (iii) verify whether a 30 mA RCD is sufficient. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

The methods of protection against indirect contact are:

**1. Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS):**
- Used in **TN, TT, and IT** systems.
- A fault current flows through the protective earth conductor, triggering a protective device (MCB / fuse / RCCB) which disconnects the supply.
- The disconnection time is limited to **0.4 s** for final circuits $\leq 32$ A in TN systems.

**2. Double or Reinforced Insulation (Class II):**
- Equipment has both **basic** and **supplementary** insulation layers.
- Even if one layer fails, the second prevents the chassis from becoming live.
- Marked with the $\square$ symbol.
- Common in patient-connected cables, plastic-cased devices.

**3. Electrical Separation / Class III (SELV):**
- Circuits powered by a Safety Extra-Low Voltage source ($U \leq 25 \, \text{V AC}$ or $60 \, \text{V DC}$).
- Used for patient environment signal-level circuits, battery-powered infusion pumps.
- Cannot produce a touch voltage hazardous to the body.

**4. Equipotential Bonding:**
- All simultaneously accessible ECPs and EtCPs are connected to a common equipotential busbar.
- Eliminates potential differences between accessible parts.
- Resistance between any two bonded parts $\leq 0.1 \, \Omega$ in patient environment.

**5. Use of Class II Equipment and RCDs (30 mA):**
- RCDs monitor the algebraic sum of currents in line and neutral; an imbalance $> I_{\Delta n}$ indicates earth leakage, and the device trips within **< 30 ms**.

> (5 marks for explanation; 2 marks for diagram — students should draw a simple TN-S fault loop with PE, or the Class II symbol with the two insulation layers labeled)

---

### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Given:** $R_A = 200 \, \Omega$, $R_B = 50 \, \Omega$, $U_0 = 230 \, \text{V}$.

**(i) Prospective Touch Voltage:**

In a TT system, the earth fault current flows from line → equipment earth → earth electrode → back to source neutral. The fault current is:

$$
I_f = \frac{U_0}{R_A + R_B}
$$

$$
I_f = \frac{230}{200 + 50} = \frac{230}{250} = 0.92 \text{ A}
$$

The touch voltage appears across $R_B$ (the equipment-to-earth resistance, which is the part of the body subject to contact):

$$
U_T = I_f \cdot R_B
$$

$$
U_T = 0.92 \times 50 = 46 \text{ V}
$$

**Verification:** $U_T = 46 \, \text{V} \lt 50 \, \text{V}$ — the prospective touch voltage is **within the safe limit**, but is dangerously close. The disconnection must be very fast.

**(ii) Required RCD Trip Current:**

For a TT system, the disconnection condition is:

$$
I_{\Delta n} \leq \frac{U_L}{R_A}
$$

$$
I_{\Delta n} \leq \frac{50}{200} = 0.25 \text{ A} = 250 \text{ mA}
$$

So the RCD must have a trip current $\leq 250 \, \text{mA}$.

**(iii) Verification of 30 mA RCD:**

The standard 30 mA RCD has $I_{\Delta n} = 30 \, \text{mA} = 0.03 \, \text{A}$.

This is **less than** the maximum allowed 250 mA → the 30 mA RCD satisfies the disconnection condition. ✔

**Additional benefit:** A 30 mA RCD provides **additional cardiac safety** by limiting let-go and ventricular fibrillation currents. Hence, the 30 mA RCD is sufficient and recommended.

> (4 marks for part i-ii numerical working; 3 marks for part iii verification and safety interpretation)

---

### **Question B — Choice Option 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Medical IT (Isolated Power) System** used in operating theatres. Discuss the role of the **Insulation Monitoring Device (IMD)** and the **Line Isolation Monitor (LIM)**. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A 10 kVA, 230 V isolation transformer feeds a critical OT circuit. The insulation resistance of the system, as monitored by the IMD, drops to $R_{\text{ins}} = 100 \, \text{k}\Omega$. Calculate: (i) the leakage current to ground, and (ii) comment on whether the system can continue to operate safely until maintenance. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Part (a) of Question B**

**Block Diagram (description for drawing):**

Mains → Step-down transformer → Main distribution board → Medical Isolation Transformer (1:1) → Isolated Power Panel (IPP) → OT circuits (line + neutral NOT bonded to earth on secondary side) → IMD + LIM in parallel → Equipotential Bonding Busbar (PEB) in OT.

**Key Components:**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Isolation Transformer** | Decouples secondary from earth; turns ratio 1:1; leakage reactance limits first-fault current |
| **Insulation Monitoring Device (IMD)** | Continuously injects a low-frequency AC measuring signal and measures insulation resistance; alarms if $R_{\text{ins}} \lt 50 \, \text{k}\Omega$ |
| **Line Isolation Monitor (LIM)** | Calculates the **hazard current** (current that would flow through a person touching line and earth simultaneously); displays on a panel in the OT |
| **Equipotential Bonding Bar (PEB)** | Bonds all chassis and extraneous parts to a common potential within the OT |

**Operating Principle:**
- Under **normal conditions**: no current flows to earth on the secondary side; the system is "floating."
- On a **first fault** (e.g., live-to-chassis): current returns through the insulation capacitance and the IMD detects the drop in insulation resistance → **audible and visual alarm** in the OT; the supply is **NOT disconnected** (surgery continues).
- On a **second fault** (different phase-to-chassis): the IMD trips the protective device to prevent overcurrent.

> (3 marks for diagram; 2 marks for IMD; 2 marks for LIM; total 7)

---

### **Model Solution — Part (b) of Question B**

**Given:** $U_0 = 230 \, \text{V}$, $R_{\text{ins}} = 100 \, \text{k}\Omega = 10^5 \, \Omega$.

**(i) Leakage Current to Ground:**

The leakage (fault) current is limited by the insulation resistance:

$$
I_{\text{leak}} = \frac{U_0}{R_{\text{ins}}}
$$

$$
I_{\text{leak}} = \frac{230}{10^5}
$$

$$
I_{\text{leak}} = 2.3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A}
$$

$$
I_{\text{leak}} = 2.3 \text{ mA}
$$

**(ii) Operational Safety Comment:**

- The standard IMD alarm threshold is $R_{\text{ins}} \lt 50 \, \text{k}\Omega$.
- At $R_{\text{ins}} = 100 \, \text{k}\Omega$, the system is **above the alarm threshold** but indicates **degrading insulation**.
- The leakage current of **2.3 mA** is below the **let-go threshold** (10 mA) and the **ventricular fibrillation threshold** (30 mA) for macroshock — so it is **not immediately hazardous** to a person in normal contact.
- However, in a **cardiac-protected environment** (Type CF applied parts), the patient may be in a microshock-susceptible state, where even 10 μA is dangerous. The 2.3 mA leakage, if it flowed through an intracardiac catheter, would be **catastrophic**.
- **Recommendation:** The system **may continue to operate** in non-cardiac applications with vigilant monitoring, but **must be scheduled for immediate maintenance** before the next cardiac procedure. The IMD will alarm if resistance falls below 50 kΩ.

> (3 marks for part i calculation; 4 marks for part ii safety reasoning with macroshock vs microshock distinction)

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Bullet Checklist**

- [ ] **Indirect contact** = touching a part that became live due to **insulation failure** (not a normally live conductor).
- [ ] **Class I equipment** uses **Protective Earth (PE)** + automatic disconnection — symbol: $\oplus$.
- [ ] **Class II equipment** uses **double / reinforced insulation** — symbol: $\square$.
- [ ] **Class III equipment** uses **SELV** ($\leq 25 \, \text{V AC}$) — symbol: Roman III.
- [ ] **Touch voltage limit (general):** $U_L \leq 50 \, \text{V AC}$ for > 1 s; in medical: **25 V AC** is preferred.
- [ ] **Disconnection time:** **0.4 s** in TN; **0.1 s** in medical TT systems; **no automatic disconnection** on first fault in IT systems.
- [ ] **RCD standard trip currents:** 30 mA (personnel); 6 mA (medical sensitive areas); 300 mA (fire protection).
- [ ] **Microshock threshold:** **10 μA** direct to heart; this is why OTs use IT systems and equipotential bonding.
- [ ] **Macroshock thresholds:** perception 1 mA; let-go 10 mA; respiratory paralysis 30 mA; ventricular fibrillation 100 mA (1 s).
- [ ] **Medical IT system:** Isolation transformer + IMD ($R_{\text{ins}} \lt 50 \, \text{k}\Omega$ alarm) + LIM + equipotential bonding.
- [ ] **Equipotential bonding resistance (patient environment):** $R \leq 0.1 \, \Omega$ (verified with 25 A, 6 V AC).
- [ ] **Adiabatic PE sizing:** $S = \dfrac{\sqrt{I^2 t}}{k}$ → always round **UP** to the next standard size.
- [ ] **Standard fault loop impedance check:** $Z_s \leq 0.35 \, \Omega$ typical for verification (TT systems may be higher).
- [ ] **Standards to remember:** IEC 60601-1 (medical equipment safety), IEC 60364-7-710 (medical locations), IEC 60479-1 (body current effects).
- [ ] **Indian nominal voltage:** **230 V** AC, 50 Hz (not 220 V).
- [ ] **Applied part types:** **B** (body), **BF** (body floating), **CF** (cardiac floating) — CF has the strictest leakage limits.
- [ ] **IMD ≠ LIM:** IMD measures insulation resistance; LIM computes hazard current. Both are needed in modern OTs.
- [ ] **Continuity of supply** in IT systems is paramount — surgery does NOT stop on a single fault, but staff are alerted.
